## Agent 1 continuation — O1 same-branch cell mass

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Same-branch O1 is not proved by the current machinery.}
}
\]

The diagonal mass is healthy. The \(\alpha\)-tilt is not the main obstruction. The cell width is acceptable if \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\). The failure point is precise:

\[
\boxed{
\text{we cannot rule out same-cell off-diagonal cancellation on the actual orbit }
m_0\log n
\text{ at } Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
}
\]

So Agent 1’s deliverable is a **no-go/reduction**: same-branch cell mass must remain an explicit actual-orbit nonrecurrence input unless someone proves the off-diagonal autocorrelation estimate below.

The coordinator’s instruction asks Agent 1 to attack exactly this same-branch piece and not use phase-relaxed models as proof.  The draft already defines O1 using paired \(B_r^+\), \(B_r^-\), and the full canonical cell vector, and records that cell mass is meant to feed the \(Z_\alpha\)-derivative row.  It also already splits O1 internally into same-branch cell mass plus folded main/dual nonalignment. 

---

## 1. Setup

For one branch, write

\[
B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)
=
\sum_{n\in \mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
a_n^\varepsilon(\alpha,m_0)
e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon m_0\log n},
\qquad
\varepsilon\in\{+,-\},
\]

where \(\sigma_+=1\), \(\sigma_-=-1\) up to the exact AFE sign convention. Then

\[
\|B^\varepsilon\|_2^2
=
\sum_r |B_r^\varepsilon|^2
=
D^\varepsilon+O^\varepsilon,
\]

with

\[
D^\varepsilon
=
\sum_r\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
|a_n^\varepsilon|^2,
\]

and

\[
O^\varepsilon
=
\sum_r
\sum_{\substack{n\ne n'\\ n,n'\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}}
a_n^\varepsilon\overline{a_{n'}^\varepsilon}
e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon m_0\log(n/n')}.
\]

The desired same-branch statement is

\[
\boxed{
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2+\|B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Equivalently, one must prove

\[
\boxed{
O^+(m_0,\alpha)+O^-(m_0,\alpha)
\ge
-(D^++D^-)+Q^{-A}.
}
\]

That is the exact missing estimate.

---

## 2. Diagonal mass is not the blocker

On the packet-frequency support

\[
\mathcal B_{\rm wide}
=
\{n:c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q\},
\qquad 0<c_-<c_+<\frac12,
\]

the AFE plateau coefficients have size schematically

\[
a_n^+(\alpha)\sim n^{-1/2-\alpha}V_+(n),
\qquad
a_n^-(\alpha)\sim n^{-1/2+\alpha}V_-(n),
\]

modulo log/gamma/normalization factors. Hence

\[
D^+
\asymp
\sum_{e^{c_-Q}\le n\le e^{c_+Q}}
n^{-1-2\alpha}|V_+(n)|^2,
\]

and similarly for \(D^-\). For \(|\alpha|Q\lesssim \log Q\), this is at worst polynomially distorted. In particular,

\[
D^++D^-\ge Q^{-C}
\]

after whatever normalization the O1 cell frame uses.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{coefficient normalization and diagonal mass do not explain failure.}
}
\]

---

## 3. The obstruction is same-cell off-diagonal cancellation

The problem is that \(D^\pm\) can be almost exactly cancelled by \(O^\pm\):

\[
\|B^\pm\|_2^2
=
D^\pm+O^\pm.
\]

A proof by diagonal dominance would need something like

\[
|O^+|+|O^-|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})(D^++D^-).
\]

No current argument gives this pointwise at a prescribed \(Z_\alpha\)-zero.

Classical exponential-sum heuristics suggest that same-cell sums should behave randomly because the phases

\[
e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

oscillate rapidly across the cells. But a heuristic, an averaged large-sieve estimate, or a relaxed random-phase model does not imply the pointwise lower bound at

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

The missing theorem is an actual-orbit autocorrelation noncollapse estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}
\Re
\sum_r
\sum_{\substack{n\ne n'\\ n,n'\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}}
a_n^\varepsilon\overline{a_{n'}^\varepsilon}
e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon m_0\log(n/n')}
\ge
-(1-Q^{-A})(D^++D^-).
}
\]

This is the exact same-branch O1a input.

---

## 4. \(\alpha\)-tilt is manageable

The tilt changes branch weights by

\[
e^{-\alpha\log n},
\qquad
e^{+\alpha\log n}
\]

or the folded-dual equivalent. On the fixed support \(\log n\asymp Q\):

- if \(|\alpha|Q\lesssim\log Q\), this changes weights by only a polynomial factor;
- if \(|\alpha|Q\gtrsim\log Q\), one branch may dominate, which helps Agent 2’s folded main/dual problem.

For Agent 1’s same-branch task, \(\alpha\)-tilt does not create the obstruction. It only changes the exponent bookkeeping in \(D^\pm\) and \(O^\pm\).

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(\alpha\)-tilt is not the same-branch blocker.}
}
\]

---

## 5. \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) does not currently help enough

The scalar zero condition gives one global equation:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

In cell language it has schematic form

\[
\sum_r
\left(
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
+
B_r^{\rm bg}
+
B_r^E
\right)
+
\text{outside support}
=
0.
\]

This does **not** imply that the individual same-branch cell vectors are large. It is compatible with

\[
B_r^+\approx0,
\qquad
B_r^-\approx0
\qquad
\text{for all }r,
\]

with the remaining pieces or outside support handling the global zero.

So the proof cannot close from the scalar zero condition alone.

---

## 6. Phase-relaxed no-go

If the phases inside each cell are allowed to vary freely, then because each cell contains many comparable coefficients, the polygon condition is satisfied:

\[
\max_{n\in\mathcal C_r}|a_n|
\le
\sum_{\substack{n'\in\mathcal C_r\\n'\ne n}}|a_{n'}|.
\]

Hence one can choose phases so that

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n e^{i\theta_n}
=
0
\]

for every cell \(r\). Thus

\[
B_r^+=0,
\qquad
B_r^-=0
\qquad
\forall r,
\]

while the diagonal mass remains large:

\[
D^++D^-\gg Q^{-C}.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{diagonal mass + cell geometry cannot prove same-branch O1.}
}
\]

Any proof must use the actual one-parameter orbit

\[
\theta_n=-m_0\log n
\]

and cannot pass through a relaxed torus model.

---

## 7. Conditional theorem Agent 1 can hand to Agent 2

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1a: Same-branch actual-orbit cell mass]
\label{hyp:O1a-same-branch-cell-mass}
Let
\[
\mathcal B_{\rm wide}
=
\{n:c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q\},
\qquad
0<c_-<c_+<\frac12,
\]
and let \(B_r^\pm\) be the paired same-branch cell sums in the ordinary
packet-frequency regime \(\lambda Q\asymp1\), with \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\).
For every admissible ordinary zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), assume
\[
\boxed{
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2+\|B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]
Equivalently, writing
\[
\|B^\pm\|_2^2=D^\pm+O^\pm,
\]
assume the actual-orbit same-cell off-diagonal lower bound
\[
\boxed{
O^+(m_0,\alpha)+O^-(m_0,\alpha)
\ge
-(D^++D^-)+Q^{-A}.
}
\]
\end{hypothesis}
```

This is the clean subinput Agent 2 should use. Agent 2’s folded problem starts only after O1a is granted.

---

}
\]

\[

}

So the precise no-go is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Same-branch O1 cannot currently be proved from diagonal mass, cell geometry,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(\alpha\)-tilt, or the scalar condition }Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
}
\]

It reduces to an unproved actual prime-log autocorrelation nonrecurrence theorem.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:24:08)

I’ll push the same-branch O1 analysis one level deeper: instead of stopping at “off-diagonal cancellation is unproved,” I’ll try to isolate concrete regimes where a proof might be possible and identify a sharper arithmetic obstruction.

A second, sharper point: the actual obstacle is not just “off-diagonal terms exist.” It is that all currently available tools seem to give only averaged-in-\(m\) cell mass, while O1a needs every admissible \(Z_\alpha\)-zero.

_Thought for 1m 21s_

## Agent 1 — continued attack on O1a same-branch mass

### Sharpened conclusion

The same-branch O1 subinput can be pushed to a very precise reduction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a is not an ordinary diagonal-dominance theorem.}
}
\]

It is equivalent to excluding **bad \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros** at which the entire same-branch cell vector is simultaneously small:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r |B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)|^2<Q^{-A}.
\]

The coordinator’s Agent 1 instruction explicitly asks for same-branch cell mass under \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), separating diagonal mass, same-cell off-diagonal cancellation, \(\alpha\)-tilt, and the actual prime-log orbit.  The draft’s current O1 structure already treats fixed-width cell nonrecurrence as a named open arithmetic input, with O1/O2/O3 jointly required for ordinary Gate 1. 

---

# 1. Define the exact same-branch mass functional

For each branch \(\varepsilon\in\{+,-\}\), write

\[
B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
a_n^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon t\log n},
\qquad
\sigma_+=1,\quad \sigma_-=-1,
\]

where the slow \(t\)-dependence of \(a_n^\varepsilon\) contains the AFE/gamma/normalization factors.

Define

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)
:=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
|B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2.
\]

Then same-branch O1a is exactly

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

The internal O1 decomposition in the draft already separates this same-branch mass from the harder folded main/dual nonalignment step; the latter is Agent 2’s problem. 

---

# 2. Averaged same-branch mass is provable

On a height interval \(I=[T,T+H]\), with \(Q=\log T\), expand

\[
\int_I \mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)\,dt
=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
\sum_{n,n'\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
a_n^\varepsilon\overline{a_{n'}^\varepsilon}
\int_I
e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon t\log(n/n')}\,dt.
\]

The diagonal contribution is

\[
H D_{\rm sb},
\qquad
D_{\rm sb}
=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
|a_n^\varepsilon|^2.
\]

The off-diagonal contribution is bounded by

\[
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
\sum_{\substack{n\ne n'\\ n,n'\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}}
|a_n^\varepsilon a_{n'}^\varepsilon|
\min\left(H,\frac1{|\log(n/n')|}\right).
\]

By the Montgomery–Vaughan mean-value theorem for Dirichlet polynomials, one gets schematically

\[
\int_T^{T+H}|B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2\,dt
=
H\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}|a_n^\varepsilon|^2
+
O\left(
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
n|a_n^\varepsilon|^2
\right),
\]

so if

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\qquad
\log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad c_+<\frac12,
\]

then

\[
\frac1H\int_T^{T+H}\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)\,dt
=
D_{\rm sb}\left(1+O(T^{-\eta})\right).
\]

Since \(D_{\rm sb}\ge Q^{-C}\) after the O1 normalization,

\[
\boxed{
\frac1H\int_T^{T+H}\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)\,dt
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

So the same-branch cell mass is true **on average in \(t\)**.

---

# 3. Why the average does not imply O1a

O1a needs the lower bound at every admissible zero:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

The averaged estimate only gives

\[
\operatorname{meas}
\left\{
t\in[T,T+H]:
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)<Q^{-A}
\right\}
\ll H
\]

for suitable \(A\), and even this is too crude unless one has a variance or small-value estimate. It does **not** imply

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

for each zero \(m_j\) of \(Z_\alpha\).

This is the main obstruction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{available mean-square estimates are continuous-in-\(t\) statements;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a is a discrete zero-conditioned pointwise statement.}
}
\]

The draft’s own terminal ledger warns that O1 is named open and only diagnostically supported, not proved by downstream repairs. 

---

# 4. Bad-zero exclusion is the missing theorem

Define the bad same-branch zero set

\[
\mathcal Z_{\rm bad}^{\rm sb}(T,\alpha;A)
=
\left\{
m\in[T,2T]:
Z_\alpha(m)=0,\ 
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m,\alpha)<Q^{-A}
\right\}.
\]

Then same-branch O1a is exactly

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z_{\rm bad}^{\rm sb}(T,\alpha;A)=\varnothing
}
\]

for some fixed polynomial exponent \(A\).

A realistic intermediate theorem would be a quantitative zero-conditioned small-value exclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\substack{m\in[T,2T]\\Z_\alpha(m)=0}}
w_m
\mathbf 1_{\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m,\alpha)<Q^{-A}}
=0,
}
\]

or, more flexibly,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\substack{m\in[T,2T]\\Z_\alpha(m)=0}}
w_m
\prod_{\varepsilon,r}
\left(
1+\frac{|B_r^\varepsilon(m,\alpha)|^2}{Q^{-A}}
\right)^{-1}
\ll T^{-100}.
}
\]

This would turn averaged cell mass into pointwise cell mass at zeros. But no current part of the draft proves such a statement.

---

# 5. The scalar zero condition is too weak

At \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the AFE gives one complex equation:

\[
0
=
Z_\alpha(m_0)
=
\sum_r
\left(
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
+
B_r^{\rm bg}
+
B_r^E
\right)
+
\text{unassigned tail}.
\]

This does not prevent

\[
B_r^+(m_0,\alpha)\approx0,
\qquad
B_r^-(m_0,\alpha)\approx0
\quad\text{for all }r.
\]

Indeed, the scalar zero condition couples only the **sum of cells**, while O1a requires a lower bound on the **vector of cells**.

So the failed implication is:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\centernot\Longrightarrow
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

without an additional prime-log nonrecurrence theorem.

---

# 6. Stronger no-go: diagonal mass cannot survive phase relaxation

For one cell, diagonal mass is

\[
D_r^\varepsilon
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
|a_n^\varepsilon|^2.
\]

But the cell sum is

\[
B_r^\varepsilon
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
a_n^\varepsilon e^{i\theta_n}.
\]

If phases \(\theta_n\) are allowed to vary independently, then, because each fixed-width cell contains many comparable coefficients, the polygon condition usually holds:

\[
\max_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}|a_n^\varepsilon|
\le
\sum_{\substack{n'\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon\\n'\ne n}}
|a_{n'}^\varepsilon|.
\]

Hence one can choose phases so that

\[
B_r^\varepsilon=0
\]

for every \(r,\varepsilon\), while

\[
D_{\rm sb}=\sum_{\varepsilon,r}D_r^\varepsilon\gg Q^{-C}.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{same-branch O1a cannot be proved from diagonal mass and cell geometry alone.}
}
\]

It must use the actual one-parameter orbit

\[
\theta_n(t)=-t\log n.
\]

This matches the draft’s guardrail that phase-relaxed models break O1 and that O1 must be actual one-parameter prime-log arithmetic. 

---

# 7. Best possible Agent 1 theorem skeleton

Here is the clean theorem skeleton that would close Agent 1 if proved.

```latex
\begin{theorem}[Same-branch O1 from zero-conditioned cell large sieve]
\label{thm:O1a-from-zero-conditioned-cell-large-sieve}
Fix \(0<c_-<c_+<1/2\), and let
\[
\mathcal B_{\rm wide}
=
\{n:c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q\}.
\]
Let \(B_r^\pm(t,\alpha)\) be the paired same-branch cell sums in the
ordinary packet-frequency regime \(\lambda Q\asymp1\), with
\(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\).  Define
\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_r|B_r^+(t,\alpha)|^2
+
\sum_r|B_r^-(t,\alpha)|^2.
\]
Assume the diagonal normalization
\[
D_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
|a_n^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-C_0}
\]
uniformly on the ordinary range.

Assume moreover the zero-conditioned actual-orbit small-value exclusion:
there exists \(A>C_0\) such that, for every admissible ordinary zero
\(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),
\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
Then the same-branch part of O1 holds:
\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2+\|B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]
\end{theorem}
```

This theorem is tautological if the small-value exclusion is assumed, but it correctly names the missing theorem. The nontrivial content must be the following lemma.

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1a: zero-conditioned actual-orbit cell small-value exclusion]
\label{hyp:O1a-zero-conditioned-cell-small-value-exclusion}
For the fixed-width support
\[
\mathcal B_{\rm wide}
=
\{n:c_-Q\le\log n\le c_+Q\},
\qquad 0<c_-<c_+<1/2,
\]
and paired cells of width \(\asymp R^{-1}\), the actual prime-log orbit obeys
\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
\left|
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
a_n^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)
e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon m_0\log n}
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
Equivalently, no admissible \(Z_\alpha\)-zero is a polynomial-scale common
small value of all same-branch cell Dirichlet polynomials.
\end{hypothesis}
```

---

# 8. What would not be enough

The following would **not** close Agent 1:

### Not enough 1 — ordinary continuous mean square

\[
\int_T^{2T}\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)\,dt\gg TQ^{-C}.
\]

This permits bad isolated zeros.

### Not enough 2 — averaged estimate over zeros

\[
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)
\gg N(T)Q^{-C}.
\]

This permits exceptional bad zeros.

### Not enough 3 — plus-only cell mass

\[
\sum_r|B_r^+(m_0,\alpha)|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Useful, but it is not stable under branch normalization unless the dual branch and \(\alpha\)-tilt are also controlled.

### Not enough 4 — phase-relaxed random model

Random-phase evidence can explain why O1a is plausible, but it cannot prove actual-orbit nonrecurrence.

---

\]

\]

## Agent 1 — O1a zero-conditioned small-value attack

I tested the four requested mechanisms: residue/contour, zero-density or zero-repulsion, \(\alpha\)-variation, and \(m\)-variation. The result is negative but sharper than before.

---

# 1. Target restatement

Let

\[
\mathcal B(t,\alpha)
=
\left(B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)\right)_{\varepsilon=\pm,r},
\]

and

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
|B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2.
\]

O1a is

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

The proposed zero-restricted bound is of the form

\[
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}
w_j
\prod_{\varepsilon,r}
\left(
1+\frac{|B_r^\varepsilon(m_j,\alpha)|^2}{\eta}
\right)^{-1}
\ll T^{-100},
\qquad
\eta=Q^{-A}.
\]

If \(w_j\ge Q^{-C_w}\) and the number of cells is

\[
N_{\rm cell}\asymp R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

then this bound would imply O1a. Indeed, if some zero had

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)<\eta,
\]

then each summand satisfies

\[
1+\frac{|B_r^\varepsilon(m_j,\alpha)|^2}{\eta}\le 2
\]

up to harmless redistribution, so the product is at least

\[
2^{-N_{\rm cell}}=Q^{-o(1)}.
\]

That single bad zero would contribute

\[
\ge Q^{-C_w-o(1)},
\]

which contradicts \(T^{-100}\) for large \(T\).

So the proposed zero-restricted inequality is a valid sufficient mechanism. The question is whether any available route proves it. I do not see a proof from current tools.

---

# 2. Route 1 — residue / contour integral

## Candidate idea

Use the explicit zero sum

\[
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0} H(m_j)
=
\frac{1}{2\pi i}
\int_{\partial\Omega}
H(z)\frac{Z_\alpha'(z)}{Z_\alpha(z)}\,dz,
\]

with

\[
H(t)
\approx
\prod_{\varepsilon,r}
\left(
1+\frac{|B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2}{\eta}
\right)^{-1}.
\]

If \(H\) were positive, analytic, small away from bad cell-collapse points, and controllable on \(\partial\Omega\), this could prove the desired zero-restricted exclusion.

## Failure

The natural penalty uses \(|B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2\), which is not holomorphic. One can replace it on the real line by

\[
B_r^\varepsilon(z,\alpha)
B_r^{\varepsilon,*}(z,\alpha),
\qquad
B^*(z)=\overline{B(\bar z)},
\]

but then the holomorphic penalty has product/subset frequencies. After multiplying over \(O(R)\) cells, the analytic object has effective frequency complexity

\[
\exp(CQR),
\qquad
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

so any contour or interpolation argument that inverts this frequency system pays a super-polynomial loss:

\[
\exp\!\left(CQ\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right),
\]

not \(Q^A\).

A second obstruction is the denominator. Using \(Z_\alpha'/Z_\alpha\) counts zeros, but gives no sign or smallness contradiction. Using \(1/Z_\alpha\) gives residues involving

\[
\frac{1}{Z_\alpha'(m_j)},
\]

which requires a lower bound for \(Z_\alpha'(m_j)\). But that derivative-row lower bound is essentially downstream of O1 itself, so this is circular.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Residue/contour methods reduce O1a to a polynomial-loss analytic Bezout theorem.}
}
\]

The required missing certificate would be something like:

\[
U_0 Z_\alpha
+
\sum_{\varepsilon,r}U_{\varepsilon,r}B_r^\varepsilon
=
1+\mathcal E,
\]

with

\[
\|U_0\|_\infty+\sum_{\varepsilon,r}\|U_{\varepsilon,r}\|_\infty\le Q^A,
\qquad
\|\mathcal E\|_\infty\le Q^{-B}.
\]

No current construction gives this with polynomial loss.

---

# 3. Route 2 — zero-density / zero-repulsion

## Candidate idea

Try to show that if all cells are small at a zero,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)<Q^{-A},
\]

then \(Z_\alpha\) must have an extra nearby zero, an anomalously small derivative, or a local zero cluster. Then use zero-density or zero-repulsion estimates.

## Failure

The smallness of the fixed-width same-branch cells does not imply

\[
Z_\alpha'(m_0)\approx0.
\]

The scalar zero equation has schematic form

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)
=
\sum_r
\left(
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
+
B_r^{\rm bg}
+
B_r^E
\right)
+
\text{outside support}
=
0.
\]

Even if every same-branch fixed-width cell is small, the derivative of \(Z_\alpha\) may be supplied by:

\[
B_r^{\rm bg},\quad B_r^E,\quad
\text{folded-dual pieces},\quad
\text{outside-support AFE terms},\quad
\text{gamma/phase variation}.
\]

So a bad O1a zero need not be multiple or near-multiple. It can be an ordinary simple zero whose local slope comes from components not measured by \(\mathcal S_{\rm sb}\).

Thus zero-density and zero-repulsion do not see the event

\[
\mathcal B(m_0,\alpha)\approx0.
\]

They count zeros of \(Z_\alpha\), not auxiliary cell-vector collapses at those zeros.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Zero-density/repulsion does not imply O1a unless augmented by a theorem linking cell collapse to zero multiplicity.}
}
\]

That link is currently absent.

---

# 4. Route 3 — \(\alpha\)-variation

## Candidate idea

At a bad zero,

\[
\mathcal B(m_0,\alpha)\approx0.
\]

Differentiate in \(\alpha\). Since AFE branch weights contain factors like \(n^{-\alpha}\) or \(n^{+\alpha}\), perhaps smallness at one \(\alpha\) forces instability or contradiction under \(\alpha\)-motion.

## Local calculation

For one branch and one cell,

\[
B_r(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n(\alpha,t)e^{-it\log n}.
\]

Ignoring slower gamma/weight terms for the moment,

\[
\partial_\alpha B_r
=
-\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
(\log n)a_n e^{-it\log n}
+
\text{lower-order weight terms}.
\]

Write

\[
\log n=\xi_r+\delta_n,
\]

where \(\xi_r\) is the cell center. Then

\[
\partial_\alpha B_r
=
-\xi_r B_r
-
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
\delta_n a_ne^{-it\log n}
+
\text{lower-order terms}.
\]

At a bad zero, \(B_r\approx0\), but the fluctuation term

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
\delta_n a_ne^{-it\log n}
\]

need not be small. Therefore \(\partial_\alpha B_r\) can be large even when \(B_r\) is tiny.

If one follows the moving zero \(m(\alpha)\), then

\[
\frac{d}{d\alpha}B_r(m(\alpha),\alpha)
=
\partial_\alpha B_r
+
m'(\alpha)\partial_m B_r,
\]

with

\[
m'(\alpha)
=
-\frac{\partial_\alpha Z_\alpha}{\partial_m Z_\alpha}.
\]

This requires a lower bound on \(\partial_m Z_\alpha\), again touching the O1 derivative-row problem. Even with that lower bound, a function can be small at a single parameter value without remaining small on an \(\alpha\)-interval.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-variation does not rule out isolated bad zeros.}
}
\]

It could prove O1a only with an additional persistence theorem:

\[
\mathcal B(m_0,\alpha_0)\approx0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal B(m(\alpha),\alpha)\approx0
\text{ on an }\alpha\text{-interval of polynomial length}.
\]

No such persistence theorem follows from the AFE weights.

---

# 5. Route 4 — \(m\)-derivative / local persistence

## Candidate idea

If all cells are small at \(m_0\), maybe they remain small on a short \(m\)-interval. Then averaged same-branch mass would contradict the bad zero.

## Local calculation

For one cell,

\[
\partial_m B_r(t,\alpha)
=
-i\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
(\log n)a_n e^{-it\log n}.
\]

Again write

\[
\log n=\xi_r+\delta_n.
\]

Then

\[
\partial_m B_r
=
-i\xi_r B_r
-
i\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
\delta_n a_n e^{-it\log n}.
\]

At a bad zero, \(B_r(m_0,\alpha)\approx0\), but the fluctuation derivative may be large. A cell sum can cross zero with slope of size roughly

\[
Q\cdot \left(\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}|a_n|^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

The natural oscillation scale is

\[
\Delta m\asymp Q^{-1},
\]

which is also the zeta zero-spacing scale. Thus smallness at one zero need not persist over enough interval length to contradict continuous-in-\(t\) mean-square mass.

Averaged mass over long intervals remains compatible with isolated exact collapses at a sparse prescribed set of points.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
m\text{-variation cannot prove O1a without a reverse Bernstein/observability theorem at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
}
\]

The missing theorem would have to say that common smallness of all cell sums at a zeta zero forces smallness on a measurable neighborhood large enough to contradict the cell mean square. Current exponential-polynomial estimates do not give that.

---

# 6. Final obstruction theorem

The strongest clean formulation is the following.

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1a-ZCO: zero-conditioned cell observability]
\label{hyp:O1a-ZCO-zero-conditioned-cell-observability}
Let
\[
\mathcal B(t,\alpha)
=
\left(B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)\right)_{\varepsilon=\pm,r}
\]
be the paired same-branch fixed-width cell vector associated to the
canonical AFE packet in the ordinary regime
\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\qquad
\mathcal B_{\rm wide}
=
\{n:c_-Q\le\log n\le c_+Q\},
\qquad
0<c_-<c_+<1/2.
\]
Assume \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\) and
\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]
There exist constants \(A,C>0\) such that every admissible ordinary zero
\(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r
|B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]
Equivalently, the actual prime-log orbit has no admissible
\(Z_\alpha\)-zero at which all same-branch fixed-width cell Dirichlet
polynomials are simultaneously polynomially small.
\end{hypothesis}
```

A stronger sufficient smoothed-zero form is:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[Smoothed zero-restricted O1a exclusion]
\label{hyp:smoothed-zero-restricted-O1a-exclusion}
Let
\[
\eta=Q^{-A}
\]
and suppose the zero weights satisfy \(w_j\ge Q^{-C_w}\).  Then
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}
w_j
\prod_{\varepsilon=\pm,r}
\left(
1+\frac{|B_r^\varepsilon(m_j,\alpha)|^2}{\eta}
\right)^{-1}
\ll T^{-100}.
}
\]
\end{hypothesis}
```

This implies O1a by the single-bad-zero contribution argument in §1.

---

# 7. Mechanism audit ledger

\[
\boxed{
\text{Residue / contour integral route: Red.}
}
\]

Reason: requires a polynomial-loss analytic Bezout certificate; natural holomorphic penalties introduce subset-frequency complexity and super-polynomial losses.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Zero-density / zero-repulsion route: Red.}
}
\]

Reason: bad O1a zeros need not be multiple, near-multiple, or clustered zeros of \(Z_\alpha\).

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-variation route: Yellow/Red.}
}
\]

Reason: may be useful only if one proves persistence of badness in \(\alpha\); isolated badness is not contradicted by AFE weight variation.

\[
\boxed{
m\text{-derivative route: Yellow/Red.}
}
\]

Reason: would need reverse Bernstein or zero-conditioned observability; ordinary derivative bounds permit fast crossing on scale \(1/Q\).

---

# 8. Final Agent 1 deliverable

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a is not closed.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a requires a new zero-conditioned actual-orbit cell observability theorem.}
}
\]

The missing theorem is not a diagonal mass estimate, not a continuous mean-square estimate, not a zero-density estimate, and not a phase-relaxed noncancellation statement. It must directly rule out

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)\approx0
\quad
\text{for all }(\varepsilon,r),
\]

for the actual prime-log phases

\[
e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

and the canonical AFE branch data.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:38:13)

I’ll push one more layer into Agent 1’s O1a problem by replacing the previous qualitative obstruction with a concrete operator-theoretic formulation: a sampling/observability inequality on the zero set.

A more promising modification is emerging: same-branch *value* mass may be too strong. A finite jet of the same cell vector might be recoverable from local mean-square plus Bernstein-type bounds, but only if the downstream O1-to-row bridge can accept jets rather than values.

_Thought for 1m 4s_

## Agent 1 — continuing O1a attack

### New angle tested

I tried to weaken the bad O1a value condition into something more robust:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\varepsilon,r}|B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Maybe this value lower bound is too strong. A natural escape is to replace it by a **finite-jet observability** statement:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\varepsilon,r}\sum_{k=0}^{K}
\Delta_r^{-2k}
\left|
\partial_m^k B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where \(\Delta_r\) is the residual frequency width of the \(r\)-th cell.

This would be useful if the downstream O1-to-derivative-row bridge can consume finite jets instead of only cell values.

The result is again negative, but it sharpens the obstruction.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Even finite-jet O1a is not available from local exponential-polynomial geometry.}
}
\]

It still requires an actual prime-log zero-conditioned nonrecurrence theorem.

---

# 1. Centered cell decomposition

For one cell, write

\[
B_r(t)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n e^{-it\log n}.
\]

Let \(\xi_r\) be the cell center and write

\[
\log n=\xi_r+\delta_n,
\qquad
|\delta_n|\le \Delta_r.
\]

Then

\[
B_r(t)
=
e^{-it\xi_r}
F_r(t),
\]

where

\[
F_r(t)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n e^{-it\delta_n}.
\]

The rapidly rotating carrier \(e^{-it\xi_r}\) is irrelevant for magnitude. The real local complexity of a cell is governed by

\[
\Delta_r\asymp \frac{Q}{M_{\rm cell}}.
\]

If \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\), then

\[
\Delta_r\asymp \frac QR.
\]

So the centered cell sum changes on scale

\[
\Delta_r^{-1}\asymp \frac RQ,
\]

which is longer than the average zeta-zero spacing \(1/Q\) by a factor of \(R\).

This looks promising at first: if \(B_r(m_0)\) is small at one zero, maybe it remains small across several neighboring zeros.

But quantitatively it does not close O1a.

---

# 2. Persistence from one bad zero is too weak

Suppose

\[
|F_r(m_0)|\le \eta^{1/2},
\qquad
\eta=Q^{-A}.
\]

The derivative bound is only

\[
|F_r'(t)|
\le
\Delta_r
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}|a_n|,
\]

or in \(L^2\)-normalized form,

\[
|F_r'(t)|
\lesssim
\Delta_r N_r^{1/2}
\left(\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}|a_n|^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Even if one uses the sharper local mean-square scale, the time needed for \(F_r\) to grow from \(Q^{-A/2}\) to its natural diagonal size \(Q^{-C/2}\) can be as small as

\[
\delta
\asymp
\frac{Q^{-A/2}}{\Delta_r Q^{-C/2}}
=
\frac{R}{Q}\,Q^{-(A-C)/2}.
\]

If \(A\) is large, this is much smaller than the zero spacing \(1/Q\). Therefore a cell can be tiny at one zero and recover before the next zero.

So the earlier “slow cell variation” intuition does not prove bad-zero exclusion unless the bad threshold is very close to the natural diagonal scale. O1a needs a polynomial lower bound with unspecified exponent, so this persistence argument is insufficient.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Single-zero collapse need not persist across neighboring zeros.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Finite-jet upgrade

Try the stronger condition:

\[
\mathcal J_{K,r}(m_0)
=
\left(
F_r(m_0),
\Delta_r^{-1}F_r'(m_0),
\ldots,
\Delta_r^{-K}F_r^{(K)}(m_0)
\right).
\]

Because

\[
F_r^{(k)}(m_0)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
(-i\delta_n)^k a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n},
\]

the jet entries are moments of the same cell coefficients:

\[
\Delta_r^{-k}F_r^{(k)}(m_0)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
(-i)^k
\left(\frac{\delta_n}{\Delta_r}\right)^k
a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n}.
\]

Thus a finite-jet lower bound would say that not all bounded-window moments

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
p_k(\delta_n/\Delta_r)
a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n},
\qquad 0\le k\le K,
\]

can be small at a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero.

This is a more plausible target than value-only O1a.

But it is still not a consequence of local geometry.

---

# 4. Finite jets also have a phase-relaxed obstruction

Fix \(K\). Inside one cell, there are vastly more than \(K+1\) frequencies. In a phase-relaxed model, one can arrange

\[
F_r^{(k)}(m_0)=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le K,
\]

while keeping diagonal mass large.

Indeed, the equations are

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n}\delta_n^k
=
0,
\qquad
0\le k\le K.
\]

This is a finite vector-balancing problem in \(\mathbb C^{K+1}\). Since the cell contains many comparable coefficient vectors

\[
a_n(1,\delta_n,\ldots,\delta_n^K),
\]

the convex hull contains the origin after allowing phases, at least in the phase-relaxed model.

Therefore no argument based only on:

\[
\text{many terms}+\text{bounded cell width}+\text{healthy diagonal mass}
\]

can prove even jet-enhanced O1a.

The actual one-parameter restriction

\[
e^{-im_0\delta_n}
\]

is again essential.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Finite-jet O1a is also an actual-orbit arithmetic statement.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Sampling inequality formulation

The cleanest current formulation is a zero-set sampling inequality.

Let

\[
\mathcal B_K(t,\alpha)
=
\left(
\Delta_r^{-k}\partial_t^kB_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)
\right)_{\varepsilon,r,0\le k\le K}.
\]

A jet-enhanced O1a input would be:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal B_K(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

A smoothed zero-restricted version would be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}
w_j
\prod_{\varepsilon,r,k}
\left(
1+
\frac{
|\Delta_r^{-k}\partial_t^kB_r^\varepsilon(m_j,\alpha)|^2
}{Q^{-A}}
\right)^{-1}
\ll T^{-100}.
}
\]

This would imply the pointwise jet lower bound exactly as before: a single bad zero contributes at least \(Q^{-o(1)}w_j\), contradicting \(T^{-100}\).

---

# 6. Can jet-O1a replace value-O1a?

Possibly, but only if the downstream bridge is modified.

Current O1a feeds a derivative-row lower bound by showing that the cell vector itself carries mass. If instead one has jet mass, the bridge must show:

\[
\|\mathcal B_K(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

or whichever derivative row O1 supplies.

This is plausible only if the operators \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\) used in the \(Z_\alpha\)-row can access the same finite cell jets. In frequency language, one needs a stable frame inequality of the form

\[
\sum_{\ell}
\left|
\sum_{\varepsilon,r,k}
c_{\ell,\varepsilon,r,k}
\Delta_r^{-k}\partial_t^kB_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|\mathcal B_K(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2
\]

after quotienting the null directions.

But this is not automatic. If the derivative-row operators only see a low-dimensional projection of the jet vector, then jet mass could live in invisible directions.

So a jet replacement creates two separate obligations:

\[
\boxed{
\text{J-O1a: zero-conditioned finite-jet observability,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{Jet-to-row frame transfer.}
}
\]

The second is finite-dimensional and may be easier than the first. The first remains arithmetic.

---

# 7. Interaction with O1b

Jet-O1a does not eliminate O1b.

Even if

\[
\|\mathcal B_K^+\|_2^2+\|\mathcal B_K^-\|_2^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

the folded vector can still anti-align jetwise:

\[
\mathcal B_K^+
\approx
-e^{i\Theta_Z}\mathcal B_K^-.
\]

Thus O1b would need a jet-level version:

\[
\left\|
\frac{\mathcal B_K^+}{\|\mathcal B_K^+\|}
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}
\frac{\mathcal B_K^-}{\|\mathcal B_K^-\|}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So jetting does not remove the phase-lock problem; it only makes O1a less brittle.

---

# 8. Stronger no-go

For any fixed finite \(K\ll \#\mathcal C_r\), local exponential-polynomial geometry permits:

\[
F_r^{(k)}(m_0)=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le K,
\]

in every cell, while maintaining

\[
\sum_r\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}|a_n|^2\gg Q^{-C}.
\]

Therefore neither value-O1a nor finite-jet-O1a can be proved from:

\[
\text{diagonal mass},
\quad
\text{cell width},
\quad
\text{mean square},
\quad
\text{Bernstein bounds},
\quad
\text{finite-dimensional frame algebra}.
\]

Both require a theorem that the actual zeta-zero orbit does not realize those finite Hermite cancellations.

---

# 9. Updated Agent 1 status

The best refinement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Replace value-only O1a by a possible jet-enhanced O1a, if the bridge allows it.}
}
\]

But this is not a proof. It merely relocates the input to a more robust form.

The exact missing theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Zero-conditioned finite-jet cell observability for actual prime-log phases.}
}
\]

That theorem would say:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\varepsilon,r,k\le K}
\Delta_r^{-2k}
\left|
\partial_t^kB_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Without such an arithmetic input, O1a remains open.

Final assessment:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a value lower bound: Red.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a finite-jet replacement: Yellow as architecture, Red as proof.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Main missing input: actual zero-set sampling/observability for cell Dirichlet jets.}
}
\]

## Agent 1 — deeper attack on O1a

### New finding

The O1a pointwise lower bound is not merely unproved. In its current finite-cell form, it is **statistically implausible** under the standard random-cell model.

The target is

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)
:=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_r |B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Previously I reduced the issue to zero-conditioned cell-vector observability. Pushing further, the obstruction is sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{With only } O(R) \text{ complex cell constraints, a polynomial small-ball event is too large}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{to be excluded at all } \asymp TQ \text{ zeros in height } [T,2T].
}
\]

Here

\[
Q=\log T,
\qquad
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

---

# 1. Random-cell model for O1a

Normalize the same-branch cell vector by its diagonal mass. Suppose

\[
D_{\rm sb}
=
\sum_{\varepsilon,r}\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r^\varepsilon}
|a_n^\varepsilon|^2
\asymp Q^{-C_0}.
\]

Let

\[
M:=\#\{(\varepsilon,r)\}\asymp R.
\]

The usual random-phase model says that at a typical zero, after normalization,

\[
X(m_j)
:=
D_{\rm sb}^{-1/2}
\left(B_r^\varepsilon(m_j,\alpha)\right)_{\varepsilon,r}
\]

should behave approximately like a complex Gaussian vector in \(\mathbb C^M\), with

\[
\mathbf E\|X\|_2^2\asymp 1.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)<Q^{-A}
\]

is equivalent to

\[
\|X(m_j)\|_2^2
<
Q^{-(A-C_0)}.
\]

For a complex Gaussian vector in \(\mathbb C^M\), the small-ball probability satisfies, up to subexponential factors,

\[
\mathbf P\left(\|X\|_2^2<Q^{-B}\right)
\approx
Q^{-BM},
\qquad
B=A-C_0>0.
\]

Taking logs,

\[
\log \mathbf P
\approx
-BM\log Q.
\]

Since

\[
M\asymp R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this gives

\[
\log \mathbf P
\approx
-
B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}.
\]

---

# 2. Compare with the number of zeros

The number of \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros in height \([T,2T]\) is expected to be of size

\[
N(T)\asymp TQ=e^Q Q.
\]

Therefore

\[
\log N(T)
=
Q+O(\log Q).
\]

The expected number of O1a-bad zeros under the random-cell model is roughly

\[
N(T)\cdot Q^{-BM}.
\]

Its logarithm is

\[
Q+O(\log Q)
-
B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}.
\]

But

\[
Q
\gg
\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Hence

\[
\log \left(N(T)\cdot Q^{-BM}\right)
\to +\infty.
\]

So the random model predicts not merely the possibility of bad zeros, but **many** bad zeros:

\[
\boxed{
N_{\rm bad}
\approx
\exp\left(
Q-
B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}
\right)
\to\infty.
}
\]

This means the current O1a demand,

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{for every admissible zero }m_j,
\]

is anti-random at the present cell dimension.

---

# 3. Consequence: finite \(R\)-cell O1a is probably too strong

This is stronger than saying “averages are insufficient.”

The dimension-counting says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A polynomial lower bound at every zero is not what a random finite-cell model predicts.}
}
\]

With

\[
M\asymp R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the small-ball codimension is only

\[
M\log Q
\asymp
\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}.
\]

But to exclude all bad zeros by small-ball counting among \(e^Q\) samples, one needs codimension at least

\[
\asymp Q.
\]

Thus one would need

\[
M\log Q\gtrsim Q,
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
M\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
}
\]

The current number of cells is far smaller:

\[
M\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\ll
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So unless the \(Z_\alpha\)-zero set is extraordinarily anti-correlated with same-branch cell collapse, O1a in its current form should be expected to fail.

---

# 4. Jet-enhanced O1a also fails unless the jet dimension is huge

Previously I considered replacing value O1a with a finite-jet version:

\[
\sum_{\varepsilon,r,k\le K}
\Delta_r^{-2k}
\left|\partial_t^kB_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The dimension becomes roughly

\[
M_{\rm jet}
\asymp R(K+1).
\]

The same small-ball calculation says that to rule out bad zeros by dimension alone, one needs

\[
R(K+1)\log Q
\gtrsim Q.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this requires

\[
K+1
\gtrsim
\frac{Q}{R\log Q}
\asymp
\frac{Q\log\log Q}{(\log Q)^2}.
\]

That is enormous compared with the current finite-depth local algebra.

So low-order jet enhancement does not fix the issue. Even \(K\asymp R\) gives

\[
M_{\rm jet}\asymp R^2
\asymp
\frac{(\log Q)^2}{(\log\log Q)^2},
\]

and therefore

\[
M_{\rm jet}\log Q
\asymp
\frac{(\log Q)^3}{(\log\log Q)^2}
\ll Q.
\]

Thus small-ball bad zeros are still expected.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Finite-depth jet O1a remains statistically too weak unless the depth is much larger.}
}
\]

---

# 5. What this means for the architecture

There are now three possibilities.

## Possibility 1 — O1a is genuinely false as a universal pointwise statement

The random model suggests that for some admissible zeros,

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_j,\alpha)<Q^{-A}
\]

should occur, perhaps many times.

If so, ordinary Gate 1 cannot require O1a at every zero.

The architecture would need a routing alternative:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If O1a fails, the zero must enter a different branch.}
}
\]

For example, O1a failure might have to trigger:

\[
\text{folded anti-lock branch},
\quad
\text{cofactor branch},
\quad
\text{O3 incidence branch},
\quad
\text{singular/transition branch},
\]

or some new “cell-collapse branch.”

But no such branch is currently formulated.

---

## Possibility 2 — increase the number of observed cells

To make polynomial lower-tail exclusion plausible by dimension alone, one needs

\[
M\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

That means replacing

\[
M_{\rm cell}\asymp R
\]

with something like

\[
M_{\rm cell}\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

But then the downstream O1-to-row bridge must handle many more cells. The current local jet space has dimension only

\[
R+2.
\]

A frame map from

\[
\mathbb C^{M_{\rm cell}}
\to
\mathbb C^{R+2}
\]

cannot be injective when

\[
M_{\rm cell}\gg R.
\]

So increasing cell count creates a new projection-nullspace problem.

One could instead use many cells only to prove that **some projection** is large, but then the downstream operators \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\) must be redesigned to capture that projection.

This is a major architectural change.

---

## Possibility 3 — prove a very strong arithmetic anti-correlation theorem

The only way to keep the current O1a statement is to prove something much stronger than random behavior:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_j)=0
\quad\text{actively avoids same-branch cell-collapse events.}
}
\]

That is, the zero set of \(Z_\alpha\) would have to be highly repelled from the small-ball set of

\[
\left(B_r^\varepsilon\right)_{\varepsilon,r}.
\]

This is much stronger than:

\[
\text{zero density},
\quad
\text{pair correlation},
\quad
\text{large sieve},
\quad
\text{mean square},
\quad
\text{generic nonvanishing}.
\]

It would be a new arithmetic theorem of the form:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zeta zeros are polynomially separated from a codimension-}O(R)
\text{ prime-log cell-collapse variety.}
}
\]

Given the dimension count, this theorem would be counter-random rather than random-compatible.

---

# 6. Refined no-go statement

The previous missing theorem was:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The sharper conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{This theorem is too strong unless one supplies non-random zero/cell anti-correlation.}
}
\]

A realistic replacement would be an alternative statement such as:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)
+
\mathcal R_{\rm collapse}(m_0,\alpha)
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where \(\mathcal R_{\rm collapse}\) is a new observable designed to become large when all same-branch cells collapse.

For example:

\[
\mathcal R_{\rm collapse}
\]

could involve:

\[
\partial_m B_r^\varepsilon,
\quad
\partial_\alpha B_r^\varepsilon,
\quad
\text{folded branch difference},
\quad
\text{outside-band AFE mass},
\quad
\text{cofactor vector},
\quad
\text{Wronskian incidence residual}.
\]

But O1a alone, as a universal same-branch value lower bound, looks untenable.

---

# 7. Concrete mathematical recommendation

Agent 1 should report O1a as **Red**, not merely Yellow.

The ordinary Gate 1 package should not depend on:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{for every }Z_\alpha\text{-zero}.
}
\]

Instead, the architecture should replace O1a with a **dichotomy**:

\[
\boxed{
\text{either same-branch cell mass is large,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{cell collapse forces another observable to be large.}
}
\]

The next mathematical task is to identify that compensating observable.

Candidate compensators, in order of plausibility:

\[
\boxed{1.\ \text{same-branch finite jets}}
\]

but only if depth is large enough or if downstream projection avoids nullspaces;

\[
\boxed{2.\ \text{folded main/dual anti-lock residual}}
\]

because same-branch collapse may make folded cancellation structurally harder or easier to classify;

\[
\boxed{3.\ \text{cofactor vector } \mathbf M_{\can}}
\]

because simultaneous small cell values could force high curvature or rank instability;

\[
\boxed{4.\ \text{O3 Wronskian incidence residual}}
\]

because cell collapse may move the burden to the derivative-row angle;

\[
\boxed{5.\ \text{outside-band / tail observability}}
\]

because the scalar zero must then be supplied by the complement of the fixed band.

---

# 8. Final Agent 1 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a as currently stated is not merely unproved; it is statistically suspect.}
}
\]

The small-ball dimension of the observed vector is

\[
O(R)
=
O\left(\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right),
\]

while the number of zeros is

\[
\asymp e^Q Q.
\]

A polynomial lower bound at **every** zero would require either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{many more independent observables: }M\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a new counter-random arithmetic anti-correlation theorem.}
}
\]

Therefore the current O1a input should be replaced by a dichotomy:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\text{cell-collapse branch activates.}
}
\]

The next attack should be to define and test that cell-collapse branch.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:42:02)

I’ll attack the proposed “cell-collapse branch” directly: assume O1a fails at a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero and ask what deterministic quantity must become large, rather than trying to rescue the same value lower bound.

_Thought for 49s_

## Agent 1 — continuing O1a attack

I pushed the proposed **cell-collapse branch** and got a stronger conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a is not just unproved; as a universal pointwise input it is structurally mis-sized.}
}
\]

The issue is dimension. The observed same-branch cell vector has only

\[
M\asymp R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\]

complex degrees of freedom, but the zero set in height \(T=e^Q\) has exponentially many samples:

\[
N(T)\asymp TQ=e^Q Q.
\]

A polynomial lower bound at **every** zero requires far more codimension than the current O1a observable supplies.

---

# 1. Conditional random model at a zero

Let

\[
X(t)=\bigl(B_r^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)\bigr)_{\varepsilon,r}\in\mathbb C^M
\]

be the same-branch cell vector, and let \(Y(t)\) denote everything else in the scalar AFE expression: folded dual mismatch, background, tails, outside-band pieces, etc.

Schematic scalar zero equation:

\[
Z_\alpha(t)=\mathbf 1\cdot X(t)+Y(t).
\]

At a zero \(m_0\),

\[
\mathbf 1\cdot X(m_0)+Y(m_0)=0.
\]

A possible hope was:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\text{repels}\quad
X(m_0)\text{ from }0.
\]

But the standard Gaussian model says the opposite: conditioning on one scalar linear equation does **not** repel a high-dimensional vector from zero enough to prove a polynomial pointwise lower bound.

Assume

\[
X\sim \mathcal N_{\mathbb C}(0,\sigma^2 I_M),
\qquad
Y\sim \mathcal N_{\mathbb C}(0,\tau^2),
\]

independent, and condition on

\[
\mathbf 1\cdot X+Y=0.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{Cov}(X\mid \mathbf 1\cdot X+Y=0)
=
\sigma^2 I_M
-
\frac{\sigma^4}{M\sigma^2+\tau^2}\mathbf 1\mathbf 1^*.
\]

The eigenvalues are:

\[
\sigma^2
\quad\text{with multiplicity }M-1,
\]

and

\[
\frac{\sigma^2\tau^2}{M\sigma^2+\tau^2}
\quad\text{in the }\mathbf 1\text{-direction}.
\]

So conditioning on \(Z_\alpha=0\) removes at most one complex direction. It leaves \(M-1\) unconstrained complex directions of same-branch cell collapse.

Thus, for a small threshold \(\eta=Q^{-B}\),

\[
\mathbb P\left(\|X\|_2^2<\eta\,\mathbb E\|X\|_2^2
\ \middle|\ 
Z_\alpha=0
\right)
\asymp
\eta^M
=
Q^{-BM},
\]

up to polynomial or subexponential factors.

With

\[
M\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is

\[
\exp\left(
-B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}
\right).
\]

That is tiny, but not remotely tiny enough to exclude all zeros among \(e^Q Q\) candidates.

---

# 2. Expected bad-zero count

Expected number of O1a-bad zeros:

\[
N_{\rm bad}
\approx
e^Q Q\cdot
\exp\left(
-B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}
\right).
\]

Taking logs:

\[
\log N_{\rm bad}
=
Q+\log Q
-
B\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Since

\[
Q\gg \frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q},
\]

this tends to \(+\infty\).

So the model predicts:

\[
\boxed{
\text{there should be many zeros where the }O(R)\text{-dimensional same-branch cell vector is polynomially small.}
}
\]

This is a serious warning against O1a as a universal pointwise theorem.

---

# 3. Correlation length does not save it

One could object that values at nearby zeros are correlated. True.

The centered cell frequencies have width roughly

\[
\Delta\asymp \frac QR,
\]

so the cell vector changes on scale

\[
\Delta^{-1}\asymp \frac RQ.
\]

The zeta-zero spacing is about

\[
\frac1Q.
\]

Thus roughly \(R\) neighboring zeros see strongly correlated cell vectors.

But the number of effectively independent cell-vector samples in \([T,2T]\) is still about

\[
N_{\rm eff}
\asymp
\frac{T}{R/Q}
=
\frac{TQ}{R}
=
\frac{e^Q Q}{R}.
\]

So

\[
\log N_{\rm eff}
=
Q+O(\log Q),
\]

unchanged at the decisive exponential scale.

Therefore correlation reduces the bad-zero count by only a polylogarithmic factor. It does not fix the dimension deficit.

---

# 4. General dimension obstruction

This argument does not only hit O1a. It hits any proposed pointwise Gate–1 input of the form

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal F(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where \(\mathcal F\) is a finite-dimensional random-like observable of dimension

\[
d=O(R^C)
\]

for fixed \(C\).

The small-ball probability is roughly

\[
Q^{-Ad}
=
\exp(-Ad\log Q).
\]

To exclude bad values across

\[
\asymp e^Q Q
\]

zeros, one needs

\[
Ad\log Q\gtrsim Q.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
}
\]

But current local algebra has dimensions like

\[
R,\quad R^2,\quad R^3,
\]

with

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Even

\[
R^3\log Q
\asymp
\frac{(\log Q)^4}{(\log\log Q)^3}
\]

is still

\[
\ll Q.
\]

So finite \(R\)-scale observables cannot be expected to supply universal polynomial lower bounds at all zeros unless there is a strong, non-random arithmetic repulsion theorem.

---

# 5. Consequence for O1a

The current O1a statement is:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\varepsilon,r}|B_r^\varepsilon(m_0,\alpha)|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This should now be marked **Red**.

Not because we found an actual zeta counterexample, but because the statement demands a level of pointwise zero/cell anti-correlation that is stronger than the natural model by an exponential margin.

The realistic replacement is not:

\[
\text{prove O1a by better diagonal mass}.
\]

It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{replace O1a with a routed alternative.}
}
\]

Namely:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a cell-collapse branch activates.}
}
\]

---

# 6. What must the cell-collapse branch detect?

Assume O1a fails:

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm sb}(m_0,\alpha)<Q^{-A}.
\]

Then every same-branch cell value is small.

This does **not** imply that the branch has no energy. It means the energy is hidden in internal oscillation inside the cells. Therefore the compensating observable must detect one of these:

## 6.1 Internal centered moments

For each cell,

\[
B_r(t)
=
e^{-it\xi_r}
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
a_n e^{-it\delta_n},
\qquad
\log n=\xi_r+\delta_n.
\]

If

\[
B_r(m_0)\approx0,
\]

then the next possible observable is

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
\delta_n a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n},
\]

or higher moments

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}
\delta_n^k a_n e^{-im_0\delta_n}.
\]

But low-order moments suffer the same dimension problem. To get enough codimension, one needs total moment dimension

\[
\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

That is much larger than current \(R\)-depth.

## 6.2 Complement-band observability

At a zero,

\[
Z_\alpha=M_{\rm fixed}+C_{\rm comp}=0.
\]

If the fixed same-branch band is small, then

\[
M_{\rm fixed}(m_0)\approx0,
\]

so

\[
C_{\rm comp}(m_0)\approx0.
\]

Thus O1a failure forces the complement to be small too, not large. That gives a **common-small-value** condition:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
M_{\rm fixed}(m_0)\approx0,
\qquad
C_{\rm comp}(m_0)\approx0.
\]

But this is still only scalar/vector smallness of low codimension unless the complement is decomposed into many independently observed packets.

So complement-band routing helps only if one introduces many independent complement observables.

## 6.3 Derivative-row burden transfer

Even if cell values vanish, derivatives may be large:

\[
\partial_m B_r(m_0)
=
-i\xi_r B_r(m_0)
-i\sum_{n\in\mathcal C_r}\delta_n a_ne^{-im_0\log n}.
\]

If \(B_r(m_0)\approx0\), then

\[
\partial_m B_r(m_0)
\]

is controlled by the centered first moment.

So one possible routed theorem is:

\[
\sum_r |B_r(m_0)|^2
+
\Delta^{-2}\sum_r |\partial_m B_r(m_0)|^2
+
\cdots
+
\Delta^{-2K}\sum_r |\partial_m^K B_r(m_0)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But as above, this only becomes statistically plausible if

\[
R(K+1)\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

With current \(K=O(R)\), it remains too low-dimensional.

---

# 7. The hard lower bound on observable dimension

If the proof insists on **pointwise polynomial lower bounds at every zero**, then the observable dimension must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
d\log Q\gtrsim Q.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
d\gtrsim Q/\log Q.}
\]

This is the most important quantitative conclusion of the current attack.

Current ordinary branch dimensions are far below this:

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Even moderate powers of \(R\) are too small.

Therefore, one of the following must happen.

---

# 8. Three possible exits

## Exit A — prove a counter-random arithmetic theorem

Keep O1a as-is and prove:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X(m_0)\notin B(0,Q^{-A}).
\]

This theorem would say that zeta zeros are repelled from a codimension-\(O(R)\) cell-collapse tube far more strongly than a random model predicts.

This is possible only as a genuinely new arithmetic theorem. Nothing in diagonal mass, large sieve, zero density, or local algebra points to it.

## Exit B — increase observable dimension

Observe many more packet/cell/moment coordinates so that

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Then polynomial lower-tail exclusion becomes dimensionally plausible.

But this clashes with the current downstream finite row map, whose dimension is only \(R+2\). A high-dimensional observable has a large nullspace unless the downstream architecture is redesigned to consume it.

## Exit C — abandon pointwise lower bounds and use global energy

Instead of requiring

\[
\mathcal F(m_j)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{for every zero},
\]

prove a global inequality over zeros:

\[
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}
\mathcal E(m_j)
\ge \text{large},
\]

then use an aggregate contradiction rather than pointwise Gate 1.

This would be a major restructuring of the proof. It may be more compatible with large-sieve and mean-square technology, but it is not the current local-obstruction architecture.

---

# 9. Agent 1 recommendation

The current O1a should not be treated as a plausible standalone input.

The replacement should be a **dimension-aware dichotomy**:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{one of many independent observables is large}.
\]

A schematic version:

\[
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal I}
|\mathcal O_\nu(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where

\[
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

But for this to feed the current Gate–1 machinery, the observables \(\mathcal O_\nu\) must not merely be large; they must project nontrivially into the derivative row, cofactor row, or incidence row used downstream.

That is the core architecture problem.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a value statement: Red.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Low-order finite-jet replacement: Red/Yellow; dimension still too small.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Cell-collapse branch: necessary if the architecture is to survive.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Minimum observable dimension for pointwise zero exclusion: }
d\gtrsim Q/\log Q.
}
\]

So the next mathematical target should not be “prove O1a harder.” It should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{construct a dimension-sufficient observable family and prove that cell-collapse activates it,}
}
\]

while also proving that this larger observable family still couples to Gate 1.

## Agent 1 — dimension-sufficient O1 replacement attack

### Current target

The previous attack showed that current O1a is dimensionally too small:

\[
d_{\rm O1a}\asymp R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\]

but pointwise polynomial exclusion at all zeros requires roughly

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So I now tested the natural repair:

\[
\boxed{
\text{increase the observed same-branch cell/moment dimension to }d\asymp Q/\log Q.
}
\]

The result is mixed:

\[
\boxed{
\text{This makes bad-zero exclusion statistically plausible, but breaks the current Gate--1 bridge.}
}
\]

More precisely, high-dimensional observability can make the zero small-ball problem plausible, but the downstream ordinary Gate–1 machinery only consumes an \(O(R)\)-dimensional derivative row. That creates a large projection-nullspace problem.

---

# 1. High-dimensional cell refinement

Let the fixed frequency support have length

\[
\log n\in[c_-Q,c_+Q],
\]

so total frequency length is \(\asymp Q\).

Instead of using

\[
M_{\rm old}\asymp R
\]

cells, use

\[
M_{\rm new}\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}
\]

cells. Then each cell has frequency width

\[
\Delta_{\rm new}
\asymp
\frac{Q}{M_{\rm new}}
\asymp
\log Q.
\]

Define refined same-branch cells

\[
B_a^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\log n\in I_a}
a_n^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)e^{-i\sigma_\varepsilon t\log n},
\qquad
a=1,\ldots,M_{\rm new}.
\]

The refined same-branch mass is

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm ref}(t,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\varepsilon=\pm}\sum_{a=1}^{M_{\rm new}}
|B_a^\varepsilon(t,\alpha)|^2.
\]

The desired replacement is

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal S_{\rm ref}(m_0,\alpha)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

---

# 2. Small-ball count now becomes plausible

The refined vector has dimension

\[
d_{\rm ref}\asymp M_{\rm new}\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

A polynomial lower-tail event of size \(Q^{-A}\) has random-model probability roughly

\[
Q^{-A d_{\rm ref}}
=
\exp(-A d_{\rm ref}\log Q)
=
\exp(-A Q).
\]

The number of zeros in height \([T,2T]\), \(T=e^Q\), is

\[
N(T)\asymp e^Q Q.
\]

Therefore the expected bad count is roughly

\[
e^Q Q\cdot e^{-AQ}
=
Qe^{-(A-1)Q}.
\]

For \(A>1\), this is small.

So unlike the old \(R\)-cell O1a, the refined-cell version is **dimensionally compatible** with universal bad-zero exclusion.

This is an important positive result:

\[
\boxed{
M\asymp Q/\log Q \text{ is the right dimensional scale for pointwise polynomial exclusion.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Correlation length still does not break the count

A refined cell has centered frequency width

\[
\Delta_{\rm new}\asymp\log Q.
\]

Therefore its time-correlation scale is

\[
\Delta_{\rm new}^{-1}\asymp \frac1{\log Q}.
\]

The number of effectively independent samples in \([T,2T]\) is approximately

\[
T\Delta_{\rm new}
\asymp e^Q\log Q.
\]

This still has logarithm

\[
Q+O(\log\log Q).
\]

So the same dimension threshold remains:

\[
d\log Q\gtrsim Q.
\]

Thus the refined dimension

\[
d\asymp Q/\log Q
\]

is still the correct scale.

---

# 4. But the current Gate–1 bridge cannot consume this vector

The current ordinary Gate–1 derivative row has dimension

\[
R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Call the refined cell vector

\[
X(t)\in\mathbb C^{M_{\rm new}},
\qquad
M_{\rm new}\asymp Q/\log Q.
\]

The derivative row is some linear projection

\[
L X(t)\in\mathbb C^{R+2}.
\]

Since

\[
R+2\ll M_{\rm new},
\]

the map \(L\) has a huge nullspace:

\[
\dim\ker L
\ge
M_{\rm new}-(R+2)
\asymp
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\|X(t)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}
\]

does **not** imply

\[
\|LX(t)\|_2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

In fact there are unit vectors \(X\) with

\[
LX=0.
\]

So refined-cell observability alone does not imply the derivative-row lower bound needed by O1.

This is the central obstruction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dimension-sufficient observability is high-dimensional, but Gate--1 sees only a low-dimensional projection.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Could one prove that actual refined cell vectors avoid the projection nullspace?

That would require a new theorem:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
\|X(m_0)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|LX(m_0)\|_2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
X(m_0)
\notin
\{x:\|Lx\|\le Q^{-A'}\|x\|\}.
\]

But this is again a zero-conditioned prime-log nonrecurrence theorem, now against a large tube around a projection nullspace.

The nullspace has codimension only

\[
R+2.
\]

Randomly, the probability that a vector in \(\mathbb C^{M_{\rm new}}\) has small projection into an \(R\)-dimensional subspace is roughly

\[
Q^{-A R},
\]

not

\[
Q^{-A M_{\rm new}}.
\]

So among \(e^Q\) zeros, such projection-small events are again expected.

Thus even with enough refined cells, the **part visible to the current Gate–1 row** is still too low-dimensional.

\[
\boxed{
\text{The small-ball problem moves from the cell vector to its Gate--1 projection.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Can we increase the Gate–1 row dimension?

The obvious repair is to increase the derivative-row depth to match the refined dimension:

\[
R_{\rm new}\asymp M_{\rm new}
\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Then the row map could potentially be injective or frame-stable on refined cells.

But this appears fatal for the rest of the architecture.

## 6.1 Determinant and exterior losses

Many existing arguments already struggle with

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

If

\[
R_{\rm new}\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\]

then any determinant loss of the form

\[
C^R,\qquad R^R,\qquad Q^{cR},
\qquad \text{or}\qquad \exp(cR\log R)
\]

becomes at least

\[
\exp\!\left(c\frac{Q}{\log Q}\log Q\right)
=
e^{cQ}
=
T^c.
\]

That is no longer polynomial in \(Q\). It breaks the fixed-polynomial bookkeeping.

## 6.2 Subset-frequency separation becomes catastrophic

For exterior products of depth \(R_{\rm new}\), subset-product frequencies satisfy

\[
\log N_S\lesssim Q R_{\rm new}
\asymp
\frac{Q^2}{\log Q}.
\]

Distinct subset frequencies may be separated only by

\[
\exp\left(-C\frac{Q^2}{\log Q}\right),
\]

which is far below polynomial scale.

Any argument needing Vandermonde inversion, resultant separation, or filtered minor recovery at that depth becomes super-exponentially ill-conditioned relative to \(Q\).

## 6.3 D4/local algebra is finite-depth

The D4 and row-energy machinery is finite-order. It is designed around fixed or slowly growing jet depth. Raising the row depth to \(Q/\log Q\) changes the proof class completely.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raising Gate--1 dimension to }Q/\log Q\text{ is not compatible with current downstream machinery.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Can we use random projections instead?

Another possible repair:

Take a high-dimensional refined cell vector

\[
X\in\mathbb C^{M_{\rm new}},
\]

and apply several random or structured projections

\[
L_1X,\ldots,L_KX,
\]

with each \(L_j\) low-dimensional, hoping one projection is large.

For a deterministic vector \(X\), if the \(L_j\) form a frame,

\[
\sum_j\|L_jX\|^2\ge c\|X\|^2.
\]

But Gate–1 needs a specific row compatible with the downstream \(q''_{\can}\) jet structure. Arbitrary projections do not correspond to admissible derivative rows.

So this only helps if one can build an admissible family of Gate–1 rows

\[
\mathcal G_1,\ldots,\mathcal G_K
\]

such that:

1. they form a frame on refined cells;
2. each one has a downstream Gate–2 / D4 / row-mobility path;
3. \(K\) and all conditioning losses remain polynomial in \(Q\).

This is a major redesign. Nothing in the current architecture supplies such a family.

---

# 8. A more realistic dichotomy

The above suggests the correct ordinary branch cannot be:

\[
\text{large refined cell vector}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{fixed Gate--1 row large}.
\]

It must be something like:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists \nu\in\mathcal I
\text{ such that }
|\mathcal G_\nu(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where

\[
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q.
\]

But then for each \(\nu\), the downstream proof must accept \(\mathcal G_\nu\) as a legitimate Gate–1 seed.

That means Gate–1 should be formulated as a **family-valued observability theorem**, not a single-row theorem.

This is probably the mathematically correct repair, but it is outside the current proof architecture.

---

# 9. Resulting classification

We now have a clean trichotomy.

## Option A — Keep current low-dimensional O1a

Then O1a needs a counter-random theorem:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|LX(m_0)\|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

for a low-dimensional projection \(L\). This is statistically implausible and unsupported.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Red}}
\]

## Option B — Use high-dimensional refined observability

This is statistically plausible:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|X(m_0)\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But it does not feed the current Gate–1 row.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Yellow as arithmetic, Red as current architecture}}
\]

## Option C — Build a family-valued Gate–1 architecture

Use many admissible rows/projections:

\[
\exists \nu,\quad |\mathcal G_\nu(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is dimensionally plausible and could feed downstream if every \(\mathcal G_\nu\) has a Gate–2/D4 route.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Architecturally possible but not currently developed}}
\]

---

# 10. Concrete next mathematical target

The current O1a should be replaced by a family-valued statement:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal I}
|\mathcal G_\nu(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-A},
}
\]

with

\[
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q.
\]

But to remain useful, one also needs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{for every }\nu\in\mathcal I,\quad
|\mathcal G_\nu|\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate--2 seed after controlled transfer}.
}
\]

The missing bridge is now explicit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{high-dimensional observability} 
\;\not\Rightarrow\;
\text{low-dimensional Gate--1 seed}
}
\]

without an admissible frame of downstream-compatible rows.

---

# 11. Final Agent 1 conclusion for this round

The attempt to repair O1a by adding enough cells succeeds only at the probabilistic level.

It fails at the architectural level because the current Gate–1 row sees only

\[
O(R)
\]

directions, while pointwise zero-exclusion requires about

\[
Q/\log Q
\]

directions.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Current O1a cannot be repaired by merely refining cells.}
}
\]

The proof must choose between:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a new counter-random theorem for a low-dimensional projection,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a new family-valued Gate--1 architecture with } \gtrsim Q/\log Q \text{ admissible rows.}
}
\]

As Agent 1, I mark the original O1a route **Red** and the family-valued replacement as the only structurally plausible continuation.

## Agent 1 — redesign O1 as a dimension-sufficient Gate–1 seed

The coordinator’s instruction is to stop trying to prove old O1a and instead test whether a dimension-sufficient ordinary Gate–1 seed can still feed the downstream Gate–2/D4 machinery. 

### Result

\[
\boxed{
\text{I do not see a viable replacement inside the current local finite-row architecture.}
}
\]

More sharply:

\[
\boxed{
\text{High-dimensional observability can be made dimensionally plausible,}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{the current downstream Gate--1 bridge only sees a low-dimensional polynomial/differential projection.}
}
\]

So the ordinary branch faces a hard fork:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Either prove a counter-random theorem for a low-dimensional projection,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{replace the current pointwise local obstruction engine with a genuinely high-dimensional downstream engine.}
}
\]

The old O1a cannot be repaired by merely adding more cells.

---

# 1. The refined-cell repair

Let the wide AFE band be

\[
c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad Q=\log T.
\]

To make pointwise bad-zero exclusion dimensionally plausible over

\[
N(T)\asymp e^QQ
\]

zeros, the observed vector needs dimension

\[
d\log Q\gtrsim Q,
\]

so

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Thus refine the band into

\[
M\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}
\]

cells of frequency width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q.
\]

Let

\[
X(m,\alpha)
=
\left(B_a^\varepsilon(m,\alpha)\right)_{\varepsilon=\pm,\ 1\le a\le M}
\in\mathbb C^{2M}.
\]

A dimension-sufficient replacement for old O1a would be

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|_2^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is at least statistically plausible: a polynomial small ball in dimension \(M\asymp Q/\log Q\) has probability about

\[
Q^{-AM}
=
\exp(-AQ),
\]

which can beat the \(e^Q\)-sized zero set if \(A\) is large.

So high-dimensional refined-cell observability is the right dimension scale.

But this is not enough.

---

# 2. The current Gate–1 bridge sees only a low-dimensional projection

The ordinary Gate–1 derivative row is not the full refined-cell vector. It is a small family of global differential moments.

Schematic form:

\[
Y_\ell(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\varepsilon,a}
p_\ell(\sigma_a^\varepsilon)
B_a^\varepsilon(m,\alpha),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L,
\]

where \(p_\ell\) are the symbols of the admissible differential operators \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\), and currently

\[
L+1\asymp R
\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Thus the current Gate–1 row is a linear map

\[
\mathcal L:\mathbb C^{2M}\to\mathbb C^{L+1},
\]

with

\[
2M\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\qquad
L+1\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\dim\ker\mathcal L
\ge
2M-(L+1)
\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So

\[
\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}
\]

does **not** imply

\[
\|\mathcal L X(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

There are many nonzero refined-cell configurations invisible to the current Gate–1 row.

This is the central obstruction.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Dimension-sufficient observability is high-dimensional; current Gate--1 is low-dimensional.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Family-valued Gate–1: rank obstruction

Could we introduce many Gate–1 observables

\[
G_\nu(m,\alpha),
\qquad \nu\in\mathcal I,
\qquad |\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
\]

and ask for

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal I}|G_\nu(m_0,\alpha)|^2\ge Q^{-A}?
\]

Only if the \(G_\nu\) span the refined-cell space.

But if every \(G_\nu\) must be an admissible finite differential row of the current type, then its symbol lies in the same low-dimensional polynomial symbol space

\[
\mathcal P_L
=
\{p(\xi):\deg p\le L\}.
\]

Even if we take many such \(G_\nu\), their span has rank at most

\[
\dim\mathcal P_L=L+1\asymp R.
\]

Thus the combined family still has a large nullspace in the refined-cell vector space.

Formally, let

\[
G_\nu(X)=\langle X,s_\nu\rangle,
\qquad
s_\nu\in S,
\]

where \(S\subset\mathbb C^{2M}\) is the admissible symbol space. If

\[
\dim S<2M,
\]

then choose

\[
0\ne X\in S^\perp.
\]

Then

\[
G_\nu(X)=0
\quad\text{for all }\nu.
\]

Therefore no family of current admissible rows can satisfy a frame inequality

\[
\sum_\nu |G_\nu(X)|^2\ge c\|X\|_2^2
\]

on refined cells unless

\[
\dim S\ge 2M.
\]

That means the admissible symbol depth must satisfy roughly

\[
L\gtrsim M\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

This is far beyond the current regime.

---

# 4. Why increasing derivative depth is not viable

One could try to set

\[
L\asymp M\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Then polynomial symbols could in principle resolve the refined cells.

But this breaks the fixed-polynomial architecture.

Any loss of type

\[
Q^{CL},
\qquad
L^L,
\qquad
e^{CL},
\qquad
\text{or}
\qquad
\exp(C L\log L)
\]

becomes exponential in \(Q\), or close to it. For example,

\[
Q^{CL}
=
\exp(CL\log Q)
=
\exp(CQ)
=
T^C.
\]

That is not a polynomial-in-\(Q\) loss.

Similarly, exterior or resultant frequencies of depth \(L\) have subset products with log-size

\[
QL\asymp \frac{Q^2}{\log Q},
\]

so separation losses become

\[
\exp\!\left(-C\frac{Q^2}{\log Q}\right),
\]

which is catastrophically smaller than any \(Q^{-A}\).

Thus

\[
\boxed{
L\asymp Q/\log Q
\text{ is incompatible with the current determinant/exterior/local algebra bookkeeping.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Projection-frame repair also fails

Another idea is to keep each projection low-dimensional but use many projections:

\[
L_\nu X,
\qquad \nu\in\mathcal I.
\]

If the projections were arbitrary, we could choose them to form a frame on \(\mathbb C^{2M}\).

But downstream compatibility forces each projection to come from admissible local differential symbols, and those symbols remain in the same low-dimensional space \(S\).

So the total map

\[
X\mapsto (L_\nu X)_\nu
\]

still has rank at most

\[
\dim S\asymp R
\]

unless the admissible symbol class is enlarged.

Therefore a projection-frame repair requires one of two things:

\[
\boxed{
\text{either nonlocal / high-degree frequency filters,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a new downstream theorem accepting arbitrary refined-cell projections.}
}
\]

The current Gate–2/D4 route accepts neither.

---

# 6. Time-shifted or \(\alpha\)-shifted probes do not solve it

A possible escape is to use shifted evaluations:

\[
G_\tau(m_0)=B(m_0+\tau).
\]

As \(\tau\) varies, these have symbols

\[
e^{-i\tau \xi_a},
\]

which can form a Fourier frame over the refined cell centers.

To resolve cells of width

\[
\Delta\asymp\log Q,
\]

one needs shifts over a time window of length about

\[
\Delta^{-1}\asymp\frac1{\log Q}.
\]

But the local ordinary scale is

\[
\lambda\asymp\frac1Q.
\]

So these shifts are larger by a factor

\[
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

They are not local Gate–1 data at \(m_0\). Transporting them back to \(m_0\) would require derivative depth

\[
\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\]

which is exactly the forbidden high-depth regime.

The same issue appears for \(\alpha\)-shifts. Resolving refined cells through weight variation requires parameter motion large enough to distinguish the \(\log n\)-intervals, and converting that back into local finite-row data again requires high symbolic dimension.

So shifted probes are mathematically natural but incompatible with the current local engine.

---

# 7. Aggregate-energy replacement

Could one abandon pointwise Gate–1 and prove an averaged lower bound over zeros?

For example,

\[
\sum_{Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}
\|X(m_j,\alpha)\|_2^2
\gg
N(T)Q^{-C}.
\]

This is much more compatible with mean-square and large-sieve technology.

But it does not feed the current proof.

The downstream engine is pointwise. It needs a Gate–1 seed at the specific zero or packet being obstructed. An aggregate inequality allows exceptional zeros. It cannot rule out one bad zero unless the proof is restructured into a global energy contradiction.

A global contradiction would require something like:

\[
\sum_{m_j}
\text{downstream row energy}(m_j)
\le
\text{global upper bound}
\]

combined with

\[
\sum_{m_j}
\text{downstream row energy}(m_j)
\ge
\text{larger lower bound}.
\]

That is a different proof architecture. It may be more compatible with the available analytic tools, but it is not the current local obstruction route.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate energy is plausible analytically but does not repair pointwise Gate--1.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Clean no-go theorem for the current local architecture

Here is the precise mathematical obstruction.

Assume refined cells give a vector space

\[
E_Q\simeq\mathbb C^{2M},
\qquad
M\asymp Q/\log Q.
\]

Assume admissible Gate–1 observables are linear functionals whose symbols lie in a space

\[
S_Q\subset E_Q
\]

with

\[
\dim S_Q=s_Q.
\]

If

\[
s_Q<2M,
\]

then no family of admissible Gate–1 observables can satisfy a uniform frame inequality on refined cells.

Indeed, choose

\[
0\ne X\in S_Q^\perp.
\]

Then every admissible observable vanishes on \(X\), while

\[
\|X\|_2>0.
\]

In the current architecture,

\[
s_Q\asymp R
\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

whereas

\[
2M\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Hence

\[
s_Q\ll 2M.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No dimension-sufficient family-valued Gate--1 seed exists inside the current symbol class.}
}
\]

---

# 9. What would be required to survive

The ordinary Gate–1 architecture can survive only by adding one of the following.

## A. Counter-random low-dimensional theorem

Keep the current low-dimensional row and prove directly:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal L X(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This says zeta zeros avoid the small projection of a codimension-\(O(R)\) observable far more strongly than random behavior predicts.

This is a new arithmetic theorem, not a consequence of current local algebra.

## B. High-dimensional downstream engine

Allow

\[
\dim S_Q\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
\]

and rebuild Gate–2/D4/row mobility to tolerate high-depth or nonlocal frequency-filtered seeds.

This would no longer be the current finite local obstruction architecture.

## C. Global aggregate proof

Replace pointwise obstruction by global energy over zeros.

This abandons local Gate–1 as presently formulated.

---

# 10. Agent 1 deliverable

\[
\boxed{
\text{No concrete replacement Gate--1 architecture closes within the current framework.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Refined high-dimensional cell observability is dimensionally plausible,}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{it cannot feed the current low-dimensional Gate--1 row because of a rank/nullspace obstruction.}
}
\]

A family-valued repair works only if the admissible symbol space has dimension

\[
\gtrsim Q/\log Q.
\]

The current admissible symbol space has dimension only

\[
\asymp R
=
\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Therefore the current local obstruction strategy cannot survive on ordinary Gate–1 unless one supplies a new counter-random arithmetic theorem for the low-dimensional projection:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal L X(m_0,\alpha)\|_2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

That is the exact missing theorem for Agent 1’s branch.
