## Agent 1 — actual-coefficient aggregate canonical Gate–1

The current prompt asks Agent 1 to stop pursuing universal lower sampling for arbitrary packets and instead attack the actual-coefficient theorem for the canonical packet \(X_{\can}\), including \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\), routed ordinary gaps, and the explicit-formula/zero-sampling second moment route. 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Actual-coefficient AggG1}_{\can}\text{ is the right target, but it is still not proved.}
}
\]

The universal lower-frame theorem is too strong. The viable replacement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual canonical packet does not concentrate its energy in ordinary-route gaps or zero-sampling nulls.}
}
\]

That is plausibly weaker than pointwise projection avoidance, but it is still a new zero-restricted second-moment theorem for the full canonical packet.

---

# 1. Exact actual-coefficient theorem

Let

\[
X_{\can}(m)
\]

be the full canonical refined packet vector associated with

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Here \(X_{\can}\) must include:

\[
\boxed{
P_2:\text{ prime/main AFE packet;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
D_2:\text{ folded-dual/completion packet;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B_2:\text{ gamma/background curvature;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_2:\text{ regularized tail/error packet.}
}
\]

The ordinary aggregate theorem should not be stated for \(P_2\)-only or for arbitrary test coefficients.

The target is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)\,
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)\,
|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

Here \(T_{\can}\) is the finite-row canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-compatible Gate–1 projection, and \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) is the routed ordinary set.

This is not a universal sampling theorem. It is a theorem about the actual canonical packet generated by zeta.

---

# 2. Why actual coefficients matter

The universal lower-frame theorem fails if one permits arbitrary packet coefficients. If \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) has a gap

\[
J=(a,a+h)
\]

with

\[
h\gg \frac{\log Q}{Q},
\]

then an adversarial bandlimited packet of bandwidth \(\asymp Q\) can be concentrated inside \(J\), with leakage outside \(J\) roughly

\[
\exp(-cQh).
\]

If there are no samples inside \(J\), sampled energy is exponentially small. Hence a polynomial lower frame for all packets forces

\[
h\lesssim \frac{\log Q}{Q}.
\]

But \(X_{\can}\) is not an arbitrary bandlimited packet. Its coefficients are tied to the AFE, gamma factors, folded duality, and the actual prime-log orbit. So the actual-coefficient theorem asks whether this specific packet can behave like an adversarial prolate packet localized into routed-center holes.

That is a much more plausible theorem.

---

# 3. Can the actual canonical packet concentrate in ordinary gaps?

I do not see a deterministic obstruction strong enough to rule it out pointwise. But aggregate concentration into routed ordinary gaps is more constrained.

Suppose ordinary centers miss a region \(J\). Then the ordinary sum misses whatever mass \(X_{\can}\) has there:

\[
\sum_{m\in \mathcal C_{\ord}\cap J}\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2=0.
\]

AggG1\(_{\can}\) can still hold if one of the following is true:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary gaps are small at sampling scale }(\log Q)/Q;
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{the canonical packet has negligible energy in ordinary gaps;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{non-ordinary regions are covered by transition/singular aggregate Gate--1.}
}
\]

The third option is best. It suggests the theorem should be branch-complete from the start:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\allbranches}
=
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\ord}
+
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\tr}
+
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\sing}.
}
\]

Then ordinary-route holes are not defects if their mass is routed to transition or singular aggregate inputs.

---

# 4. Explicit-formula / zero-sampling second moment

Try to estimate, for an actual canonical packet \(P_{\can}\),

\[
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)|P_{\can}(\gamma)|^2.
\]

Using the zero counting measure,

\[
dN(t)
=
\frac{1}{2\pi}\log\frac{t}{2\pi}\,dt+dS(t)+\text{lower order},
\]

we get

\[
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)|P_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
=
\frac{Q}{2\pi}
\int \rho(t)|P_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt
+
\int \rho(t)|P_{\can}(t)|^2\,dS(t)
+
\cdots.
\]

The main term is good:

\[
\asymp Q\int \rho |P_{\can}|^2.
\]

The error is

\[
\int F\,dS,
\qquad
F(t)=\rho(t)|P_{\can}(t)|^2.
\]

Integrating by parts,

\[
\int F\,dS
=
-\int S(t)F'(t)\,dt
+
\text{boundary}.
\]

The crude estimate

\[
|S(t)|\ll Q
\]

and

\[
|F'(t)|\lesssim QF(t)
\]

gives

\[
\left|\int F\,dS\right|
\lesssim
Q^2\int F,
\]

which is too large compared with the main term

\[
Q\int F.
\]

So the naive explicit-formula quadrature does **not** prove the lower bound.

A usable theorem would need cancellation:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\int S(t)\frac{d}{dt}
\left(\rho(t)|P_{\can}(t)|^2\right)\,dt
\right|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})
\frac{Q}{2\pi}
\int\rho(t)|P_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt.
}
\]

Equivalently, one needs a zero-restricted second-moment theorem for the actual canonical packet.

---

# 5. What the exact second-moment theorem should say

A realistic ordinary theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\gamma\in I_{\ord}}
\rho(\gamma)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
Q\int_{I_{\ord}}
\rho(t)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt
}
\]

and similarly

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\gamma\in I_{\ord}}
\rho(\gamma)|X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
\le
Q^{A}
Q\int_{I_{\ord}}
\rho(t)|X_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt.
}
\]

Then it remains to compare the continuous canonical row energy with the continuous refined packet energy:

\[
\int \rho |T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int \rho |X_{\can}|^2.
\]

This latter inequality is a continuous symbol/frame statement and is much easier than a zero-restricted pointwise theorem. The hard part is sampling at zeros/routed centers.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}
\text{ reduces to zero-sampling comparability for actual canonical packet energies.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Ordinary-routing gaps and branch-complete coverage

If \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) is only a subset of all centers, then even a theorem over all zeros is insufficient unless routing preserves sampling.

The clean branch-complete condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\branch\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\branch}}
\rho(m)E_{\branch}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
}
\]

Here:

\[
E_{\ord}(m)
=
\frac{|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2}{|X_{\can}(m)|^2},
\]

\[
E_{\tr}(m)
=
\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2,
\]

and

\[
E_{\sing}(m)
=
\|\mathcal S_R(m)\|^2,
\]

with the transition and singular vectors formulated for the same canonical source \(q''_{\can}\).

This avoids requiring ordinary centers to sample every region. If ordinary routing has holes, those holes must be occupied by transition/singular centers with their own aggregate energy.

So the actual theorem should be branch-complete unless one can prove strong local density of \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\).

---

# 7. Does \(B_2\) help?

The background term

\[
B_2(t)=\theta'''(t)
\]

is smooth and nonoscillatory at the prime-log scale. It may prevent some exact cancellations, but it cannot by itself prove aggregate sampling for the prime packet.

It should be included in \(X_{\can}\), not used as a standalone lower-bound source.

If treated perturbatively, the needed estimate is:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)|B_2(m)|^2
+
\sum_m\rho(m)|D_2(m)+E_2(m)|^2
\le
\varepsilon
\sum_m\rho(m)|P_2(m)|^2,
\]

with \(\varepsilon\le Q^{-A}\). I do not see a reason this is uniformly true. So perturbative handling is unsafe.

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
B_2,D_2,E_2\text{ must be built into the canonical packet, not discarded.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Current best theorem target

The best theorem target is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete actual-coefficient aggregate canonical Gate--1.}
}
\]

For a routed branch decomposition

\[
\mathcal C=
\mathcal C_{\ord}\sqcup\mathcal C_{\tr}\sqcup\mathcal C_{\sing},
\]

prove

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
+
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\tr}}
\rho(m)\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
+
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\sing}}
\rho(m)\|\mathcal S_R(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho(m)\mathcal N_{\can}(m).
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal N_{\can}\) is the corresponding canonical normalization/packet energy in each branch.

This theorem avoids three failures:

\[
\boxed{
\text{not universal over arbitrary adversarial packets;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{not dependent on ordinary centers sampling every interval;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{not proxy-only; includes }B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
}
\]

---

# 9. Remaining arithmetic input

The exact missing arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-sampling second moment for the actual canonical packet, branch-complete.}
}
\]

In analytic terms, it must control

\[
\int F_{\can}\,dS
\]

for

\[
F_{\can}
=
\rho(t)\cdot
\text{canonical packet energy},
\]

better than the trivial bound.

A sufficient estimate would be:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\int F_{\can}(t)\,dS(t)
\right|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})
\frac{Q}{2\pi}
\int F_{\can}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is still difficult, but it is dimensionally natural and aggregate-compatible.

---

# 10. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Universal lower frame for all packets: too strong.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Ordinary actual-coefficient AggG1: plausible with strong route-density assumptions.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Branch-complete actual-coefficient AggG1: best formulation.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Main missing theorem: branch-complete zero-sampling second moment for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

This is the strongest current Agent 1 position: Stack C2 remains viable only if the aggregate input is stated for the **actual canonical packet** and **branch-complete routing**, not as a universal lower large-sieve theorem for arbitrary Dirichlet packets.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:37:44)

## Agent 1 — branch-complete actual-coefficient zero-sampling second moment

The assigned task is to formulate and attack the branch-complete aggregate Gate–1 theorem for the full canonical source \(q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\), not a universal sampling theorem for arbitrary packets. 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The right theorem is branch-complete and actual-coefficient.}
}
\]

The ordinary-only theorem is too fragile because \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) can have routed gaps. The universal lower frame for all packets is too strong because adversarial packets can concentrate in such gaps. The best current target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-sampling second moment for the actual canonical packet, summed over all branches.}
}
\]

This is still a serious arithmetic input, but it is no longer dimensionally counter-random.

---

# 1. Correct normalization

The right-hand side should not be merely

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho(m).
\]

It should measure the canonical source mass available in the relevant branch coordinates. Define branch normalizations:

\[
\mathcal N_{\ord}(m):=|X_{\can}(m)|^2,
\]

where \(X_{\can}\) is the full ordinary refined canonical packet vector;

\[
\mathcal N_{\tr}(m):=\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2_{\mathrm{nat}}
\]

or, preferably, the comparable local \(L^2\) source mass of \(\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\);

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}(m):=|S_R(m)|^2
\]

provided \(S_R\) is the actual coefficient vector of a local expansion of \(\lambda^3q''_{\can}\). If \(S_R\) is only diagnostic, this is not valid and requires a transfer theorem.

So the branch-complete aggregate Gate–1 input should be stated as

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
+
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\tr}}\rho(m)\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
+
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\sing}}\rho(m)\|S_R(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}}\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m).
}
\]

Equivalently, define normalized branch energies

\[
E_b(m):=\frac{\text{branch Gate--1 output}(m)}{\mathcal N_b(m)}
\]

and require

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m).
}
\]

This measure

\[
d\mu_b(m)=\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m)
\]

is the correct measure for later good-subset extraction.

---

# 2. Ordinary branch: full canonical packet

The ordinary theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

Here \(X_{\can}\) must include all components of

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

More explicitly:

\[
X_{\can}
=
X_{P_2}^{+}
\oplus
X_{P_2}^{-}
\oplus
X_{D_2}
\oplus
X_{B_2}
\oplus
X_{E_2}.
\]

The \(B_2\) component should not be treated as a removable scalar correction unless one proves an aggregate perturbation estimate strong enough to prevent affine cancellation. The safer formulation embeds \(B_2\) as part of the canonical vector.

The ordinary missing theorem is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual ordinary canonical packet energy is not hidden from }T_{\can}
\text{ on average over }\mathcal C_{\ord}.
}
\]

This is weaker than pointwise projection avoidance and weaker than universal lower sampling.

---

# 3. Transition branch is scalar-source compatible

The transition vector should be

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}
\partial_x^r
\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]_{x=0}
\right)_{0\le r\le R},
\qquad
\mu=\lambda Q.
\]

This is automatically scalar-source compatible because it is directly formed from \(q''_{\can}\), not from arbitrary projected source components.

The transition aggregate theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\tr}}\rho(m)\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\tr}}\rho(m)\mathcal N_{\tr}(m).
}
\]

A natural choice is to take

\[
\mathcal N_{\tr}(m)
=
\int_{|x|\le c}
|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2\,dx,
\]

or a comparable weighted finite-jet norm. Then the theorem becomes a weighted moment observability theorem for the actual canonical transition packet.

---

# 4. Singular branch: exact transfer gap

The singular vector \(S_R(m)\) is valid only if it is the actual coefficient vector in a local canonical expansion:

\[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
=
\sum_{k=3}^{R+3} S_k(m)\Phi_k(x)
+
\operatorname{Rem}_R(x;m).
\]

Then the singular aggregate theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\sing}}\rho(m)|S_R(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\sing}}\rho(m)\mathcal N_{\sing}(m).
}
\]

But this is valid only if:

\[
\int\left|\sum_{k=3}^{R+3} S_k\Phi_k(x)\right|^2dx
\ge
e^{-O(R\log R)}|S_R|^2,
\]

and

\[
\|\operatorname{Rem}_R\|_{L^2}
\le
\frac12 e^{-O(R\log R)/2}|S_R|.
\]

If \(S_R\) is merely a diagnostic sine-moment vector rather than an actual expansion coefficient vector for \(q''_{\can}\), then singular transfer is not closed.

So the singular branch has a deterministic but critical identity gap:

\[
\boxed{
S_R\text{ must be an actual canonical coefficient vector, not a diagnostic anti-design vector.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Explicit-formula zero-sampling route

For an actual canonical nonnegative energy \(F_{\can}(t)\), the desired zero-sampling estimate has the form

\[
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)F_{\can}(\gamma)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\cdot
\frac{Q}{2\pi}
\int \rho(t)F_{\can}(t)\,dt.
\]

Using

\[
dN(t)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\log\frac{t}{2\pi}\,dt+dS(t)+\text{lower order},
\]

we get

\[
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)F_{\can}(\gamma)
=
\frac{Q}{2\pi}\int \rho F_{\can}
+
\int \rho F_{\can}\,dS
+
\text{lower order}.
\]

Thus the exact error term is

\[
\boxed{
\int \rho(t)F_{\can}(t)\,dS(t).
}
\]

After integration by parts,

\[
\int \rho F_{\can}\,dS
=
-\int S(t)\frac{d}{dt}(\rho F_{\can})(t)\,dt
+
\text{boundary}.
\]

The trivial estimate is too large, because

\[
|S(t)|\ll Q,
\qquad
|(\rho F_{\can})'|\lesssim Q\rho F_{\can}
\]

would give an error of order

\[
Q^2\int \rho F_{\can},
\]

while the main term is only

\[
Q\int \rho F_{\can}.
\]

So the needed analytic input is a cancellation estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\int \rho(t)F_{\can}(t)\,dS(t)
\right|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})
\frac{Q}{2\pi}
\int \rho(t)F_{\can}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is the precise zero-sampling second-moment theorem. It must hold for the actual canonical branch energy, not for arbitrary adversarial \(F\).

---

# 6. Ordinary-route gaps and branch coverage

If \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) has gaps, ordinary AggG1 alone can fail. Branch-complete coverage repairs this only if the missing ordinary weight is captured by transition or singular branches.

The necessary coverage theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For every routed window, either ordinary centers sample it with polynomial weight,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{the transition/singular branch energy in that window contributes to the branch-complete sum.}
}
\]

In measure form, let

\[
\mu_{\can}(J)
=
\sum_{b}\sum_{m\in \mathcal C_b\cap J}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m).
\]

The required coverage condition is

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}(J)
\ge Q^{-C}\mu_{\mathrm{all}}(J)
}
\]

for all relevant windows \(J\) at the sampling scale, with the branch energy assigned to the correct local regime.

Without this, ordinary-route holes can still defeat aggregate sampling by removing all branch mass from a region.

---

# 7. Best current theorem target

The strongest defensible target is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete actual-coefficient zero-sampling second moment.}
}
\]

For the full canonical source \(q''_{\can}\), prove

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho(m)\mathcal G_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m),
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal G_{\ord}=|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2,
\]

\[
\mathcal G_{\tr}=\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}\|^2,
\]

\[
\mathcal G_{\sing}=|S_R|^2,
\]

and each \(\mathcal N_b\) is the matching canonical branch energy.

A sufficient proof package is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-sampling second moment for actual canonical branch energies}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch coverage at the sampling scale}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{deterministic transition/singular-to-scalar transfer.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Universal lower frame: Red/too strong.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Ordinary actual-coefficient AggG1: Yellow, requires ordinary route-density or no concentration in gaps.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Branch-complete actual-coefficient AggG1: best target.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Main missing theorem: branch-complete zero-sampling second moment for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

This is the most viable Agent 1 formulation of Stack C2. It avoids the old pointwise counter-random theorem, avoids arbitrary source-family downstream, and keeps the canonical scalar source \(u=q''_{\can}\) throughout.

## Agent 1 — deeper attack on branch-complete actual-coefficient AggG1

I pushed the explicit zero-sampling second-moment route. The key refinement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ should be treated as a twisted second moment over zeros, not as generic sampling.}
}
\]

For the actual canonical packet, the zero-sampling Gram is not approximately diagonal. It has a same-size arithmetic off-diagonal coming from the explicit formula. That off-diagonal is not merely an error; it encodes the fact that some AFE combinations vanish at zeros.

So the next target is sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove positivity of the zero-sampling Gram after quotienting the scalar zero direction.}
}
\]

This is the actual-coefficient analogue of the old projection problem, but now in aggregate form.

---

# 1. Zero-sampling Gram for Dirichlet packets

For a packet

\[
P(t)=\sum_n a_n n^{-it},
\]

sampled at zeros \(\gamma\), the second moment is

\[
\sum_{\gamma} \rho(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}.
\]

Set

\[
G(n,n')
=
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
=
\langle a,Ga\rangle.
\]

For continuous \(t\)-sampling, \(G\) is approximately diagonal. For zero-sampling, \(G\) has substantial arithmetic structure.

---

# 2. Landau-type off-diagonal is same order as diagonal

For ratios

\[
\frac n{n'}=k,
\]

one expects a Landau/Gonek-type main term

\[
\sum_{\gamma\le T} k^{-i\gamma}
\sim
-\frac{T}{2\pi}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\]

up to normalization and smoothing.

Thus off-diagonal pairs with integer ratio \(k\) contribute roughly

\[
-\frac{T}{2\pi}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\sum_{n'} a_{kn'}\overline{a_{n'}}.
\]

For AFE-size coefficients

\[
a_n\sim n^{-1/2}
\]

on a band \(n\in[T^{c_-},T^{c_+}]\), we have

\[
a_{kn'}a_{n'}\sim \frac{1}{n'\sqrt{k}}.
\]

Summing over \(n'\) in the overlap gives size roughly

\[
Q\cdot \frac1{\sqrt{k}}.
\]

So the \(k\)-ratio contribution is approximately

\[
-TQ\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}.
\]

Summing over \(k\le T^{c_+-c_-}\),

\[
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\asymp Q,
\]

so the total off-diagonal can be as large as

\[
TQ^2,
\]

which is the same order as the diagonal

\[
\#\{\gamma\}\sum_n|a_n|^2
\asymp
TQ\cdot Q
=
TQ^2.
\]

Therefore the zero-sampling Gram is not a small perturbation of the diagonal.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Diagonal dominance is false at the natural AFE scale.}
}
\]

This explains why crude lower-frame arguments fail.

---

# 3. This off-diagonal is not bad news; it encodes the zero equation

The same-size off-diagonal is expected. If \(P(t)\) were the scalar AFE packet for \(Z_\alpha(t)\), then at zeros

\[
P(\gamma)+\text{dual/background/tail}=0.
\]

So the sampled scalar energy can be extremely small even though the continuous energy is large.

Thus a lower frame for arbitrary packets is impossible not only because of sampling gaps, but also because the zero set is arithmetically correlated with the AFE packets.

For the canonical Gate–1 packet, we do **not** want a lower bound for the scalar \(Z_\alpha\)-value direction. We want a lower bound after applying the canonical derivative/curvature row \(T_{\can}\).

So the right theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the zero-sampling Gram is positive on the canonical Gate--1 quotient,}
}
\]

not on the whole packet space.

---

# 4. Operator form of the zero-sampling Gram

The explicit formula suggests a decomposition of the zero-sampling quadratic form:

\[
\langle a,Ga\rangle
\approx
W\langle a,a\rangle
-
T\langle a,\mathcal L_\Lambda a\rangle
+
\text{dual/conjugate pieces}
+
\text{smoothing errors},
\]

where \(\mathcal L_\Lambda\) is a ratio/divisibility operator:

\[
(\mathcal L_\Lambda a)_n
\sim
\sum_{k} \frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}a_{n/k}
+
\sum_{k}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}a_{nk},
\]

with the sums restricted to the ordinary band.

The operator norm of \(\mathcal L_\Lambda\) on AFE-scale coefficient spaces is naturally size \(\asymp Q\), matching the diagonal coefficient \(W/T\asymp Q\). So one cannot treat it as error.

Instead, AggG1\(_{\can}\) asks for a lower bound of the form

\[
\langle T_{\can}a,G\,T_{\can}a\rangle
\ge
Q^{-A}
\langle X_{\can}a,G\,X_{\can}a\rangle
\]

after including the full canonical vector and quotienting null/scalar directions.

This is now an explicit arithmetic-operator positivity problem.

---

# 5. Full canonical packet is essential

The cancellation operator above is sensitive to the full AFE structure. Therefore the theorem must include

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

If we use only \(P_2\), the zero-sampling Gram may show spurious positivity or spurious negativity because the scalar cancellation at zeros is split across:

\[
P_2,\quad D_2,\quad B_2,\quad E_2.
\]

Thus AggG1 must use the full canonical row:

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can}
=
T_{\can}(X_{P_2}+X_{D_2}+X_{B_2}+X_{E_2}).
\]

The off-diagonal Landau terms should be computed for the combined object, not for isolated pieces.

---

# 6. Branch-complete version

The ordinary branch alone is still fragile. If \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) omits a region, ordinary sampling may miss mass. But the explicit-formula Gram calculation suggests a branch-complete formulation:

\[
\sum_{\branch}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\branch}
\rho(m)\mathcal G_\branch(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\branch}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\branch}
\rho(m)\mathcal N_\branch(m).
\]

The ordinary branch uses

\[
\mathcal G_{\ord}=|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2.
\]

The transition branch uses

\[
\mathcal G_{\tr}=\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}\|^2.
\]

The singular branch uses

\[
\mathcal G_{\sing}=\|S_R\|^2,
\]

provided \(S_R\) is an actual coefficient vector in a local expansion of \(\lambda^3q''_{\can}\).

This branch-complete theorem allows the sampling mass to move between regimes instead of requiring ordinary centers to sample every window.

---

# 7. What can be attacked concretely now?

The next concrete mathematical object is the **zero-sampling matrix for the canonical row**:

\[
G_{\can}^{(\ell,\ell')}(n,n')
=
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
\rho(m)
\sigma_\ell(n,m)
\overline{\sigma_{\ell'}(n',m)}
e^{-im\log(n/n')}.
\]

The aggregate Gate–1 inequality is positivity of this block matrix against the canonical coefficient vector.

Using explicit formula, one should derive:

\[
G_{\can}
=
G_{\diag}
+
G_{\Lambda}
+
G_{\dual}
+
G_{\bg}
+
G_{\err}.
\]

Then the theorem becomes a finite/operator inequality:

\[
\boxed{
G_{\can}\ge Q^{-A}G_{\cell}
}
\]

on the actual canonical packet subspace or after quotienting scalar zero directions.

This is more concrete than prior formulations.

---

# 8. A useful diagnostic computation

A feasible numerical/model test is:

1. Build a truncated AFE packet on a height \(T\).
2. Build the zero-sampling Gram using known zeros or a zero model.
3. Compute the Rayleigh quotient

\[
\frac{
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
}{
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)|X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
}.
\]

4. Compare with the same quotient after replacing zeros by a uniform grid.

If the quotient is consistently \(Q^{-1+o(1)}\) or larger, AggG1 is plausible. If it decays faster than any polynomial, Stack C2 dies at input one.

This is not a proof, but it tests the new aggregate theorem directly.

---

# 9. Current refined theorem target

The best theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Canonical zero-sampling Gram positivity.}
}
\]

For the branch-complete routed sampling measure \(\mu\), prove

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\branch}\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\branch}
\rho(m)\mathcal G_\branch(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\branch}\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\branch}
\rho(m)\mathcal N_\branch(m),
}
\]

where all \(\mathcal G_\branch,\mathcal N_\branch\) are built from the same scalar canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

For the ordinary branch, prove this by analyzing the zero-sampling Gram

\[
G(n,n')=\sum_m\rho(m)e^{-im\log(n/n')}
\]

using explicit-formula arithmetic, including the same-order Landau off-diagonal terms rather than treating them as error.

---

# 10. Final status of Agent 1 attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ remains viable, but its proof is not a generic lower large sieve.}
}
\]

It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{an explicit-formula positivity theorem for the actual canonical zero-sampling Gram.}
}
\]

The new main risk is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Landau/Gonek off-diagonal terms may create a large nullspace beyond the scalar zero direction.}
}
\]

The next mathematical step is to compute or bound that operator on the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-row quotient.

## Agent 1 — zero-sampling Gram operator attack

I pushed the Landau/Gonek off-diagonal into an operator model.

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}
\text{ reduces to a quotient spectral-gap theorem for the zero-sampling Gram.}
}
\]

The main question is now precise:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is the near-nullspace of the zero-sampling Gram only the scalar }Z_\alpha\text{ direction,}
}
\]

or does it contain additional directions invisible to the canonical row \(T_{\can}\)?

If the near-nullspace is essentially just the scalar AFE cancellation direction, Stack C2 remains viable. If the near-nullspace has dimension \(\gg R\), then aggregate Gate–1 fails for the same reason pointwise Gate–1 failed, only at the operator level.

---

# 1. Zero-sampling Gram as an operator

For a Dirichlet packet

\[
P_a(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathcal B}a_n n^{-it},
\]

zero sampling gives

\[
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)|P_a(\gamma)|^2
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}G(n,n'),
\]

where

\[
G(n,n')
=
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}.
\]

For a smooth height window of mass \(W\), the diagonal part is approximately

\[
G(n,n)\approx W.
\]

The Landau/Gonek contribution says that if \(n/n'=k\in\mathbb N\), then

\[
G(n,n')
\approx
-\frac{T}{2\pi}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}},
\]

with the conjugate contribution when \(n'/n=k\).

Thus the Gram has the schematic form

\[
G
\approx
W I
-
\frac{T}{2\pi}
\left(\mathcal L_\Lambda+\mathcal L_\Lambda^*\right)
+
\text{dual/background/window errors},
\]

where

\[
(\mathcal L_\Lambda a)_n
\sim
\sum_{k:\,kn\in\mathcal B}
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}a_{kn}.
\]

After normalizing by the diagonal size \(W\asymp TQ\), this becomes

\[
\widetilde G
\approx
I
-
\frac{1}{Q}
\left(\mathcal L_\Lambda+\mathcal L_\Lambda^*\right)
+
\cdots.
\]

The operator

\[
\frac{1}{Q}(\mathcal L_\Lambda+\mathcal L_\Lambda^*)
\]

is not small. On AFE-length packets its norm can be order \(1\).

So the lower-frame problem is not diagonal dominance. It is spectral.

---

# 2. Continuous log-frequency approximation

Write

\[
y=\log n,
\qquad
a_n\leftrightarrow f(y),
\]

with

\[
y\in[c_-Q,c_+Q].
\]

The Landau shift operator becomes approximately

\[
(\mathcal K f)(y)
=
\int_0^{c_+Q-y}
f(y+u)\,d\psi(u)e^{-u/2},
\]

or, after coefficient normalization appropriate to \(n^{-1/2}\)-scale AFE packets, roughly an operator with kernel built from

\[
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta(u-\log k).
\]

The cumulative measure satisfies

\[
\sum_{\log k\le U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
\sim U.
\]

So the Landau operator is large on slowly varying log-frequency modes.

This explains why the scalar AFE packet can vanish at zeros: the zero-sampling Gram has a large cancellation direction tied to the zeta functional equation and the divisor/prime convolution structure.

The issue is whether that cancellation is one-dimensional or whether many low-frequency modes also nearly cancel.

---

# 3. Fourier-symbol heuristic

On an idealized translation-invariant log-frequency interval, the Landau convolution has symbol

\[
\sum_{k}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}e^{-i\tau\log k}
=
-\frac{\zeta'}{\zeta}(1+i\tau),
\]

with truncation at packet length.

Thus the normalized Gram has a model symbol of the form

\[
g(\tau)
\approx
1
+
\frac{2}{Q}\Re\frac{\zeta'}{\zeta}(1+i\tau)
\]

up to sign conventions and truncation.

Near \(\tau=0\),

\[
-\frac{\zeta'}{\zeta}(1+i\tau)
\sim
\frac{1}{i\tau}
+\text{regular terms},
\]

and the truncated version produces a contribution of size \(\asymp Q\). This is where scalar AFE cancellation lives.

For \(|\tau|\gg 1/Q\), the singular contribution weakens. Therefore the model suggests:

\[
\boxed{
\text{near-null modes should be very low log-frequency modes.}
}
\]

That is good news: if the near-nullspace is low-dimensional, a finite canonical row may see the quotient.

But this remains only a heuristic. Boundary effects, dual terms, and the exact AFE weights matter.

---

# 4. Quotient spectral-gap theorem

The precise theorem Stack C2 needs is not a lower frame on the whole packet space. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Canonical zero-Gram quotient gap.}
}
\]

Let \(\mathcal H_{\can}\) be the finite canonical packet Hilbert space containing the ordinary \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) packet data. Let \(G_Z\) be the zero-sampling Gram. Let \(N_Z\) denote the scalar zero direction or scalar AFE nullspace generated by

\[
Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0.
\]

Then prove

\[
\boxed{
\langle T_{\can}v,G_ZT_{\can}v\rangle
\ge
Q^{-A}
\langle v,G_Zv\rangle
}
\]

for the actual canonical packet vector \(v=X_{\can}\), or more ambitiously for all

\[
v\perp_{G_Z} N_Z.
\]

A stronger operator version is

\[
\boxed{
T_{\can}^*G_ZT_{\can}
\ge
Q^{-A}G_Z
\quad
\text{on }N_Z^\perp.
}
\]

This is the clean quotient version of aggregate Gate–1.

---

# 5. Why quotienting only the scalar direction may not be enough

The continuous-symbol heuristic suggests possible additional near-null modes: not just the constant scalar AFE direction, but low log-frequency modulations of it.

For example, if the scalar near-null packet is \(a^{(0)}\), then modulated packets

\[
a^{(r)}_n
=
p_r(\log n/Q)a^{(0)}_n
\]

for low-degree \(p_r\) may also have small sampled energy, because the Landau operator varies slowly across the band.

This is dangerous.

If the near-nullspace includes

\[
\operatorname{span}\{p_r(\log n/Q)a^{(0)}_n:0\le r\le r_0\}
\]

with \(r_0\asymp R\), then \(T_{\can}\) might still control it.

If \(r_0\gg R\), the finite-row canonical projection is again dimensionally underpowered.

So the next spectral question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{What is the effective dimension of the zero-Gram near-nullspace?}
}
\]

The old pointwise dimension problem reappears only if this near-nullspace has dimension \(\gtrsim Q/\log Q\).

---

# 6. Commutator test

A useful way to test the dimension of the near-nullspace is via commutators with log-frequency multiplication.

Let

\[
Y a_n = \left(\frac{\log n}{Q}\right)a_n.
\]

If the scalar zero direction is \(a^{(0)}\), then \(Ya^{(0)}\), \(Y^2a^{(0)}\), etc. generate slow modulations.

Compute the Rayleigh quotient

\[
\mathcal R_r
=
\frac{
\langle Y^r a^{(0)},G_ZY^r a^{(0)}\rangle
}{
\langle Y^r a^{(0)},Y^r a^{(0)}\rangle
}.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal R_r\ll Q^{-A}
\]

for many \(r\), the quotient gap fails unless \(T_{\can}\) captures all those directions.

The commutator

\[
[G_Z,Y]
\]

measures how quickly the zero-Gram cancellation changes across log-frequency. If

\[
\|[G_Z,Y]\|\ll Q^{-1}
\]

on the relevant subspace, then many slow modulations may remain near-null.

If instead

\[
[G_Z,Y]
\]

is polynomially large after one or two commutators, then the near-nullspace is small and finite-row \(T_{\can}\) may suffice.

So the commutator estimate is a concrete next attack.

---

# 7. Relation to \(T_{\can}\)

The canonical Gate–1 map \(T_{\can}\) consists of finite derivative/curvature symbols. In log-frequency language, these are low-degree polynomial multipliers and canonical curvature multipliers.

Thus \(T_{\can}\) is naturally designed to detect slow modulations such as

\[
p(\log n/Q)a^{(0)}_n,
\qquad
\deg p\le R.
\]

Therefore, if the zero-Gram near-nullspace is generated by only \(O(R)\) slow modulations of the scalar direction, then AggG1\(_{\can}\) is plausible.

The needed theorem can be sharpened:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Every zero-Gram near-null packet is approximable by an }O(R)\text{-degree canonical modulation}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
T_{\can}\text{ is elliptic on the quotient of that modulation space.}
}
\]

This is much more structured than a generic lower sampling theorem.

---

# 8. Full canonical inclusion changes the near-nullspace

If one uses only the main prime packet \(P_2\), the near-nullspace is distorted. The scalar zero relation involves

\[
P_2+D_2+B_2+E_2.
\]

Therefore the scalar null direction is not a vector in \(P_2\)-space alone; it lives in the full canonical packet space.

The quotient must therefore be built in

\[
\mathcal H_{\can}
=
\mathcal H_{P_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{D_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{B_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{E_2}.
\]

The scalar null vector is

\[
v_Z
=
v_{P_2}+v_{D_2}+v_{B_2}+v_{E_2}.
\]

The near-nullspace analysis should be done for modulations of \(v_Z\), not of \(v_{P_2}\) alone.

This is important because \(D_2\) may kill some false near-null modes by folded-dual interference.

---

# 9. Current refined theorem target

The new best statement for ordinary AggG1 is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-Gram canonical quotient spectral theorem.}
}
\]

Let \(G_Z\) be the routed zero-sampling Gram on the full ordinary canonical packet space. Let \(\mathcal N_{\mathrm{slow}}\) be the span of slow canonical modulations of the scalar zero packet up to the depth relevant to \(R\). Then:

1. **Near-null classification**

\[
\boxed{
\langle v,G_Zv\rangle\le Q^{-A}\|v\|^2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{dist}(v,\mathcal N_{\mathrm{slow}})\le Q^{-A'}\|v\|.
}
\]

2. **Canonical ellipticity on slow quotient**

\[
\boxed{
\|T_{\can}v\|_{G_Z}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|v\|_{G_Z}^2
\quad
(v\in\mathcal N_{\mathrm{slow}}\ominus \mathbb C v_Z).
}
\]

Together these imply aggregate ordinary Gate–1 after quotienting the scalar zero direction.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ remains viable, but now hinges on zero-Gram spectral structure.}
}
\]

The exact next mathematical bottleneck is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{classify the near-nullspace of the canonical zero-sampling Gram.}
}
\]

The key risk is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{there may be many slow modulation near-null modes beyond the scalar zero direction.}
}
\]

The key hope is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{those modes may be finite-depth and visible to }T_{\can}.
}
\]

So the next concrete attack should compute/estimate the commutators

\[
[G_Z,Y],\quad [G_Z,Y^2],\quad \ldots
\]

or the Rayleigh quotients of slow modulations of the scalar canonical packet.

## Agent 1 — continued zero-Gram attack

I pushed the operator model further. The previous fear was:

\[
\text{Landau/Gonek off-diagonal may create many zero-Gram near-null modes.}
\]

The refined conclusion is more favorable:

\[
\boxed{
\text{In the leading log-frequency model, the Landau off-diagonal is essentially rank one.}
}
\]

That means the zero-sampling Gram’s main near-nullspace is plausibly just the scalar AFE zero direction, not a large \(Q/\log Q\)-dimensional invisible space. This is a substantial improvement for Stack C2.

But this remains conditional on proving that the discrete prime-log Landau operator, folded-dual completion, smoothing, and routed branch restriction do not introduce additional near-null modes.

---

# 1. Correct normalization of the Landau operator

Take a one-branch AFE-scale packet

\[
P(t)=\sum_n a_n n^{-it}.
\]

For natural AFE coefficients, write

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n).
\]

Then

\[
\sum_n |a_n|^2
\sim
\int |f(y)|^2\,dy,
\qquad y=\log n.
\]

The zero-sampling off-diagonal contains, for ratios \(k\),

\[
-\frac{T}{2\pi}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\sum_n a_{kn}\overline{a_n}.
\]

Substitute \(a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n)\). With \(u=\log k\),

\[
a_{kn}\overline{a_n}
=
(k n)^{-1/2}n^{-1/2}
f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}
=
k^{-1/2}n^{-1}f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}.
\]

Multiplying by

\[
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\]

gives

\[
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\,n^{-1}f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}.
\]

Since

\[
\sum_n n^{-1}F(\log n)\sim\int F(y)\,dy,
\]

the off-diagonal kernel is governed by the measure

\[
d\psi_1(u)
=
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}(u).
\]

By partial summation and PNT,

\[
\sum_{k\le e^U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
=
U+O(1)
\]

up to standard prime-number-theorem errors. Thus \(d\psi_1(u)\) is, to leading order, Lebesgue measure \(du\).

This is the key correction: after natural AFE normalization, the Landau operator is not an arbitrary large convolution. Its leading kernel is flat in log-frequency.

---

# 2. Leading continuous model is rank one

Let the one-branch log interval be

\[
I=[a,b],
\qquad
L=b-a.
\]

The forward Landau shift operator is approximately

\[
(K_+f)(y)=\int_y^b f(z)\,dz.
\]

The adjoint/backward contribution is

\[
(K_-f)(y)=\int_a^y f(z)\,dz.
\]

Therefore

\[
(K_++K_-)f(y)
=
\int_a^b f(z)\,dz.
\]

This is the rank-one operator

\[
Jf=\langle f,1\rangle\,1.
\]

After normalizing by the diagonal zero density \(Q\), the leading zero-sampling Gram is

\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathrm{lead}}
=
I-\frac1QJ.
}
\]

Thus the spectrum on a one-branch interval of length \(L\) is:

\[
1-\frac{L}{Q}
\quad\text{on constants,}
\]

and

\[
1
\quad\text{on functions orthogonal to constants.}
\]

If

\[
L\le cQ
\qquad(c<1),
\]

then the one-branch Gram has a uniform spectral gap

\[
1-\frac{L}{Q}\ge 1-c.
\]

So in the leading model, a **strict subcritical branch band** has no large near-nullspace.

This is important: the earlier “many slow modulations may be near-null” concern is false for the leading flat-kernel operator. Slow modulations orthogonal to the constant are not near-null; they have eigenvalue \(1\).

---

# 3. Full AFE main+dual gives one scalar null direction

For the full scalar AFE, the relevant space is not a single short interval but a direct sum of main and folded-dual components. Idealize it as

\[
H=L^2(I_+)\oplus L^2(I_-),
\]

with total effective log-length

\[
|I_+|+|I_-|\approx Q.
\]

The leading zero-sampling Gram is then

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}
=
I-\frac1Q\,e\otimes e,
\]

where \(e\) is the scalar AFE direction combining the main branch, folded-dual branch, and the scalar background/completion contribution.

If

\[
\|e\|^2=Q,
\]

then

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}e=0.
\]

On the orthogonal complement,

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}=I.
\]

Therefore the leading model has exactly one null direction:

\[
\boxed{
\ker G_{\mathrm{lead}}=\mathbb C e.
}
\]

This is precisely the scalar zero direction \(Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0\).

So the Stack C2 hope is now sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{after including the full canonical AFE, zero sampling kills only the scalar value direction.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Why finite canonical rows can detect the scalar null direction

The scalar value row vanishes at zeros. But Gate–1 does not rely only on value. It uses finite derivative/curvature rows.

In log-frequency, differentiation multiplies by \(y=\log n\). Let

\[
Yf(y)=y f(y).
\]

If the null vector is \(e=1\) on an interval of total length \(Q\), then

\[
Ye
\]

is not proportional to \(e\). Its quotient component is

\[
Ye-\frac{\langle Ye,e\rangle}{\|e\|^2}e.
\]

The squared norm is

\[
\int_0^Q \left(y-\frac Q2\right)^2dy
=
\frac{Q^3}{12}.
\]

After the usual \(Q\)-normalizations of derivative rows, this is polynomially large.

Thus the first derivative row sees the scalar null direction. This is exactly the aggregate analogue of the fact that \(Z(\gamma)=0\) but \(Z'(\gamma)\) need not vanish.

So, in the leading model:

\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathrm{lead}}\text{ kills }e,\text{ but }G_{\mathrm{lead}}\text{ does not kill }Ye.
}
\]

This strongly supports the canonical finite-row approach.

---

# 5. Commutator computation

Let

\[
J=e\otimes e.
\]

Then

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}=I-\frac1QJ.
\]

The commutator with \(Y\) is

\[
[G_{\mathrm{lead}},Y]
=
-\frac1Q[J,Y].
\]

For any \(f\),

\[
[J,Y]f
=
e\langle Yf,e\rangle
-
Ye\langle f,e\rangle.
\]

Thus

\[
[J,Y]
\]

has rank at most two, with range contained in

\[
\operatorname{span}\{e,Ye\}.
\]

Higher commutators generate only finite polynomial spans:

\[
\operatorname{span}\{e,Ye,\ldots,Y^k e\}.
\]

This is highly favorable. It means the slow modulation family generated by commutators has dimension \(k+1\), not \(Q/\log Q\).

Therefore, if the real zero-sampling Gram is a perturbation of \(G_{\mathrm{lead}}\), the relevant near-null/commutator dynamics should live in a finite polynomial modulation space, exactly the kind of space \(T_{\can}\) is designed to see.

---

# 6. What must now be proved

The leading model suggests a concrete theorem.

Let \(G_Z\) be the actual smoothed zero-sampling Gram on the full canonical AFE packet space

\[
\mathcal H_{\can}
=
\mathcal H_{P_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{D_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{B_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{E_2}.
\]

Let \(e_Z\) be the scalar AFE direction satisfying

\[
Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0.
\]

One needs an operator approximation:

\[
\boxed{
G_Z
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z
+
\mathcal E_Z,
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal E_Z\|_{\mathcal H_{\can}\to\mathcal H_{\can}}
\le Q^{-c}
}
\]

or at least polynomially smaller than the quotient gap.

Then one obtains:

\[
\boxed{
G_Z\ge Q^{-A}I
\quad\text{on }e_Z^\perp.
}
\]

And because \(T_{\can}\) includes derivative/curvature rows, one expects:

\[
\boxed{
\|T_{\can}e_Z\|_{G_Z}^2\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Z\|^2.
}
\]

Together, this would prove aggregate ordinary Gate–1.

---

# 7. The actual difficulty: proving the perturbation bound

The operator error \(\mathcal E_Z\) contains:

1. prime-number-theorem error in

\[
\sum_{k\le e^U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}=U+O(1);
\]

2. smoothing/window boundary errors;

3. ratio terms \(n/n'\) not exactly integer and corresponding non-Landau frequencies;

4. folded-dual and functional-equation phase errors;

5. gamma/background \(B_2\) and completion terms \(D_2,E_2\);

6. routing restriction to \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) rather than all zeros.

For all zeros in a smooth window and the full AFE packet, items 1–5 look potentially manageable. The leading flat-kernel/rank-one structure is robust.

Item 6 is the hardest. Routing to ordinary centers can destroy the rank-one approximation unless transition/singular branches are included branch-completely.

Thus the theorem should be branch-complete:

\[
\boxed{
G_{\ord}+G_{\tr}+G_{\sing}
=
I-\frac1Qe_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E,
}
\]

in the appropriate direct-sum branch coordinate system.

---

# 8. Revised status of the near-nullspace concern

Earlier concern:

\[
\text{many slow modulations might be near-null.}
\]

Current leading-model conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No: the leading Landau operator is rank one, so only the scalar direction is null.}
}
\]

Slow modulations \(Y^r e\) are not null after quotienting the scalar direction. They are detected by derivative rows.

The remaining risk is not many slow modes from the leading operator; it is uncontrolled perturbations:

\[
\mathcal E_Z
\]

from discrete primes, smoothing, routing, and branch splitting.

So the next mathematical problem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove the actual zero-sampling Gram is a polynomial perturbation of the rank-one model.}
}
\]

---

# 9. New refined theorem target

The strongest current Agent 1 theorem target is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Canonical zero-sampling rank-one law.}
}
\]

For the full branch-complete canonical packet space, the smoothed zero-sampling Gram satisfies

\[
\boxed{
G_{\can,Z}
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z
+
\mathcal E,
\qquad
\|\mathcal E\|\le Q^{-c}
}
\]

after natural AFE normalization and branch-complete routing.

Then:

1. \(G_{\can,Z}\) has a spectral gap on \(e_Z^\perp\);

2. \(T_{\can}\) sees the scalar null direction through derivative/curvature rows;

3. aggregate ordinary/branch-complete Gate–1 follows.

This theorem is much more concrete and more plausible than the old pointwise projection theorem.

---

# 10. Final conclusion for this attack

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ has a credible operator route.}
}
\]

The key positive discovery is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}\approx du
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{Landau off-diagonal }\approx\text{ rank one}.
}
\]

The exact next proof obligation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-complete canonical zero-sampling rank-one law with polynomial error.}
}
\]

If this rank-one law holds, Stack C2’s first input becomes genuinely plausible.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:43:24)

I’ll now stress-test the rank-one law. The main issue is that the prime measure \(\sum \Lambda(k)k^{-1}\delta_{\log k}\) is close to Lebesgue only after smoothing; as an unsmoothed \(L^2\) operator it is not a small perturbation.

_Thought for 26s_

## Agent 1 — stress test of the rank-one zero-Gram law

The previous attack found a favorable leading model:

\[
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}\approx du
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{Landau off-diagonal}\approx\text{rank one}.
\]

The new stress test shows the precise caveat:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The rank-one law is plausible for the actual smooth canonical packet, not for arbitrary refined-cell vectors.}
}
\]

As an unsmoothed \(L^2\) operator, the prime measure is not close to Lebesgue. It becomes close only after using the smoothness/AFE structure of the actual coefficients. This reinforces the move away from universal lower sampling and toward actual-coefficient AggG1.

---

# 1. The prime measure is only weakly close to Lebesgue

The relevant measure is

\[
d\psi_1(u)
=
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}(u).
\]

Its cumulative distribution is

\[
\Psi_1(U)
=
\sum_{\log k\le U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
=
U+C+O(e^{-c\sqrt U})
\]

under a standard PNT-strength estimate, ignoring endpoint smoothing.

So

\[
d\psi_1(u)=du+dE(u),
\]

where the primitive

\[
E(U):=\Psi_1(U)-U
\]

is bounded or slowly varying.

But this does **not** imply

\[
dE
\]

is a small measure in total variation. It is still a discrete prime measure minus Lebesgue. On arbitrary high-frequency test functions, it can be large.

Therefore the operator approximation

\[
K_{\psi_1}\approx K_{du}
\]

is false on arbitrary \(L^2\) packet spaces.

It is only true on smooth enough log-frequency profiles.

---

# 2. Smooth actual packets make the error small

Let

\[
(K_E f)(y)
=
\int_0^{b-y} f(y+u)\,dE(u).
\]

Integrating by parts gives

\[
K_E f(y)
=
f(b)E(b-y)-f(y)E(0)
-
\int_0^{b-y} f'(y+u)E(u)\,du.
\]

So the error is controlled by the smoothness of \(f\), not merely by \(\|f\|_2\).

If \(f\) is an arbitrary refined-cell profile, \(f'\) may be as large as the cell resolution permits. Then \(K_E\) is not small.

But for the actual AFE coefficient profile,

\[
a_n\sim n^{-1/2}V(n/T^\theta)
\]

or its canonical differentiated/weighted variants, the log-frequency profile

\[
f(y)
\]

is smooth on scale \(1\), and often varies only on scale \(Q\) after normalization. Then

\[
\|f'\|_2
\ll Q^{-1}\|f\|_2
\]

or at worst polynomially controlled.

In that regime,

\[
\|K_E f\|_2
\ll Q^{-c}\|K_{du}f\|_2
\]

is plausible after smoothing, or at least

\[
\|K_E f\|_2
\le Q^{O(1)}\|f\|_{\text{controlled Sobolev}}
\]

with a normalized error small enough for the aggregate theorem.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{PNT error is controllable for smooth actual coefficients, not for adversarial packets.}
}
\]

This is exactly why the actual-coefficient theorem is the correct target.

---

# 3. Leading rank-one law on smooth packets

On a log interval

\[
I=[a,b],
\]

the Lebesgue replacement gives

\[
(K_+f)(y)=\int_y^b f(z)\,dz,
\]

\[
(K_-f)(y)=\int_a^y f(z)\,dz.
\]

Thus

\[
(K_++K_-)f(y)
=
\int_a^b f(z)\,dz.
\]

So

\[
K_++K_-=1\otimes 1.
\]

After diagonal normalization, the zero-sampling Gram has leading form

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}
=
I-\frac{1}{Q}(1\otimes1)
\]

on a one-branch interval.

For the full AFE main+dual packet, the scalar AFE vector is

\[
e_Z=e_+ \oplus e_- \oplus e_{B_2}\oplus e_{D_2}\oplus e_{E_2}.
\]

The leading full canonical Gram should be

\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathrm{lead}}
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z.
}
\]

If

\[
\|e_Z\|^2=Q,
\]

then

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}e_Z=0
\]

and

\[
G_{\mathrm{lead}}=I
\]

on \(e_Z^\perp\).

So the leading model has exactly one null direction.

---

# 4. Non-integer ratios are the second major error

The Landau main term applies cleanly when

\[
\frac n{n'}=k\in\mathbb N
\]

or the reciprocal relation holds. For general rational ratios \(n/n'\) that are not integers, the explicit formula gives no comparable von Mangoldt main term.

But there are many such pairs.

Thus the zero-Gram decomposes as:

\[
G_Z
=
G_{\diag}
+
G_{\mathrm{integer\ ratio}}
+
G_{\mathrm{noninteger\ remainder}}
+
G_{\mathrm{dual/bg/tail}}
+
G_{\mathrm{window}}.
\]

The rank-one law controls

\[
G_{\diag}+G_{\mathrm{integer\ ratio}}.
\]

A genuine theorem must show

\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathrm{noninteger\ remainder}}
}
\]

is a polynomially small or harmless operator on the **actual canonical coefficient class**.

For arbitrary coefficients, this is again false or at least not accessible by diagonal dominance. For actual AFE coefficients, there may be cancellation from smoothness and multiplicative structure.

The required estimate is a zero-sum off-ratio bound:

\[
\sum_{\substack{n,n'\\ n/n'\notin\mathbb N,\ n'/n\notin\mathbb N}}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}
=
\text{controlled error}.
\]

This is a concrete analytic input.

---

# 5. Boundary truncation creates finite-rank errors

The interval is not translation-invariant. Forward shifts only exist when

\[
y+u\le b,
\]

and backward shifts only when

\[
y-u\ge a.
\]

For the Lebesgue kernel this was harmless because

\[
\int_y^b+\int_a^y=\int_a^b.
\]

But after inserting actual AFE cutoffs, gamma weights, and dual terms, exact cancellation may fail near boundaries.

Boundary errors should be finite-rank or low-complexity, generated by endpoint cutoff functions and their derivatives. These are likely visible to the finite canonical row \(T_{\can}\), but this must be proved.

A useful target is:

\[
G_Z
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z
+
B_{\partial}
+
E_{\mathrm{small}},
\]

where

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_{\partial}=O(R)
\]

or \(B_{\partial}\) is controlled on the canonical row quotient.

If boundary errors generate many near-null modes, AggG1 fails. If they are finite-rank and visible, they are harmless.

---

# 6. Canonical derivative row detects the null direction

The scalar null direction satisfies

\[
Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0.
\]

But Gate–1 uses derivative/curvature data.

In the leading model, multiplication by log-frequency

\[
Yf=yf
\]

sends the null vector \(e_Z\) to

\[
Ye_Z.
\]

The quotient component is

\[
Ye_Z-\frac{\langle Ye_Z,e_Z\rangle}{\|e_Z\|^2}e_Z.
\]

For an interval of length \(Q\), this has norm squared

\[
\asymp Q^3
\]

before derivative normalization, hence polynomially large after the ordinary \(Q\)-normalization.

So the finite canonical derivative row should detect the scalar null direction unless the zero is multiple or a separate derivative-row degeneracy occurs.

In aggregate form, this is favorable:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero sampling kills value, but derivative rows recover energy.}
}
\]

---

# 7. The correct theorem is Sobolev-restricted rank-one law

The previous rank-one law should be refined as follows.

Let \(\mathcal H_{\can}^{(s)}\) be the actual canonical packet Sobolev class in log-frequency, with \(s\) large enough to control PNT-discreteness and cutoff errors, but small enough to remain polynomial in \(Q\).

Then prove:

\[
\boxed{
G_{Z,\can}
=
I-\frac1Qe_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E
}
\]

on \(\mathcal H_{\can}^{(s)}\), with

\[
\boxed{
|\langle f,\mathcal E f\rangle|
\le
Q^{-c}\|f\|^2_{\mathcal H_{\can}^{(0)}}
+
Q^{-A_s}\|f\|^2_{\mathcal H_{\can}^{(s)}}.
}
\]

For the actual canonical packet, the Sobolev norm satisfies

\[
\|X_{\can}\|_{\mathcal H^{(s)}}\le Q^{C_s}\|X_{\can}\|.
\]

Therefore the error is polynomially controlled.

This theorem is much more plausible than an \(L^2\)-operator bound on arbitrary packets.

---

# 8. Branch-complete version

Routing restriction remains the largest practical issue.

The rank-one law is most plausible for all zeros in a smooth height window. If one restricts to ordinary centers only, the sampling measure changes:

\[
\mu_Z
\quad\leadsto\quad
\mu_{\ord}.
\]

That can destroy the rank-one law.

Thus the correct branch-complete theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
G_{\ord,\can}
+
G_{\tr,\can}
+
G_{\sing,\can}
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E.
}
\]

Each branch uses its own coordinates:

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can}
\quad\text{ordinary},
\]

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}
\quad\text{transition},
\]

\[
S_R
\quad\text{singular}.
\]

This says the full routed system samples the canonical source even if the ordinary branch alone has holes.

---

# 9. Current refined proof route

The route for AggG1 now has four concrete steps.

## Step 1 — full-zero rank-one law

Prove for all zeros in a smooth window:

\[
G_{Z,\can}
=
I-\frac1Qe_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E.
\]

Use explicit formula, PNT for \(\sum\Lambda(k)/k\), and actual AFE smoothness.

## Step 2 — route decomposition

Show that ordinary/transition/singular branch coordinates partition this full-zero Gram up to polynomially controlled error:

\[
G_{Z,\can}
=
G_{\ord}+G_{\tr}+G_{\sing}
+
E_{\route}.
\]

## Step 3 — branch coordinate ellipticity

Show each branch coordinate system sees the corresponding part of

\[
e_Z^\perp
\]

or detects the scalar null direction by derivative/jet coefficients.

## Step 4 — aggregate Gate–1

Conclude

\[
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal G_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m).
\]

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ is now reduced to a Sobolev-restricted rank-one law.}
}
\]

The promising fact:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the leading Landau operator is rank one after correct } \Lambda(k)/k\text{ normalization.}
}
\]

The main remaining dangers:

\[
\boxed{
\text{noninteger-ratio zero sums;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{PNT-discreteness on insufficiently smooth packets;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{boundary/cutoff finite-rank modes;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{routing destroying the full-zero rank-one law.}
}
\]

The next attack should focus on the noninteger-ratio remainder and whether actual AFE smoothness is enough to make it harmless.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:44:44)

I’ll now attack the noninteger-ratio remainder. The useful reframing is to express it through the pair-correlation kernel for \(\log n-\log n'\), then test whether actual AFE smoothness gives enough cancellation.

_Thought for 6s_

## Agent 1 — noninteger-ratio remainder attack

We now isolate the dangerous term in the zero-sampling Gram:

\[
G_Z(n,n')
=
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}.
\]

The integer-ratio terms \(n=kn'\) or \(n'=kn\) produce the Landau/Gonek main term and, after the correct \(n^{-1/2}\) AFE normalization, lead to the favorable rank-one operator.

The remaining obstacle is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{\mathrm{nonint}}
=
\sum_{\substack{n,n'\\ n/n'\notin\mathbb N,\ n'/n\notin\mathbb N}}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma\log(n/n')}.
}
\]

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{The noninteger-ratio remainder is not automatically small.}
}
\]

But for **actual smooth AFE coefficients**, it plausibly becomes a lower-order pseudodifferential error after summation. The required theorem is a **smoothed zero pair-correlation estimate against the actual log-ratio density**.

---

# 1. Rewriting the noninteger term by log-ratio

Let

\[
u=\log(n/n').
\]

Define the coefficient autocorrelation measure

\[
A(u)
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\,\delta\!\left(u-\log(n/n')\right).
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)|P_a(\gamma)|^2
=
\int \widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\,dA(u),
\]

where

\[
\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)=\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma u}.
\]

The integer-ratio Landau piece corresponds to atoms where

\[
u=\log k
\quad\text{or}\quad
u=-\log k.
\]

The noninteger-ratio remainder is the contribution from all other log-ratios.

Thus the problem is to control

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathbb R\setminus\{\pm\log k\}}
\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\,dA(u).
}
\]

For arbitrary \(a_n\), \(A(u)\) can concentrate on bad frequencies \(u\). For actual smooth AFE coefficients, \(A(u)\) is itself smooth at scale roughly \(e^{-cQ}\) in frequency but has a structured density after aggregation.

---

# 2. Continuous approximation of the autocorrelation

Take

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n).
\]

Then

\[
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\sim
(n n')^{-1/2}f(y)\overline{f(y')}.
\]

Set

\[
u=y-y',
\qquad
y' = y-u.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_n n^{-1}F(\log n)\sim\int F(y)\,dy,
\]

the smooth approximation gives

\[
A(u)
\approx
\int f(y)\overline{f(y-u)}\,dy.
\]

So the autocorrelation is the usual log-frequency autocorrelation:

\[
\boxed{
A(u)\approx (f\ast \widetilde f)(u).
}
\]

This is smooth if \(f\) is smooth.

Therefore the noninteger-ratio term should be controlled by pairing the zero Fourier transform \(\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\) against a smooth autocorrelation.

This is much easier than bounding \(\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\) pointwise.

---

# 3. Explicit formula decomposition for \(\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\)

For \(u>0\), the smoothed zero exponential sum has the schematic explicit formula

\[
\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)
=
\text{diagonal density term near }u=0
-
\frac{T}{2\pi}e^{-u/2}\Lambda(e^u)
\quad\text{when }e^u\in\mathbb N
+
\mathcal E_Z(u).
\]

More carefully, the prime term is atomic at

\[
u=\log k.
\]

Thus

\[
\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)
=
Q\,T\,\delta_0(u)
-
T\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}\delta_{\log k}(u)
-
T\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}\delta_{-\log k}(u)
+
\mathcal E_Z(u),
\]

in distributional form, after smoothing.

The integer-ratio Landau operator comes from the atomic \(\delta_{\pm\log k}\) terms.

The noninteger-ratio remainder is governed by

\[
\int \mathcal E_Z(u)A(u)\,du.
\]

So the required estimate is:

\[
\boxed{
\left|\int \mathcal E_Z(u)A_{\can}(u)\,du\right|
\le
Q^{-c}\cdot TQ\cdot \|f_{\can}\|_2^2
}
\]

or at least polynomially smaller than the quotient gap.

---

# 4. What explicit formula can plausibly give

If the test function in \(u\) is smooth and compactly supported at scale \(Q\), then the explicit formula error should depend on derivatives of the \(u\)-test function.

Let

\[
\phi(u)=A_{\can}(u).
\]

The desired estimate is roughly a smoothed explicit-formula bound:

\[
\sum_\gamma \rho(\gamma)\widehat{\phi}(\gamma)
=
\text{density term}
-
\text{prime atomic term}
+
O(Q^{-c}TQ\|\phi\|_{\mathrm{Sob}}).
\]

For actual AFE \(f\), the autocorrelation \(\phi=f\ast\tilde f\) has controlled Sobolev norms.

So the noninteger term is plausibly harmless **if**:

\[
\boxed{
\|A_{\can}\|_{\mathrm{Sob}}\le Q^C\|A_{\can}\|_{L^1/L^2}
}
\]

and the explicit formula is applied with sufficient smoothing.

This is another reason the universal lower frame fails: adversarial \(a_n\) can make \(A(u)\) highly oscillatory, and then \(\mathcal E_Z\) is not small.

---

# 5. However: support length creates high derivative cost

The band has length

\[
Q.
\]

If \(f\) has cutoff endpoints, its autocorrelation \(A(u)\) has corners at the difference-set boundaries. These are not smooth unless the AFE cutoffs are smoothed strongly.

Therefore the canonical theorem must use smooth packets:

\[
V(n/T^\theta)
\]

with enough derivatives satisfying

\[
\|V^{(j)}\|\le Q^{Cj}
\]

or better.

If hard refined cells are used, the autocorrelation has jumps, and the explicit-formula error may be large. Hence:

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1 must be formulated using smooth cell partitions / smooth canonical packets, not hard cells.}
}
\]

This is consistent with the O1b cell-symbol bridge warning.

---

# 6. Finite-rank boundary terms

Even with smooth cutoffs, the leading flat-kernel rank-one law acquires endpoint corrections from the support interval. In autocorrelation language, these arise from the fact that

\[
A(u)=\int_{I\cap(I+u)}f(y)\overline{f(y-u)}\,dy
\]

depends on the overlap length.

For constant \(f\), this is triangular:

\[
A(u)=L-|u|
\quad (|u|\le L).
\]

The prime atomic term integrated against this triangular function gives

\[
\sum_{k\le e^L}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}(L-\log k)
\sim
\frac{L^2}{2}.
\]

This matches the integral kernel computation and rank-one cancellation.

Boundary corrections are therefore not random; they are part of the exact rank-one law.

For smooth \(f\), boundary terms can be expressed through endpoint jets of \(f\). These likely generate a finite-dimensional correction space:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{span}\{\text{endpoint jets of }f\}.
}
\]

This should be visible to \(T_{\can}\) if the derivative depth \(R\) is large enough.

But this must be proved.

---

# 7. What about off-ratio pairs with \(n/n'\) close to an integer?

Suppose

\[
\frac n{n'}=k+\epsilon,
\qquad |\epsilon|\ll1.
\]

Then

\[
u=\log(k+\epsilon)
=
\log k+\frac{\epsilon}{k}+O(\epsilon^2).
\]

The zero sum \(\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\) may be large near \(\log k\), depending on smoothing width.

If the height window has length \(H\), then \(\widehat{\mu_Z}(u)\) has frequency resolution roughly

\[
1/H.
\]

So near-integer ratios within

\[
|u-\log k|\lesssim 1/H
\]

can contribute to the Landau peak.

But with \(n,n'\le T^{c_+}\) and a height window \(H\) comparable to a macroscopic fraction of \(T\), the spacing between distinct log-ratios is typically much larger than \(1/H\) only after accounting for Diophantine coincidences. There may be many near-rational coincidences.

Thus the clean integer/noninteger split should be replaced by a smoothed prime peak:

\[
\delta_{\log k}
\quad\leadsto\quad
\widehat{\rho}(\,\cdot-\log k\,).
\]

Then the Landau operator is

\[
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
\widehat{\rho}(\log(n/n')-\log k).
\]

For smooth \(f\), this should again approximate convolution with \(du\). But proving this requires a bilinear estimate over near-ratio pairs.

The exact needed estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{n,n',k}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
\widehat{\rho}\!\left(\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right)
=
\int\!\!\int f(y)\overline{f(z)}K_{\rho}(y-z)\,dy\,dz
+
O(Q^{-c}\|f\|^2).
}
\]

This is a smoothed shifted-convolution estimate in log variables.

---

# 8. The main risk

The main risk is that actual AFE coefficients are not smooth enough after all canonical operations and branch partitions.

If \(X_{\can}\) uses refined cells of width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q
\]

with sharp cutoffs, then \(f'\sim 1/\Delta\), acceptable, but sharp boundaries still cause Fourier tails. Smooth partition of unity removes this.

If we require dimension-sufficient refined cells, the partition has about

\[
Q/\log Q
\]

cells. Smooth cell cutoffs have derivatives of size

\[
(\log Q)^{-j},
\]

which is fine. The number of cells is large, but the actual packet is not arbitrary across them; its envelope remains smooth.

Therefore the actual-coefficient theorem should not use arbitrary cell amplitudes. It should use the genuine smooth AFE envelope.

This is a major restriction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1}_{\can}\text{ must be for the actual packet, not for all refined-cell amplitude vectors.}
}
\]

---

# 9. Refined theorem target

The correct theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Smoothed canonical zero-Gram rank-one theorem.}
}
\]

Let \(f_{\can}\) be the log-frequency coefficient profile of the full canonical packet. Let \(G_Z\) be the smoothed zero-sampling Gram. Then

\[
\boxed{
\langle f_{\can},G_Z f_{\can}\rangle
=
\left\langle f_{\can},
\left(I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z\right)
f_{\can}
\right\rangle
+
O(Q^{-c}\|f_{\can}\|^2)
}
\]

with analogous vector-valued/branch-complete formulation for

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

This theorem should be proved using:

1. smoothed explicit formula;
2. PNT for \(\sum \Lambda(k)/k\);
3. Sobolev bounds on the actual canonical coefficient profiles;
4. smoothed shifted-convolution control for near-ratio pairs;
5. branch-complete routing or all-zero sampling.

---

# 10. Final status of noninteger remainder

\[
\boxed{
\text{Noninteger-ratio remainder is not a small }L^2\text{ operator.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{It may be small on actual smooth canonical coefficients.}
}
\]

The precise missing estimate is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{smoothed explicit-formula error against the canonical autocorrelation }A_{\can}(u).
}
\]

The next attack should try to derive this estimate from the explicit formula with a concrete smooth test function and quantify the required Sobolev norms.

_Thought for 39s_

## Agent 1 — smoothed explicit-formula route for AggG1\(_{\can}\)

The noninteger-ratio remainder becomes tractable if the aggregate zero sum is taken over a **macroscopic smooth height window**. This is the first concrete route that looks like it can actually prove the zero-Gram rank-one law.

The key condition is:

\[
H \gg T^{c_+ + \delta},
\]

where \(H\) is the height-window length and the ordinary packet satisfies

\[
n\le T^{c_+},\qquad c_+<\frac12.
\]

Under that condition, the smoothed explicit formula resolves log-ratios sharply enough that **only exact integer ratios survive**. The dangerous noninteger-ratio remainder is then rapidly decaying.

So the next viable theorem is not a local-in-height sampling statement. It is a **macroscopic smoothed aggregate theorem**.

---

# 1. Smoothed zero Gram

Let

\[
w_T(t)=w\!\left(\frac{t-T}{H}\right),
\]

with \(w\in C_c^\infty\), \(w\ge0\), and \(H\) to be chosen.

Define

\[
K_H(u)
=
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma u}.
\]

For

\[
P(t)=\sum_n a_n n^{-it},
\]

we have

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
K_H\!\left(\log\frac n{n'}\right).
\]

Apply the smoothed explicit formula to

\[
h_u(t)=w_T(t)e^{-iut}.
\]

With the convention

\[
\widehat h(\xi)=\int h(t)e^{-it\xi}\,dt,
\]

one gets schematically

\[
K_H(u)
=
\frac{Q}{2\pi}H e^{-iTu}\widehat w(Hu)
-
H\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\left[
e^{-iT(u-\log k)}\widehat w(H(u-\log k))
+
e^{-iT(u+\log k)}\widehat w(H(u+\log k))
\right]
+
\operatorname{Err}_{\Gamma,\rho}(u).
\]

The first term is the diagonal density near \(u=0\). The two prime sums are concentrated near

\[
u=\pm \log k.
\]

---

# 2. Exact-ratio selection when \(H\) is large

Now set

\[
u=\log(n/n').
\]

The prime term near \(+\log k\) is significant when

\[
\log(n/n')-\log k
=
\log\frac{n}{kn'}
\]

is \(O(1/H)\).

If

\[
n\ne kn',
\]

then

\[
\left|\frac{n}{kn'}-1\right|
\ge \frac{1}{kn'}.
\]

Since

\[
n,n'\le T^{c_+},
\]

and \(k n'\) is comparable to \(n\) in the relevant range,

\[
\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|
\gg T^{-c_+}.
\]

Therefore if

\[
H\ge T^{c_+ + \delta},
\]

then

\[
H\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|
\gg T^\delta.
\]

Because \(\widehat w\) is rapidly decaying,

\[
\widehat w\!\left(H\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right)
\ll_A T^{-A}
\]

for every \(A\). Hence nonexact ratios are negligible.

Thus, in a smooth height window of length

\[
\boxed{
H\gg T^{c_+ +\delta},
}
\]

the noninteger-ratio remainder is killed by Fourier localization.

This is much stronger than the previous vague “smoothness may help” statement.

---

# 3. Resulting exact-ratio Gram

Under this condition, the zero Gram reduces to:

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
=
\frac{Q}{2\pi}H\sum_n |a_n|^2
-
H\sum_{k}
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\sum_{n'} a_{kn'}\overline{a_{n'}}
-
H\sum_{k}
\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\sum_{n'} a_{n'}\overline{a_{kn'}}
+
\text{controlled errors}.
\]

For natural AFE coefficients

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n),
\]

the forward prime term is

\[
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}
\sum_{n'}a_{kn'}\overline{a_{n'}}
=
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
\sum_{n'}\frac{1}{n'}
f(\log n'+\log k)\overline{f(\log n')}.
\]

Approximating

\[
\sum_{n'}\frac1{n'}F(\log n')\sim \int F(y)\,dy,
\]

and writing \(u=\log k\), this becomes

\[
\int\int_{y+u\in I}
f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}\,dy\,d\psi_1(u),
\]

where

\[
d\psi_1(u)=\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}(u).
\]

Using

\[
d\psi_1(u)=du+dE(u),
\]

the leading term is

\[
\int_a^b\int_a^b f(z)\overline{f(y)}\,dz\,dy
=
\left|\int_a^b f(y)\,dy\right|^2.
\]

Together with the backward term, this gives the rank-one Landau operator.

---

# 4. Rank-one law with macroscopic smoothing

After natural normalization, the Gram has the form

\[
\boxed{
G_Z
=
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z
+
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{PNT}}
+
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{disc}}
+
\mathcal E_{\Gamma}
+
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{cut}}
}
\]

on the full canonical packet space.

Here:

\[
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{PNT}}
\]

comes from

\[
\sum_{k\le e^U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}-U;
\]

\[
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{disc}}
\]

comes from replacing

\[
\sum_n n^{-1}F(\log n)
\]

by

\[
\int F(y)\,dy;
\]

\[
\mathcal E_{\Gamma}
\]

comes from gamma/background terms and explicit-formula archimedean contributions;

\[
\mathcal E_{\mathrm{cut}}
\]

comes from smooth window and endpoint cutoffs.

The noninteger-ratio term is no longer a leading error if

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta}.
\]

That is a major simplification.

---

# 5. Size of PNT/discretization errors

Let

\[
E(U)=\sum_{\log k\le U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}-U.
\]

If \(E(U)=O(1)\), then for the forward operator

\[
\int f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}\,dE(u),
\]

integration by parts gives an error controlled by the log-frequency derivative of \(f\):

\[
\ll
\|f\|_2\|f'\|_2
+
\text{endpoint terms}.
\]

If \(f\) varies on scale \(Q\), then

\[
\|f'\|_2\ll Q^{-1}\|f\|_2,
\]

and the error is \(O(Q^{-1})\|f\|_2^2\), safely polynomially small.

If \(f\) is only smooth on scale \(\log Q\), then

\[
\|f'\|_2\ll (\log Q)^{-1}\|f\|_2,
\]

which is still acceptable for a \(Q^{-A}\)-type theorem after adjusting exponents.

The key requirement is:

\[
\boxed{
\|f_{\can}'\|_2\le Q^{-\epsilon}\|f_{\can}\|_2
\quad\text{or at least}\quad
\le (\log Q)^{-C}\|f_{\can}\|_2.
}
\]

Sharp cell cutoffs violate this. Smooth partitions are necessary.

---

# 6. Implication for refined cells

Dimension-sufficient refined cells had width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q.
\]

A smooth partition at that scale gives derivative cost

\[
\Delta^{-1}\asymp \frac1{\log Q}.
\]

That is acceptable.

But arbitrary cell amplitudes are not acceptable. If one lets the vector \(X\) vary arbitrarily from cell to cell, its log-frequency profile can oscillate at scale \(\log Q\), still perhaps acceptable, but if arbitrary signs are allowed inside cells, the PNT error can be large.

Thus the aggregate theorem should be formulated for:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual smooth canonical envelopes, possibly resolved by smooth cells,}
}
\]

not for arbitrary refined-cell coefficient vectors.

This is now a precise regularity condition.

---

# 7. What happens to the scalar null direction?

In the full AFE packet, the leading rank-one term is

\[
\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z.
\]

The zero condition kills the scalar value direction:

\[
G_Z e_Z\approx0.
\]

But the derivative row sees it. Multiplication by log-frequency \(Y\) gives

\[
Ye_Z
\]

whose component orthogonal to \(e_Z\) has norm

\[
\asymp Q \|e_Z\|
\]

before normalization. Therefore the finite canonical derivative row should recover scalar-null energy.

In aggregate form, this means the rank-one null is not fatal. It is exactly what the downstream \(T_{\can}\) is designed to handle.

---

# 8. Routing remains the largest obstacle

The macroscopic zero-Gram argument is cleanest for **all zeros** in the window.

If one restricts to

\[
\mathcal C_{\ord},
\]

the sampling measure is no longer the full zero measure. The explicit formula for all zeros no longer applies directly.

Therefore the branch-complete theorem should use a measure decomposition:

\[
\mu_Z
=
\mu_{\ord}+\mu_{\tr}+\mu_{\sing}
+\mu_{\bad}.
\]

To preserve the rank-one law, one needs either:

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\bad}\text{ has polynomially small canonical energy;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\ord}+\mu_{\tr}+\mu_{\sing}
\text{ captures the full zero-Gram up to polynomial loss.}
}
\]

This is now the main routing theorem.

Ordinary-only AggG1 may fail. Branch-complete AggG1 can survive.

---

# 9. Concrete theorem stack for AggG1

The proof of AggG1 should be divided into four lemmas.

## Lemma 1 — smoothed explicit zero Gram

For \(H\gg T^{c_++\delta}\),

\[
G_Z
=
G_{\diag}+G_{\mathrm{Landau}}+\mathcal E_{\mathrm{small}}
\]

on actual smooth canonical packets.

## Lemma 2 — Landau rank-one law

After natural AFE normalization,

\[
G_{\diag}+G_{\mathrm{Landau}}
=
I-\frac1Qe_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E_{\mathrm{rank}},
\]

with \(\mathcal E_{\mathrm{rank}}\) controlled by PNT and smoothness.

## Lemma 3 — canonical row ellipticity on quotient

\[
T_{\can}
\]

detects the scalar null direction and is elliptic on the quotient:

\[
\|T_{\can}v\|_{G_Z}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|v\|_{G_Z}^2
\]

for the actual canonical packet directions.

## Lemma 4 — branch routing

The ordinary/transition/singular routed measures reproduce the all-zero Gram energy up to polynomial loss.

Then branch-complete AggG1 follows.

---

# 10. Final status of this attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{Noninteger-ratio remainder is controlled if }H\gg T^{c_++\delta}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Rank-one law is plausible for smooth actual canonical packets.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1 should be macroscopic and branch-complete.}
}
\]

The refined missing inputs are now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{1. Smoothed zero-Gram explicit formula on actual canonical packets;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{2. PNT/discretization error controlled by smooth log-frequency Sobolev norms;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{3. Canonical quotient ellipticity of }T_{\can};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{4. Branch-routing preservation of the all-zero Gram energy.}
}
\]

This is the first version of AggG1 that looks like a real proof route rather than a named arithmetic assumption.
