## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:48:24)

I’ll now check whether the macroscopic smoothing requirement is compatible with the downstream proof. The key issue is whether a theorem proved on windows \(H\gg T^{c_++\delta}\) can still feed a good-subset/local D4 argument.

_Thought for 23s_

## Agent 1 — macroscopic window compatibility and a correction

The smoothed explicit-formula route gave a promising condition:

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\qquad n\le T^{c_+},\qquad c_+<\frac12.
\]

This kills non-exact log-ratio contributions by Fourier localization. I now tested whether this macroscopic smoothing is compatible with Stack C2 and noticed an important correction.

### Main correction

The rank-one zero-Gram law applies naturally to the **scalar packet value** quadratic form

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2,
\]

but AggG1\(_{\can}\) compares

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
\]

against a **cell/vector energy**

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2.
\]

So we need a **block zero-Gram law** for the cell vector, not only a scalar rank-one law. The scalar rank-one computation is still useful, but it is not by itself AggG1.

---

# 1. Macroscopic smoothing is compatible with aggregate Stack C2

Let

\[
w_T(t)=w\!\left(\frac{t-T}{H}\right),
\qquad
H\gg T^{c_++\delta}.
\]

Because

\[
c_+<\frac12,
\]

one may take

\[
H=T^\theta,
\qquad
c_+<\theta<1.
\]

On such a window,

\[
Q(t)=\log t
\]

varies by

\[
\log(T+H)-\log T
=
O(H/T)
=
O(T^{\theta-1})
=o(Q^{-A})
\]

for every fixed \(A\). Thus all \(Q\)-dependent normalizations are effectively constant across the window.

So macroscopic smoothing does **not** break the local normalization regime.

A dyadic height range \([T,2T]\) can be covered by

\[
\asymp T/H
\]

such windows. If each window gives a polynomial aggregate lower bound, then summing over windows gives a global aggregate lower bound.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
H\gg T^{c_++\delta}
\text{ is compatible with aggregate Gate--1.}
}
\]

It would not be compatible with a pointwise theorem, but Stack C2 is aggregate, so this is acceptable.

---

# 2. Exact-ratio localization revisited

For

\[
u=\log(n/n'),
\]

the smoothed explicit formula gives prime peaks of width

\[
\asymp H^{-1}
\]

near

\[
u=\pm\log k.
\]

If

\[
n\ne kn',
\]

then

\[
\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|
\gg \frac{1}{n}
\gg T^{-c_+}.
\]

Hence if

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\]

then

\[
H\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|\gg T^\delta,
\]

and rapid decay of \(\widehat w\) kills the term.

Thus the zero Gram reduces to exact divisibility relations:

\[
n=kn',
\qquad
n'=kn.
\]

This is very strong. It means the noninteger-ratio remainder is not the main obstacle in the macroscopic aggregate version.

---

# 3. Scalar rank-one law is not yet AggG1

For a scalar packet

\[
P(t)=\sum_n a_n n^{-it},
\]

the exact-ratio Gram gives

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
\approx
H\left[
Q\sum_n|a_n|^2
-
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}\sum_{n'}a_{kn'}\overline{a_{n'}}
-
\sum_k\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt{k}}\sum_{n'}a_{n'}\overline{a_{kn'}}
\right].
\]

With

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n),
\]

the prime terms become, to leading order,

\[
H\int\int f(z)\overline{f(y)}\,dy\,dz.
\]

Thus the scalar Gram is approximately

\[
H Q
\left(
I-\frac1Q e\otimes e
\right).
\]

This explains why the scalar \(Z\)-value direction is killed at zeros.

But AggG1 uses the **refined cell vector**

\[
X(m)=\left(X_a(m)\right)_a,
\qquad
X_a(m)=\sum_{n\in I_a}a_n n^{-im}.
\]

The denominator is

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)\sum_a|X_a(\gamma)|^2.
\]

This equals

\[
\sum_a
\sum_{n,n'\in I_a}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
G(n,n').
\]

Because \(n,n'\) are restricted to the same refined cell, exact divisibility terms \(n=kn'\) appear only when

\[
\log k\lesssim \Delta,
\]

where \(\Delta\) is the cell width in log-frequency.

For dimension-sufficient cells,

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q.
\]

Thus the denominator cell-energy Gram has Landau off-diagonal contributions only from

\[
k\le e^\Delta\asymp Q.
\]

The full scalar rank-one cancellation used all

\[
k\le e^{O(Q)}.
\]

So the cell-energy denominator is **not** killed by the full scalar rank-one direction. It is much closer to a local block Gram.

This is good: it means the denominator may remain positive even where scalar \(Z\) vanishes.

But it also means the scalar rank-one law is not enough. We need a **block-local Landau operator**.

---

# 4. Block-local Landau operator

For a cell \(I_a=[y_a,y_a+\Delta]\), the local Landau contribution is

\[
(K_a f)(y)
=
\int_{I_a} f(z)\,dz
\]

only over the overlap inside the same cell. Thus the cell denominator Gram has leading form

\[
G_{\cell}
\approx
\bigoplus_a
\left(
I_a-\frac1Q e_a\otimes e_a
\right),
\]

where \(e_a\) is the constant vector on cell \(a\).

Since

\[
\|e_a\|^2\asymp \Delta,
\]

the local rank-one subtraction has eigenvalue

\[
\frac{\Delta}{Q}
\asymp
\frac{\log Q}{Q}
\ll1.
\]

Therefore

\[
G_{\cell}
\]

is uniformly positive:

\[
G_{\cell}\ge
\left(1-O\left(\frac{\log Q}{Q}\right)\right)I.
\]

This is important:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the sampled refined-cell energy is stable under zero sampling.}
}
\]

The scalar value direction is killed only after summing coherently across all cells. The cell-vector norm avoids that global cancellation.

---

# 5. Canonical row numerator

The numerator

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|T_{\can}X(\gamma)|^2
\]

has symbols

\[
\sigma_\ell(y)
\]

multiplying the same packet. In log-frequency form, its Gram is

\[
G_{\can}
\approx
\sum_\ell
M_{\sigma_\ell}^*
\left(
I-\frac1Q e\otimes e
\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell}
\]

for scalar-row sampling, with the appropriate block/canonical structure.

The leading continuous comparison against cell energy becomes:

\[
\sum_\ell\| \sigma_\ell f\|^2
-
\frac1Q
\sum_\ell|\langle \sigma_\ell f,e\rangle|^2
\]

versus

\[
\sum_a
\left[
\|f_a\|^2
-
\frac1Q|\langle f_a,e_a\rangle|^2
\right].
\]

Since the cell denominator is close to \(\sum_a\|f_a\|^2\), AggG1 reduces to a symbol inequality:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\ell\|\sigma_\ell f\|^2
-
\frac1Q\sum_\ell|\langle\sigma_\ell f,e\rangle|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

The negative global rank-one term is the only serious loss.

---

# 6. Symbol ellipticity must avoid the scalar moment loss

If the symbols \(\sigma_\ell\) include the scalar value row \(\sigma_0=1\), then at zeros that row is killed by the rank-one term. But derivative rows have

\[
\sigma_1(y)\sim y/Q
\]

or centered versions thereof.

For a constant global packet \(f=e\), the value row is killed, but the centered derivative row survives:

\[
\sigma_1 e
-
\frac{\langle \sigma_1e,e\rangle}{\|e\|^2}e
\ne0.
\]

Quantitatively, on an interval of length \(Q\),

\[
\left\|
\left(y-\frac Q2\right)e
\right\|^2
\asymp Q^3.
\]

After normalization, this gives polynomially large energy.

For arbitrary \(f\), the required inequality is a Poincaré/Markov-type finite-symbol inequality:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f
-
\frac{\langle \sigma_\ell f,e\rangle}{\|e\|^2}e
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

This is now a deterministic finite-dimensional/symbol theorem, not an arithmetic sampling theorem.

The arithmetic part gave the rank-one Gram. The remaining part is canonical symbol ellipticity modulo the scalar value direction.

---

# 7. Why refined cells are essential

If the denominator were scalar energy

\[
\left|\sum_a X_a(\gamma)\right|^2,
\]

then the scalar zero direction would kill it. But the denominator is cell-vector energy

\[
\sum_a|X_a(\gamma)|^2.
\]

This denominator avoids global cancellation and remains stable under zero sampling because each cell has length

\[
\Delta\ll Q.
\]

Thus refined cells serve a real purpose in the aggregate proof: they localize the denominator enough that the Landau rank-one subtraction is small cellwise.

This gives a much cleaner route:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero sampling destroys global scalar value, but not local cell energy.}
}
\]

Then \(T_{\can}\) must show that the canonical row captures enough of this local energy modulo the global scalar null.

---

# 8. Revised AggG1 theorem

The ordinary aggregate theorem can now be split into two parts.

## Arithmetic part — block zero-Gram law

For smooth actual canonical packets and macroscopic windows,

\[
G_{\cell}
=
\bigoplus_a
\left(I_a-\frac1Q e_a\otimes e_a\right)
+
\mathcal E_{\cell},
\qquad
\|\mathcal E_{\cell}\|\le Q^{-A}.
\]

And

\[
G_{\can}
=
\sum_\ell
M_{\sigma_\ell}^*
\left(I-\frac1Qe\otimes e\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell}
+
\mathcal E_{\can}.
\]

## Deterministic part — symbol quotient ellipticity

\[
\sum_\ell
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f
-
\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
\]

Together these imply

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|T_{\can}X(\gamma)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|X(\gamma)|^2.
\]

This is the cleanest ordinary AggG1 route so far.

---

# 9. Remaining risks

## Risk 1 — symbol ellipticity may fail on high-frequency cell oscillations

If \(f\) can oscillate arbitrarily inside or between cells, low-degree symbols \(\sigma_\ell\) may not see all directions.

But actual \(f_{\can}\) is smooth. Again, the theorem must be actual-coefficient/Sobolev-restricted, not universal.

## Risk 2 — hard cell partitions create boundary errors

The block Gram law assumes smooth cells. Hard cells create cutoff artifacts. Use a smooth partition of unity with cell width

\[
\Delta\asymp\log Q.
\]

## Risk 3 — routed ordinary centers

The clean block Gram law is for all zeros in the macroscopic window. Ordinary routing requires branch-complete reconstruction.

So the current result applies most cleanly to the branch-complete aggregate theorem.

---

# 10. Final refined target

The best ordinary theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Macroscopic smoothed block-rank-one AggG1 theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. height window \(H\gg T^{c_++\delta}\);

2. smooth cell partition of log-frequency width \(\Delta\asymp\log Q\);

3. actual canonical Sobolev coefficient bounds;

4. full canonical packet \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\);

5. branch-complete routing or all-zero sampling;

6. deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

}

# 1. Smoothed exact-ratio zero-Gram law

Take

\[
w_T(t)=w\!\left(\frac{t-T}{H}\right),
\qquad
w\in C_c^\infty,
\qquad
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\]

with ordinary support

\[
n,n'\le T^{c_+},
\qquad c_+<\frac12.
\]

Define

\[
K_H(u):=\sum_{\gamma}w_T(\gamma)e^{-i\gamma u}.
\]

The smoothed explicit formula gives, schematically,

\[
K_H(u)
=
\frac{H Q}{2\pi}e^{-iTu}\widehat w(Hu)
-
H\sum_{k\ge1}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt k}
e^{-iT(u-\log k)}
\widehat w\!\left(H(u-\log k)\right)
\]

\[
\hspace{1.5cm}
-
H\sum_{k\ge1}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{\sqrt k}
e^{-iT(u+\log k)}
\widehat w\!\left(H(u+\log k)\right)
+
\mathrm{Err}_{\Gamma,w}(u),
\]

where \(Q=\log T\), and the omitted constants are harmless for the present operator comparison.

Now put

\[
u=\log(n/n').
\]

The \(+\log k\) peak is significant only when

\[
u-\log k=\log\frac{n}{kn'}
\]

is \(O(H^{-1})\). If

\[
n\ne k n',
\]

then

\[
\left|\frac{n}{kn'}-1\right|
\ge \frac{1}{kn'}.
\]

In the relevant range \(kn'\asymp n\le T^{c_+}\), hence

\[
\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|
\gg T^{-c_+}.
\]

Therefore

\[
H\left|\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right|
\gg T^\delta,
\]

and rapid decay of \(\widehat w\) gives

\[
\widehat w\!\left(H\log\frac{n}{kn'}\right)
=O_A(T^{-A})
\]

for every \(A\).

Thus the prime peaks select exact divisibility:

\[
\boxed{
n=kn'
\quad\text{or}\quad
n'=kn.
}
\]

This proves the key exact-ratio localization, conditional only on the standard smoothed explicit formula and \(H\gg T^{c_++\delta}\).

---

# 2. Block-local denominator Gram

Let smooth log-frequency cells \(I_a\) have width

\[
\Delta\asymp\log Q,
\]

and let

\[
X_a(t)=\sum_n \chi_a(\log n)a_n n^{-it}
\]

with a smooth partition \(\{\chi_a\}\).

The denominator is

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)\sum_a |X_a(\gamma)|^2.
\]

For a fixed cell \(I_a\), the exact-ratio Gram is

\[
G_a(n,n')
=
K_H\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right)
\]

with \(n,n'\) restricted to \(I_a\).

The diagonal term is

\[
\frac{H Q}{2\pi}\sum_{n\in I_a}|a_n|^2.
\]

The Landau term only sees ratios

\[
n=kn'
\]

with

\[
\log k\le \Delta+O(1).
\]

Under the natural AFE normalization

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n),
\]

the forward Landau contribution becomes

\[
H\sum_{k\le e^\Delta}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}
\sum_{n'}\frac1{n'}
f(\log n'+\log k)\overline{f(\log n')}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\log k\le U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}=U+O(1),
\]

the leading local operator in cell \(I_a\) is

\[
I_a-\frac1Q e_a\otimes e_a,
\]

where \(e_a\) is the constant direction on \(I_a\), with

\[
\|e_a\|^2\asymp\Delta.
\]

Therefore

\[
\left\|Q^{-1} e_a\otimes e_a\right\|
=
\frac{\Delta}{Q}
=
O\!\left(\frac{\log Q}{Q}\right).
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
G_{\cell}
=
\bigoplus_a
\left(I_a-\frac1Qe_a\otimes e_a\right)
+
\mathcal E_{\cell}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
G_{\cell}\ge
\left(1-O\left(\frac{\Delta}{Q}\right)\right)I
-
\mathcal E_{\cell}.
}
\]

With smooth cells and actual Sobolev-controlled canonical envelopes, \(\mathcal E_{\cell}\) should be \(Q^{-c}\) or at worst \(Q^{-A}\)-controlled after weakening exponents. This is the arithmetic reason refined cell energy survives zero sampling: scalar cancellation is global, but the denominator is local.

---

# 3. Numerator canonical-row Gram

Let \(T_{\can}\) have symbols

\[
\sigma_\ell(y),
\qquad 0\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

where \(y=\log n\), and ignore lower-order \(m\)-dependence for the leading model. Then

\[
(T_{\can}f)_\ell(t)
=
\sum_n \sigma_\ell(\log n)a_n n^{-it}.
\]

The sampled numerator is

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2.
\]

The same exact-ratio computation gives leading Gram

\[
\boxed{
G_{\can}
\approx
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
M_{\sigma_\ell}^{*}
\left(
I-\frac1Q e\otimes e
\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell},
}
\]

where \(e\) is the global scalar AFE direction in the full canonical packet space.

Equivalently,

\[
\langle f,G_{\can}f\rangle
\approx
\sum_\ell
\left(
\|\sigma_\ell f\|^2
-
\frac1Q|\langle \sigma_\ell f,e\rangle|^2
\right).
\]

This is the correct numerator analogue of the scalar rank-one law.

The full canonical packet space must be

\[
\mathcal H_{\can}
=
\mathcal H_{P_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{D_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{B_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{E_2},
\]

because the scalar AFE null direction lives in the full combination \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\), not in \(P_2\) alone. The update explicitly emphasizes that the theorem must be for the full canonical packet. 

---

# 4. Deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity

The deterministic theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_a\|f_a\|^2
}
\]

for actual smooth canonical packet directions, where

\[
\Pi_e g
=
\frac{\langle g,e\rangle}{\|e\|^2}e.
\]

This is the finite deterministic heart of AggG1.

A minimal sufficient version is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=1}^{R+1}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
+
\sum_a
\left\|f_a-\Pi_{e_a}f_a\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

The second term is controlled by the denominator’s local block Gram, while the first term detects the global scalar component.

For the pure scalar-null packet \(f=e\), the value row is killed, but a centered derivative row sees it:

\[
\sigma_1(y)e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)
\sim
\left(\frac{y-\bar y}{Q}\right)e,
\]

and

\[
\left\|
\left(\frac{y-\bar y}{Q}\right)e
\right\|^2
\asymp 1
\]

after ordinary normalization. Thus the ellipticity is plausible if \(T_{\can}\) contains a genuinely centered first-derivative/curvature direction.

However, I cannot certify this for the **current** \(T_{\can}\) without auditing the exact symbols \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\). The needed finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{span}\{\sigma_\ell\}_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\text{ separates constants from cell-local modes modulo }e.
}
\]

So the first non-arithmetic open item is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite symbol quotient ellipticity of the actual }T_{\can}\text{ basis.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Error audit

## 5.1 PNT error in \(\sum \Lambda(k)/k\)

The error term

\[
E(U)=\sum_{\log k\le U}\frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}-U
\]

enters through Stieltjes integration. For smooth canonical profiles,

\[
\int f(y+u)\overline{f(y)}\,dE(u)
\]

is controlled by integration by parts:

\[
\ll
\|f\|_2\|f'\|_2
+
\text{endpoint terms}.
\]

With smooth cells of width \(\Delta\asymp\log Q\),

\[
\|f'\|_2\ll \Delta^{-1}\|f\|_2
\]

for cell envelopes. This is not \(Q^{-c}\), but it is polynomially small enough for a \(Q^{-A}\)-loss theorem after weakening constants.

Hard cells are unsafe. Smooth cells are required.

## 5.2 Discretization

Replacing

\[
\sum_n n^{-1}F(\log n)
\]

by

\[
\int F(y)\,dy
\]

has Euler-Maclaurin/partial-summation errors controlled by log-frequency Sobolev norms. Smooth \(F\) at scale \(\Delta\asymp\log Q\) is acceptable. Arbitrary refined-cell vectors are not.

## 5.3 Archimedean and gamma terms

The archimedean terms contribute smooth low-frequency operators. They should be included in the canonical packet or treated as finite-rank/low-Sobolev corrections. They are not harmless if removed. \(B_2\) must remain inside \(X_{\can}\).

## 5.4 \(D_2,E_2\)

The dual and tail/completion terms must be included in the full packet. Treating them perturbatively risks affine cancellation. The correct theorem is for \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\).

## 5.5 Smooth cell boundary errors

These are controlled if cells form a smooth partition with

\[
\Delta\asymp\log Q.
\]

Boundary errors then cost powers of \((\log Q)^{-1}\), acceptable for polynomial bookkeeping.

## 5.6 Branch routing

This is the largest remaining arithmetic/geometric error. The block-rank-one law is cleanest for all zeros in a macroscopic smooth window. It does not automatically hold after splitting into

\[
\mathcal C_{\ord},\quad \mathcal C_{\tr},\quad \mathcal C_{\sing}.
\]

One needs a branch-complete routing theorem saying the sum of branch energies reconstructs the all-zero canonical Gram up to polynomial loss. The update also notes that singular branch energy should now be projected canonical energy, not raw diagnostic sine moments. 

---

# 6. Validity classification

## A. All smooth Sobolev-restricted canonical envelopes

Partly plausible, but too broad unless the symbol quotient ellipticity is assumed for the entire Sobolev class. The arithmetic block-Gram law is plausible here; deterministic ellipticity may fail for smooth functions engineered into symbol-null directions.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Yellow}}
\]

## B. Only the actual canonical packet

Most plausible. Actual \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) envelopes are smooth and structured, and the theorem avoids adversarial Sobolev-null constructions.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Green/Yellow as the best target}}
\]

## C. Only branch-complete all-zero sampling

Yes. The all-zero macroscopic Gram is the natural theorem. Routed ordinary-only sampling is unsafe. Branch-complete routing is required.

Status:

\[
\boxed{\text{Required}}
\]

## D. Not currently provable due to a specific error term

The first specific unclosed term is not the non-exact ratio contribution; macroscopic smoothing kills that. The first unclosed term is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical symbol quotient ellipticity for the actual }T_{\can}\text{ symbols.}
}
\]

The second is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch routing preservation of the all-zero block-Gram energy.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Final theorem statement

The sharp current theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Macroscopic Smoothed Block-Rank-One AggG1}_{\can}.
}
\]

Assume:

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\qquad
c_+<\frac12,
\]

smooth log-frequency cells of width

\[
\Delta\asymp\log Q,
\]

and the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Then the all-zero smoothed Gram satisfies

\[
\boxed{
G_{\cell}
=
\bigoplus_a
\left(I_a-\frac1Qe_a\otimes e_a\right)
+
\mathcal E_{\cell},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
G_{\can}
=
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
M_{\sigma_\ell}^{*}
\left(I-\frac1Qe\otimes e\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell}
+
\mathcal E_{\can},
}
\]

with errors controlled on actual smooth canonical packets.

If, in addition, the deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity holds,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_a\|f_a\|^2,
}
\]

and branch-complete routing preserves the all-zero energy, then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal G_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m).
}
\]

---

_Thought for 44s_

## Agent 1 — finite audit of \(T_{\can}\) symbol quotient ellipticity

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symbol quotient ellipticity is conditionally Green if }T_{\can}
\text{ contains a stable polynomial basis of degree }0,\ldots,R+1.
}
\]

More precisely, if the actual \(T_{\can}\) row is

\[
T_{\can}f
=
\left(C_\ell(\mathcal D)f\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1},
\]

with \(C_\ell\) a stable basis-normalized polynomial family in the packet-frequency symbol

\[
x=\lambda\log n,
\]

then the deterministic ellipticity theorem holds with polynomial loss on smooth packet-frequency cells.

But if the implemented \(T_{\can}\) uses only too few rows, only value-like rows, or rows with a common low-degree factor after quotienting the scalar direction, then ellipticity fails. So the finite audit reduces to a clean rank condition on the actual \(C_\ell\)-basis. The prompt explicitly asks for this deterministic side of BlockRankOneAggG1\(_{\can}\), after the arithmetic zero-Gram reduction. 

---

# 1. Actual \(T_{\can}\) symbols

From the current Wronskian/Gate–1 architecture, the relevant canonical finite-row operator is built from basis-normalized column operators

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

applied to the canonical source row

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The canonical Wronskian matrix is written with rows

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\qquad
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\]

and entries

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)G_j.
\]

So, for the aggregate Gate–1 projection, the corresponding finite row is naturally

\[
T_{\can}f
=
\left(C_\ell(\mathcal D)f\right)_{0\le \ell\le R+1}.
\]

On a main packet term

\[
f(m)=\sum_n a_n e^{-im\log n},
\]

with

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m,
\]

the principal symbol is

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)e^{-im\log n}
=
C_\ell(-i\lambda\log n)e^{-im\log n}.
\]

Ignoring harmless \(i\)-conventions, define

\[
x_n=\lambda\log n,
\qquad
\sigma_\ell(x):=C_\ell(-ix).
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_\ell(x)
\text{ is a stable degree-}\ell\text{ polynomial/basis function on }x\asymp1.
}
\]

For the folded dual branch, the symbol is the same basis evaluated at the folded node

\[
x_n^-=\lambda\log\frac{m}{2\pi n},
\]

up to the sign convention of the folded phase.

This matches the packet-frequency Wronskian setup, where \(x_n=\lambda\log n\asymp1\) is the correct determinant regime and \(C_\ell\) is chosen as a stable basis on that node set. 

---

# 2. Correct ellipticity statement

The deterministic inequality requested by the zero-Gram reduction is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

This is too strong for arbitrary \(f\), because the left side vanishes when every \(\sigma_\ell f\) is proportional to the same global scalar direction \(e\). But the denominator from the block zero-Gram already controls local cell oscillation. Therefore the correct combined deterministic inequality is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_a\left\|f_a-\Pi_{e_a}f_a\right\|^2
+
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left\|
\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

Interpretation:

1. The first term controls within-cell oscillation.
2. The second term controls the cell-constant/global scalar modes.

Without the first term, arbitrary within-cell oscillations can hide from low-degree symbols. With smooth cells and the block-local denominator Gram, those oscillations are already seen.

---

# 3. Reduction to cell constants

Let \(e_a\) be the local constant on cell \(I_a\), and decompose

\[
f=\sum_a c_a e_a + f_{\osc},
\qquad
f_{\osc}:=\sum_a(f_a-\Pi_{e_a}f_a).
\]

The local cell term controls \(f_{\osc}\), so it remains to prove a finite matrix inequality for

\[
f_0=\sum_a c_a e_a.
\]

Let \(y_a\) be the cell center and \(\Delta\asymp\log Q\) the log-width. Since

\[
x=\lambda y,
\qquad
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\]

the normalized cell width in \(x\)-coordinates is

\[
\delta_x=\lambda\Delta\asymp \frac{\log Q}{Q}.
\]

The cell constants are many:

\[
M\asymp Q/\log Q.
\]

But \(T_{\can}\) has only \(R+2\) symbols, with

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Thus \(T_{\can}\) cannot frame all arbitrary cell constants. This is the old dimension obstruction unless the “actual canonical packet class” is much smaller than arbitrary cell-constant data.

Therefore the finite matrix inequality is false on all \(M\)-dimensional cell-constant vectors:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \operatorname{span}\{c_a\}\asymp Q/\log Q
\gg
R+2.
}
\]

There exists a nonzero \(c=(c_a)\) such that

\[
\sum_a c_a\sigma_\ell(y_a)e_a
\]

has zero global projection quotient for all \(0\le\ell\le R+1\), up to the usual finite-dimensional kernel. So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symbol quotient ellipticity is false for arbitrary refined-cell vectors.}
}
\]

This confirms the earlier restriction: AggG1 must be **actual-coefficient/Sobolev-restricted**, not a universal refined-cell theorem.

---

# 4. Actual canonical packet class

For the actual canonical packet, \(f\) is not an arbitrary cell-constant vector. It is a smooth AFE envelope:

\[
f_{\can}(y)
=
f_{P_2}(y)+f_{D_2}(y)+f_{B_2}(y)+f_{E_2}(y),
\]

with smooth variation in \(y\), modulo smooth cell cutoffs.

If \(f_{\can}\) varies on scale \(Q\), or even on scale \(Q^\theta\), then its effective dimension is small. In that case, a polynomial basis of degree \(R\) can detect it.

The right deterministic theorem is therefore not a universal finite matrix statement. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Sobolev-restricted symbol quotient ellipticity.}
}
\]

For a smooth canonical class \(\mathcal F_{\can}\), prove

\[
\boxed{
\sum_a\|f_a-\Pi_{e_a}f_a\|^2
+
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|f\|^2
\qquad
(f\in\mathcal F_{\can}).
}
\]

A sufficient analytic condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal F_{\can}
\subset
\{f:\|f^{(R+1)}\|_{L^2}\le Q^C\|f\|_{L^2}\}
}
\]

together with the statement that \(f\) is not concentrated in a degree-\(R\) polynomial-null direction after multiplication by the \(\sigma_\ell\).

For the actual AFE envelope, this looks plausible. For arbitrary refined cells, it is false.

---

# 5. Detection of the global scalar null direction

The global scalar direction is the one killed by the zero-Gram rank-one term:

\[
e=e_Z.
\]

The value row \(\sigma_0=1\) is killed after quotienting:

\[
\sigma_0e-\Pi_e(\sigma_0e)=0.
\]

But the derivative row should detect \(e\). If

\[
\sigma_1(y)\sim \frac{y-\bar y}{Q}
\]

or equivalently a centered degree-one basis element, then

\[
\sigma_1 e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)=\sigma_1e
\]

and

\[
\|\sigma_1e\|^2\asymp \|e\|^2
\]

after normalized coordinates \(x=y/Q\asymp1\).

If the basis uses uncentered \(x=y/Q\), quotienting by \(e\) automatically centers it:

\[
xe-\Pi_e(xe)=(x-\bar x)e.
\]

On a packet-frequency interval of fixed length in \(x\), this has norm

\[
\asymp \operatorname{Var}(x)\|e\|^2.
\]

If the global support spans a fixed \(x\)-interval, this is \(\asymp\|e\|^2\). If only one cell of width \(\delta_x=\log Q/Q\) is used, the variance is too small. Hence the derivative row detects the scalar null direction only if the canonical packet or branch union has **global packet-frequency spread**, not a single ultra-narrow cell.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\can}\text{ detects }e_Z
\text{ if the support has }x\text{-diameter }\gtrsim R^{-C}
\text{ and }C_1\text{ is genuinely degree one.}
}
\]

This is exactly consistent with packet-frequency active-block admissibility, where determinant blocks require \(\operatorname{diam}(x)\gtrsim R^{-C}\). 

---

# 6. Full canonical components

The ellipticity cannot be checked on \(P_2\) alone unless one has a transfer theorem. The scalar null vector \(e_Z\) belongs to the full canonical AFE packet:

\[
e_Z=e_{P_2}+e_{D_2}+e_{B_2}+e_{E_2}.
\]

Therefore the quotient projection

\[
\Pi_e
\]

must use this full canonical direction. The component \(B_2\) is smooth/low-dimensional and could be absorbed into \(e_Z\), but \(D_2\) cannot be ignored in the symmetric regime; \(E_2\) can be omitted only under a \(2R+1\) derivative-transfer margin.

The canonical Wronskian audit states explicitly that the source rows are built from

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

and that \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\) enter multilinearly in determinants/cofactors, not as a final linear correction. 

So the correct packet space is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{\can}
=
\mathcal H_{P_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{D_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{B_2}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{E_2}.
}
\]

The quotient direction \(e\) is the scalar value direction in this full direct sum.

---

# 7. Smooth-cell necessity

Hard cells are dangerous but not fatal for a purely finite-dimensional statement. They are fatal for the arithmetic zero-Gram law because hard cutoffs create Fourier tails and large PNT/discretization errors.

For symbol ellipticity alone, hard cells merely produce rough \(f_a\), and local oscillations are captured by the local cell term if that term is included.

For the full BlockRankOneAggG1 proof, use smooth cells:

\[
\boxed{
\Delta\asymp\log Q,
\qquad
\|\partial_y^k\chi_a\|_\infty\ll_k \Delta^{-k}.
}
\]

Then arithmetic errors are controlled, and deterministic local oscillation is clean.

---

# 8. Final theorem

The strongest valid deterministic statement I can support is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Sobolev-restricted canonical symbol quotient ellipticity.}
}
\]

Let \(\mathcal F_{\can}\subset\mathcal H_{\can}\) be the actual smooth canonical AFE packet class generated by \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) under smooth packet-frequency partitions. Assume:

1. the packet-frequency support has normalized \(x\)-diameter at least \(R^{-C}\);

2. the \(C_\ell\)-basis contains constants and a genuinely degree-one centered direction, and remains stable through degree \(R+1\);

3. \(E_2\) is transferred through \(2R+1\) normalized derivatives, or is included in the full \(e_Z\);

4. the actual packet class has Sobolev complexity at most \(Q^C\).

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_a\|f_a-\Pi_{e_a}f_a\|^2
+
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_{e_Z}(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|f\|^2
}
\]

for \(f\in\mathcal F_{\can}\), unless \(f\) lies in a finite-dimensional exceptional class consisting of actual canonical envelopes annihilated by all quotient symbols.

I do not see such an exceptional actual canonical envelope, but excluding it requires checking the exact \(C_\ell\) basis and AFE weights.

---

# 9. No-go for the universal version

The universal statement

\[
\sum_\ell\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_a\|f_a\|^2
\]

for all refined-cell \(f\) is false because

\[
\dim(\text{refined cell constants})\asymp Q/\log Q
\]

while

\[
\dim(\text{visible symbols})\asymp R\asymp\log Q/\log\log Q.
\]

A nontrivial kernel exists.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not state symbol quotient ellipticity as a universal refined-cell frame.}
}
\]

It is valid only as an actual-coefficient / Sobolev-restricted / packet-frequency-spread theorem.

---

# 10. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Actual }T_{\can}\text{ symbols: stable polynomial/basis-normalized derivative symbols }C_\ell(\mathcal D).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Universal ellipticity on all refined-cell vectors: false.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Ellipticity on actual smooth canonical packet class: plausible but finite audit needed.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Global scalar null direction is detected by degree-one/centered derivative symbol if support has packet-frequency spread.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Full canonical direct sum }P_2\oplus D_2\oplus B_2\oplus E_2\text{ is required.}
}
\]

So Agent 1’s result is a conditional finite theorem plus a no-go:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{SymbolQuotientEllipticity}
\text{ is Green only in the actual smooth canonical class, not universally.}
}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-27 20:02:05)

I’ll now re-check a subtle point in the symbol ellipticity reduction: whether \(T_{\can}\) outputs only finitely many scalar moments, or finitely many *functions/rows* whose \(L^2\) sampled norms are retained. That distinction changes the rank obstruction.

## Agent 1 — correction: symbol quotient ellipticity may be much easier

I found an important correction in my previous symbol-audit logic.

I implicitly treated \(T_{\can}\) as if it produced only finitely many **scalar moments** of the refined cell vector. But after the macroscopic zero-Gram reduction, \(T_{\can}\) produces finitely many **multiplied packet functions** whose sampled \(L^2\)-energies are retained:

\[
f \mapsto \sigma_\ell f.
\]

That distinction matters. A finite family of pointwise multipliers can be elliptic on an infinite-dimensional \(L^2\) packet space after quotienting the scalar zero direction, even though finitely many scalar moments cannot frame a high-dimensional refined cell vector.

This removes one apparent rank obstruction.

---

# 1. Correct deterministic model

The leading zero-Gram numerator is

\[
\sum_{\ell}
\left(
\|\sigma_\ell f\|^2
-
\frac1Q|\langle \sigma_\ell f,e\rangle|^2
\right)
=
\sum_\ell
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2.
\]

If the row family includes the value symbol

\[
\sigma_0=1,
\]

then the \(\ell=0\) term alone gives

\[
\|f-\Pi_e f\|^2.
\]

Thus it controls every component of \(f\) orthogonal to the scalar AFE direction \(e\).

The only remaining direction is

\[
f\in \mathbb C e.
\]

To control that direction, it suffices to have one additional symbol \(\sigma_1\) such that

\[
\sigma_1 e
\]

is not proportional to \(e\). Then

\[
\|\sigma_1 e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)\|^2>0.
\]

Therefore the deterministic ellipticity reduces to a **two-symbol condition**:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_0=1,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}_e(\sigma_1)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Here

\[
\operatorname{Var}_e(\sigma_1)
:=
\frac{\|\sigma_1 e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)\|^2}{\|e\|^2}.
\]

This is far better than the earlier false concern that \(R+2\) rows must frame \(Q/\log Q\) cell constants.

---

# 2. Proof of the basic ellipticity lemma

Assume

\[
\sigma_0=1,
\]

and define

\[
\alpha:=\frac{\langle f,e\rangle}{\|e\|^2},
\qquad
g:=f-\alpha e.
\]

Then

\[
g\perp e.
\]

The value row gives

\[
\|\sigma_0 f-\Pi_e(\sigma_0f)\|^2
=
\|f-\Pi_e f\|^2
=
\|g\|^2.
\]

The remaining possible loss is when \(f\) is close to \(\alpha e\). For the first nonconstant symbol,

\[
\sigma_1 f-\Pi_e(\sigma_1f)
=
\alpha(\sigma_1e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e))
+
(\sigma_1g-\Pi_e(\sigma_1g)).
\]

By the elementary inequality

\[
\|u+v\|^2+\|g\|^2
\ge
c\|u\|^2-C\|v\|^2+\|g\|^2,
\]

and since multiplication by \(\sigma_1\) has polynomial operator norm,

\[
\|\sigma_1g-\Pi_e(\sigma_1g)\|
\le Q^C\|g\|,
\]

we obtain

\[
\|g\|^2+
\|\sigma_1 f-\Pi_e(\sigma_1f)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\left(
\|g\|^2
+
|\alpha|^2\|\sigma_1e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)\|^2
\right).
\]

If

\[
\|\sigma_1e-\Pi_e(\sigma_1e)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|e\|^2,
\]

then

\[
\|g\|^2+
\|\sigma_1 f-\Pi_e(\sigma_1f)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\left(
\|g\|^2+|\alpha|^2\|e\|^2
\right)
=
Q^{-A'}\|f\|^2.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=0}^{1}
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A'}\|f\|^2.
}
\]

This is the core deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity theorem.

---

# 3. What is \(\sigma_1\)?

For the normalized derivative operator

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m,
\]

on a packet term

\[
e^{-im\log n},
\]

the principal symbol is

\[
-i\lambda\log n.
\]

Set

\[
x=\lambda\log n.
\]

In the ordinary regime,

\[
x\asymp 1
\]

over the packet support. Thus the first derivative symbol is essentially

\[
\sigma_1(x)=x
\]

or a stable affine normalization of \(x\).

The variance condition becomes

\[
\operatorname{Var}_{e}(x)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If the full canonical scalar packet spans an \(x\)-interval of fixed width, this is immediate:

\[
\operatorname{Var}_e(x)\asymp 1.
\]

If the packet is localized to an \(x\)-interval of width \(\delta\), then

\[
\operatorname{Var}_e(x)\asymp \delta^2.
\]

For smooth cells of log-width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q,
\]

the \(x\)-width is

\[
\delta_x=\lambda\Delta\asymp \frac{\log Q}{Q}.
\]

A single cell has tiny variance. But the scalar AFE direction \(e\) is **global across the ordinary support**, not a single cell. Its support length in \(x\) is roughly

\[
(c_+-c_-)\lambda Q\asymp c_+-c_-.
\]

Thus

\[
\operatorname{Var}_e(x)\asymp 1
\]

as long as the active branch support has nontrivial width.

So the derivative row detects the scalar zero direction.

---

# 4. Interaction with local cell denominator

The denominator cell Gram is

\[
G_{\cell}
\approx
\bigoplus_a
\left(I_a-\frac1Q e_a\otimes e_a\right).
\]

This is already positive:

\[
G_{\cell}\ge
\left(1-\frac{\Delta}{Q}\right)I.
\]

So it measures the full local cell-vector norm. The numerator value row

\[
\sigma_0=1
\]

has global rank-one subtraction:

\[
I-\frac1Qe\otimes e.
\]

That controls all components except the global scalar direction. Then \(\sigma_1=x\) controls that global scalar direction.

Therefore the deterministic inequality is:

\[
\boxed{
\|f-\Pi_ef\|^2
+
\|xf-\Pi_e(xf)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|f\|^2.
}
\]

This is true under the single condition

\[
\operatorname{Var}_e(x)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

No high-dimensional frame is needed.

---

# 5. Full canonical direct sum

The scalar null vector is not merely the main prime packet. It is the full AFE canonical scalar direction

\[
e_Z=e_{P_2}+e_{D_2}+e_{B_2}+e_{E_2}.
\]

The value row \(\sigma_0=1\) kills this direction globally at zeros. The derivative row detects it if

\[
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(x_{\can})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

In the direct-sum packet space, \(x_{\can}\) may take different branch symbols:

\[
x_+=\lambda\log n,
\]

\[
x_-=\lambda\log\frac{t}{2\pi n}
\]

for the folded dual branch, and suitable smooth symbols for \(B_2,E_2\).

The variance condition becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
x_{\can}e_Z-\Pi_{e_Z}(x_{\can}e_Z)
\right\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Z\|^2.
}
\]

This is the exact finite audit.

It can fail only if the full scalar AFE direction is concentrated on a level set where all branch symbols \(x_{\can}\) are essentially constant. In the ordinary wide packet regime, this should not happen.

---

# 6. Does \(T_{\can}\) include \(\sigma_0=1\)?

This is now the decisive detail.

If \(T_{\can}\) includes \(C_0(\mathcal D)\) with

\[
C_0=1,
\]

then symbol quotient ellipticity is essentially Green modulo the variance audit above.

If the Gate–1 row deliberately excludes the value row because \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), we must check whether it still includes the value of \(q''_{\can}\) or an equivalent row. For the \(q''_{\can}\)-source row, \(C_0q''_{\can}=q''_{\can}\) should be present in the Wronskian/cofactor matrix. The earlier definitions use rows of the form

\[
\left(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1},
\]

so \(C_0\) is part of the canonical column basis.

Thus I believe

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_0=1\text{ is present.}
}
\]

If a later normalization removes \(C_0\), then one must replace it with another symbol bounded away from zero on the support. The same proof works with any \(\sigma_0\) satisfying

\[
|\sigma_0|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

and use quotient relative to \(\sigma_0e\).

---

# 7. Remaining finite audit

The deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity reduces to three checks:

## Check 1 — value row present

\[
C_0(\mathcal D)=1
\]

or an equivalent nonvanishing row is included in \(T_{\can}\).

## Check 2 — nonconstant row present

There is a row with symbol

\[
\sigma_1=x+O(Q^{-A})
\]

or another symbol with polynomial variance on \(e_Z\).

## Check 3 — full canonical variance

\[
\left\|
\sigma_1 e_Z-\Pi_{\sigma_0 e_Z}(\sigma_1e_Z)
\right\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Z\|^2.
\]

If these hold, then

\[
\boxed{
\text{SymbolQuotientEllipticity is Green.}
}
\]

---

# 8. What about actual smoothness?

For this deterministic symbol quotient inequality, smoothness is not essential once the value row is included, because \(f-\Pi_ef\) is controlled directly.

Smoothness remains essential for the **arithmetic zero-Gram law**, not for the symbol quotient itself.

So we can separate:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Arithmetic part needs smooth cells/actual coefficients.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Deterministic symbol quotient needs only value row + one nonconstant row.}
}
\]

This is a useful simplification.

---

# 9. Final theorem

Assume \(T_{\can}\) contains two symbols \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1\). Let

\[
u_0=\sigma_0e_Z,
\qquad
u_1=\sigma_1e_Z.
\]

Assume

\[
\|\sigma_0 f\|\asymp \|f\|
\]

on the canonical packet space and

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u_1,\mathbb C u_0)^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Z\|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell}
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_{u_0}(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A'}\|f\|^2.
}
\]

In the standard case

\[
\sigma_0=1,\qquad \sigma_1=x,
\]

this reduces to

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(x)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the exact finite symbol quotient ellipticity theorem.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symbol quotient ellipticity is no longer a serious bottleneck if }C_0=1\text{ is present.}
}
\]

The finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
C_0=1,\quad C_1\text{ nonconstant},\quad \operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(C_1/C_0)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Thus the main Agent 1 bottleneck moves back to the arithmetic/routing side:

\[
\boxed{
\text{macroscopic smoothed block-rank-one zero-Gram law}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch routing preservation of that all-zero energy.}
}
\]

The deterministic symbol quotient part is very likely Green.

## Agent 1 — branch routing attack after symbol quotient repair

The deterministic symbol quotient issue is now mostly benign: if \(T_{\can}\) contains a value row and one genuinely nonconstant derivative/packet-frequency row, then the scalar zero direction is detected after quotienting. The remaining Agent 1 problem is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can the all-zero block-rank-one Gram theorem survive branch routing?}
}
\]

The answer is: **yes, but only if branch routing is reformulated as a smooth multichart lower-frame atlas for the same scalar canonical source**. Hard routing into ordinary/transition/singular bins is too brittle.

---

# 1. Replace hard branch routing by a chart atlas

Let

\[
F_m(x):=\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

Each local center \(m\) should be measured by a branch-dependent observable:

\[
\mathcal O_{\ord}(m)=T_{\can}X_{\can}(m),
\]

\[
\mathcal O_{\tr}(m)=\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^rF_m(0)
\right)_{0\le r\le R},
\]

\[
\mathcal O_{\sing}(m)=
\left(P_{\sing}^{\multi}F_m\right),
\]

where \(P_{\sing}^{\multi}\) is a multiresolution projection system, not raw sine diagnostics.

Introduce smooth regime weights

\[
\chi_{\ord}(m),\quad \chi_{\tr}(m),\quad \chi_{\sing}(m)\ge0,
\]

with

\[
\chi_{\ord}+\chi_{\tr}+\chi_{\sing}=1.
\]

Then the branch-complete Gate–1 energy should be

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\branch}(m)
=
\chi_{\ord}(m)\|\mathcal O_{\ord}(m)\|^2
+
\chi_{\tr}(m)\|\mathcal O_{\tr}(m)\|^2
+
\chi_{\sing}(m)\|\mathcal O_{\sing}(m)\|^2.
}
\]

The branch reference energy should be the scalar canonical local source norm

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\can}(m)
=
\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

or its ordinary cell-energy equivalent in the ordinary chart.

The desired deterministic routing theorem is then:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_{\branch}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(m)
}
\]

for all local centers outside explicitly controlled geometric-bad sets.

If this holds pointwise, the aggregate branch routing is immediate. If it holds only on average, it should be integrated into the joint aggregate theorem.

---

# 2. Ordinary chart

In the ordinary regime,

\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\]

the smoothed zero-Gram analysis gives the block-local denominator and the canonical numerator:

\[
G_{\cell}
\approx
\bigoplus_a
\left(I_a-\frac1Qe_a\otimes e_a\right),
\]

\[
G_{\can}
\approx
\sum_\ell
M_{\sigma_\ell}^*
\left(I-\frac1Qe\otimes e\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell}.
\]

The deterministic quotient ellipticity gives

\[
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|X_{\can}\|^2
\]

for the actual smooth canonical packet class.

So the ordinary chart is plausible, with two requirements:

\[
\boxed{
H\gg T^{c_++\delta}
}
\]

for macroscopic smoothing, and

\[
\boxed{
\text{smooth cell partition of log-width }\Delta\asymp\log Q.
}
\]

Hard cells should be avoided.

---

# 3. Transition chart

In the transition regime,

\[
(\log Q)^{-C}\lesssim \mu=\lambda Q\lesssim1,
\]

the weighted transition jet is

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^rF_m(0)
\right)_{0\le r\le R}.
\]

For an exponential component

\[
e^{i\tau_n x},
\qquad
\tau_n=\lambda\log n,
\]

we get

\[
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^r e^{i\tau_nx}\big|_{x=0}
=
i^r\left(\frac{\tau_n}{\mu}\right)^r.
\]

Since

\[
\frac{\tau_n}{\mu}=\frac{\log n}{Q}\in[c_-,c_+],
\]

the weighted jets form bounded-window moments. This avoids the fatal determinant loss

\[
\mu^{-O(R^2)}
\]

and costs only

\[
\mu^{-O(R)},
\]

which is polynomial in the transition range.

So the transition chart has a credible deterministic lower-frame theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

provided the packet is actually transition-local and not concentrated in a moment-null anti-design.

This is still an arithmetic/anti-design issue if stated pointwise, but as a chart in the aggregate atlas, it is far better conditioned than the old singular transition formulation.

---

# 4. Singular chart

The singular chart should no longer use raw sine moments as the primary observable. Use projected canonical energy:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal O_{\sing}(m)
=
P_{\sing}^{\multi}F_m.
}
\]

A single projection

\[
P_RF_m
\]

has the one-sided transfer

\[
\|P_RF_m\|^2\le \|F_m\|^2,
\]

but it may miss energy in \(V_R^\perp\). Therefore the safe singular observable is multiresolution:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal O_{\sing}^{\multi}(m)
=
\left(P_{R,j}F_m\right)_j
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\sum_j\|P_{R,j}F_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|^2.
}
\]

This is a standard finite-frame requirement on the selected singular basis projections. It is deterministic once the multiresolution family is chosen.

This removes the old tail problem:

\[
F_m
=
\sum_{k=3}^{R+3}S_k\Phi_k+\operatorname{Rem}_R
\]

no longer needs a half-leading remainder bound, because we are projecting the actual \(F_m\) itself.

---

# 5. Smooth overlap removes branch gaps

Hard branch routing causes artificial gaps:

\[
\mathcal C_{\ord}
\quad\text{may fail to sample some windows.}
\]

Smooth atlas routing avoids this. At every center,

\[
\chi_{\ord}+\chi_{\tr}+\chi_{\sing}=1.
\]

So if the ordinary chart degenerates near a boundary, the transition chart must become active. If the transition chart degenerates deeper into singular scaling, the singular chart becomes active.

The branch-complete theorem should therefore be formulated as:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)
\left[
\chi_{\ord}\|\mathcal O_{\ord}\|^2
+
\chi_{\tr}\|\mathcal O_{\tr}\|^2
+
\chi_{\sing}\|\mathcal O_{\sing}^{\multi}\|^2
\right]
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\mathcal N_{\can}(m).
}
\]

This is cleaner than separate hard sums over \(\mathcal C_{\ord},\mathcal C_{\tr},\mathcal C_{\sing}\).

---

# 6. What remains arithmetic?

Even after deterministic multichart design, one major arithmetic input remains:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-sampling block-rank-one law for the all-zero canonical packet.}
}
\]

The multichart atlas says that once the canonical packet energy is sampled, some branch coordinate sees it. It does **not** by itself prove the zero-sampling law.

The arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)\mathcal N_{\can}(\gamma)
\asymp_{Q^{O(1)}}
Q\int w_T(t)\mathcal N_{\can}(t)\,dt
}
\]

with the scalar rank-one null properly handled and with actual \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) included.

The macroscopic exact-ratio proof route still applies here.

---

# 7. Current theorem stack for Agent 1

The branch-complete AggG1 proof should be split into three theorems.

## Theorem A — all-zero block-rank-one zero-Gram law

For macroscopic \(H\),

\[
G_Z
=
I-\frac1Qe_Z\otimes e_Z+\mathcal E
\]

in the global scalar packet sense, and

\[
G_{\cell}
\ge Q^{-A}I
\]

on smooth local cell energy.

## Theorem B — ordinary symbol quotient ellipticity

In the ordinary chart,

\[
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|X_{\can}\|^2
\]

after quotienting the scalar zero direction.

This is Green if

\[
C_0=1,\qquad C_1\text{ nonconstant},\qquad
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(C_1/C_0)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

## Theorem C — multichart atlas lower frame

For all local canonical source profiles \(F_m\),

\[
\chi_{\ord}\|\mathcal O_{\ord}\|^2
+
\chi_{\tr}\|\mathcal O_{\tr}\|^2
+
\chi_{\sing}\|\mathcal O_{\sing}^{\multi}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|^2.
\]

This is deterministic if the transition weighted jets and singular multiresolution projections are chosen correctly.

Together:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BlockRankOneAggG1}_{\can}
=
A+B+C.
}
\]

---

# 8. New risk: double counting and incompatible norms

The atlas uses different local norms:

\[
|X_{\can}|^2
\quad\text{ordinary},
\]

\[
\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\quad\text{transition/singular}.
\]

These must be shown comparable on overlaps. Otherwise the branch sum may be measuring incompatible energies.

The required overlap theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\ord}(m)
\asymp_{Q^{O(1)}}
\mathcal N_{\tr}(m)
\asymp_{Q^{O(1)}}
\mathcal N_{\sing}(m)
}
\]

on regions where the branch weights overlap.

If this fails, dyadic normalization must be branch-specific.

This is probably manageable, but it must be included.

---

# 9. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Branch routing can be repaired by replacing hard routing with a smooth multichart atlas.}
}
\]

The best Agent 1 theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete multichart AggG1}_{\can}.
}
\]

It requires:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all-zero macroscopic block-rank-one zero-Gram law;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary symbol quotient ellipticity;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition weighted-jet lower frame;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular multiresolution projection lower frame;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{overlap norm comparability.}
}
\]

This is significantly better than the prior hard-routed formulation and removes the “ordinary gaps” problem in a clean mathematical way.

## Agent 1 — multichart atlas stress test

The previous repair was:

\[
\chi_{\ord}\|\mathcal O_{\ord}\|^2
+
\chi_{\tr}\|\mathcal O_{\tr}\|^2
+
\chi_{\sing}\|\mathcal O_{\sing}^{\multi}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

I now tested the transition piece

\[
\mathcal O_{\tr}(m)
=
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^r F_m(0)
\right)_{0\le r\le R}.
\]

### New conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{A point-jet transition observable is not a lower frame for local }L^2\text{ source energy.}
}
\]

The transition branch should be changed from **point jets** to **local projected canonical energy**, exactly as we repaired the singular branch.

So the multichart atlas should use:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal O_{\tr}^{\mathrm{proj}}(m)
=
\left(P_{\tr,j}F_m\right)_j
}
\]

rather than only

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m).
\]

---

# 1. Why finite point jets fail

Let

\[
F(x)=\sum_n a_n e^{i\tau_n x},
\qquad
\tau_n/\mu=\frac{\log n}{Q}\in[c_-,c_+].
\]

The transition jet is

\[
\mu^{-r}F^{(r)}(0)
=
\sum_n a_n (i\tau_n/\mu)^r,
\qquad 0\le r\le R.
\]

Thus \(\mathcal J_R^{\tr}\) records only \(R+1\) bounded-window moments of the coefficient measure.

But the packet has many more degrees of freedom. One can choose coefficients so that

\[
\sum_n a_n(\tau_n/\mu)^r=0,
\qquad 0\le r\le R,
\]

while

\[
F\not\equiv0
\]

and

\[
\int_{|x|\le c}|F(x)|^2dx>0.
\]

This is just the finite moment-nullspace obstruction.

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
\not\gtrsim
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

for arbitrary transition packets.

This is the same structural issue as old low-dimensional Gate–1, but now inside the transition chart.

---

# 2. Actual-coefficient restriction is still not enough by itself

Could the actual canonical \(F_m=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\) avoid these finite moment nullspaces?

Possibly, but that would again be a counter-random/anti-design theorem:

\[
F_m\text{ actual transition packet}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This is not deterministic.

So if transition is to be part of the **deterministic branch atlas**, point jets are the wrong observable.

---

# 3. Correct transition observable: projected local energy

Define a finite or multiresolution projection family on \(L^2(I)\):

\[
P_{\tr,j}:L^2(I)\to V_{\tr,j}.
\]

Then set

\[
\boxed{
E_{\tr}^{\multi}(m)
=
\sum_j
\left\|
P_{\tr,j}
\left[
\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

Choose the projections so that

\[
\boxed{
\sum_j\|P_{\tr,j}F\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|F\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

on the transition packet class.

This can be made deterministic if the \(P_{\tr,j}\) form a finite frame or multiresolution partition of \(L^2(I)\) over the relevant bandwidth.

This is directly parallel to the singular repair:

\[
E_{\sing}^{\multi}(m)
=
\sum_j
\left\|
P_{\sing,j}
\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

---

# 4. What about the old weighted jets?

The weighted jets are still useful, but they should be downgraded.

They are good **coordinates** for low-frequency transition components. They are not a complete lower-frame observable.

A safe formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}
\text{ is a diagnostic or one block of a larger transition frame.}
}
\]

For example, let \(P_{\tr,0}\) be projection onto low-degree polynomials or low Fourier modes. Then

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}
\]

can coordinate \(P_{\tr,0}F_m\), while additional \(P_{\tr,j}\) capture higher local frequencies.

---

# 5. Revised multichart atlas

The branch atlas should now be:

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_{\ord}(m)
=
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2
}
\]

for ordinary packets;

\[
\boxed{
E_{\tr}(m)
=
\sum_j\|P_{\tr,j}F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

for transition packets;

\[
\boxed{
E_{\sing}(m)
=
\sum_j\|P_{\sing,j}F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

for singular packets.

Then require the deterministic atlas inequality:

\[
\boxed{
\chi_{\ord}E_{\ord}
+
\chi_{\tr}E_{\tr}
+
\chi_{\sing}E_{\sing}
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

This is now a true lower-frame statement: all branches measure actual canonical source energy, not proxy moments.

---

# 6. Ordinary chart mismatch

The ordinary chart currently measures

\[
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2,
\]

not directly

\[
\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

So we need an ordinary source equivalence theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2
\asymp_{Q^{O(1)}}
\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
}
\]

in the ordinary regime.

This is plausible because \(X_{\can}\) is just a smooth packet-frequency decomposition of \(F_m\). But it should be stated explicitly.

Then ordinary AggG1 gives

\[
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|X_{\can}\|^2
\asymp Q^{-A}\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

---

# 7. Aggregate theorem becomes cleaner

The branch-complete aggregate theorem should use a single scalar reference norm:

\[
\boxed{
N(m):=\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)
\left[
\chi_{\ord}(m)E_{\ord}(m)
+
\chi_{\tr}(m)E_{\tr}(m)
+
\chi_{\sing}(m)E_{\sing}(m)
\right]
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)N(m).
}
\]

This removes branchwise normalization mismatch.

For extraction, use

\[
d\mu_N=N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

Define normalized energy

\[
E_1(m)
=
\frac{
\chi_{\ord}E_{\ord}
+
\chi_{\tr}E_{\tr}
+
\chi_{\sing}E_{\sing}
}{
N(m)
}.
\]

Then the aggregate theorem is simply

\[
\int E_1\,d\mu_N
\ge Q^{-A}\mu_N(\mathcal C).
\]

This is exactly the right interface for Agent 2’s dyadic extraction.

---

# 8. What remains arithmetic?

With projected transition and singular energies, the branch atlas becomes deterministic.

The only remaining arithmetic part of AggG1 is ordinary zero-sampling:

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)\|^2
\]

versus

\[
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)\|X_{\can}(\gamma)\|^2.
\]

But once everything is expressed through \(F_m\), even ordinary can be phrased as a projection of actual \(F_m\). This suggests a unified theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)
\sum_j\|P_jF_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2,
}
\]

where the \(P_j\) include ordinary canonical row projections, transition projections, and singular projections with smooth branch weights.

This is simply a **global multichart frame for the canonical source sampled at zeros**.

---

# 9. New best formulation

The cleanest Agent 1 theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Canonical multichart source-frame AggG1.}
}
\]

Let

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

Let branch-weighted projection operators

\[
\mathcal P_j(m):L^2(I)\to V_j
\]

include:

1. ordinary \(T_{\can}\)-packet projections;
2. transition multiresolution projections;
3. singular multiresolution projections.

Assume:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|F_m\|^2
}
\]

for each \(m\), outside geometric-bad sets.

Then aggregate Gate–1 is automatic:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\|F_m\|^2.
\]

The ordinary zero-Gram work is then needed only to justify that the ordinary \(T_{\can}\) projections are indeed sampled/stable at zeros in the ordinary chart.

---

# 10. Final status of this attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{Transition finite point jets are not sufficient as a deterministic chart.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use projected/multiresolution canonical transition energy instead.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use projected/multiresolution canonical singular energy as already proposed.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Normalize all branches by }N(m)=\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggG1 becomes a canonical multichart source-frame theorem.}
}
\]

This is cleaner and more robust than the previous ordinary/transition/singular split. The remaining genuinely arithmetic step is the ordinary zero-sampling block-rank-one law; the transition and singular branch observability should be made deterministic by choosing actual \(L^2\)-projection frames.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 20:06:36)

_Thought for 12s_

## Agent 1 — source-frame repair vs downstream-compatible Gate–1

The previous move repaired transition/singular observability by replacing finite point jets/raw moments with actual projected canonical source energy:

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x),
\]

\[
E_{\tr}^{\multi}(m)=\sum_j\|P_{\tr,j}F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2,
\qquad
E_{\sing}^{\multi}(m)=\sum_j\|P_{\sing,j}F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This is much safer as an observability statement, but it creates a new interface question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does downstream require a specific finite Gate--1 row, or only local }L^2\text{ source energy?}
}
\]

This matters because arbitrary projections of \(F_m\) may certify scalar source energy, but they may not certify the same finite-row structure used in O2/O3 and D4.

---

# 1. Two possible downstream interfaces

There are two possible versions of “Gate–1.”

## Interface A — finite-row Gate–1

The old local machinery expects a specific finite row, schematically

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can}(m),
\]

or a Wronskian/canonical derivative row built from

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}.
\]

Then an arbitrary projection lower bound

\[
\sum_j\|P_jF_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A}\|F_m\|^2
\]

is not enough. It proves \(F_m\) has local \(L^2\) energy, but not that the particular canonical finite row is large.

Under Interface A, the multichart repair is still incomplete: every projection family must be downstream-compatible, not merely a frame for \(L^2\).

## Interface B — \(L^2\)-source Gate–1

The downstream Gate–2 step is described as:

\[
\text{Gate 1}
+
\text{source thickening}
+
L^2\text{-input real rigidity}
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate 2}.
\]

If the actual input to real rigidity is only

\[
\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then the multichart projected-energy repair is ideal. It certifies exactly the scalar canonical source energy needed by Gate–2:

\[
F_m=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

Then branch observability becomes deterministic once the projections form an \(L^2\) frame.

So the next audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can Gate--2 be invoked from local }L^2\text{ source energy alone?}
}
\]

If yes, the branch multichart repair is strong. If no, it must be refined back into finite-row-compatible projections.

---

# 2. If Gate–2 accepts \(L^2\) source energy

Assume the downstream theorem has the form:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I |\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2\,dx
\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{Gate--2}(m).
}
\]

Then define the unified branch reference norm

\[
N(m):=\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

Let \(\{\mathcal P_j(m)\}_j\) be a branch-weighted multichart projection family satisfying the deterministic frame inequality

\[
\boxed{
\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A} \|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

Then the aggregate Gate–1 theorem becomes almost tautological after choosing a complete enough projection family:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)N(m).
\]

Good-subset extraction with

\[
d\mu_N=N(m)\,d\rho(m)
\]

gives a polynomial-weight subset where

\[
\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|^2\ge Q^{-A}N(m).
\]

But because the projections frame \(F_m\), this only says

\[
N(m)\ge \text{nontrivial branch energy}.
\]

To run downstream, we still need \(N(m)\) itself to be polynomially non-small after normalization. Thus the normalized energy should be

\[
E_1(m)
=
\frac{\sum_j\|\mathcal P_j(m)F_m\|^2}{N(m)}.
\]

This is bounded below by the frame inequality, but says nothing about the absolute size of \(N(m)\).

Therefore the extraction measure must be

\[
d\mu_N=N(m)d\rho(m),
\]

not \(d\rho(m)\). Downstream lower bounds then sum with \(N\)-weight.

That is acceptable if downstream row energy is normalized relative to \(N(m)\). It is not acceptable if downstream requires many centers in raw \(\rho\)-measure.

So under Interface B, the theorem stack should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate and extract in }N\,d\rho\text{-measure.}
}
\]

This is consistent with Agent 2’s dyadic normalization.

---

# 3. If Gate–2 requires finite canonical rows

If the downstream theorem instead requires

\[
\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2\ge Q^{-A}N(m),
\]

then arbitrary multiresolution projections are insufficient.

Then transition and singular charts must be designed so that their projections imply finite-row canonical data. That is much harder.

For transition, one would need something like:

\[
\sum_j\|P_{\tr,j}F_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}N(m)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2\ge Q^{-A'}N(m),
\]

which is false in general because \(P_{\tr,j}\) may capture high-frequency local components invisible to the point jet.

For singular, similarly:

\[
\sum_j\|P_{\sing,j}F_m\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}N(m)
\not\Longrightarrow
|S_R(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A'}N(m)
\]

unless \(S_R\) is a complete enough coordinate system.

Thus if downstream needs finite rows, we should not use arbitrary multiresolution projections. We need branch projections whose ranges are precisely downstream-admissible.

This is the dangerous case:

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-frame observability may not equal Gate--1 admissibility.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Proposed repair: define Gate–1 as \(L^2\)-source energy

The cleanest Stack C2 version should explicitly redefine aggregate Gate–1 as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}_{L^2}(m):
\quad
N(m)=\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

in the local normalization, or aggregate:

\[
\boxed{
\int N(m)\,d\rho(m)\ge Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then branch projections are only tools for proving/extracting \(N(m)\)-weighted good subsets, not separate downstream sources.

This fits the phrase “\(L^2\)-input real rigidity” better than the old finite-row view.

However, this changes the role of O1/O2/O3. O2/O3 were originally needed to produce a Wronskian/angle condition, not merely source energy. If O2/O3 are still required for Gate–2 or D4, they must remain separate aggregate factors:

\[
E_{23}(m)=\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\]

with joint extraction.

Thus the clean interface becomes:

\[
\boxed{
N(m)\text{ supplies source size,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}(m)\text{ supplies cofactor/incidence nondegeneracy,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\text{ supplies forbidden D4 visibility.}
}
\]

This is coherent.

---

# 5. What does AggG1 become?

If Gate–1 is source energy \(N(m)\), the ordinary AggG1 theorem should not compare \(T_{\can}X\) to \(X\). Instead, it should prove that the sampled canonical source energy is nontrivial in aggregate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)N(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or relative to a global normalization.

But this may be much easier or much harder depending on normalization. If \(N(m)\) can be tiny at many zeros, we still need a lower sampling theorem.

The block-rank-one zero-Gram law gives this kind of statement for cell/source energy. It says zero sampling does not annihilate local cell energy except in the scalar value direction. Since \(N(m)\) is local \(L^2\) energy of \(q''_{\can}\), not scalar \(Z\)-value energy, it should be stable.

So the theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)
\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(\gamma+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
Q\int w_T(t)
\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(t+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2\,dt.
}
\]

This is a pure zero-sampling second moment for the actual scalar source \(q''_{\can}\).

It avoids the finite-row \(T_{\can}\) symbol issue entirely.

---

# 6. Does this lose ordinary \(T_{\can}\)?

Possibly not. \(T_{\can}\) may be needed only to turn scalar zero cancellation into source energy when working directly at a zero. But if the source is \(q''_{\can}\), the scalar zero equation \(Z(\gamma)=0\) does not make \(q''_{\can}\) vanish. So the source energy may already be the right Gate–1 quantity.

The old \(T_{\can}\) row was needed because the proof tried to extract a derivative-row obstruction from the scalar value. In the aggregate \(L^2\)-source formulation, we can bypass that.

This is a major simplification if downstream accepts \(L^2\)-source Gate–1.

---

# 7. New theorem stack

Under the \(L^2\)-source interface, Agent 1’s Stack C2 input becomes:

## Source sampling

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\gamma w_T(\gamma)
N(\gamma)
\ge Q^{-A}
Q\int w_T(t)N(t)\,dt,
}
\]

where

\[
N(t)=\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(t+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

## Branch/source frame

\[
\boxed{
\chi_{\ord}E_{\ord}
+
\chi_{\tr}E_{\tr}^{\multi}
+
\chi_{\sing}E_{\sing}^{\multi}
\ge Q^{-A}N(t).
}
\]

But if we use \(N(t)\) directly as the measure, even this branch frame is not strictly needed for Gate–1. It is needed only if we want branch-specific observables for extraction or localization.

## O2/O3 aggregate

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{23}\,N\,d\rho\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
}
\]

## Forbidden wall aggregate

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{\rm wall}^{\forb}F_{23}\,N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
}
\]

This is cleaner.

---

# 8. Critical audit required

Everything now depends on one question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the existing Gate--2 theorem accept }N(m)=\|F_m\|_{L^2}^2\text{ as Gate--1 input?}
}
\]

If yes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{replace finite-row AggG1 by aggregate }L^2\text{-source sampling.}
}
\]

If no:

\[
\boxed{
\text{we must keep finite-row }T_{\can}\text{ and prove source-to-row compatibility.}
}
\]

Given the phrase “\(L^2\)-input real rigidity,” I suspect the answer is yes, but this must be verified against the draft’s actual Gate–2 statement.

---

# 9. Final status of this attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{Projected/multiresolution branch energies are excellent if Gate--2 consumes }L^2\text{ source energy.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{They are insufficient if Gate--2 requires specific finite rows.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The cleanest architecture is to define aggregate Gate--1 as local }L^2\text{ energy of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Then }O2/O3\text{ and forbidden wall remain separate joint aggregate factors.}
}
\]

The next Agent 1 audit should read the Gate–2 theorem exactly and determine whether its input is truly \(L^2\)-source energy or a finite-row Wronskian seed.

## Agent 1 — Gate–2 \(L^2\)-input audit

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{A. \(L^2\)-Gate--1 is valid for the ordinary smooth-packet Gate--2 theorem.}
}
\]

The current Gate–2 theorem **does not require** a finite Wronskian/source row such as \(T_{\can}X_{\can}\) as its Gate–1 input. Its Gate–1 hypothesis is exactly an \(L^2\) lower bound for the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

So, for the ordinary smooth branch, Gate–1 can be redefined as local scalar \(L^2\) source energy:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I |q''_{\can}(m)|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-B}|I|.
}
\]

Equivalently, in a local branch coordinate \(m+\lambda x\),

\[
\boxed{
\int_{I_x}\left|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\right|^2dx\ge Q^{-B'}
}
\]

after applying the branch’s fixed normalization.

This supports the proposed simplification in the update: branch-specific objects such as \(T_{\can}X_{\can}\), transition jets, and singular projected modes should be treated as **proof tools for establishing \(L^2\)-source energy**, not as the definition of Gate–1. The update explicitly identifies this as the main audit point. 

---

# 1. Exact Gate–1 \(\Rightarrow\) Gate–2 implication

The downstream package states the active route as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 1}
+
|u'|_\infty\text{ source thickening}
+
L^2\text{-input real rigidity}
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate 2}.
}
\]

In the draft’s theorem “Gate 2 from Gate 1 and \(L^2\)-input rigidity,” the variables are

\[
u=q''_{\can}=q'',
\]

\[
g=\Gamma_G=q''-6q+2q^3,
\]

\[
h=\Gamma_{G,2}=2q(q')^2-\frac16g''.
\]

The Gate–1 hypothesis is

\[
\boxed{
\int_I |u|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-B}|I|.
}
\]

There is no \(T_{\can}X_{\can}\) hypothesis in this theorem.

The proof then uses:

1. source Lipschitz control,

\[
\|u'\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^{C_u}|I|^{-1};
\]

2. source thickening, which turns the \(L^2\) lower bound into a subinterval \(J\subset I\) where

\[
|u|\ge \frac12Q^{-B/2};
\]

3. contradiction by assuming Gate–2 failure,

\[
\int_I |u|^2(g^2+h^2)\,dm\le Q^{-A}|I|;
\]

4. the \(L^2\)-input real-rigidity lemma, which says that small \(g,h\) on the source-large interval force \(u\) to be small there, contradicting the thickened lower bound.

The conclusion is Gate–2:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I |u|^2(g^2+h^2)\,dm\ge Q^{-A}|I|
}
\]

for sufficiently large \(A\), quantitatively

\[
A>\frac{13}{2}B+C_u+5C_R+O(1).
\]

So the answer to the audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--2 consumes scalar \(L^2\)-energy of \(u=q''_{\can}\), not a finite row seed.}
}
\]

---

# 2. What the actual \(L^2\) input is

The \(L^2\) input is the full canonical scalar source

\[
\boxed{
u=q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
}
\]

It is not:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a projected source;}
}
\]

not:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a whitened source;}
}
\]

not:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a finite-row thickening of }q''_{\can};
}
\]

and not:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a quotient/carrier input.}
}
\]

The finite-row/canonical Wronskian machinery may still be used upstream to **prove** that \(u\) has \(L^2\) mass, but once

\[
\int_I |u|^2\ge Q^{-B}|I|
\]

is established, Gate–2 does not care how that mass was detected.

This is the key cleanup.

---

# 3. New Gate–1 theorem statement

For the ordinary smooth branch, the Gate–1 object should now be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}_{L^2}(I):
\qquad
\int_I |q''_{\can}(m)|^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-B}|I|.
}
\]

In local branch variables \(m+\lambda x\), use the scale-normalized form:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}_{L^2}(m,\lambda):
\qquad
\int_{I_x}
\left|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\right|^2dx
\ge Q^{-B}.
}
\]

Then the ordinary Gate–2 implication is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Gate1}_{L^2}
+
\text{source Lipschitz}
+
\text{real-canonical compatibility}
+
\text{safe primitive bounds}
+
L^2\text{-input rigidity}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{Gate2}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Side hypotheses required

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 simplification is valid only under the ordinary smooth-packet hypotheses of the theorem.

The required side hypotheses are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary smooth packet / no unregularized critical-line zero in the chart;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{real completed canonical gauge;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
u=q''_{\can}=q'';
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
g=\Gamma_G=q''-6q+2q^3;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
h=\Gamma_{G,2}=2q(q')^2-\frac16g'';
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\|u'\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^{C_u}|I|^{-1};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\|q\|_\infty+\|q'\|_\infty+\|q''\|_\infty\le Q^C
\text{ on the normalized source interval;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{chart denominators and real-gauge denominators are bounded below by }Q^{-C}.
}
\]

There is also a routing caveat:

\[
\boxed{
\text{packets with unregularized critical-line zero or nonsmooth real phase are not covered by this ordinary theorem.}
}
\]

Those must be routed to singular/transition or handled by a regularized convention.

---

# 5. If finite rows were required

They are **not** required for this Gate–2 theorem.

So the source-to-row compatibility theorem

\[
\int_I|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2dx\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

is **not needed for Gate–2**.

Also, in general that implication is false without additional assumptions: local \(L^2\) mass can lie in components not visible to a chosen finite row. But this no longer blocks the downstream Gate–2 route, because the theorem consumes scalar \(L^2\)-mass directly.

---

# 6. Role of O2/O3

O2/O3 are **not part of the Gate–2 input** in the \(L^2\)-input theorem.

They remain separate nondegeneracy factors needed for the later Wronskian/cofactor/incidence side of the downstream architecture, especially where one needs the augmented complex-frame condition or D4-facing nondegeneracy.

Thus the revised stack should separate:

\[
\boxed{
E_1(m)=\text{local scalar \(L^2\)-source energy;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}(m)=\text{augmented O2/O3 exterior-pivot or harmonic-ratio energy;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\forb}(m)=\text{\(\Lambda\)-visible D4 wall/forbidden energy.}
}
\]

They should be joined only at extraction:

\[
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}^{\forb}.
\]

---

# 7. Strategic result

The upstream Gate–1 side should be simplified:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Adopt local scalar \(L^2\)-Gate--1 globally, branch by branch.}
}
\]

Old branch-specific observables now become proof tools:

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can},
\qquad
\mathcal J_R^{\tr},
\qquad
P_R[\lambda^3q''_{\can}],
\qquad
E_{\sing}^{\multi},
\]

but not definitions of Gate–1.

The aggregate Gate–1 theorem should therefore be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b}\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is scalar-canonical and branch-complete.

---

# 8. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--2 input type: A. local \(L^2\) source energy of \(q''_{\can}\).}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Finite-row Gate--1 is not required for Gate--2.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2/O3 remain separate downstream nondegeneracy factors.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The correct Gate--1 object going forward is }E_1(m)=\int|\lambda^3q''_{\can}|^2.
}
\]

This is a major simplification of the upstream architecture. The main remaining downstream obstruction is now exactly the one identified in the update:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No }\Lambda,\text{ no contradiction.}
}
\]
