## Agent 1 — continuing after \(L^2\)-Gate–1 audit

Since Gate–2 accepts local scalar \(L^2\)-energy of

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

the upstream Gate–1 problem changes substantially.

The old target was a finite-row projection:

\[
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

The new target is simply local canonical source energy:

\[
E_1(m)
=
\int_I
\left|
\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

This is a much better object. It is positive, scalar-canonical, branch-independent, and directly feeds Gate–2.

The new aggregate theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
\rho(m)
\int_I
\left|
\lambda_m^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_m x)
\right|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho(m).
}
\]

I now attack this theorem.

---

# 1. Convert aggregate local \(L^2\) into a convolution weight

Let

\[
u(t):=q''_{\can}(t).
\]

For one scale \(\lambda\), define

\[
\psi_\lambda(t)
=
\lambda^5
\int_I
\mathbf 1_{t=m+\lambda x}\,dx
\]

more concretely, if \(I=[-c,c]\),

\[
\int_I |\lambda^3u(m+\lambda x)|^2dx
=
\lambda^5
\int_{m-c\lambda}^{m+c\lambda}
|u(t)|^2\,dt.
\]

Therefore the aggregate Gate–1 energy is

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)
\int_I|\lambda^3u(m+\lambda x)|^2dx
=
\int |u(t)|^2 W_\lambda(t)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
W_\lambda(t)
=
\lambda^5
\sum_m
\rho(m)
\mathbf 1_{|t-m|\le c\lambda}.
\]

With a smooth local window \(\psi\), this becomes

\[
W_\lambda(t)
=
\lambda^5
\sum_m
\rho(m)
\psi\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda}\right).
\]

Thus aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1 is not a pointwise zero-sampling theorem. It is a **weighted continuous \(L^2\) lower bound**:

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2 W_\lambda(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m).
}
\]

This is far more accessible.

---

# 2. The sampling problem becomes a coverage problem

If the zero/routed centers satisfy local density

\[
\sum_{m\in[t-\lambda,t+\lambda]}\rho(m)
\ge Q^{-C} \rho_{\rm av}\lambda
\]

on most of the height window, then

\[
W_\lambda(t)
\ge Q^{-C}\lambda^5\rho_{\rm av}\lambda
\]

on most \(t\).

So instead of proving that sampled Dirichlet values avoid a projection kernel, we need:

\[
\boxed{
\text{centers cover the interval at the local scale,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
u=q''_{\can}\text{ has continuous }L^2\text{ mass in that covered region.}
}
\]

This is much weaker than pointwise projection avoidance.

---

# 3. Ordinary scale and zero spacing

In the ordinary regime, the natural local scale is comparable to the zero spacing:

\[
\lambda Q\asymp 1.
\]

Zeros have mean spacing

\[
\asymp \frac1Q.
\]

Therefore local windows of radius

\[
c\lambda\asymp \frac{c}{Q}
\]

centered at zeros should cover a positive fraction of the height interval, modulo large zero gaps.

If a gap has length

\[
G\gg \lambda,
\]

then the middle of the gap is not sampled. But this is not fatal unless \(u\) concentrates its \(L^2\) mass in such gaps.

So the new obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ must not concentrate its }L^2\text{ mass in regions far from routed centers.}
}
\]

This is a continuous mass-distribution theorem, not a refined-cell projection theorem.

---

# 4. Branch-complete coverage

Hard ordinary-only coverage can fail. But branch-complete routing helps.

Let

\[
\mathcal C
=
\mathcal C_{\ord}
\cup
\mathcal C_{\tr}
\cup
\mathcal C_{\sing}.
\]

Each branch has its own scale \(\lambda_b(m)\) and local window. Define

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_{b}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b(m)^5
\psi_b\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b(m)}\right).
\]

Then aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1 becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2 W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

The branch coverage theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)\ge Q^{-C}W_{\rm ref}(t)
}
\]

on the relevant height window, where \(W_{\rm ref}\) is the natural continuous reference weight.

If this holds, then it remains to prove

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2 W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt
\ge Q^{-C}\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is now a global/continuous \(L^2\) statement for \(q''_{\can}\).

---

# 5. Continuous \(L^2\) mass of \(q''_{\can}\)

The source

\[
u=q''_{\can}
\]

is the second derivative of the canonical phase/source object, with

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

We need a lower bound for its local/weighted \(L^2\) mass.

There are two possibilities.

## Option A — analytic lower bound for \(q''_{\can}\)

Prove

\[
\int_T^{T+H}|q''_{\can}(t)|^2w_T(t)\,dt
\ge Q^{-A}H
\]

for macroscopic windows.

This resembles a standard mean-square theorem for an explicit formula derivative. It is much more plausible than pointwise lower bounds.

## Option B — localize to zeta/zero structure

If \(q''_{\can}\) is built from zeros/primes and could vanish in mean square on some windows, use the explicit formula to show that cannot happen unless there is an extreme cancellation between

\[
B_2,\quad P_2,\quad D_2,\quad E_2.
\]

This becomes an actual canonical \(L^2\)-nontriviality theorem.

Either way, the target is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{weighted mean-square lower bound for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

This is a standard-shaped analytic number theory problem.

---

# 6. Does scalar zero cancellation matter?

The old rank-one zero-Gram issue arose because scalar \(Z(\gamma)=0\) kills the value packet at zeros.

But \(q''_{\can}\) is not \(Z\). It is a curvature/source object. There is no reason for

\[
q''_{\can}(\gamma)
\]

or its local \(L^2\) norm near \(\gamma\) to vanish systematically at zeros.

Thus the dangerous scalar value null direction is largely irrelevant after switching to \(L^2\)-source Gate–1.

This is a major simplification:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the scalar AFE value null no longer drives Gate--1.}
}
\]

The source \(q''_{\can}\) is already the differentiated/curvature object.

---

# 7. New AggGate1 theorem

The clean theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete aggregate \(L^2\)-source theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Let

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)
\lambda_b(m)^5
\psi_b\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b(m)}\right).
\]

Assume branch coverage:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)\ge Q^{-C}W_{\rm ref}(t)
}
\]

outside a bad set \(B\), and

\[
\int_B |u(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt
\le \frac12
\int |u(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt.
\]

Assume continuous source mean square:

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt
\ge Q^{-C}\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m).
}
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)
\int_I
|\lambda_b^3u(m+\lambda_bx)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is the new Gate–1 aggregate theorem.

---

# 8. What remains hard?

The hard parts are now clearly separated.

## 8.1 Branch coverage

We need to know that the routed centers and scales cover the height window with enough weighted overlap.

This is a geometric/dynamical statement about the routing algorithm, not a prime-log projection theorem.

## 8.2 Source mean square

We need a weighted lower bound for

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt.
\]

This is an analytic theorem about the canonical explicit formula.

## 8.3 Bad-set avoidance

If there are intervals where the branch atlas fails or \(W_{\branch}\) is small, we need to show \(q''_{\can}\) does not concentrate its mean square there.

This can be integrated into branch coverage or handled by a separate bad-set mass estimate.

---

# 9. Interaction with O2/O3

Switching Gate–1 to \(L^2\)-source energy does **not** remove O2/O3.

The joint extraction measure becomes

\[
d\nu
=
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho,
\]

with

\[
N(m)=\int_I|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2dx.
\]

Now \(F_1\) may simply be

\[
F_1=\min(N/\eta_1,1)
\]

if using raw \(\rho\)-measure, or \(F_1\equiv1\) if the extraction measure is already \(N\,d\rho\).

This simplifies extraction.

O2/O3 remains:

\[
\int F_{23}\,d\nu\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Forbidden wall remains:

\[
\int F_{23}F_{\wall}^{\forb}\,N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

So the stack becomes cleaner:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
+
\mathrm{WeightedPivotAntiTrough}_{23}^{\Lambda}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenWall}^{\Lambda}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4}.
}
\]

---

# 10. A possible direct proof of source mean square

Because

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

the mean square expands as

\[
\int |B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2|^2W
=
\int |P_2+D_2|^2W
+
2\Re\int (P_2+D_2)\overline{(B_2+E_2)}W
+
\int |B_2+E_2|^2W.
\]

A lower bound can be proved if:

1. the main prime/dual mean square is

\[
\int |P_2+D_2|^2W\ge Q^{-C}H;
\]

2. the background/tail cross terms do not cancel more than half:

\[
\left|
2\Re\int (P_2+D_2)\overline{(B_2+E_2)}W
\right|
\le \frac12\int |P_2+D_2|^2W
+
Q^C\int|B_2+E_2|^2W.
\]

But exact cancellation between \(P_2+D_2\) and \(B_2+E_2\) is possible in principle. Safer is to prove lower bound for the full canonical source directly using its explicit formula structure.

This is now a concrete analytic target.

---

# 11. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--1 should be redefined as local scalar }L^2\text{ energy of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Aggregate Gate--1 becomes a branch-coverage + source mean-square theorem.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The zero-Gram rank-one machinery is no longer the central route for Gate--1,}
}
\]

though it remains useful for proving mean-square lower bounds if sampling is over zeros.

The decisive current tasks are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{1. prove branch coverage of local windows;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{2. prove weighted mean-square lower bound for }q''_{\can};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{3. identify }\Lambda\text{ for downstream contradiction.}
}
\]

This is the cleanest architecture so far.

## Agent 1 — continued attack on \(L^2\)-Gate–1

We have reduced Gate–1 to the scalar local source-energy statement

\[
E_1(m)
=
\int_I
\left|
\lambda_m^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_m x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

Aggregate Gate–1 becomes

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_1(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

After Fubini, this is a weighted continuous mean-square statement:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_1(m)
=
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2 W(t)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
W(t)
=
\sum_m
\rho(m)\lambda_m^5
\psi\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_m}\right).
\]

So the new problem has two independent inputs:

\[
\boxed{\text{Coverage: }W(t)\text{ must not miss where }q''_{\can}\text{ has mass.}}
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{Source nontriviality: }q''_{\can}\text{ must have weighted }L^2\text{ mass.}}
\]

The simplification is real, but it exposes a new coverage theorem.

---

# 1. Coverage is not automatic from zero density

Suppose the local scale is ordinary:

\[
\lambda\asymp Q^{-1}.
\]

Zeros have average spacing

\[
\asymp Q^{-1}.
\]

If every gap between routed centers were

\[
\ll Q^{-1}Q^C
\]

and the local windows had radius comparable to the gap size, then coverage would be straightforward.

But a lower bound like

\[
W(t)\ge Q^{-C}W_{\rm ref}(t)
\]

does not follow from average zero density. A set can have correct average density and still have gaps. If \(q''_{\can}\) concentrates in those gaps, the aggregate source energy can be missed.

Thus branch coverage must be stated as a theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is weaker and better than pointwise coverage

\[
W(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\rm ref}(t)
\]

everywhere. We only need coverage **with respect to the source-energy measure**

\[
d\mu_u(t)=|q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt.
\]

So the correct theorem is not a geometric max-gap theorem. It is an **energy-weighted coverage theorem**.

---

# 2. Energy-weighted coverage theorem

The best form is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2 W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |u(t)|^2 W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt,
\qquad u=q''_{\can}.
}
\]

Here

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b(m)^5
\psi_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b(m)}\right),
\]

and \(b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}\).

This theorem says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary/transition/singular routed windows sample the }q''_{\can}\text{ energy measure.}
}
\]

This is much less brittle than asking ordinary centers alone to cover every interval.

---

# 3. Can branch coverage be deterministic?

Only partly.

If the branch routing is a smooth atlas over all relevant centers, with weights

\[
\chi_{\ord}+\chi_{\tr}+\chi_{\sing}=1,
\]

and the centers themselves are dense enough, then

\[
W_{\branch}\asymp W_{\rm ref}
\]

is deterministic.

But if centers are only zeta zeros, coverage still depends on zero gaps. Smooth branch weights do not create centers where there are none.

So deterministic branch coverage requires either:

\[
\boxed{\text{local density of routed centers at the relevant scale;}}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{\text{a theorem that }q''_{\can}\text{ does not concentrate in center-free gaps.}}
\]

The second is more realistic and can be folded into energy-weighted coverage.

---

# 4. Source mean-square lower bound

Assume coverage holds. We still need:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2 W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt
\ge Q^{-A}\int W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is now the main analytic number theory input for Gate–1.

Because

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

the direct expansion is

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2 W
=
\int |P_2+D_2|^2W
+
2\Re\int (P_2+D_2)\overline{(B_2+E_2)}W
+
\int |B_2+E_2|^2W.
\]

A proof by “main term dominates error” is dangerous unless one has strong control of cross-cancellation. The full canonical source should be handled as one object.

The right theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Canonical source mean-square nontriviality.}
}
\]

For every macroscopic admissible window \(W\),

\[
\boxed{
\int |B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2|^2 W
\ge
Q^{-A}\int W.
}
\]

This is now a clean, direct analytic input.

---

# 5. Can \(B_2\) alone prove it?

No, not at the right scale.

The background term

\[
B_2(t)=\theta'''(t)\sim -\frac{1}{2t^2}
\]

is too small in the global height variable to serve as a robust lower bound after the local normalizations unless the theorem is normalized to make it visible.

Moreover, earlier canonicalization already established that \(B_2\) is a background curvature term, not a candidate-producing source by itself. So using \(B_2\)-only as a lower bound would be misleading.

The mean-square theorem must use the full source

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

---

# 6. Can cancellation make \(q''_{\can}\) tiny?

In principle, yes. Since \(q''_{\can}\) is a signed/complex canonical explicit-formula object, one cannot rule out cancellation from positivity alone.

But aggregate mean-square lower bounds for explicit-formula Dirichlet-type sums are plausible. The packet contains many oscillatory prime/dual terms. Unless \(q''_{\can}\) is structurally zero, its \(L^2\) mean square should be governed by diagonal coefficient mass plus controlled off-diagonal terms.

The proof should be a standard mean-square calculation:

\[
\int \left|\sum_n a_n n^{-it}\right|^2 W(t)\,dt
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}\widehat W(\log(n/n')).
\]

For a macroscopic smooth window, \(\widehat W\) localizes near \(n=n'\), so the diagonal should dominate for the continuous \(t\)-integral.

This is easier than zero-sampling. We no longer need Landau/Gonek off-diagonal analysis here.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{continuous source mean-square is much easier than sampled value mean-square.}
}
\]

---

# 7. The real issue: lower bound relative to \(\sum\rho\)

Even if

\[
\int |u|^2W_{\rm ref}\ge Q^{-A}\int W_{\rm ref},
\]

the aggregate theorem wants

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

Because

\[
E_1(m)=\int |\lambda^3u(m+\lambda x)|^2dx,
\]

there are scale factors. The correct reference weight must match these factors.

Let

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_m\rho(m)\lambda_m^5\psi\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_m}\right).
\]

Then

\[
\int W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
=
\left(\int \psi\right)\sum_m\rho(m)\lambda_m^6.
\]

So if \(\lambda_m\) varies by branch, the right-hand side is not simply \(\sum_m\rho(m)\). We need normalized weights.

Define instead the branch-normalized energy as

\[
\widetilde E_1(m)
=
\lambda_m^{-6}
\int_I |\lambda_m^3u(m+\lambda_m x)|^2dx
=
\int_I |u(m+\lambda_m x)|^2dx
\]

or choose \(\rho_m\) already scaled by \(\lambda_m^6\).

This is a normalization issue but important. The aggregate theorem should be written in a scale-invariant way:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m \widetilde\rho(m)
\int_I |u(m+\lambda_m x)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\widetilde\rho(m)\lambda_m
}
\]

or, equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\int W(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This avoids inconsistent powers of \(\lambda\).

So the clean invariant form is:

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\int |u(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt}{\int W_{\branch}(t)\,dt}
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 8. New exact AggGate1 statement

The aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete weighted source mean-square.}
}
\]

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Let

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\omega_b(m)\psi_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b(m)}\right),
\]

with nonnegative weights \(\omega_b(m)\) chosen so that

\[
\int W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\asymp
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)
\]

in the intended downstream normalization.

Then prove

\[
\boxed{
\int |u(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int W_{\branch}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is the clean Gate–1 aggregate input.

---

# 9. Interaction with extraction

If

\[
E_1(m)
=
\frac{
\int_I |u(m+\lambda_m x)|^2\psi(x)\,dx
}{
\int_I \psi(x)\,dx
}
\]

is the local normalized energy, then

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_1(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m)
\]

has the usual form.

Good-subset extraction can use

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1)
\]

with \(d\rho\)-measure. Alternatively, if the natural theorem is unnormalized, use \(d\mu_N=E_1\,d\rho\). The normalized formulation is cleaner.

---

# 10. Final refined inputs for Agent 1

The new Gate–1 route requires two precise theorems:

## Theorem 1 — weighted source mean-square

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\int W_{\branch}(t)\,dt.
}
\]

## Theorem 2 — branch coverage / weight admissibility

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}
\text{ is an admissible macroscopic/nondegenerate weight}
}
\]

for the mean-square theorem: smooth enough, not concentrated on pathological short gaps, and correctly normalized.

If \(W_{\branch}\) is built from zero-centered windows at scale \(1/Q\), it may be rough. Therefore use smoothed windows and possibly aggregate over macroscopic \(T^\theta\)-windows.

---

# 11. Final conclusion

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 simplification is a major improvement.

The old hard problem:

\[
\text{zero-conditioned projection avoidance}
\]

has become the much cleaner analytic problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{weighted mean-square lower bound for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

The remaining risk is not finite-row visibility. It is whether the branch-generated weight

\[
W_{\branch}
\]

is admissible enough for the source mean-square theorem.

So Agent 1’s next target should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove or reduce the weighted mean-square lower bound for }q''_{\can}
\text{ under branch-generated weights.}
}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:46:16)

I’ll treat the atlas-loop idea as a finite functoriality problem: first define what a lifted transition must be, then check whether its loop defect can be both globally forbidden and nonzero on the D4 live carrier plane.

## Agent 1 — carrier-lifted atlas loop \(\Lambda_{\atlas}\)

The prompt asks whether the ordinary/transition/singular branch atlas can supply the missing downstream forbidden operator

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-\mathrm{Id}
\]

on carrier quotient row spaces, and whether it sees the live D4 plane. 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The naive carrier-lifted atlas loop is rank zero.}
}
\]

If the lifted transition maps are the natural functorial maps induced by coordinate changes of the same scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

then the atlas loop is exactly flat:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
=
\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\atlas}=0
}
\]

on the live carrier plane. This gives an exact global forbidden upper, but it is useless because it is D4-invisible:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\big|_{\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}}
\right)
=0.
}
\]

A nontrivial atlas-loop \(\Lambda\) is possible only if the carrier lift is **not** the exact functorial scalar-source lift, but a projected/truncated/gauge-fixed carrier connection. Then the live-rank may become nonzero, but the global forbidden upper is no longer automatic. That is the real fork.

---

# 1. Carrier quotient spaces

For each chart

\[
b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\},
\]

define a local carrier module

\[
\mathcal C_{\car}^{(b)}
\]

containing the order-seven and order-nine live D4 carriers plus nuisance rows. The relevant quotient is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{(b)}
=
\mathcal C_{\car}^{(b)}
/
\mathcal N_{\car}^{(b)}.
}
\]

The live D4 plane in each chart is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal L_{\D4}^{(b)}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{[\mathsf C_7]^{(b)},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{(b)}\}
\subset
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{(b)}.
}
\]

The clean D4 output is

\[
v_m^{\live}
=
H(m)E_{\car}^{(b)}(m)L_{\clean}^{(b)}(m)\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T,
\]

and

\[
L_{\clean}=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\det L_{\clean}\ne0
\]

on Gram-good patches.

Thus the rank of any forbidden operator is determined by its action on

\[
E_{\car}^{(b)}\mathcal L_{\D4}^{(b)}.
\]

---

# 2. Natural lifted transitions

A scalar chart transition from chart \(a\) to chart \(b\) is a local coordinate change

\[
x_b=\phi_{ab}(x_a),
\]

with the same scalar source represented as

\[
F_b(x_b)
=
F_a(\phi_{ab}^{-1}(x_b)).
\]

The exact jet transition is the finite Faà di Bruno pullback

\[
J_{\le9}^{(b)}F
=
\mathcal T_{a\to b}J_{\le9}^{(a)}F.
\]

If the carrier quotient is functorial under this jet transition, then the natural carrier lift is

\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{a\to b}
=
\pi_{\car}^{(b)}
\mathcal T_{a\to b}
s_{\car}^{(a)},
}
\]

where \(s_{\car}^{(a)}\) is a representative/splitting and \(\pi_{\car}^{(b)}\) is projection to the carrier quotient.

If this lift is independent of arbitrary splitting modulo nuisance, it is the correct natural lift.

---

# 3. Naive atlas loop is flat

For exact scalar coordinate changes,

\[
\phi_{\sing\to\ord}
\circ
\phi_{\tr\to\sing}
\circ
\phi_{\ord\to\tr}
=
\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Therefore the exact scalar jet transition satisfies

\[
\mathcal T_{\sing\to\ord}
\mathcal T_{\tr\to\sing}
\mathcal T_{\ord\to\tr}
=
\mathrm{Id}
\]

on scalar jets.

If the nuisance submodules are invariant under the transitions,

\[
\mathcal T_{a\to b}\mathcal N_{\car}^{(a)}
\subseteq
\mathcal N_{\car}^{(b)},
\]

then the induced quotient maps also compose to identity:

\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
=
\mathrm{Id}
\quad
\text{on }
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{(\ord)}.
}
\]

Hence

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}=0.
\]

This is an exact forbidden identity, but it annihilates everything, including D4.

So the natural lift gives:

\[
\boxed{
\text{global upper: Green;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 visibility: Red/rank zero.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Can a projected lift create nonzero rank?

Suppose instead that the lifted transition is defined by a projected or gauge-fixed connection

\[
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{a\to b}
=
\Pi_b\mathcal T_{a\to b}\iota_a,
\]

where \(\Pi_b\) discards or normalizes some nuisance/carrier components. Then the loop defect

\[
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-\mathrm{Id}
\]

can be nonzero.

On the live D4 plane, the finite rank test becomes

\[
\boxed{
M_{\forb}^{\atlas}
=
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}.
}
\]

Because \(L_{\clean}\) is invertible, the rank is

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\forb}^{\atlas}
=
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}
\big|_{\mathcal L_{\D4}}
\right).
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank two}
\iff
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}
\text{ is injective on the D4 carrier plane;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank one}
\iff
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}
\text{ sees one D4 carrier combination;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank zero}
\iff
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}
\text{ kills the live D4 plane.}
}
\]

But now the global forbidden upper is no longer automatic. For the actual global scalar source,

\[
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}V_{\glob}
\]

need not vanish. It measures the defect introduced by projection/gauge choices. To use it downstream, one must prove

\[
\boxed{
\|\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

That is a new atlas-flatness theorem.

So a nontrivial projected lift trades one problem for another:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero D4 rank requires nonfunctoriality;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonfunctoriality destroys automatic forbiddenness.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Scalar transitions alone are insufficient

A scalar atlas loop can provide only:

\[
\text{same scalar source represented in different coordinates}.
\]

Exact scalar consistency implies flatness. Therefore scalar transitions alone cannot produce a D4-visible forbidden identity.

To get a useful \(\Lambda\), one needs an additional structure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a carrier connection whose curvature is globally forbidden}
}
\]

but whose local curvature is exactly what D4 detects.

This is a stronger object than a coordinate transition map. It is a row/carrier connection over the branch atlas.

The desired theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Carrier Atlas Flatness Theorem.}
}
\]

There exist carrier-lifted transition maps

\[
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{a\to b}
\]

such that:

1. **global flatness / forbidden upper**

\[
\|\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C);
\]

2. **D4 visibility**

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)
\ge1;
\]

3. **nuisance stability**

\[
\widehat\Lambda_{\atlas}
\mathcal N_{\car}
=
O(Q^{-B})
\]

or exactly zero.

This is the precise finite downstream theorem needed.

---

# 6. Positive atomic version

A single global loop is still vulnerable to cancellation. A better version is a local plaquette family.

Let \(p\) index local overlaps/plaquettes of the branch atlas, and define

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}
=
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{C\to A}^{(p)}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{B\to C}^{(p)}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}^{(p)}
-\mathrm{Id}.
\]

Then a Type I forbidden upper would be

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_{\atlas,p}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is much stronger than a single nonlocal identity. It also matches extraction: local D4 certificates can be assigned to plaquettes.

The local D4 visibility condition becomes

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}E_{\car}^{(p)}L_{\clean}^{(p)}
\right)
\ge1
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set of plaquettes.

This is the best form of the atlas-loop idea.

---

# 7. Why this is different from \(M_A/M_C\)

The \(M_A/M_C\) identities are local algebraic connection identities inside the corrected carrier package. They are likely used to define or normalize the package, so they probably vanish on the D4 live plane.

The atlas loop would be external if its transition maps compare **different branch charts** of the same scalar source. That gives it a chance to see D4.

But if the carrier-lifted transition maps are constructed using the same local quotient relations that define \(M_A/M_C\), the loop may again be rank zero.

So the finite audit should check whether \(\widehat{\mathcal T}_{a\to b}\) is genuinely external to the local D4 carrier quotient, not a restatement of local connection closure.

---

# 8. Current no-go and conditional path

### No-go for the naive lift

\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\sing\to\ord}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\tr\to\sing}
\widetilde{\mathcal T}_{\ord\to\tr}
-\mathrm{Id}
=0
}
\]

on the exact functorial carrier quotient, hence

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
(\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean})=0.
}
\]

### Conditional path for a useful lift

One must construct a **nontrivial carrier connection** over the branch atlas satisfying:

\[
\boxed{
\text{global/plaquette flatness upper}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero D4 image.}
}
\]

This is not currently supplied by scalar branch transitions alone.

---

# 9. Deliverable theorem / missing theorem

The exact missing downstream input is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4-visible carrier atlas flatness.}
}
\]

There are local carrier transition maps

\[
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{a\to b}
\]

and local plaquette operators

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}
=
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{C\to A}^{(p)}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{B\to C}^{(p)}
\widehat{\mathcal T}_{A\to B}^{(p)}
-\mathrm{Id}
\]

such that:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p\|\Lambda_{\atlas,p}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C);
}
\]

and on the extracted good set,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\atlas,p}E_{\car}^{(p)}L_{\clean}^{(p)}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

If this theorem fails, the carrier-lifted atlas-loop route does not close finite D4.

---

# 10. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Natural functorial atlas lift: exact upper, rank zero.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Projected/gauge-fixed lift: possible nonzero rank, but forbidden upper becomes new theorem.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Useful route requires local plaquette/Type I carrier atlas flatness.}
}
\]

So Agent 1’s answer is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the naive }\Lambda_{\atlas}\text{ fails; a D4-visible carrier atlas connection is a new required structure.}
}
\]

This is not a small finite audit anymore. It is a precise new downstream theorem.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:56:58)

## Agent 1 — transition–singular carrier leakage audit

The current instruction asks whether the transition-to-singular carrier projection can leak the D4 live plane into discarded/nuisance singular directions, because this is the most plausible finite-D4 source for a nonzero atlas \(\Lambda\). The prompt explicitly identifies the block

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}:
W_{\tr}=\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}
\longrightarrow
\mathcal C_{\sing}
\]

as the decisive finite audit. 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The current singular branch does not yet define enough carrier structure to perform this audit.}
}
\]

Because Gate–1 has now been simplified to local scalar \(L^2\) energy, the singular branch no longer needs a carrier quotient for upstream purposes. That is good for Gate–1, but it means the downstream atlas lift lacks a defined singular carrier module

\[
M_{\sing}=W_{\sing}\oplus C_{\sing}.
\]

So the immediate result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition--singular leakage is neither Green nor Red; the singular carrier lift is missing.}
}
\]

However, once such a lift is specified, the audit becomes a clean finite projection-commutator calculation.

---

# 1. Transition live carriers

The transition live D4 plane should be represented as

\[
W_{\tr}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\}.
\]

These are the transition-chart versions of the same live carrier classes appearing in the clean D4 output

\[
v_m^{\live}
=
H(m)E_{\car}^{\tr}(m)
L_{\clean}^{\tr}(m)
\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T,
\]

and

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\det L_{\clean}\ne0
\]

on Gram-good patches.

Concretely:

\[
[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}
\]

is the transition representative of the order-seven carrier that receives the \(H\Gamma_G\) coefficient, and

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\]

is the transition representative of the order-nine mixed carrier that receives the \(H\Gamma_{G,2}\) coefficient.

The prompt’s D4 setup is precisely this active carrier-quotient row-energy formulation. 

---

# 2. Singular carrier lift is missing

The current singular branch has been repaired upstream by replacing raw sine diagnostics with actual scalar \(L^2\) source energy or projected canonical source energy. That avoids the old coefficient/tail problems, but it also means the singular chart is no longer carrying a defined finite D4 carrier quotient.

The singular branch currently supplies a scalar source object such as

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\]

and possibly projections

\[
P_{\sing,j}F_m.
\]

That is enough for Gate–1, but not enough for a D4 atlas lift. To compute

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW},
\]

we need a finite singular carrier module.

Minimal required structure:

\[
\boxed{
M_{\sing}=W_{\sing}\oplus C_{\sing},
}
\]

where

\[
W_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
[\mathsf C_7]^{\sing},
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\sing}
\right\}
\]

is the retained singular live carrier plane, and

\[
C_{\sing}
\]

is the discarded/nuisance/projection complement.

Without this decomposition, the map

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}:W_{\tr}\to C_{\sing}
\]

is undefined.

So the first finite audit result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a singular carrier quotient must be added if the atlas route is to be tested.}
}
\]

This is consistent with the broader update: branches should no longer define upstream Gate–1, but they may still define downstream carrier holonomy. 

---

# 3. Minimal singular carrier lift

The least invasive lift is:

\[
M_{\sing}
=
J_{\le 9}^{\sing}(F_m)/\mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

where \(J_{\le9}^{\sing}\) is the finite singular chart expansion of the scalar source through the orders relevant to D4, and \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) is the singular nuisance submodule.

Then define

\[
W_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
[\mathsf C_7]^{\sing},
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\sing}
\right\}.
\]

The projection

\[
P_{\sing}:M_{\sing}\to W_{\sing}
\]

defines the retained live part, and

\[
C_{\sing}=(I-P_{\sing})M_{\sing}
\]

is the leakage/discarded complement.

Then the exact transition map on representatives is

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}:M_{\tr}\to M_{\sing}.
\]

The desired block is

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
}
\]

Thus:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}([\mathsf C_i]^{\tr})
=
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr},
\qquad
i\in\{7,Y12\}.
\]

This is the finite computation to perform.

---

# 4. When is leakage automatically zero?

If the singular retained live plane is defined functorially by

\[
[\mathsf C_i]^{\sing}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr},
\qquad
i\in\{7,Y12\},
\]

then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}(W_{\tr})\subseteq W_{\sing}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}=0.
}
\]

This is the likely outcome if the singular carrier lift is chosen to be a complete order-\(7/9\) jet carrier quotient.

In that case, transition-to-singular transport is D4-functorial, and no atlas \(\Lambda\) is generated there.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{functorial singular carrier lift } \Rightarrow \text{rank zero}.
}
\]

This matches the known no-go for exact/full chart transitions: the exact scalar atlas loop is flat and rank-zero on D4. 

---

# 5. When can leakage be nonzero?

Leakage can occur only if the singular retained space \(W_{\sing}\) is **not** defined as the full image of \(W_{\tr}\).

For example, suppose the singular chart uses a finite projection basis

\[
P_{\sing}:L^2(I)\to V_{\sing}^{(R)}
\]

or a singular-mode carrier basis that does not retain the exact Taylor/order-\(7/9\) carrier images. Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}
\]

may decompose as

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}
=
[\mathsf C_i]^{\sing}
+
c_i^{\sing},
\qquad
c_i^{\sing}\in C_{\sing}.
\]

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}=c_i^{\sing}.
\]

So leakage is nonzero iff

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}\ne0
}
\]

for \(i=7\) or \(Y12\).

This can happen if:

1. the singular carrier basis is an \(L^2\)-projection basis rather than a Taylor-jet basis;
2. the singular quotient discards modes needed to represent order-\(7/9\) transition carriers;
3. the transition-to-singular coordinate map mixes order \(7/9\) carrier data into singular high modes or nuisance modes.

That is precisely why the transition–singular overlap is the most promising leakage site. 

---

# 6. Return map and holonomy

Leakage alone is not enough. The leaked component must return to a live carrier under the rest of the atlas loop.

Let

\[
C_{\sing}\xrightarrow{R_{\sing\to\ord}}W_{\ord}
\]

be the return block of the singular-to-ordinary transition, possibly after the intermediate chart if using a three-chart loop.

Then the first-order holonomy on \(W_{\tr}\) contains a term

\[
\boxed{
H_{\atlas}
\sim
R_{\sing\to\ord}
\,
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}.
}
\]

More fully, if

\[
P_b
\]

is the projection to the retained carrier part in chart \(b\), then the projected loop is

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}
=
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
P_{\sing}
T_{\tr\to\sing}
P_{\tr}
-
P_{\tr}.
\]

The leakage contribution comes from the omitted complement:

\[
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
P_{\tr}.
\]

Thus the finite rank test is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left[
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\big|_{W_{\tr}}
\right].
}
\]

If this rank is zero, transition–singular leakage does not return to the live plane, and the atlas loop still fails downstream.

If rank is one or two, finite D4 has a candidate forbidden channel.

---

# 7. Rank classification

Let

\[
L:W_{\tr}\to W_{\tr}
\]

denote the holonomy leakage operator

\[
L
=
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
\]

Then

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\]

has the same rank as

\[
L L_{\clean}
\]

on the two-dimensional D4 plane. Since

\[
L_{\clean}
\]

is invertible, the rank is simply

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}L.
}
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}L=2
\Rightarrow
\text{both }\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ are forbidden-visible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}L=1
\Rightarrow
\text{one linear combination }\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ is forbidden-visible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}L=0
\Rightarrow
\text{atlas D4 channel is dead.}
}
\]

This is the exact finite rank audit.

---

# 8. Present status

At present, because the singular branch has been recast as scalar \(L^2\)-Gate–1, I do not see a defined singular carrier quotient

\[
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{\sing}
\]

or retained live plane

\[
W_{\sing}
\]

in the active architecture.

So I cannot honestly claim

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}\ne0
\]

or

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}=0
\]

for the actual draft machinery.

The exact current status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular carrier lift missing;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition–singular leakage audit blocked until }W_{\sing},C_{\sing},P_{\sing}\text{ are defined.}
}
\]

---

# 9. Minimal missing structure

To proceed, define:

\[
\boxed{
M_{\sing}=J_{\le9}^{\sing}(q''_{\can})/\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

Choose a projection

\[
P_{\sing}:M_{\sing}\to W_{\sing}
\]

where

\[
W_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
[\mathsf C_7]^{\sing},
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\sing}
\right\}.
\]

Then compute:

\[
c_7^{\sing}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
c_{Y12}^{\sing}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

If

\[
c_7^{\sing}=c_{Y12}^{\sing}=0,
\]

then transition–singular has no D4-visible leakage.

If at least one is nonzero, compute the return matrix

\[
R_{\sing\to\tr}
=
P_{\tr}T_{\ord\to\tr}P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}
\big|_{C_{\sing}}
\]

and evaluate

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left[
R_{\sing\to\tr}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7^{\sing} & c_{Y12}^{\sing}
\end{pmatrix}
\right].
\]

That is the finite audit.

---

# 10. Final Agent 1 deliverable

\[
\boxed{
\text{No definitive leakage result yet: the singular carrier lift is undefined.}
}
\]

But the finite test is now exact:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
}
\]

and the atlas rank is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\big|_{W_{\tr}}
\right).
}
\]

The most likely outcomes are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If singular carrier lift is functorial: rank zero.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{If singular lift uses a non-functorial projection basis: possible rank one/two, but requires a new carrier-coherence upper.}
}
\]

So the next concrete step is to define the singular carrier lift. Without it, the atlas \(\Lambda\) route cannot be evaluated.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:57:45)

I’ll now try to define a minimal singular carrier lift rather than just declaring it missing. The key is whether the singular lift is a Taylor-jet truncation, which is functorial and D4-invisible, or an \(L^2\)-projection lift, which can leak but needs a new upper.

_Thought for 14s_

## Agent 1 — constructing candidate singular carrier lifts

We reached the point where the transition–singular leakage audit is blocked because the singular carrier lift is not defined. I now tested the two natural definitions.

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{A Taylor-jet singular lift is functorial and gives rank zero.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{An \(L^2\)-projection singular lift can give nonzero leakage, but its global upper is a new theorem.}
}
\]

So the atlas-\(\Lambda\) route has a concrete fork:

\[
\boxed{
\text{functorial lift} \Rightarrow \text{exact upper but D4-invisible;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{projection lift} \Rightarrow \text{possibly D4-visible but forbidden upper not automatic.}
}
\]

This is the same tension as before, but now expressed in a finite transition–singular model.

---

# 1. Model the transition–singular coordinate change

Let \(z\) be the transition coordinate and \(\theta\) the singular coordinate. Write the local transition as

\[
z=\phi(\theta),
\]

with

\[
\phi(0)=0,\qquad \phi'(0)\neq0
\]

in the overlap region. For a scalar source profile

\[
F(z),
\]

the singular representation is

\[
F^{\sing}(\theta)=F^{\tr}(\phi(\theta)).
\]

The exact jet transition is the Faà di Bruno map

\[
J_{\le N}^{\sing}F
=
\mathcal T_{\tr\to\sing}^{(N)}J_{\le N}^{\tr}F.
\]

For \(N=9\), this map is exact on Taylor jets through order nine.

The D4 live transition plane is modeled by two order components:

\[
W_{\tr}
=
\operatorname{span}\{z^7,z^9\}
\]

or, more invariantly,

\[
W_{\tr}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}.
\]

Under coordinate change,

\[
z^7=\phi(\theta)^7,
\qquad
z^9=\phi(\theta)^9.
\]

---

# 2. Taylor-jet lift: rank zero

Define the singular carrier module as the Taylor jet quotient

\[
M_{\sing}^{\mathrm{jet}}=J_{\le9}^{\sing}/\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{jet}},
\]

and define the singular live plane by exact images:

\[
[\mathsf C_7]^{\sing}
:=
J_{\le9}^{\sing}(\phi(\theta)^7),
\]

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\sing}
:=
J_{\le9}^{\sing}(\phi(\theta)^9).
\]

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\subseteq W_{\sing}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}|_{W_{\tr}}=0.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}=0.
}
\]

Consequently, the atlas holonomy contribution

\[
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\big|_{W_{\tr}}
\]

vanishes.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
(\Lambda_{\atlas}E_{\car}L_{\clean})=0
}
\]

for the Taylor-jet singular lift.

This is bad for downstream contradiction but expected: exact finite Taylor jets through the D4 order are functorial.

---

# 3. \(L^2\)-projection lift: possible leakage

Now define the singular carrier representation by an \(L^2\)-basis

\[
V_{\sing}^{(N)}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_0,\ldots,\Phi_N\}
\subset L^2(I_\theta),
\]

with projection

\[
P_{\sing}^{(N)}:L^2(I_\theta)\to V_{\sing}^{(N)}.
\]

Let the retained live singular plane be some fixed two-dimensional subspace

\[
W_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{w_7^{\sing},w_9^{\sing}\}
\subset V_{\sing}^{(N)}.
\]

Then the transition of a live carrier is

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}z^7
=
\phi(\theta)^7,
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}z^9
=
\phi(\theta)^9.
\]

The leakage is

\[
c_7^{\sing}
=
(I-P_{W_{\sing}})
P_{\sing}^{(N)}
\phi(\theta)^7,
\]

\[
c_9^{\sing}
=
(I-P_{W_{\sing}})
P_{\sing}^{(N)}
\phi(\theta)^9.
\]

Thus

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}\neq0
\]

iff at least one of these vectors is nonzero.

For a generic \(L^2\)-basis and a nonlinear \(\phi\), this is expected to be nonzero unless \(W_{\sing}\) is deliberately defined to contain the projected images of \(\phi^7\) and \(\phi^9\).

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(L^2\)-projection singular lifts can create D4-visible leakage.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Leakage criterion in finite matrix form

Let

\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
P_{\sing}^{(N)}\phi^7 & P_{\sing}^{(N)}\phi^9
\end{pmatrix}
\]

be an \(N+1\) by \(2\) matrix in the singular basis. Let

\[
P_W
\]

be the orthogonal projection onto the retained two-dimensional live space \(W_{\sing}\).

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}^{CW}
=
(I-P_W)A.
\]

The leakage rank is

\[
\boxed{
r_{\mathrm{leak}}
=
\operatorname{rank}\bigl((I-P_W)A\bigr).
}
\]

Cases:

\[
r_{\mathrm{leak}}=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{span}\{P\phi^7,P\phi^9\}\subseteq W_{\sing}.
\]

\[
r_{\mathrm{leak}}=1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{one independent leaked carrier combination.}
\]

\[
r_{\mathrm{leak}}=2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{both live D4 directions leak.}
\]

Because \(L_{\clean}\) is invertible, this is also the rank before return-map effects, up to the rank of the return block.

---

# 5. Return-map condition

Leakage alone is insufficient. The leaked vectors must return to a retained live carrier after completing the atlas loop.

Let

\[
R_{\sing\to\tr}:C_{\sing}\to W_{\tr}
\]

denote the return block

\[
R_{\sing\to\tr}
=
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
\big|_{C_{\sing}}.
\]

Then the loop-rank matrix is

\[
\boxed{
L_{\atlas}
=
R_{\sing\to\tr}(I-P_W)A.
}
\]

The D4 forbidden rank is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
L_{\atlas}L_{\clean}
\right)
=
\operatorname{rank}(L_{\atlas})
}
\]

because \(L_{\clean}\) is invertible.

Thus the final finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
R_{\sing\to\tr}(I-P_W)
\begin{pmatrix}
P\phi^7 & P\phi^9
\end{pmatrix}
\in\{0,1,2\}.
}
\]

This is a concrete finite-dimensional calculation once the singular projection basis and retained live plane are chosen.

---

# 6. The global upper problem

The \(L^2\)-projection lift is non-functorial. Therefore the exact scalar atlas identity no longer gives

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}V_{\glob}=0.
\]

Instead, for an actual global row/source \(F\), the atlas defect is a projection/gluing error:

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(N)}F
=
P_{\tr}
T_{\ord\to\tr}
P_{\ord}
T_{\sing\to\ord}
P_{\sing}
T_{\tr\to\sing}
P_{\tr}F
-
P_{\tr}F.
\]

This is small only if the finite projection system approximates the actual source well on chart overlaps.

So the required upper is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_{\atlas,p}^{(N)}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is a new projection-coherence theorem.

It is plausible only if actual global rows have effective bandwidth/regularity such that discarded singular components are small. But D4 leakage requires discarded singular components of \(\phi^7,\phi^9\) to be nonzero. The route therefore depends on a delicate separation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows have small discarded projection;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers have non-small discarded projection.}
}
\]

This is possible only if D4 carriers are precisely the obstruction to finite carrier gluing.

---

# 7. The key design choice

We must choose \(W_{\sing}\) and \(P_{\sing}^{(N)}\) so that:

1. actual rows are well approximated:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing}^{(N)})F_{\glob}^{\sing}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\|F_{\glob}^{\sing}\|^2;
\]

2. D4 carrier images are not fully retained:

\[
(I-P_W)P_{\sing}^{(N)}\phi^7
\quad\text{or}\quad
(I-P_W)P_{\sing}^{(N)}\phi^9
\]

is nonzero;

3. leaked components return to the live plane:

\[
R_{\sing\to\tr}(I-P_W)P_{\sing}^{(N)}\phi^i\neq0
\]

for \(i=7\) or \(9\).

This is exactly a **nonconforming projection design** problem.

---

# 8. Most likely finite outcome

If \(W_{\sing}\) is chosen naturally to retain the projected images of the D4 carriers, then leakage is zero.

If \(W_{\sing}\) is chosen naturally as low singular modes, and \(\phi^7,\phi^9\) have components outside those low modes, leakage is nonzero.

However, if \(N\) is large enough to approximate actual global rows well, it may also retain most of \(\phi^7,\phi^9\), making leakage small. To get both small global upper and visible D4, we need D4 carriers to be exactly in the “bad finite mode” region where actual rows have cancellation but local D4 does not.

That is not automatic.

So the status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero leakage is easy; useful forbidden leakage is hard.}
}
\]

---

# 9. Concrete next audit

Pick a singular basis, for example:

\[
\Phi_k(\theta)=\sin(k\theta)
\quad\text{or orthonormalized singular modes}.
\]

Compute the projection coefficients

\[
a_{k,7}=\langle \phi(\theta)^7,\Phi_k\rangle,
\]

\[
a_{k,9}=\langle \phi(\theta)^9,\Phi_k\rangle.
\]

Let \(W_{\sing}\) be the retained live plane. Then:

\[
c_7=(I-P_W)(a_{k,7})_k,
\qquad
c_9=(I-P_W)(a_{k,9})_k.
\]

If

\[
c_7=c_9=0,
\]

no leakage.

If not, compute the return coefficients into \(W_{\tr}\). In a symmetric basis, parity may force one carrier to leak and another not. This could naturally produce rank one.

So the practical finite audit is an explicit projection-coefficient calculation.

---

# 10. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{Taylor-jet singular lift: rank zero.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
L^2\text{-projection singular lift: possible rank one/two.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{But a projection lift requires a new global carrier-coherence upper.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Useful atlas \(\Lambda\) requires nonconforming projection design.}
}
\]

Thus the transition–singular carrier leakage route is not dead, but it is no longer a simple finite audit. It becomes a paired theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Projection-coherence upper for actual rows}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonzero D4 leakage through the same projection.}
}
\]

That is the exact opportunity and risk.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:59:40)

I’ll now test whether the projection-coherence upper and D4 leakage can coexist. The key is a spectral-gap design: actual rows must lie in a retained subspace, while D4 carrier images must have a controlled component in the discarded subspace.

_Thought for 4s_

## Agent 1 — projection-coherence versus D4 leakage

We are now testing whether a non-functorial singular projection can simultaneously satisfy:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows have small atlas projection defect}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers have nonzero atlas projection defect.}
}
\]

This is the core viability condition for a carrier-lifted atlas \(\Lambda\).

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The route is viable only if there is a spectral separation between actual rows and D4 carrier jets.}
}
\]

More precisely, we need a singular projection \(P_{\sing}\) such that:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})F_{\glob}^{\sing}\|^2
\ll Q^{-B}\|F_{\glob}^{\sing}\|^2
\]

for actual global canonical rows, but

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}\|^2
\]

for at least one live D4 carrier \(i\in\{7,Y12\}\).

That is possible only if D4 carrier images live in singular modes that are **not significantly occupied by actual global row data**.

So the atlas route becomes a **mode-separation theorem**.

---

# 1. Abstract projection setup

Let

\[
H_{\sing}=L^2(I_\theta)
\]

be the singular local Hilbert space. Choose a finite retained subspace

\[
V_{\sing}\subset H_{\sing}
\]

and projection

\[
P=P_{\sing}:H_{\sing}\to V_{\sing}.
\]

Let the discarded subspace be

\[
V_{\sing}^{\perp}.
\]

For an actual global row/source \(F\), the atlas defect is controlled by

\[
(I-P)F.
\]

For a D4 live carrier transported into the singular chart,

\[
c_i(\theta):=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr},
\qquad
i\in\{7,Y12\},
\]

the leakage is

\[
(I-P)c_i.
\]

The desired projection must satisfy:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
\|(I-P)F_m\|^2\,d\mu(m)
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

while

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-P)c_i\|^2\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for at least one \(i\).

This is a standard separation problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual row family lies near }V_{\sing},
\quad
\text{D4 carrier does not.}
}
\]

---

# 2. Why low-mode projection is the natural candidate

Suppose actual singular rows are low-bandwidth or smooth in \(\theta\). Then choose

\[
V_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_k:|k|\le K\}
\]

for a singular Fourier/sine basis.

Then for actual rows,

\[
\|(I-P_{\le K})F_m\|^2
\]

is a high-mode tail. If the actual singular source has effective bandwidth \(K\), then the atlas upper is plausible.

Now consider the D4 carrier images

\[
c_7(\theta)=\phi(\theta)^7,
\qquad
c_9(\theta)=\phi(\theta)^9
\]

or their carrier-normalized analogues. If \(\phi(\theta)\) is nonlinear or singular near the overlap, these functions may have high-mode tails even if actual global rows are smooth after regularization.

Then

\[
(I-P_{\le K})c_i
\]

can be nonzero.

So a low-mode singular projection is plausible.

But if \(K\) is too large, it captures \(c_7,c_9\) as well and leakage vanishes. If \(K\) is too small, actual rows are not approximated and the global upper fails.

Therefore we need a spectral window:

\[
\boxed{
K_{\actual}\ll K\ll K_{\D4}
}
\]

where \(K_{\actual}\) is the effective bandwidth of actual rows and \(K_{\D4}\) is the frequency scale at which D4 carrier images have nontrivial tail.

This is the precise spectral separation condition.

---

# 3. Potential problem: polynomial carriers are low frequency

If

\[
\phi(\theta)
\]

is analytic and nonsingular on the overlap, then

\[
\phi(\theta)^7,\quad \phi(\theta)^9
\]

are smooth analytic functions. Their Fourier/singular-mode tails decay exponentially.

Then for any \(K\) large enough to approximate actual rows well, it likely also approximates \(\phi^7,\phi^9\) well. Leakage becomes exponentially small:

\[
\|(I-P_{\le K})\phi^7\|^2\ll e^{-cK}.
\]

If \(K\asymp R\asymp \log Q/\log\log Q\), this is

\[
e^{-cR}=Q^{-c/\log\log Q},
\]

which is subpolynomial, not necessarily enough for a \(Q^{-A}\) lower leakage. If \(K\) is larger, leakage is worse.

Thus, for analytic nonsingular chart transitions, low-mode projection may not yield polynomial D4 leakage.

This is a serious warning:

\[
\boxed{
\text{analytic chart transitions tend to make D4 carriers low-complexity, hence projection-invisible.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Where leakage can still arise

Leakage can arise if the singular projection basis is not simply low Fourier modes of the transition coordinate. Three possibilities:

## 4.1 Singular basis adapted to nonlinear geometry

If

\[
V_{\sing}
\]

is adapted to singular sine modes

\[
\sin(k\Theta(\theta))
\]

rather than ordinary polynomials in \(\theta\), then a transition polynomial

\[
\phi(\theta)^7
\]

may not lie mostly in the low singular basis.

This can create leakage even for analytic \(\phi\).

The relevant quantity is the projection tail

\[
\sum_{k>K}
|\langle \phi^7,\Phi_k\rangle|^2.
\]

We need a lower bound:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{k>K}|\langle \phi^7,\Phi_k\rangle|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

## 4.2 Singular chart has a degenerating phase

If the singular coordinate satisfies

\[
\Theta(\theta)\sim \theta^\beta
\]

or has a turning/branch behavior, then smooth transition carriers may look oscillatory or non-polynomial in the singular basis.

This is exactly where transition-to-singular leakage is most plausible.

## 4.3 Projection discards quotient-relevant components

If the singular retained subspace \(W_{\sing}\) is not “all low modes,” but a quotient-selected carrier plane, then even low-mode components can leak into discarded quotient directions.

That is, leakage may be algebraic rather than high-frequency:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}
\]

may contain low singular modes, but not the particular two retained carrier combinations. Then

\[
(I-P_W)T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_i]^{\tr}\ne0.
\]

This is more promising than pure high-mode leakage.

---

# 5. Algebraic projection leakage is the better target

The finite carrier quotient is not necessarily an \(L^2\) low-mode truncation. It is a quotient by nuisance directions. Therefore \(P_{\sing}\) should be treated as a quotient projection:

\[
M_{\sing}=W_{\sing}\oplus C_{\sing},
\]

not merely as a low-pass filter.

Then the leakage condition is:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\not\subseteq W_{\sing}.
\]

This is an algebraic condition. It can hold even if all functions are smooth and low-frequency.

The atlas upper then requires actual global rows to satisfy

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}^{\tr}
\approx W_{\sing},
\]

i.e. actual rows obey the singular carrier constraint, while local D4 carrier perturbations violate it.

This is exactly a finite compatibility theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows satisfy singular carrier compatibility;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers violate singular carrier compatibility.}
}
\]

This is more plausible than spectral high-mode separation.

---

# 6. Reinterpretation: singular carrier compatibility identity

Define a singular compatibility functional

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}:M_{\sing}\to C_{\sing}
\]

by

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}=I-P_{\sing}.
\]

Then the transition–singular leakage is

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}v
\]

for \(v\in W_{\tr}\).

The global upper needed is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}^{\tr}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This says actual global row data transformed into the singular chart satisfy the singular compatibility constraints.

The D4 rank condition is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

This is a clean formulation.

The atlas loop may not even need a full three-chart loop initially. A two-chart compatibility residual could suffice if it has a global forbidden upper and D4 visibility.

So the candidate \(\Lambda\) becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\tr\sing}
=
\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}.
}
\]

If actual rows satisfy

\[
\Lambda_{\tr\sing}V_{\glob}=0
\]

or small, and D4 has nonzero image under \(\Lambda_{\tr\sing}\), this already gives a forbidden channel.

This is simpler than a full atlas loop.

---

# 7. Why a two-chart residual may be enough

Originally, we wanted an atlas loop because exact chart transitions compose to identity. But if one chart has intrinsic compatibility constraints, then a transition into that chart must land in the compatible subspace.

For actual global rows:

\[
V_{\glob}^{\sing}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}^{\tr}
\]

and

\[
V_{\glob}^{\sing}\in W_{\sing}
\]

or satisfies the singular carrier constraint. Hence

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}^{\tr}=0.
\]

For local D4 perturbations in the transition chart, this may fail.

Thus a **two-chart compatibility residual** is a valid forbidden identity if the singular chart has a genuine carrier compatibility condition.

This avoids requiring leakage to return around a full loop.

The finite D4 rank test becomes simply:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1.
}
\]

This is strictly easier than the loop-rank test.

---

# 8. Does this reopen the functorial no-go?

If \(W_{\sing}\) is defined as the full image of \(T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\), then

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}=0.
\]

So the two-chart residual is rank zero.

To avoid this, \(W_{\sing}\) must be defined independently by singular compatibility, not by transport of transition live carriers.

This is legitimate if the singular chart has an intrinsic carrier quotient coming from singular normal form, not merely from transition transport.

Therefore the next audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the singular normal form define intrinsic carrier constraints independent of transition transport?}
}
\]

If yes, \(\Lambda_{\tr\sing}\) is plausible.

If no, singular carrier lift is arbitrary and the upper is not meaningful.

---

# 9. Minimal theorem for two-chart \(\Lambda\)

The best new candidate is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Transition-to-singular carrier compatibility residual.}
}
\]

There exists a singular carrier constraint map

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}:M_{\sing}\to C_{\sing}
\]

such that:

1. **Global compatibility**

\[
\sum_p
\|\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}^{\tr}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. **D4 visibility**

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)
\ge1.
\]

3. **Locality / positivity**

The residuals are local atoms, so

\[
\sum_p\|\cdot\|^2
\]

has a positive Type I upper and avoids complement cancellation.

This is better than the full atlas-loop formulation.

---

# 10. Final updated opportunity

The previous route was:

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}
=
\text{three-chart holonomy}.
\]

The improved route is:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\tr\sing}
=
\text{two-chart singular compatibility residual}.
}
\]

This may be enough for downstream contradiction and is easier to audit.

The decisive finite object is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}
\big|_{\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}}.
}
\]

Rank one or two gives a viable forbidden wall factor. Rank zero kills this route.

So the next precise task is not to compute a full loop. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{define intrinsic singular carrier compatibility constraints } \mathcal K_{\sing}
}
\]

and test whether D4 live transition carriers violate them.

## Agent 1 — defining \(R_{\sing}\) and testing D4 transversality

The instruction asks Agent 1 to define the singular retained/coherence space \(R_{\sing}\), define the projection \(P_{\sing}\), and test whether

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}
\]

is nonzero. This is now the decisive finite-D4 downstream audit. 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The best candidate is not an EF atom-span projection, but an EF-coherence tangent projection.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}\text{ should be the local linearized coherence space of the actual truncated EF packet,}
}
\]

not the span of all truncated EF atoms and not the full Taylor jet space. With this choice, the singular-discard channel has a real chance to see D4.

The finite rank condition becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left[
(I-P_{R_{\sing}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\right]\ge 1.
}
\]

This is equivalent to saying:

\[
\boxed{
\text{at least one D4 carrier violates singular EF-coherence to polynomial scale.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Three possible definitions of \(R_{\sing}\)

## A. Taylor-complete singular space

Take

\[
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{jet}}
=
J_{\le 9}^{\sing}.
\]

Then every order-seven and order-nine transition carrier has an exact singular Taylor representation. Hence

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\subset R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{jet}},
\]

so

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}=0.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{jet}}\Rightarrow \operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}=0.
}
\]

This gives exact upper but no forbidden D4 channel.

---

## B. Full EF atom-span

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

and define

\[
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{atom}}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\text{all singular-chart atoms appearing in }F_X\}.
\]

Then actual rows lie in \(R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{atom}}\) up to the residual

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X,
\]

so the forbidden upper is plausible:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|
\lesssim
\|R_X\|.
\]

But this space is too large. Since the truncated EF contains many atoms, the span may approximate or contain the order-seven and order-nine algebraic carrier profiles. On a compact singular interval, a large exponential/oscillatory atom span can approximate polynomial-type carrier functions extremely well.

So this choice risks:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\subseteq R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{atom}}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\operatorname{dist}(T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr},R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{atom}})
\ll Q^{-A}.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{atom}}\Rightarrow \text{upper plausible, D4 visibility doubtful.}
}
\]

---

## C. EF-coherence tangent space

The better choice is the local linearized coherence space of the actual truncated EF packet.

Let the local singular source be

\[
F_{X,p}^{\sing}(x)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}F_X(x),
\]

where \(p\) denotes a local transition–singular overlap plaquette.

Define a finite-dimensional coherence tangent space

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing,p}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
F_{X,p}^{\sing},
\partial_{\tau_1}F_{X,p}^{\sing},
\ldots,
\partial_{\tau_s}F_{X,p}^{\sing}
\right\}
+
R_{\mathrm{bg},p}.
}
\]

Here \(\tau_i\) are legitimate local parameters of the coherent EF packet: center, scale, branch phase, truncation-smooth cutoff, and possibly \(\alpha\) if the \(\alpha\)-family is admitted. \(R_{\mathrm{bg},p}\) contains mandatory background/gamma coherent directions.

Then:

\[
P_{\sing,p}
=
\text{orthogonal projection onto }R_{\sing,p}.
\]

This is narrow enough that D4 carrier profiles may be transverse, but broad enough to give the actual-row upper via Taylor/coherence estimates.

This is the best candidate.

---

# 2. Forbidden upper with tangent \(R_{\sing}\)

For actual rows,

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X.
\]

After transition to the singular chart,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)
+
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

By construction,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)
\in R_{\sing,p}
\]

up to controlled linearization error. Therefore

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)
+
\mathrm{LinErr}_p.
\]

Thus the desired global upper follows from:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p\|\mathrm{LinErr}_p\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is exactly where partial EF residual/coherence estimates enter. The same residual theorem used for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\) can supply this upper.

So the upper is plausible if \(R_{\sing}\) is the EF-coherence tangent space.

---

# 3. D4 transversality condition

Let

\[
W_{\tr}
=
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}.
\]

Define

\[
K_{\D4,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}.
\]

Since

\[
L_{\clean}
\]

is invertible, the rank is controlled by

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
\]

Equivalently, compute the two leakage vectors:

\[
v_{7,p}^{\perp}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
v_{Y,p}^{\perp}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4,p}
=
\dim\operatorname{span}\{v_{7,p}^{\perp},v_{Y,p}^{\perp}\}.
}
\]

Rank cases:

\[
\boxed{
0:
v_{7,p}^{\perp}=v_{Y,p}^{\perp}=0.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
1:
v_{7,p}^{\perp},v_{Y,p}^{\perp}\text{ nonzero but dependent.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2:
v_{7,p}^{\perp},v_{Y,p}^{\perp}\text{ independent.}
}
\]

---

# 4. What would make rank zero?

Rank zero holds if both D4 carrier images lie in the EF-coherence tangent space:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}\in R_{\sing,p},
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\in R_{\sing,p}.
\]

This would mean the formal D4 carrier perturbations are indistinguishable from legitimate local variations of the truncated EF packet.

In that case, the singular-discard \(\Lambda\) fails:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is EF-coherent, hence not forbidden by singular coherence.}
}
\]

This is the main no-go possibility.

---

# 5. Why rank should plausibly be nonzero

The EF-coherence tangent space is generated by variations of the actual packet:

\[
F_X,\quad
\partial_mF_X,\quad
\partial_\lambda F_X,\quad
\partial_\alpha F_X,\quad
\text{cutoff variations},\quad
\text{background variations}.
\]

These directions preserve the explicit-formula structure: they vary phases, scales, and smooth amplitudes of the actual atoms.

The D4 carriers

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\quad [\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

are formal local carrier patterns produced by the downstream carrier quotient. They are algebraic obstruction directions, not obviously obtainable by differentiating the EF packet parameters.

So generically:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\not\subset R_{\sing,p}.
}
\]

The rank is likely at least one unless the D4 carriers were already absorbed as legitimate EF parameter variations.

This is now a meaningful finite question, not a vague global identity search.

---

# 6. The finite audit in matrix form

Choose a basis

\[
\{r_1,\ldots,r_s\}
\]

for \(R_{\sing,p}\), and let

\[
G_R=(\langle r_i,r_j\rangle)
\]

be its Gram matrix. Let

\[
c_7=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\qquad
c_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

The projection is

\[
P_R c
=
\sum_{i,j}r_i(G_R^{-1})_{ij}\langle c,r_j\rangle.
\]

The leakage Gram is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_p
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle c_7,(I-P_R)c_7\rangle
&
\langle c_7,(I-P_R)c_Y\rangle
\\
\langle c_Y,(I-P_R)c_7\rangle
&
\langle c_Y,(I-P_R)c_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4,p}
=
\operatorname{rank}\mathcal K_p.
}
\]

Polynomial D4 visibility requires

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\max}(\mathcal K_p)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set of plaquettes. Rank two requires

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(\mathcal K_p)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the explicit finite computation.

---

# 7. Forbidden wall factor

With this \(R_{\sing}\), define

\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

Then

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\left\|
K_{\D4,p}\Gamma(m)
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
},
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

More precisely,

\[
K_{\D4,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4,p}=1\), then the wall condition is a single moving linear form

\[
|\ell_p(\Gamma)|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If rank two, it is comparable to raw anisotropy

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

---

# 8. The resulting theorem

The correct downstream theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Forbidden EF-Coherence Singular Discard.}
}
\]

There exists a family of singular coherence spaces \(R_{\sing,p}\) such that:

1. **Actual-row coherence upper**

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

2. **D4 transversality**

\[
\lambda_{\max}
\left[
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle c_7,(I-P_R)c_7\rangle
&
\langle c_7,(I-P_R)c_Y\rangle\\
\langle c_Y,(I-P_R)c_7\rangle
&
\langle c_Y,(I-P_R)c_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}
\right]
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

3. **Locality**

The residuals are indexed by local plaquettes \(p\), so the upper is Type-I/atomic and complement cancellation is impossible.

If this theorem holds, finite D4 has a real forbidden channel.

---

# 9. Current status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Taylor-complete }R_{\sing}: \text{rank zero.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{full EF atom-span }R_{\sing}: \text{upper easy, D4 visibility doubtful.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{EF-coherence tangent }R_{\sing}: \text{best candidate.}
}
\]

The next concrete computation is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_p
=
\left(
\langle c_i,(I-P_{R_{\sing,p}})c_j\rangle
\right)_{i,j\in\{7,Y\}}.
}
\]

If this matrix is polynomially nonzero, the singular-discard route is viable. If it vanishes identically, finite D4 remains downstream-red.
