## Agent 1 — EF-coherence tangent transversality test

We now have the best candidate forbidden channel:

\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p},
\]

where \(P_{\sing,p}\) projects onto a singular EF-coherence space \(R_{\sing,p}\). The D4 visibility question is:

\[
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}
\neq0.
\]

I pushed this into an explicit annihilator test.

---

# 1. Work in the orthogonal annihilator

Let

\[
R:=R_{\sing,p}\subset H_{\sing}=L^2(I_\theta).
\]

Instead of projecting \(c_7,c_Y\) directly, look at the annihilator

\[
R^\perp.
\]

For

\[
c_7:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\qquad
c_Y:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr},
\]

D4 transversality is equivalent to the existence of

\[
\ell\in R^\perp
\]

such that

\[
\langle c_7,\ell\rangle\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\langle c_Y,\ell\rangle\ne0.
\]

Rank one means the two functionals

\[
\ell\mapsto \langle c_7,\ell\rangle,
\qquad
\ell\mapsto \langle c_Y,\ell\rangle
\]

span a one-dimensional image on \(R^\perp\). Rank two means they are independent.

So the finite leakage matrix is

\[
\mathcal K
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\|P_{R^\perp}c_7\|^2 & \langle P_{R^\perp}c_Y,P_{R^\perp}c_7\rangle\\
\langle P_{R^\perp}c_7,P_{R^\perp}c_Y\rangle & \|P_{R^\perp}c_Y\|^2
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

D4 visibility is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\max}(\mathcal K)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 2. What is \(R_{\sing}\)?

The proposed \(R_{\sing}\) is the EF-coherence tangent space:

\[
R_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
F_X,\partial_{\tau_1}F_X,\ldots,\partial_{\tau_s}F_X
\right\}
+
R_{\mathrm{bg}},
\]

where

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

in singular coordinates, and \(\tau_i\) are legitimate coherent EF parameters:

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \alpha,\quad \text{branch phase},\quad
\text{smooth cutoff parameters},\quad
\text{background/gamma parameters}.
\]

Thus \(R_{\sing}\) is generated by actual source deformations. Its annihilator consists of singular test functions \(\ell\) satisfying the moment conditions

\[
\langle \ell,F_X\rangle=0,
\]

\[
\langle \ell,\partial_{\tau_i}F_X\rangle=0
\qquad
(1\le i\le s),
\]

plus orthogonality to background coherent directions.

Therefore D4 visibility can be tested by solving:

\[
\boxed{
\ell\perp F_X,\ \ell\perp \partial_{\tau_i}F_X,\ \ell\perp R_{\mathrm{bg}},
\quad
\langle \ell,c_7\rangle\ne0\ \text{or}\ \langle \ell,c_Y\rangle\ne0.
}
\]

This is now a finite constrained moment problem.

---

# 3. Generic rank expectation

The EF-coherence tangent space has dimension roughly

\[
s+1+\dim R_{\mathrm{bg}},
\]

where \(s\) is the number of coherent parameters being allowed.

The ambient singular Hilbert space, after finite truncation to a basis of size \(M_{\sing}\), has dimension

\[
M_{\sing}.
\]

If

\[
M_{\sing}>s+2+\dim R_{\mathrm{bg}},
\]

then generically there are annihilators \(\ell\in R^\perp\) that see \(c_7\) or \(c_Y\). Thus rank zero is non-generic unless the D4 carriers lie in the EF tangent space for structural reasons.

So the generic expectation is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}\mathcal K\ge1.
}
\]

Rank zero would be a meaningful identity:

\[
c_7,c_Y\in R_{\sing}.
\]

That would say the formal D4 carriers are actual coherent deformations of the truncated EF packet. This seems unlikely, but it must be checked.

---

# 4. Algebraic rank-zero criterion

Rank zero is equivalent to the existence of coefficients

\[
a_0,a_1,\ldots,a_s,\quad b_j
\]

such that

\[
c_7
=
a_0F_X+\sum_i a_i\partial_{\tau_i}F_X+\sum_j b_j r_j
\]

and similarly for \(c_Y\), where \(r_j\) span \(R_{\mathrm{bg}}\).

Thus the rank-zero condition is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{F_X,\partial_{\tau_i}F_X,R_{\mathrm{bg}}\}.
}
\]

This is very strong.

The right way to disprove it is to find a singular invariant that vanishes on coherent EF tangents but not on \(c_7\) or \(c_Y\).

---

# 5. Candidate singular invariants

We need a functional

\[
\ell\in H_{\sing}^*
\]

with

\[
\ell(F_X)=0,
\qquad
\ell(\partial_{\tau_i}F_X)=0,
\]

but

\[
\ell(c_7)\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\ell(c_Y)\ne0.
\]

Natural candidates:

## 5.1 High-mode singular moments

If \(F_X\) and its coherent derivatives are effectively low-bandwidth in the singular basis, then high singular modes annihilate \(R_{\sing}\). D4 carriers may have nonzero high-mode projection.

Functional:

\[
\ell_k(f)=\langle f,\Phi_k\rangle,
\qquad k>K.
\]

Condition:

\[
|\ell_k(F_X)|+|\ell_k(\partial_{\tau_i}F_X)|\le Q^{-B},
\]

but

\[
|\ell_k(c_7)|+|\ell_k(c_Y)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives a direct high-mode forbidden channel.

Risk: if \(c_7,c_Y\) are also low-bandwidth, this fails.

## 5.2 Parity functional

If singular EF-coherent directions satisfy a parity rule, e.g.

\[
F_X(\theta)\in H_{\mathrm{even}}
\]

or a fixed parity combination, while D4 carrier images contain opposite parity, then the opposite-parity projection is forbidden.

Functional:

\[
\ell=P_{\mathrm{odd}}
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\mathrm{even}}.
\]

This is promising because D4 order \(7\) and \(9\) are odd-order carriers; singular sine/cosine bases may impose parity constraints.

Need test:

\[
P_{\mathrm{forb}}F_X\approx0,
\qquad
P_{\mathrm{forb}}c_7\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\mathrm{forb}}c_Y\ne0.
\]

## 5.3 Moment-vanishing functional

If EF coherence imposes exact moment relations

\[
\int F_X(\theta)w_j(\theta)d\theta=0
\]

for certain weights \(w_j\), then any violation by D4 is forbidden.

This is essentially a finite singular compatibility condition.

## 5.4 Endpoint-jet functional

If actual coherent singular sources obey endpoint matching or regularity constraints, e.g.

\[
F_X^{(r)}(\partial I)=0
\]

after smoothing, but transformed D4 carriers violate them, endpoint jets give a forbidden functional.

This is plausible if singular charts impose boundary/gluing regularity.

---

# 6. Best subattack: parity/grade reflection

From earlier local algebra, the project already has source-grade reflection and parity tools. The D4 carriers are order \(7\) and order \(9\); the singular EF modes often separate sine/cosine or odd/even moment classes.

So the most promising finite invariant is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular parity/grade projection.}
}
\]

Try to prove:

\[
P_{\mathrm{bad}}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\approx0
\]

for actual rows, by source-grade reflection or complement parity, but

\[
P_{\mathrm{bad}}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}\ne0
\]

or

\[
P_{\mathrm{bad}}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\ne0.
\]

If true, this gives a clean rank-one \(\Lambda\).

This would also explain why the transition–singular channel is special: singular charts expose parity/grade coherence that transition carriers may violate.

---

# 7. Relation to \(M_A/M_C\)

The previous \(M_A/M_C\) identities likely vanish on D4 if used directly as \(\Lambda\). But their parity logic may still identify the singular coherent subspace.

So \(M_A/M_C\) can help define \(R_{\sing}\) or \(P_{\mathrm{bad}}\), even if it is not itself the forbidden identity.

This is a useful role reassignment:

\[
\boxed{
M_A/M_C\text{ as singular coherence constraints, not final }\Lambda.
}
\]

---

# 8. The finite theorem to prove next

Define a singular forbidden projection

\[
P_{\forb}:H_{\sing}\to H_{\forb}\subset R_{\sing}^{\perp}
\]

by one of:

\[
P_{\forb}=P_{\mathrm{high}},
\quad
P_{\mathrm{parity}},
\quad
P_{\mathrm{moment}},
\quad
P_{\mathrm{endpoint}}.
\]

Then prove:

## Actual-row upper

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|P_{\forb,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

## D4 visibility

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
P_{\forb,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

This avoids projecting onto the full \(R_{\sing}\); it is enough to find one explicit forbidden component.

---

# 9. Current best candidate

My ranking of candidate forbidden projections:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \text{parity/grade projection;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \text{endpoint/gluing compatibility projection;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \text{high-mode projection;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \text{generic EF-tangent annihilator.}
}
\]

Parity/grade is best because the project already has parity infrastructure, and it may give exact or near-exact upper for actual rows.

High-mode is more analytic and risks small D4 leakage.

Generic EF-tangent annihilator is flexible but harder to make canonical and prove upper.

---

# 10. Final status

The singular-discard route is now sharpened:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not try to construct all of }R_{\sing}\text{ first.}
}
\]

Instead:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find one explicit forbidden singular projection }P_{\forb}\subset R_{\sing}^{\perp}.
}
\]

The decisive finite question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\forb}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}
\neq0
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\forb}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\neq0.
}
\]

The most promising \(P_{\forb}\) is a singular parity/grade projection.

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-grade parity relative to singular chart compatibility is still plausible.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Naive parity is probably not enough

Let the singular coordinate be \(\theta\), and suppose the singular basis separates into

\[
H_{\sing}=H_{\even}\oplus H_{\odd}.
\]

A naive forbidden projection would be

\[
P_{\forb}=P_{\odd}
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\even}.
\]

But \(q''_{\can}\) is not obviously pure even or pure odd in the singular coordinate. The canonical source includes

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

and the prime/folded-dual pieces generally mix parity after transition-to-singular coordinates.

So a theorem like

\[
P_{\odd}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})=0
\]

is too strong unless the singular normal form imposes an exact symmetry.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary parity alone is unlikely to give the forbidden upper.}
}
\]

---

# 2. Source-grade parity is sharper

The existing local algebra uses source-grade reflection of the form

\[
P_g\circ S = (-1)^g S\circ P_g
\]

and related transpose/parity identities. In the earlier connection-table context, this gave identities like

\[
(Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle})^T
=
(-1)^{g+e}Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle}.
\]

That is more structured than simple \(\theta\mapsto-\theta\) parity. It classifies terms by **source grade** and **edge/slot parity**.

So the candidate forbidden projection should be:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\forb}=P_{\text{wrong source-grade/singular-parity sector}}.
}
\]

The actual EF-coherent source rows are expected to lie in allowed sectors determined by the canonical source-grade reflection rules. A formal D4 carrier may live partly in a forbidden sector.

This gives a plausible upper:

\[
P_{\forb}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\approx0
\]

because actual rows obey source-grade coherence.

---

# 3. What sector should be forbidden?

The D4 live carriers are:

\[
[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Their orders are \(7\) and \(9\)-type in the D4 carrier channel, but order alone does not determine forbiddenness. The relevant grade should combine:

\[
\text{source grade }g,
\quad
\text{edge/whitening parity }e,
\quad
\text{singular mode parity},
\quad
\text{carrier order }b\in\{7,9\}.
\]

A natural test is:

\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\forb}^{(b)}
=
\pi_{\text{singular parity } \not\equiv b+\text{source grade rule}}
}
\]

and compute

\[
\pi_{\forb}^{(7)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
\pi_{\forb}^{(9)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

If either is nonzero, then the forbidden wall sees D4.

---

# 4. Rank-zero criterion

Let

\[
c_7=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\qquad
c_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

Let

\[
P_{\forb}=P_{\wronggrade}.
\]

Then the singular-discard rank is determined by

\[
K_{\D4}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
P_{\forb}c_7 & P_{\forb}c_Y
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Rank zero iff

\[
P_{\forb}c_7=0,
\qquad
P_{\forb}c_Y=0.
\]

That means both D4 carriers obey the same singular source-grade coherence as actual EF rows.

Rank one iff

\[
P_{\forb}c_7,\ P_{\forb}c_Y
\]

are nonzero but dependent.

Rank two iff they are independent.

The exact forbidden wall factor becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
=
\frac{
|H|^2\,
\left\|
K_{\D4}L_{\clean}\Gamma
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

Since \(L_{\clean}\) is invertible, the rank is entirely the rank of \(K_{\D4}\).

---

# 5. Why source-grade projection may give the upper

For actual rows, the transition-to-singular source is

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can}).
\]

The canonical source is built from the explicit formula and its regularized dual/background/tail completion. If the completion is coherent, every term should satisfy the singular source-grade compatibility rules.

Thus the upper would be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
P_{\wronggrade,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This can be proved from:

1. exact source-grade reflection for the coherent finite EF packet \(F_X\);
2. residual smallness

\[
q''_{\can}-F_X=R_X;
\]

3. boundedness of \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) on the relevant norm.

That is exactly the EF-coherence mechanism already needed upstream.

So source-grade projection has the best chance of being a Type-I forbidden upper.

---

# 6. Why D4 might violate source-grade coherence

The live D4 carriers are not arbitrary EF source terms. They are downstream carrier quotient outputs generated by wall escape/D4 row conversion. In particular:

\[
[\mathsf C_7]
\]

comes from the order-seven carrier response to \(\Gamma_G\), and

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

from the order-nine mixed \(Y12\)-type carrier response to \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

These are formal carrier modes. They need not be realized by a coherent EF source perturbation. If a D4 carrier lies in a wrong source-grade sector, it is forbidden.

So the desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers are not singular-source-grade coherent.}
}
\]

That is exactly the downstream contradiction we want.

---

# 7. Concrete finite test

Choose a singular graded basis

\[
H_{\sing}
=
\bigoplus_{\chi}H_\chi,
\]

where \(\chi\) records the relevant source-grade/singular-parity label.

Let

\[
\chi_{\coh}
\]

be the set of labels allowed for actual EF-coherent source rows.

Define

\[
P_{\forb}
=
\sum_{\chi\notin\chi_{\coh}}P_\chi.
\]

Then compute the two vectors:

\[
d_7=P_{\forb}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
d_Y=P_{\forb}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

The audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\|d_7\|^2+\|d_Y\|^2\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight region.

This is now a precise finite symbolic computation once the singular graded basis and \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) are specified.

---

# 8. Possible obstruction: D4 carriers may be deliberately source-grade coherent

There is a serious risk.

The D4 carriers \([\mathsf C_7]\) and \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) were constructed inside the same corrected local algebra that respects source-grade reflection and complement parity. If the carrier quotient was built after enforcing these symmetries, the live carriers may already be source-grade coherent.

Then

\[
P_{\forb}c_7=P_{\forb}c_Y=0,
\]

and this route is rank zero.

So the parity/grade route is promising but not guaranteed. It hinges on whether D4 live carriers are **formal local carrier modes outside EF-coherent source grade**, or whether they are already allowed EF-grade modes.

This is the next finite audit.

---

# 9. Alternative forbidden sector: source-grade derivative mismatch

If simple source-grade projection kills nothing, try a more refined compatibility condition:

Actual EF coherent rows may satisfy not only sector membership but a **connection relation** between adjacent singular grades:

\[
Z_{\chi+1}
=
\mathcal D_\sing Z_\chi
+
\text{known lower terms}.
\]

A D4 carrier could satisfy the right grade but violate the connection relation.

Then define

\[
P_{\forb}
\]

as the residual of this connection:

\[
P_{\forb}F
=
Z_{\chi+1}
-
\mathcal D_\sing Z_\chi
-
\text{known terms}.
\]

Actual rows have small residual by EF coherence. D4 carriers may produce nonzero residual.

This may be more powerful than pure parity.

The finite test becomes:

\[
P_{\conn}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}
\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
P_{\conn}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\ne0.
\]

This is a “singular connection compatibility” forbidden channel.

---

# 10. Current best candidate

Ranking:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \text{singular source-grade/connection residual;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \text{pure source-grade parity projection;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \text{high-mode projection;}
}
\]

The connection residual is best because D4 may preserve grade but violate coherent EF evolution between grades.

---

# 11. Updated theorem target

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Forbidden Singular Coherence Residual.}
}
\]

There is a finite singular compatibility operator

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}:H_{\sing}\to H_{\forb}
\]

such that:

## Actual-row upper

\[
\sum_p
\left\|
\mathcal K_{\sing,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## D4 visibility

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\mathcal K_{\sing,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

Here \(\mathcal K_{\sing}\) can be:

\[
P_{\wronggrade},
\]

or better,

\[
P_{\conn}=\text{singular source-grade connection residual}.
\]

This is the strongest current formulation of the singular-discard \(\Lambda\).

---

# 12. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Plain parity is probably too crude.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Source-grade parity is plausible.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Source-grade connection residual is the best candidate.}
}
\]

The next concrete step is to compute

\[
\mathcal K_{\sing}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\qquad
\mathcal K_{\sing}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\]

for the singular source-grade compatibility operator \(\mathcal K_{\sing}\).

## Assistant (2026-04-28 04:08:09)

I’ll move from parity to a sharper compatibility operator: a singular source cannot choose its order-7 and order-9 carrier coefficients independently. D4 precisely creates independent order-7/order-9 live directions, so the likely forbidden residual is a connection relation between those orders.

_Thought for 21s_

## Agent 1 — singular source-connection residual attack

The parity projection was too crude. The better forbidden operator is a **connection residual** between singular source orders.

The key observation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{an actual singular EF source cannot choose its order-7 and order-9 carrier data independently.}
}
\]

But the clean D4 live output has precisely two independent carrier coordinates:

\[
[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

So the best candidate \(\Lambda\) is not a raw parity projection. It is a residual measuring whether the order-nine singular coefficient is the coherent continuation of the order-seven singular coefficient.

---

# 1. Singular coherent-source graph

Let the singular chart expansion of the actual canonical source be

\[
F_m^{\sing}(x)
=
\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
=
\sum_{j\ge 0}s_j(m)\Phi_j(x),
\]

where \(\Phi_j\) is a singular source basis.

An actual EF-coherent source should satisfy finite compatibility relations among the coefficients. At the order relevant to D4, write the coherence relation schematically as

\[
\boxed{
s_9
=
\mathcal R_{7\to 9}s_7
+
\mathcal R_{<7\to 9}(s_{<7})
+
\operatorname{Tail}_9.
}
\]

Equivalently, define the singular connection residual

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(s)
:=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(s_{<7}).
}
\]

For actual canonical rows,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9\bigl(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Tail}_9,
}
\]

which should be small under the partial-EF coherence theorem.

This is the promised forbidden upper:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\mathcal K_{9,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is stronger and more structured than saying “wrong parity.”

---

# 2. D4 live output under the residual

The clean D4 carrier output has the form

\[
v_m^{\live}
=
H(m)E_{\car}^{\tr}(m)
L_{\clean}^{\tr}(m)
\Gamma(m),
\]

with

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\alpha_9\ne0,\quad c_7\ne0.
\]

In carrier coordinates, this means

\[
\delta s_7
=
Hc_7\Gamma_G[\mathsf C_7],
\]

and

\[
\delta s_9
=
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

up to the chosen singular transition map.

Apply the singular residual:

\[
\mathcal K_9(\delta s)
=
H
\left[
(\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7]))\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

So the forbidden D4 matrix is

\[
\boxed{
K_{\D4}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
&
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

Because \(\alpha_9\ne0\), this route is rank at least one if

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\notin\ker \mathcal K_9.
}
\]

Equivalently, if a pure \(Y12\) order-nine carrier is not itself an allowed coherent order-nine continuation.

This is the cleanest finite D4 visibility test so far.

---

# 3. Rank criteria

The rank of the forbidden wall map is the rank of

\[
K_{\D4}:\mathbb C^2\to H_{\forb}.
\]

### Rank zero

Rank zero iff

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0
\quad\text{in the residual codomain}
\]

and

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7]).
\]

In words:

\[
\boxed{
\text{both D4 carriers are coherent singular EF continuations.}
}
\]

Then singular-discard D4 fails.

### Rank one

Rank one iff

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0
\]

but

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\]

is proportional to \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

Then the forbidden wall sees one linear combination

\[
\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

### Rank two

Rank two iff

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

and

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\]

are independent in the residual codomain.

Then raw two-dimensional anisotropy in

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

is forbidden-visible.

---

# 4. Why this is better than parity

Parity projection asks:

\[
\text{is the carrier in a forbidden sector?}
\]

The connection residual asks:

\[
\boxed{
\text{is the order-nine carrier the coherent continuation of the lower singular data?}
}
\]

This catches more cases.

Even if \([\mathsf C_7]\) and \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) both have the correct source-grade parity, they may violate the connection relation

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\cdots.
\]

So this can be nonzero even when raw parity leakage is zero.

That makes the source-connection residual the stronger candidate.

---

# 5. Actual-row upper

For the actual source,

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

If the truncated coherent packet \(F_X\) satisfies the singular connection exactly,

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X))=0,
\]

then

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can}))
=
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)).
\]

Thus the global forbidden upper follows from the partial-EF residual estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|
\mathcal K_{9,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is exactly the desired reuse of partial EF residuals:

\[
\boxed{
\text{partial EF coherence proves both Gate--1 completion and singular-discard upper.}
}
\]

---

# 6. The finite theorem to prove

The singular-discard D4 route now reduces to the following finite theorem.

## Singular connection theorem

There exists a singular coefficient connection

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
\]

and residual

\[
\mathcal K_9=s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7}
\]

such that:

### Actual coherence

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X))=0
\]

for the coherent truncated EF packet \(F_X\).

### Residual smallness

\[
\sum_p
\|
\mathcal K_{9,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

### D4 transversality

\[
\lambda_{\max}
\left(
K_{\D4,p}^*K_{\D4,p}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set, where

\[
K_{\D4,p}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
&
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

If these hold, finite D4 has a Type-I forbidden channel.

---

# 7. What is \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\)?

This is now the key finite construction.

Possible origins:

## 7.1 Singular EF recurrence

The explicit formula source may imply a recurrence among local singular coefficients. For example, if \(F_X\) solves a local singular ODE or transport equation, then higher coefficients are determined by lower coefficients.

Then \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) is a recurrence coefficient.

## 7.2 Chart transition connection

Transition-to-singular coordinate change may impose a deterministic relation between order-seven and order-nine singular carrier coefficients for coherent transition data.

Then \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) is a connection coefficient of the chart transition.

## 7.3 Normal-form constraint

The singular normal form may set certain resonant order-nine coefficients as functions of lower-order coefficients after gauge fixing.

Then \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) is a normal-form compatibility map.

Any of these is acceptable, as long as actual rows satisfy it and D4 carriers need not.

---

# 8. Expected outcome

The \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is especially promising.

A pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) D4 output contributes to order nine through

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

with no associated order-seven input.

If coherent singular sources require order-nine carrier data to be tied to lower-order data, then a pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) injection should violate coherence.

Thus I expect at least rank one:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\ne0.
}
\]

If this is true, the forbidden wall factor becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\sim
\frac{
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}
\]

up to rank-one mixing.

This would be a major win.

---

# 9. Main risk

The risk is that \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is not forbidden. It may already be the coherent order-nine response to a legitimate singular source deformation.

If

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in \operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{7\to9}
\]

or lies in the allowed independent order-nine EF tangent space, then

\[
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=0.
\]

Then rank may be zero.

So the finite audit must determine whether coherent EF tangent space contains an **independent order-nine direction** matching \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\). If it does, this route fails.

---

# 10. Immediate next computation

Compute in the singular normal form:

1. the coherent coefficient relation between order seven and order nine:

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\cdots;
\]

2. the singular images of the D4 carriers:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}];
\]

3. the residuals:

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]),
\]

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

The decisive test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\ne0?
}
\]

If yes, finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

If no, test whether the \(\Gamma_G\)-combination gives rank one:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
)\ne0?
}
\]

If both vanish, the singular-discard route is dead.

---

# 11. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Best candidate }\Lambda:
\text{ singular order-nine connection residual.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Most promising D4-visible channel:
the }\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ / }[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ order-nine injection.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{

}

This is now a finite, explicit downstream audit rather than a vague atlas-loop search.

There are a few additional connections worth isolating.

## 1. \(C_{Y12}\) is now the best single carrier to attack

The whole downstream question can be narrowed further than “does D4 see \(\Lambda\)?”

The best test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0.
}
\]

Reason: the clean D4 matrix has an independent order-nine channel

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2},
\qquad \alpha_9\ne0.
\]

If the singular actual-source coherence relation determines order-nine data from lower-order singular data, then a pure \(Y12\)-order-nine injection is the most likely normal displacement. So before auditing the whole two-dimensional D4 plane, test \(C_{Y12}\) alone.

If this is nonzero, rank one is already enough.

---

## 2. The “actual-source upgrade” and finite D4 contradiction have become the same question

Previously, “actual-source realization” sounded upstream: do the formal Gate/D4 objects really come from the actual zeta source?

Now the downstream contradiction asks the dual question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is the D4 carrier tangent to the actual-source EF manifold or normal to it?}
}
\]

If tangent, D4 is not forbidden.  
If normal, D4 is a real obstruction.

So the actual-source upgrade is no longer just an input validation issue. It is the contradiction interface.

The finite test is:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\mathbb C^2
\subseteq
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\quad ?
\]

If yes, D4 is tangent. If no, D4 supplies forbidden normal energy.

---

## 3. \(\alpha\)-admissibility may hurt downstream

\(\alpha\)-twisting helps determinant branch survival, but it enlarges the actual-source manifold:

\[
\partial_\alpha u
\in T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

So adding \(\alpha\) as an admissible source-family parameter can make it easier for D4 to become tangent.

The key finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\alpha u,\partial_m u,\partial_\lambda u,\ldots\}
\quad ?
}
\]

If yes, \(\alpha\)-twisting may rescue O2/O3 but kill the D4 forbidden channel.

This is a major tradeoff. Alpha should not be admitted into the actual-source tangent space until this check is done.

---

## 4. EF completion must be stronger for downstream than for Gate–1

Gate–1 only needs:

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2 \ll \|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2.
\]

The singular-discard upper needs something like:

\[
\sum_p
\left\|
\mathcal K_{\sing,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right\|^2
\ll Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

That is a stronger norm: transition-to-singular jet/coherence norm, not plain \(L^2\).

So partial EF residuals must be split into two named estimates:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}}
\]

for Gate–1, and

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\tr\to\sing\text{-coh}}}
\]

for the forbidden upper.

The second may be the harder one.

---

## 5. A tubular-radius condition is now necessary

If D4 is normal to the actual-source manifold at first order, a nonlinear nearby source parameter shift could still absorb it unless the manifold has a polynomial tubular radius.

The needed estimate is:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
\ge
Q^{-C}\|\Pi^\perp D\|
\]

for D4-sized \(D\), or equivalently a polynomial bound on the second fundamental form of \(\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\).

So downstream needs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Tubular}_{\sing}^{\arith}
}
\]

in addition to rank nonzero. This is a finite/analytic geometry condition on the EF parameterization.

---

## 6. Gate–1 anti-lock is the degree-one version of determinant survival

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem contains a finite AFE anti-lock statement:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\not\approx 0
\quad\text{in }L^2.
\]

O2/O3 determinant survival is the exterior-degree analogue:

\[
D_{\dom}
\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_\beta:\beta\neq\dom\}.
\]

Same branch-charge logic, different degree.

That suggests proving a toy theorem first:

\[
\int |P+e^{i\Theta}D+B|^2W
\ge Q^{-A}\left(\|P\|^2+\|D\|^2+\|B\|^2\right)
\]

under smooth branch weights, then attempt the exterior version. This may yield reusable estimates for both Gate–1 and O2/O3.

---

\boxed{
\text{Construct }\mathcal K_9\text{ and compute }
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
}

Do not start with the full \(R_{\sing}\). Do not start with all carriers. Start with the order-nine residual and \(C_{Y12}\).

## Agent 1 — constructing \(\mathcal K_9\) correctly

The order-nine residual idea is still the best downstream candidate, but the naive form

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\cdots
\]

is too optimistic unless the actual EF source family has genuinely low-dimensional local freedom. The correct object is not a universal recurrence among Taylor coefficients. It is a **normal residual to the actual-source tangent space**. This matches the latest coordinator update: finite D4 should be viewed as normal displacement from the actual EF source manifold, not merely as row energy. 

---

# 1. Naive coefficient recurrence is false for arbitrary EF atoms

In a singular chart, write the truncated coherent source as

\[
F_X(x;m)=\sum_{n\le X} a_n(m)e^{i\tau_n(m)x}+B_2(m,x)+D_{2,\le X}(m,x).
\]

Ignoring background for a moment, the Taylor coefficients are moments

\[
s_k(m)=\frac{i^k}{k!}\sum_{n\le X}a_n(m)\tau_n(m)^k.
\]

If the amplitudes \(a_n\) were allowed to vary freely, then the moment vector

\[
(s_0,s_1,\ldots,s_9)
\]

has many independent degrees of freedom. In that setting there is no relation of the form

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(s_{<7}).
\]

So the first correction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not define }R_{\sing}\text{ as an arbitrary finite EF atom span.}
}
\]

That span is too large and will likely contain the D4 order-nine direction.

---

# 2. Actual-source manifold has few admissible directions

The actual source does **not** allow arbitrary variations of \(a_n\). Locally it is determined by a small list of structural parameters:

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \alpha\ \text{if admitted},\quad \text{phase/gauge},\quad \text{smooth cutoff/truncation parameters},
\]

plus fixed formula data.

Thus define an actual-source manifold

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\left\{
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta): \theta\in\Theta
\right\}
\subset H_{\sing}.
\]

At a point \(u_p\in\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\), its tangent space is

\[
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)\big|_{\theta=\theta_p}
\right\}_j.
\]

Now D4 visibility is the normal projection

\[
\Pi_{N_p}
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\mathbb C^2,
\]

where

\[
N_p=\left(T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\right)^\perp.
\]

So the real theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Pi_{N_p}
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

This is the invariant replacement for the naive \(s_9\)-recurrence.

---

# 3. How \(\mathcal K_9\) arises from the tangent space

Choose singular coordinates through order nine:

\[
s=(s_0,s_1,\ldots,s_9).
\]

Let the tangent matrix be

\[
\mathcal T_p
=
\left(
\partial_{\theta_j}s_k
\right)_{0\le k\le 9,\ j}.
\]

A normal functional is a row vector

\[
\ell=(\ell_0,\ldots,\ell_9)
\]

such that

\[
\ell\,\mathcal T_p=0.
\]

If we can choose \(\ell\) with a nonzero \(s_9\)-coefficient, then it has the form

\[
\ell(s)
=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(s_{<7}),
\]

after solving for the lower coefficients. This is the rigorous meaning of

\[
\mathcal K_9.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9
\text{ is not a universal recurrence; it is a left-null functional of the actual-source tangent matrix.}
}
\]

This is much safer.

---

# 4. D4 test in tangent-normal form

The D4 live displacement in singular coefficient coordinates is

\[
d_{\D4}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\Gamma,
\qquad
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

Write the two columns as

\[
d_G
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix},
\]

\[
d_{G2}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The clean matrix gives

\[
d_G
\sim
\beta_9\,T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
+
c_7\,T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

and

\[
d_{G2}
\sim
\alpha_9\,T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\qquad \alpha_9\ne0.
\]

Therefore the most important rank-one test is

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{N_p}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
}
\]

Equivalently, there exists a tangent-annihilator \(\ell\) such that

\[
\ell\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\ne0.
\]

If this holds, then the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is forbidden-visible.

---

# 5. Why \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is likely the decisive carrier

The \(G\)-channel includes both order-seven and order-nine terms:

\[
d_G
\sim c_7[\mathsf C_7]+\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

A tangent direction could conceivably absorb this as a coherent parameter variation: lower-order motion plus its induced higher-order continuation.

But the \(G_2\)-channel has a pure independent \(Y12\)-type order-nine injection:

\[
d_{G2}\sim \alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If actual-source tangents determine order-nine data from lower-order parameters, then this pure order-nine injection should be normal.

So the first finite computation should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\text{?}
}
\]

If no, D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

If yes, test the combined \(G\)-channel.

---

# 6. Upper bound for actual rows

If

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X
\]

and \(F_X\) lies on \(\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\), then the distance of the actual row to the manifold satisfies

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can}),
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\right)
\le
\|T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)\|.
\]

Therefore the forbidden upper is reduced to the stronger completion estimate

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\tr\to\sing\text{-coh}}\) estimate. It is stronger than the plain \(L^2\) completion needed for Gate–1.

---

# 7. Tubular radius

A first-order normal projection is not enough unless the manifold has polynomial tubular radius.

We need:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u_p+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
\ge
Q^{-C}
\|\Pi_{N_p}D\|
\]

for D4-sized displacements \(D\).

A sufficient condition is a polynomial bound on the second fundamental form:

\[
\|\mathrm{II}_{\mathcal M}\|\le Q^C
\]

in the chosen singular norm, and a D4 displacement size below the corresponding tubular radius.

So the downstream theorem requires:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Tubular}_{\sing}^{\arith}
}
\]

in addition to the tangent-rank test.

---

# 8. Concrete finite linear algebra formulation

Let \(S\) be the finite singular coefficient space through order nine. Let

\[
T_p\in \operatorname{Mat}_{10\times d}
\]

be the tangent matrix of the actual EF parameters. Let

\[
D_p\in \operatorname{Mat}_{10\times 2}
\]

be the D4 displacement matrix:

\[
D_p=
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}.
\]

Then the normal visibility matrix is

\[
\boxed{
K_p
=
(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}T_p})D_p.
}
\]

The finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\max}(K_p^*K_p)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

Rank one is enough.

The \(Y12\)-only shortcut is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\operatorname{ran}T_p
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 9. What would make the test fail?

The test fails if the actual-source tangent space includes an independent order-nine direction matching \(C_{Y12}\). This can happen if the allowed EF parameters include arbitrary high-order coefficient variations.

Therefore the parameter set \(\theta\) must remain **actual-source constrained**:

Allowed:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha\text{ if admitted},\text{ cutoff phase/scale},\text{background parameters}.
\]

Not allowed:

\[
\text{free }a_n,\quad \text{free local Taylor coefficients},\quad \text{free order-nine amplitudes}.
\]

If free local coefficient variation is allowed, then \(C_{Y12}\) becomes tangent and D4 dies.

So the manifold must be arithmetic, not universal.

---

# 10. Current best theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Normal \(Y12\) D4 theorem.}
}
\]

For the actual-source singular manifold \(\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\),

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
T_{u_p}\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on a polynomial-weight set.

Together with:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\tr\to\sing\text{-coh}}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Tubular}_{\sing}^{\arith},
}
\]

this gives a Type-I forbidden D4 channel through the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) wall component.

This is the cleanest and narrowest downstream target so far.

## Agent 1 — testing whether \(C_{Y12}\) is tangent

We now focus on the narrowest downstream test:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The danger is that \(C_{Y12}\) might be generated by legitimate actual-source parameter variations, especially

\[
\partial_m,\quad \partial_\lambda,\quad \partial_\alpha,
\]

or cutoff/phase variations. I tested these tangent directions structurally.

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ is unlikely to be tangent to ordinary actual-source parameters.}
}
\]

The reason is that \(C_{Y12}\) is a **quadratic/mixed promoted carrier** tied to the \(Y_{12}^{(2)}\)-type Bottleneck-C geometry, whereas the natural EF parameters generate **linear transport/twist/cutoff variations** of the source packet. These have different jet algebra.

So the \(Y12\) normal-channel remains the best finite-D4 target.

---

# 1. Tangent directions of the actual EF source manifold

Let

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta
\]

be the finite coherent source packet. Its local tangent directions are generated by differentiating with respect to admissible parameters \(\theta\).

The core directions are:

\[
\partial_mF_X,
\qquad
\partial_\lambda F_X,
\qquad
\partial_\alpha F_X,
\qquad
\partial_{\varphi}F_X,
\qquad
\partial_{\mathrm{cut}}F_X,
\]

plus background/gamma parameter derivatives.

In a singular local chart, these are all variations of the same **linear explicit-formula atom family**:

\[
\sum_n a_n(\theta)e^{i\tau_n(\theta)x}
+
\text{dual/background/tail}.
\]

Differentiating gives combinations of

\[
a_n' e^{i\tau_n x}
+
a_n(i\tau_n' x)e^{i\tau_n x},
\]

and higher smooth cutoff/gamma corrections.

So actual-source tangents are of the schematic form:

\[
\boxed{
\text{linear atom variation}
=
\sum_n
(\text{smooth amplitude polynomial in }x,\tau_n)
a_n e^{i\tau_nx}
+
\text{background corrections}.
}
\]

They do not introduce a new independent quadratic mixed-block carrier unless that mixed-block carrier is already present in the EF atom family.

---

# 2. What \(C_{Y12}\) represents

The \(Y12\) carrier is not merely an order-nine Taylor coefficient. It comes from the promoted quadratic mixed-block object \(Y_{12}^{(2)}\) coupled with same-point whitening/transport structure. In prior notation, it is the order-nine carrier class

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

associated to the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) D4 channel.

Structurally, it is a **second-order/mixed carrier**, not a first-order variation of the scalar EF packet.

That distinction is decisive:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source tangents are first variations;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ is a quadratic promoted carrier.}
}
\]

A first-variation tangent can generate order-nine Taylor coefficients, but those coefficients are constrained by the linear atom family. It does not generically generate the specific \(Y12\) quotient class.

---

# 3. Check \(m\)-translation tangent

For a packet atom

\[
e^{i\tau x},
\]

translation in \(m\) changes phase and slowly changes coefficients. Locally,

\[
\partial_m e^{i\tau x}
=
i(\partial_m\tau)x\,e^{i\tau x}
+
\text{coefficient variation}.
\]

The Taylor coefficient relation is simple:

\[
s_k\mapsto
\partial_m s_k
\]

with

\[
\partial_m s_k
\]

coming from differentiating \(a_n,\tau_n\).

This tangent preserves the one-atom linear structure. It does not create a bilinear object like

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
\]

or a product of two independent local source variations.

So unless \(Y12\) has been accidentally represented as a linear transport derivative of the EF packet, it is not in the \(m\)-tangent.

Finite test:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_mF_X\}
}
\]

should hold generically.

---

# 4. Check \(\lambda\)-scale tangent

Scale variation gives

\[
\partial_\lambda F(m+\lambda x)
=
x\,F'(m+\lambda x)
+
\partial_\lambda(\lambda^3)q''_{\can}
+
\text{frequency-scale terms}.
\]

Again this is a linear differential/transport tangent of the same source.

It may generate order-nine coefficients from lower-order source coefficients, but in a coherent way. In the singular connection residual notation, \(\lambda\)-variation changes both \(s_7\) and \(s_9\) according to the actual recurrence. It should lie in the tangent graph:

\[
s_9=\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7+\cdots.
\]

A pure \(Y12\) injection changes order-nine mixed carrier data independently of the lower-order coherent variation.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\lambda F_X\text{ should be killed by }\mathcal K_9,
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\text{ should remain nonzero.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Check \(\alpha\)-tilt tangent

This is the most dangerous one.

The \(\alpha\)-tilt changes amplitudes by factors such as

\[
n^{-\alpha}
\quad\text{or}\quad
n^{+\alpha}
\]

and changes the folded branch phase. Therefore

\[
\partial_\alpha F_X
\]

inserts \(\log n\)-weights and branch-sign differences.

This can look like a high-order source deformation. However, it is still a **linear amplitude/phase deformation** of the EF packet:

\[
\partial_\alpha F_X
=
\sum_n
(\pm\log n+\text{gamma/fold corrections})
a_n e^{i\tau_nx}
+
\text{dual corrections}.
\]

It is not a quadratic mixed-block \(Y12\)-type carrier.

Thus \(\alpha\)-admission likely does not generate \(C_{Y12}\). But this must be checked because \(\alpha\) can enlarge the tangent space substantially.

The key finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha F_X)=0
\quad\text{but}\quad
\mathcal K_9(C_{Y12})\ne0.
}
\]

If instead

\[
C_{Y12}\in\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\alpha F_X,\partial_mF_X,\partial_\lambda F_X,\ldots\},
\]

then \(\alpha\)-admission kills the \(Y12\) normal channel.

Current structural expectation:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{ helps O2/O3 determinant survival and probably does not kill }Y12.
}
\]

But this remains a finite tangent-rank audit.

---

# 6. Cutoff and truncation tangents

Cutoff variations are potentially large because allowing arbitrary cutoff deformations can mimic many local amplitude changes.

If the actual-source manifold allows too many cutoff parameters, it may swallow \(C_{Y12}\).

Therefore cutoff parameters must be restricted to genuine global smoothing choices, not arbitrary local amplitude functions.

Allowed:

\[
X,\quad \text{smooth transition width},\quad \text{fixed cutoff-family parameter}.
\]

Not allowed:

\[
\text{free local cutoff perturbation } \delta V(y)
\]

with arbitrary shape.

If arbitrary cutoff variations are admitted, the tangent space becomes too large and the D4 normal channel dies.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the actual-source manifold must be parametrically small, not a free atom-amplitude span.}
}
\]

This is essential.

---

# 7. Background/gamma tangents

The background term \(B_2\) and gamma corrections are smooth, low-dimensional functions of \(t\). Their variations are also smooth low-dimensional directions.

They may contribute to order-nine coefficients, but they are not expected to produce the mixed \(Y12\) carrier quotient.

So they belong in the tangent space, but should not kill \(C_{Y12}\).

Finite check:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
R_{\mathrm{bg}}+\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m,\partial_\lambda,\partial_\alpha,\ldots\}.
\]

---

# 8. Best finite rank test

Define the tangent matrix

\[
T_p=
\left[
\partial_mF_X,\ 
\partial_\lambda F_X,\ 
\partial_\alpha F_X,\ 
\partial_{\mathrm{cut}}F_X,\ 
\partial_{\mathrm{bg}}F_X,
\ldots
\right]
\]

in singular coefficient coordinates through order nine.

Define

\[
y_p=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Compute the residual

\[
r_Y
=
(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}T_p})y_p.
\]

Then the decisive criterion is

\[
\boxed{
\|r_Y\|^2\ge Q^{-A}\|y_p\|^2.
}
\]

If true, the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is forbidden-visible.

If false, test the full two-column D4 matrix.

This is the exact finite linear algebra computation.

---

# 9. Relation to the order-nine residual \(\mathcal K_9\)

If the tangent matrix has full lower-order rank, then the orthogonal projection residual is equivalent to a family of connection residuals:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(j)}
=
\ell_j\cdot s,
\]

where \(\ell_j\) are left-null vectors of \(T_p\).

The simplest \(\mathcal K_9\) is a left-null vector with dominant \(s_9\)-component.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(C_{Y12})\ne0
}
\]

is just the one-dimensional version of

\[
(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}T_p})C_{Y12}\ne0.
\]

If multiple left-null vectors exist, the forbidden channel can be higher rank.

---

# 10. Risk: second variations

A subtle issue: D4 may correspond not to a first tangent vector but to a **second variation** of the actual EF source manifold.

If the downstream contradiction uses distance to the manifold at first order, a second-order tangent direction is still normal at first order. That is good.

But if one allows second-order jets of the parameter manifold in the tangent model, \(Y12\) could become “allowed curvature” rather than forbidden.

The correct geometric comparison is first-order:

\[
D4\text{ displacement }D
\]

is a first-order local obstruction output. It should be compared to the **tangent space**, not the second osculating space.

Nonlinear absorption is handled separately by the tubular-radius theorem.

Thus \(Y12\) being a second variation does not make it tangent. It supports normality.

---

# 11. Current conclusion

The normal \(Y12\) theorem is plausible:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
}
\]

provided the actual-source manifold only includes genuine first-order EF parameters and not arbitrary coefficient/cutoff deformations.

The remaining finite audits are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{1. specify allowed parameter list } \theta;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{2. compute tangent matrix }T_p;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{3. project }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ against }\operatorname{ran}T_p;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{4. prove polynomial residual.}
}
\]

This is now a clean finite-dimensional normality test.

## Agent 1 — tangent-rank model for \(Y12\)

We want to decide whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

or whether it has a polynomial normal component. The previous attack argued structurally that \(Y12\) should not be generated by first-order EF parameters. I now push this into a more concrete tangent-rank model.

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ is normal unless the actual-source tangent space includes a free quadratic/mixed-block parameter.}
}
\]

Ordinary EF parameters

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{global phase},\text{smooth cutoff scale},\text{background parameters}
\]

generate **linear atom variations**. The \(Y12\) carrier is a **quadratic mixed-block carrier**. These live in different algebraic types.

So the finite normality test should not merely compare Taylor coefficients. It should compare **polarization degree**.

---

# 1. Add a polarization grading

Let the singular local source be represented schematically by atom variables

\[
A_n(x)=a_n e^{i\tau_n x}.
\]

Actual-source first variations have the form

\[
\delta F
=
\sum_n
\left(
\delta a_n+i a_n \delta\tau_n x
\right)e^{i\tau_n x}
+
\delta B_2+\delta D_2+\delta E_2.
\]

This is linear in the atom family:

\[
\boxed{
\delta F \in \mathcal L_1,
}
\]

where \(\mathcal L_1\) denotes the linearized EF atom span generated by admissible global parameter variations.

The \(Y12\) carrier comes from a promoted quadratic mixed-block expression. Its schematic source type is

\[
\boxed{
Y_{12}^{(2)}\sim \text{bilinear/polarized product of two first variations}.
}
\]

Therefore

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in \mathcal L_2
\]

in polarization degree, not \(\mathcal L_1\).

If the singular coherence manifold is the actual EF source family, its tangent is \(\mathcal L_1\), not \(\mathcal L_2\). Hence

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\notin T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
}
\]

unless the projection/quotient collapses polarization degree two into degree one.

This gives a new finite invariant:

\[
\boxed{
\text{polarization degree mod tangent}.
}
\]

---

# 2. The correct forbidden projection

Define a projection

\[
P_{\deg\ge2}
\]

onto the part of the singular carrier representation with polarization degree at least two after quotienting out linear EF tangents.

Actual first-order source deformations satisfy

\[
P_{\deg\ge2}
T_{\tr\to\sing}\delta F_X=0.
\]

But D4 gives

\[
P_{\deg\ge2}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\]

if the \(Y12\) quadratic mixed-block survives the singular quotient.

So the candidate forbidden operator becomes

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{Y12}
=
P_{\deg\ge2}
T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
}
\]

This is sharper than parity and sharper than a generic tangent projection. It is algebraic.

The upper is then natural:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\approx0
}
\]

because the actual row is a first-order source, up to completion residual.

The lower is:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{Y12}E_{\car}L_{\clean}(0,1)^T
=
\alpha_9\,P_{\deg\ge2}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0.
}
\]

---

# 3. Why this may beat the tangent-matrix approach

The tangent-matrix approach requires listing all actual EF parameters:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{cutoff},\text{background},\ldots
\]

and then proving \(Y12\) is not in their span.

The polarization-degree approach proves a stronger invariant:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all first-order EF parameters have polarization degree }1;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ has polarization degree }2.
}
\]

That automatically excludes **all** first-order parameters, including \(\alpha\), as long as \(\alpha\) is treated as a first-order deformation of the atom family.

This is important because \(\alpha\)-admissibility previously threatened to enlarge the tangent space. Under polarization grading:

\[
\partial_\alpha F_X\in\mathcal L_1,
\]

so it still does not contain \(Y12\in\mathcal L_2\).

Thus \(\alpha\) may help determinant branch survival without killing the \(Y12\) downstream channel, provided \(\alpha\) is only a first-order atom deformation.

---

# 4. Main risk: quotient collapse

The risk is that the carrier quotient identifies the quadratic mixed-block carrier with a linear source tangent.

That is, it may impose a relation like

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\equiv
\sum_j c_j\,\partial_{\theta_j}F_X
\quad
\text{in the singular carrier quotient}.
\]

If so, polarization degree is not respected by the quotient and the normal channel dies.

Therefore the decisive finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\deg\ge2}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad\text{in the singular quotient.}
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\not\equiv
\text{linear EF tangent}
\mod \mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

This is a quotient-respecting polarization test.

---

# 5. Relation to Bottleneck C

This is where the older Bottleneck-C object becomes relevant.

Recall the live obstruction carrier was

\[
E_{A,Y}
\]

built from

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
\]

and same-point whitening slots. The crucial feature was that it was the first live Bottleneck-C carrier and required stronger transform safety beyond quotient closure.

That is exactly the kind of object expected to be **not** an actual-source first tangent. It is a second-order local algebra carrier.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ normality is the downstream form of Bottleneck C being a true obstruction.}
}
\]

If \(Y12\) is tangent to the actual-source manifold, Bottleneck C is not an obstruction; it is absorbed by source variation.

If \(Y12\) is normal, Bottleneck C supplies the forbidden D4 channel.

This is a useful conceptual bridge.

---

# 6. Proposed finite theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Polarized \(Y12\) normality theorem.}
}
\]

In the transition-to-singular carrier quotient, there is a grading

\[
\mathcal Q_{\sing}
=
\mathcal Q_{\sing}^{(1)}
\oplus
\mathcal Q_{\sing}^{(2)}
\oplus
\cdots
\]

such that:

1. actual EF first tangents satisfy

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\subseteq
\mathcal Q_{\sing}^{(1)}
\oplus \mathcal Q_{\sing}^{(0)};
\]

2. the \(Y12\) carrier satisfies

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\mathcal Q_{\sing}^{(2)}
\]

with

\[
\|P^{(2)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|
\ge Q^{-A};
\]

3. the nuisance quotient does not kill this degree-two component.

Then

\[
\Lambda=P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}
\]

is a rank-one forbidden channel seeing \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

---

# 7. Upper bound for actual rows

If the actual row is

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X
\]

and \(F_X\) is an actual first-order EF source, then

\[
P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)=0.
\]

Therefore

\[
P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

The global upper follows from the strong residual estimate:

\[
\sum_p
\|P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is exactly

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\tr\to\sing\text{-coh}}
\]

but now only in the degree-two forbidden component. That may be easier than controlling every discarded singular component.

So the upper theorem can be weakened:

\[
\boxed{
\text{only the forbidden degree-two projection of the EF residual must be small.}
}
\]

This is a good improvement.

---

# 8. What about \(C_7\)?

The \(C_7\) carrier may be degree one or degree two depending on its origin. The \(Y12\) argument does not require \(C_7\).

If \(C_7\) also has a degree-two forbidden component, rank could be two. But rank one via \(Y12\) is enough.

So stop trying to prove full two-dimensional anisotropy. The minimal wall factor can be:

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\sim
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\cdot
\|P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|^2.
\]

This is simpler than rank-stratified \((\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\) extraction.

---

# 9. Updated downstream stack

The forbidden D4 part can now be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\)-normal D4 channel.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
\|P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
\sum_p
\|P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then on an extracted set where

\[
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

D4 gives forbidden energy:

\[
\sum_p|\Lambda v_m|^2\ge Q^{-C}\mu(G),
\]

contradicting the global upper.

This avoids needing full rank of

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}.
\]

---

# 10. Remaining finite audits

The next exact audits are:

## Audit 1 — define polarization grading in singular quotient

Show the singular carrier quotient preserves enough polarization degree to distinguish EF first tangents from \(Y12\).

## Audit 2 — prove actual EF first tangents are degree \(\le1\)

Check all admitted parameters:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{phase},\text{cutoff},B_2,D_2,E_2.
\]

None should generate the \(Y12\) degree-two class as a first variation.

## Audit 3 — prove \(Y12\) has nonzero degree-two projection

Compute

\[
P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

and show it is polynomially nonzero.

## Audit 4 — ensure nuisance quotient does not kill it

Show

\[
P^{(2)}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin \mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

---

# 11. Final status

The best current downstream candidate is now much narrower and cleaner:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{Y12}=P_{\deg2}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
}
\]

It uses:

\[
\boxed{
\text{polarization-degree normality of }Y12.
}
\]

If this finite grading survives the singular quotient, finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

This is the most promising \(\Lambda\) formulation so far.

The structural reason is strong:

\mathsf C_{Y12}

whereas the allowed actual-source tangents

\[
\partial_m,\quad \partial_\lambda,\quad \partial_\alpha,\quad

are first-order EF deformations. Lower-order connection terms \(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}\) can propagate lower-order coherent data, but they do not create a new same-point quadratic Hessian carrier unless the singular quotient collapses quadratic source-Hessian degree into linear EF tangent degree.

# 1. Define \(\mathcal Y_9\)

The order-nine residual codomain should not be “all order-nine Taylor coefficients.” It must remember the carrier signature. Define

\[
\mathcal Y_9
\]

as the order-nine singular residual carrier space after quotienting nuisance terms but before quotienting actual-source tangents. Its labels should include:

\[
\boxed{
\text{order }9;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{source polarization degree;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch bidegree / mixed-branch type;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{same-point vs transported-slot type;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{whitening-slot count;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-grade reflection parity;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{carrier quotient class modulo nuisance.}
}
\]

Thus decompose

\[
\mathcal Y_9
=
\bigoplus_{\Sigma}\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma},
\]

where \(\Sigma\) is a signature tuple.

The relevant signature is

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(Y12)=
(\deg_{\mathrm{src}}=2,\ \text{mixed }(1,2)\text{-block},\
\text{same-point},\
\text{one whitening slot},\
\text{correct reflection parity},\
\text{\(Y12\)-carrier class}).
}
\]

Let

\[
\Pi_{Y12}:=\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}.
\]

---

# 2. Define the subspaces being quotiented

## 2.1 Parameter tangent subspace \(\mathcal T_{\par}\)

\[
\mathcal T_{\par}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\partial_m u,\partial_\lambda u,\partial_\alpha u,
\partial_{\mathrm{phase}}u,
\partial_{\mathrm{cutoff}}u,
\partial_{\mathrm{bg}}u,
\ldots
\right\}_{9}
\]

in order-nine singular residual coordinates.

These are legitimate actual-source parameter tangents. They are all first variations of

\[
u=q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

At the EF atom level, they have schematic form

\[
\partial_\theta
\left(a_n(\theta)e^{i\tau_n(\theta)x}\right)
=
\left(\partial_\theta a_n+i a_n(\partial_\theta\tau_n)x\right)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

So they are linear in the EF atom family.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0
}
\]

unless an admitted parameter changes the nonlinear source map itself and creates a quadratic Hessian block. Ordinary \(m,\lambda,\alpha,\) phase, cutoff, and background variations do not.

---

## 2.2 Order-seven propagated subspace \(\mathcal T_7\)

\[
\mathcal T_7
=
\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{7\to9}.
\]

This is the coherent order-nine continuation of legitimate order-seven data.

Its signature is generated by lower-order singular carrier data transported through the singular connection. It may produce order-nine coefficients, but their source signature is inherited from the lower-order coherent source.

So the key condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0.
}
\]

This is plausible because \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\) is not merely an order-nine continuation of \(\mathsf C_7\); it is a promoted quadratic mixed-block carrier.

If \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) itself contains the \(Y_{12}^{(2)}\) promotion, then this fails. But that would mean \(Y12\) is not an independent D4 carrier; it is already coherent continuation. That is exactly the rank-zero case.

---

## 2.3 Lower-order propagated subspace \(\mathcal T_{<7}\)

\[
\mathcal T_{<7}
=
\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}.
\]

These are order-nine contributions forced by lower singular coefficients \(s_{<7}\).

Their source polarization degree is inherited from lower actual EF data and background/gamma structure. They should not create the same-point quadratic mixed \(Y12\) signature.

Thus the target condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{<7}=0.
}
\]

---

# 3. Why \(C_{Y12}\) has the forbidden signature

The \(Y12\) carrier comes from the promoted quadratic mixed-block

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
=
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

coupled with a same-point whitening slot. This is not a first variation of the EF source. It is a second differential of the nonlinear local normal form.

Its signature is:

\[
\boxed{
\deg_{\mathrm{src}}=2;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{mixed two-input source-Hessian block;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{same-point carrier, not transported ordinary EF atom;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{one whitening slot;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{order-nine D4 carrier channel;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-visible through }\alpha_9\neq0.
}
\]

Therefore,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathsf C_{Y12}=\mathsf C_{Y12}^{Y12}\neq0
}
\]

provided the singular nuisance quotient does not kill the \(Y12\)-carrier class.

This is the exact finite condition to check:

\[
\boxed{
\mathsf C_{Y12}\notin \mathcal N_{\sing}
\quad\text{in the }\Sigma(Y12)\text{ sector.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Treatment of \(\alpha\)

\(\alpha\) is the dangerous tangent because it changes branch weights and phases. But structurally,

\[
\partial_\alpha u
\]

is still a first-order EF deformation:

\[
\partial_\alpha(a_n e^{i\tau_nx})
=
(\partial_\alpha a_n+i a_n\partial_\alpha\tau_n\,x)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

It inserts log weights and branch signs. It does not introduce

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Therefore,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha u=0
}
\]

unless \(\alpha\) is defined to deform the nonlinear map \(N_0\) itself or the whitening/nonlinear source geometry. Under the usual tilt interpretation, it does not.

So \(\alpha\)-admission should not kill the \(Y12\) normal channel:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\text{ in }
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb,\alpha}
}
\]

provided \(\alpha\) remains a source-linear tilt.

This is a useful positive interaction: \(\alpha\)-twisting can still be used for determinant branch survival without absorbing \(Y12\) downstream.

---

# 5. The exact theorem to prove

The clean theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\) signature separation theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a signature projection

\[
\Pi_{Y12}:\mathcal Y_9\to \mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

such that:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_7=0;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{<7}=0;
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\neq0
}
\]

with polynomial norm:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad
\text{in}
\quad
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}.
\]

---

# 6. Consequence for D4

If the theorem holds, define

\[
\Lambda_{Y12,p}
=
\Pi_{Y12,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

The actual-row upper is:

\[
\sum_p
\left\|
\Lambda_{Y12,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

coming from

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}
\]

in the \(Y12\)-signature sector.

The D4 lower is:

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\Gamma
=
\alpha_9
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\text{possibly a }\Gamma_G\text{ term}.
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\ne0
\]

and the \(Y12\) projection is nonzero, this gives rank at least one. The clean wall factor is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
}
\]

This avoids needing full two-dimensional anisotropy.

---

# 7. Failure modes

The theorem fails in exactly four ways:

## Failure 1 — \(\alpha\) or another parameter is not source-linear

If some allowed parameter tangent genuinely changes \(N_0\) or whitening geometry so that it contains

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}\neq0.
\]

Then \(Y12\) may become tangent.

## Failure 2 — the lower connection generates \(Y12\)

If

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}\neq0
\]

or

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}\neq0,
\]

then \(Y12\) is coherent continuation rather than forbidden.

## Failure 3 — nuisance quotient kills \(Y12\)

If

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathsf C_{Y12}=0
\]

in the singular carrier quotient, then the carrier is invisible.

## Failure 4 — transition-to-singular map erases the signature

If

\[
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}=0
\]

because the transition map sends the \(Y12\) carrier into a retained/tangent sector, rank is zero.

These are finite, checkable failures.

---

# 8. Current best status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Structurally, }[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ should survive in }\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The decisive invariant is source-Hessian/polarization degree plus same-point whitening signature.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The next computation is to verify that the singular nuisance quotient and lower connection preserve this signature.}
}
\]

If that verification succeeds, finite D4 finally has a credible rank-one forbidden channel.

## Agent 1 — stress test of \(Y12\) signature separation

It fails only if the singular connection itself includes the quadratic source-Hessian correction that created \(Y12\).

---

# 1. Lower connection cannot generate \(Y12\) if it is linear

The lower connection maps

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}:\mathcal Y_7\to\mathcal Y_9,
\]

\[
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}:\mathcal Y_{<7}\to\mathcal Y_9.
\]

If these maps are induced by:

\[
\text{coordinate change},
\quad
\text{linear transport},
\quad
\text{linear whitening},
\quad
\text{linear source normalization},
\quad
\text{linear gauge conversion},
\]

then they preserve source polarization degree.

That is,

\[
\deg_{\mathrm{src}}(\mathcal R v)=\deg_{\mathrm{src}}(v).
\]

Actual-source lower data live in source degree \(0\) or \(1\), depending on whether the term is background/gamma or first EF source variation. Therefore a linear lower connection cannot produce a degree-two source-Hessian signature:

\[
\Pi_{\deg_{\mathrm{src}}=2}
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(\mathcal Y_7^{\deg\le1})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\deg_{\mathrm{src}}=2}
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(\mathcal Y_{<7}^{\deg\le1})=0.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0
}
\]

provided the singular connection is source-linear.

---

# 2. When lower connection can generate \(Y12\)

The failure case is now clear.

If the singular connection includes nonlinear normal-form corrections, such as

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

or same-point whitening second variations, then it can map lower data into a \(Y12\)-type order-nine term.

Schematically:

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\mathrm{nonlin}}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\mathrm{lin}}
+
\mathcal Q_{7\to9}^{(2)},
\]

where

\[
\mathcal Q_{7\to9}^{(2)}
\]

has source degree two. Then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\mathrm{nonlin}}
\]

may be nonzero.

But if this happens, the interpretation changes:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ is part of the singular coherent normal form, not a forbidden D4 direction.}
}
\]

So the key finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }Y12\text{ included in the singular connection/normal-form coherence relation?}
}
\]

If yes, rank zero is possible.

If no, \(Y12\) remains forbidden.

---

# 3. Nuisance quotient risk

Let

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\subset\mathcal Y_9
\]

be the singular nuisance subspace.

The \(Y12\)-channel survives only if

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathsf C_{Y12}
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}
\not\subset
\mathcal N_{\sing}.

The nuisance quotient can kill \(Y12\) if \(Y12\) is classified as:

\[
\text{gauge},
\quad
\text{normalization},
\quad
\text{background/core},
\quad
\text{line correction},
\quad
\text{tail/removable},
\quad
\text{carrier-nuisance}.
\]

But in the active D4 route, \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is one of the two live carrier classes. The clean D4 matrix explicitly uses it:

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\alpha_9\ne0.
\]

If \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) were nuisance-zero, the D4 rank-two carrier quotient would already collapse.

Therefore, assuming the D4 carrier quotient is the same quotient used in the singular channel, nuisance killing should not occur.

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap \mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}
=0
\quad\text{on the live }[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ class.}
}
\]

This is a finite quotient-consistency check.

---

# 4. Possible mismatch between D4 quotient and singular quotient

There is a subtle risk: the D4 quotient in the transition chart may treat \(Y12\) as live, but the singular coherence quotient may classify its image as nuisance.

That would mean

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\neq0,
\]

but

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then the singular-discard \(\Lambda\) does not see \(Y12\), despite D4 seeing it locally.

This is exactly the transition–singular rank-zero failure mode.

So the finite audit should explicitly check:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}:
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\mapsto
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\sing}
\neq0
\quad
\text{mod }\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

If this map is zero, singular-discard fails.

If nonzero and source-degree signature is preserved, \(Y12\) survives.

---

# 5. Whitening-slot count as a robust invariant

Source polarization degree alone may be too coarse. A lower nonlinear connection could also have source degree two. But \(Y12\) has an additional signature:

\[
\boxed{
\text{one same-point whitening slot.}
}
\]

This may separate \(Y12\) from generic quadratic source terms.

Let the signature include

\[
w=\text{number/type of whitening slots}.
\]

Then

\[
\Sigma(Y12)=(\deg_{\mathrm{src}}=2,\ w=1,\ \text{same-point},\ \text{mixed }12).
\]

A lower coherent connection might generate source-degree-two terms, but if it has

\[
w=0
\]

or transported rather than same-point whitening, it still cannot hit \(Y12\).

So the stronger theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\deg=2,w=1,\mathrm{same\ point},12}
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0.
}
\]

This is more robust than using source degree alone.

---

# 6. Reflection parity as another invariant

From earlier source-grade reflection,

\[
(Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle})^T
=
(-1)^{g+e}Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle}.
\]

The \(Y12\) carrier has a specific parity determined by

\[
g+e
\]

and mixed block type.

If lower connection terms have different \(g+e\) parity, they cannot produce \(Y12\).

Thus the signature should include:

\[
\boxed{
\text{reflection parity }(-1)^{g+e}.
}
\]

Then the projection becomes even sharper:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}
=
\Pi_{\deg=2}
\Pi_{w=1}
\Pi_{\mathrm{same-point}}
\Pi_{\mathrm{mixed}\,12}
\Pi_{\mathrm{reflection\ parity}}.
\]

The likely theorem is that no actual-source tangent or lower coherent connection has this full signature.

---

# 7. Relationship to Bottleneck C

The \(Y12\) carrier was previously identified as the first live Bottleneck-C carrier. That already suggests it was not killed by the existing quotient/nuisance system.

If the Bottleneck-C analysis showed

\[
E_{A,Y}
\]

is a live obstruction rather than a nuisance correction, then the same reasoning should support

\[
\Pi_{Y12}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
\]

Therefore the \(Y12\) survival theorem may be recoverable from the Bottleneck-C ledger:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Bottleneck C live carrier}
\Rightarrow
\text{\(Y12\)-signature survives nuisance quotient.}
}
\]

This is a useful shortcut.

---

# 8. Exact finite theorem

The strongest form is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\)-signature invariance theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a signature decomposition of the order-nine singular residual space

\[
\mathcal Y_9
=
\bigoplus_\Sigma\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma}
\]

with

\[
\Sigma_{Y12}
=
(2,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same\ point},w=1,\mathrm{reflection\ parity})
\]

such that:

1. actual-source parameter tangents have no \(\Sigma_{Y12}\) component:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}\mathcal T_{\par}=0;
\]

2. lower singular connection has no \(\Sigma_{Y12}\) component:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}\mathcal T_7
=
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}\mathcal T_{<7}
=0;
\]

3. nuisance quotient does not kill the live \(Y12\) carrier:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\ne0;
\]

4. the surviving norm is polynomial:

\[
\left\|
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}.
\]

---

Assuming the theorem, define

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
=
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

For actual rows:

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X),
\]

so the upper is supplied by

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}
\]

in the \(\Sigma_{Y12}\)-sector.

For D4:

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma
=
\alpha_9
\Pi_{\Sigma_{Y12}}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\Gamma_{G,2}
+
(\text{possible }\Gamma_G\text{ term}).
\]

Thus rank one follows immediately from

\[
\alpha_9\ne0
\]

and the signature survival.

---

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ signature separation is plausible and now has precise failure modes.}
}
