## Agent 1 — graded-operator proof of \(Y12\)-signature survival

The previous endpoint was:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
\]

The next attack is to prove this using a **graded operator calculus**. The core idea:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source tangents and lower connections are generated by operators that cannot create the }Y12\text{ signature.}
}
\]

The forbidden \(Y12\) channel survives if all allowed coherence operations preserve or lower the signature filtration, while the D4 \(Y12\) carrier has a primitive component in the forbidden graded piece.

---

# 1. Define the signature filtration

Let the order-nine singular residual space have a finite filtration

\[
\mathcal Y_9
\supset
\mathcal F_{\deg\ge1}
\supset
\mathcal F_{\deg\ge2}
\supset
\cdots
\]

by source polarization degree, refined by additional labels:

\[
\Sigma=
(d_{\src}, b_{\mix}, p_{\same}, w_{\white}, \rho_{\refl}, q_{\carrier}).
\]

Here:

\[
d_{\src}=\text{source polarization degree},
\]

\[
b_{\mix}=\text{branch/mixed-block bidegree},
\]

\[
p_{\same}=\text{same-point versus transported-slot status},
\]

\[
w_{\white}=\text{whitening-slot count/type},
\]

\[
\rho_{\refl}=(-1)^{g+e}\text{ source-grade reflection parity},
\]

\[
q_{\carrier}=\text{carrier quotient class}.
\]

The \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma_{Y12}
=
(2,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},1,\rho_{Y12},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
}
\]

Let

\[
\Pi_{Y12}
\]

be the projection to this graded component.

---

# 2. Allowed actual-source tangents are degree one

For the finite coherent packet

\[
F_X^\theta=B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta,
\]

allowed parameter tangents are:

\[
\partial_m F_X,\quad
\partial_\lambda F_X,\quad
\partial_\alpha F_X,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{phase}}F_X,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{cut}}F_X,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{bg}}F_X.
\]

Each acts on an EF atom by

\[
\partial_\theta(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
(\partial_\theta a_n+i a_n(\partial_\theta\tau_n)x)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

This is source-linear. It changes amplitude, phase, scale, log weight, or background, but it does not insert

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Thus the tangent space satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal T_{\par}\subset \mathcal Y_9^{d_{\src}\le1}
\oplus \mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{bg}}.
}
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0
}
\]

provided \(\alpha\) and cutoff variations are restricted to source-linear EF deformations, not arbitrary nonlinear normal-form deformations.

This is the first important condition.

---

# 3. Lower connection operators are signature-nonincreasing unless nonlinear

The lower connection pieces are

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9},\qquad \mathcal R_{<7\to9}.
\]

Decompose them as

\[
\mathcal R=\mathcal R^{\lin}+\mathcal R^{\nonlin}.
\]

The linear part consists of chart transport, linear whitening, gauge conversion, normalization, and lower-order source transport. It cannot raise source polarization degree:

\[
d_{\src}(\mathcal R^{\lin}v)\le d_{\src}(v).
\]

So if lower coherent data has \(d_{\src}\le1\), then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R^{\lin}v=0.
\]

Hence:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_7=
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{<7}=0
}
\]

unless \(\mathcal R^{\nonlin}\) contains a degree-two mixed same-point whitening term with exactly the \(Y12\) signature.

So the only way lower connection can kill the \(Y12\)-normal channel is if the singular coherence law already includes a nonlinear correction of the form

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R^{\nonlin}_{7\to9}
\supset
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\ \text{with one same-point whitening slot}.
}
\]

That becomes the exact finite audit:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R^{\nonlin}_{7\to9}=0?
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) is forbidden.  
If no, \(Y12\) is coherent and D4 loses the rank-one channel.

---

# 4. Nuisance quotient must preserve the \(Y12\) live class

Let

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\subset\mathcal Y_9
\]

be the singular nuisance subspace. The \(Y12\) carrier survives if

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

Because \(Y12\) is live in the D4 carrier quotient, the natural expectation is that it should not be killed. But this must be checked because transition-to-singular projection may send a live transition carrier into a singular nuisance direction.

So we need the quotient-compatibility condition:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\left(
\mathcal Q_{\car}^{\tr,Y12}
\right)
\cap
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y12}
=
0
\quad\text{on the live }Y12\text{ component.}
}
\]

This is the second finite audit.

---

# 5. The \(Y12\)-survival theorem

The strongest usable theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Graded \(Y12\)-survival theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. actual-source parameter tangents are source-linear:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0;
\]

2. lower singular connection has no \(Y12\)-signature nonlinear correction:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_7=
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{<7}=0;
\]

3. transition-to-singular transport preserves a nonzero live \(Y12\) component:

\[
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\|
\ge Q^{-A};
\]

4. the singular nuisance quotient does not kill that component.

Then

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}.
}
\]

This yields a rank-one forbidden D4 channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

---

# 6. Consequence for D4

Define

\[
\Lambda_{Y12,p}
=
\Pi_{Y12,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

Then the D4 output satisfies

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma
=
\alpha_9
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\,\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\beta_9
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\,\Gamma_G
-
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])\Gamma_G.
\]

Under the survival theorem,

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])=0,
\]

so

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\ne0,
\]

rank one follows.

The forbidden wall factor can be written as

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
\left|
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right|^2
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
}
\]

In the clean case where the \(\Gamma_G\)-term is absorbed or separately controlled, it reduces to a \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-visible wall factor.

---

# 7. Actual-row upper

For actual rows,

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X.
\]

The coherent finite packet \(F_X\) has no \(Y12\)-signature:

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(F_X)=0.
\]

Thus

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

The global upper becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\Lambda_{Y12,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the \(Y12\)-sector version of

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}.
\]

It is weaker than controlling all singular discarded components. We only need tail/completion smallness in the \(Y12\)-signature sector.

That is a useful reduction.

---

# 8. Potential hidden failure: nonlinear EF completion

If \(E_2\) or the regularized tail includes nonlinear correction terms with the \(Y12\) signature, then

\[
F_X
\]

without \(E_2\) is not \(Y12\)-coherent. The upper would then require \(E_2\) to be included in the coherent manifold, or treated in the residual.

So one must decide:

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

or

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}+E_{2,\le X}^{Y12\text{-coh}}.
\]

If \(E_2\) has a \(Y12\)-signature component that is actual-source coherent, it belongs in the tangent/coherence side, not the forbidden residual.

This is a refinement of completion coherence.

---

# 9. Practical finite checks

The next explicit checks are:

## Check 1

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha u=0?
\]

If yes, \(\alpha\)-admission is D4-compatible.

## Check 2

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0?
\]

If yes, lower connection does not absorb \(Y12\).

## Check 3

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}=0?
\]

If yes, lower coefficients cannot generate \(Y12\).

## Check 4

\[
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\|\ge Q^{-A}?
\]

If yes, D4 sees the forbidden component.

These four checks settle the rank-one downstream channel.

---

# 10. Final status

The \(Y12\)-signature route is now sharper than the general tangent-normal formulation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{It is enough to find a graded invariant that actual-source tangents and lower connections cannot generate.}
}
\]

The likely invariant is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-degree two, mixed \(12\), same-point, one-whitening-slot, reflection-compatible carrier class.}
}
\]

If this invariant survives the singular quotient, finite D4 has a credible rank-one contradiction channel.

I’ll now turn the graded \(Y12\)-survival theorem into a decision tree. The main question is whether \(E_2\) or regularized tail completion already contains an actual-source \(Y12\)-signature term; if yes, it belongs in the coherent manifold and changes the quotient.

## Agent 1 — \(E_2\) / completion interaction with \(Y12\)-signature

The \(Y12\)-signature route currently says:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
\]

But there is one important unresolved issue:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the canonical completion }E_2\text{ contain an actual-source }Y12\text{-signature component?}
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) may not be forbidden; it may be part of the coherent actual source manifold. If no, the rank-one forbidden channel remains strong.

---

# 1. Why \(E_2\) matters

The coherent finite packet has been written as

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The residual is

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X.
\]

But

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

So

\[
R_X
=
(P_2-P_{2,\le X})
+
(D_2-D_{2,\le X})
+
E_2.
\]

If \(E_2\) has a \(Y12\)-signature component, then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X)
\]

may not be small. That would break the forbidden upper

\[
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_{Y12,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Therefore the \(Y12\)-upper requires either:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2)\text{ is small;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
E_2^{Y12}\text{ is included in the actual-source coherence manifold.}
}
\]

These are different architectures.

---

# 2. Two cases

## Case A — \(E_2\) has no \(Y12\)-signature

If

\[
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2)=O(Q^{-B}),
\]

then the earlier theorem stands.

The coherent manifold can be

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X})\},
\]

and

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X)
\]

is small by residual completion.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ is forbidden.}
}
\]

This is the best case.

---

## Case B — \(E_2\) has actual-source \(Y12\)-signature

If

\[
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2)
\]

is not small, but is an actual deterministic correction attached to the canonical source, then \(E_2^{Y12}\) must be included in the coherent manifold:

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}+E_{2,\le X}^{Y12})\}.
\]

Then the tangent space includes variations of \(E_2^{Y12}\). The forbidden quotient changes:

\[
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}).
\]

The decisive test becomes

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}.
}
\]

If \(E_2^{Y12}\) spans exactly the same \(Y12\)-signature direction, then the forbidden channel dies:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ is coherent completion, not obstruction.}
}
\]

If \(E_2^{Y12}\) has the same broad signature but a constrained actual coefficient profile, D4 may still be normal to the actual \(E_2\)-manifold. Then the test becomes a more refined actual-coefficient transversality.

---

# 3. The distinction: signature vs free amplitude

Even if \(E_2\) contains a \(Y12\)-signature component, it may not contain a **free** \(Y12\)-amplitude.

Suppose

\[
E_2^{Y12}(m)
=
c_{E}(m)\,\mathsf C_{Y12}
\]

with \(c_E(m)\) fixed by the EF tail/completion formula.

Then the coherent tangent includes only

\[
\partial_\theta c_E(m)\,\mathsf C_{Y12}
\]

for allowed parameters \(\theta\), not arbitrary multiples of \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\).

The D4 channel is

\[
H\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\,\mathsf C_{Y12}.
\]

It is tangent only if

\[
H\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

can be represented as a legitimate parameter derivative of \(c_E(m)\).

Thus the refined test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathsf C_{Y12}\text{ signature in }E_2\text{ does not kill D4 unless its amplitude direction is free/tangent.}
}
\]

So the actual quotient should be:

\[
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}
/
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12}\}.
\]

Then test whether

\[
\mathsf C_{Y12}
\]

survives.

---

# 4. Practical audit for \(E_2\)

The finite audit should compute:

\[
e_{Y12}:=\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2).
\]

Then classify:

### Type 0: absent/small

\[
\|e_{Y12}\|\le Q^{-B}.
\]

Then \(Y12\) is forbidden.

### Type 1: present but fixed

\[
e_{Y12}=c_E(m)\mathsf C_{Y12},
\]

with no admissible parameter giving arbitrary \(c_E\)-variation. Then \(Y12\) likely remains normal unless D4 coefficient matches \(\partial_\theta c_E\).

### Type 2: present with tangent amplitude

Allowed parameters generate

\[
\partial_{\theta}e_{Y12}
\]

spanning the same local direction as \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\). Then D4 \(Y12\) may be tangent, and the rank-one channel may die.

### Type 3: nuisance

\[
e_{Y12}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

or the \(Y12\)-signature is killed by regularization. Then D4 image may also be killed; check transition \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

---

# 5. Relation to actual-source upgrade

This is a direct instance of the actual-source upgrade problem.

If \(Y12\) appears in the actual canonical completion \(E_2\), then the formal \(Y12\) carrier may not be an obstruction. It may be part of the actual-source realization.

So the D4 normality theorem must be stated relative to the **full actual-source manifold**, including all canonical completion components.

The correct theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\full},
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\full}
\]

includes admissible variations of

\[
B_2,\quad P_{2,\le X},\quad D_{2,\le X},\quad E_2^{\coh}.
\]

This avoids false positives.

---

# 6. Possible rescue if \(Y12\) is tangent

If

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\full},
\]

then the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is not forbidden. But D4 might still have a normal channel through the \(\Gamma_G\) combination:

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7]).
\]

Even if \(Y12\) itself is tangent, this **combination** may be normal because it couples order-seven and order-nine data in a way not realized by the actual-source tangent.

So the fallback test is:

\[
\boxed{
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\notin
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\full}.
}
\]

If nonzero, D4 has a rank-one channel through \(\Gamma_G\).

If both \(Y12\) and this combination are tangent, finite D4 is normality-dead.

---

# 7. Updated decision tree

1. Compute

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2.
\]

2. If small, \(Y12\) forbidden channel survives.

3. If non-small, include \(E_2^{Y12}\) in the actual-source manifold.

4. Compute tangent span of all admissible \(Y12\)-signature variations:

\[
\mathcal T_{Y12}^{\full}
=
\Pi_{Y12}
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\full}.
\]

5. Test:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal T_{Y12}^{\full}.
\]

6. If false, test:

\[
\beta_9T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7])
\notin
\mathcal T_{Y12}^{\full}.
\]

7. If both false, D4 has no order-nine normal channel.

---

# 8. Final status

The \(Y12\)-signature theorem remains plausible, but it must be upgraded:

\[
\boxed{
\text{test \(Y12\) against the full canonical actual-source manifold, including }E_2.
}
\]

The key new audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2\text{ absent, fixed, tangent, or nuisance?}
}
\]

This is now a necessary step before claiming a \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel.

## Extra finite audit: \(E_2\) and \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) are the real threats

The original first-order quotient

\[
\mathcal Y_9/(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})

is probably too weak, because \(Y12\) is quadratic. The true threats are:

and

directly.

## Hidden risk: \(\alpha\) could enter at second order

Earlier, \(\alpha\) seemed safe because \(\partial_\alpha u\) is source-linear. But since \(Y12\) is quadratic, the relevant audit is not only

\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha u=0.
\]

It is also

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha^2 u=0,\quad
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_m\partial_\alpha u=0,\quad
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\lambda\partial_\alpha u=0.
}
\]

If these mixed second derivatives still only alter EF weights/phases, they remain safe. If they affect whitening/nonlinear source geometry, they may enter \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) and kill the channel.

So \(\alpha\)-admissibility must be checked against **second-order** actual-source curvature, not just first tangent.

---

The \(L^2\) Gate–1 anti-lock problem

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\not\approx0
\]

is the degree-one version of branch survival.

The fold ratio

\[
x \leftrightarrow 1-x
\]

and curvature asymmetry

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1
\]

should be treated as the toy model for O2/O3 determinant survival. If that degree-one theorem fails, the exterior theorem probably fails. If it works, its mechanism may lift.

So the next upstream work should prove a clean degree-one theorem before attacking determinant survival.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 04:59:24)

I’ll attack the hardest remaining quotient term: \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\). The question is whether legitimate second-order curvature of the actual EF source manifold can already contain the \(Y12\) source-Hessian signature.

_Thought for 11s_

## Agent 1 — testing \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) and \(E_2\) against \(Y12\)

We need decide whether

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}
\right).
\]

The new hard terms are

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}.
\]

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\text{ should not contain the }Y12\text{ signature if the actual EF family is source-linear.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ should also be }Y12\text{-free if it is a linear regularized completion/tail of the explicit formula.}
}
\]

So the \(Y12\)-normal channel remains plausible. The only way it fails is if the canonical completion or the admitted source-family parameters already include nonlinear normal-form Hessian data of the same type as

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

That would be an actual-source upgrade failure in the downstream direction.

---

# 1. Separate parameter curvature from source-Hessian degree

This is the key distinction.

The actual EF source family has the schematic form

\[
F_X^\theta(x)
=
B_2^\theta(x)
+
\sum_{n\le X}a_n^\theta e^{i\tau_n^\theta x}
+
\sum_{n\le X}b_n^\theta e^{-i\tilde\tau_n^\theta x}
+
E_X^\theta(x).
\]

Even if we take second derivatives in parameters,

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta,
\]

we still get a **linear combination of the same EF atoms** with polynomial/logarithmic weights:

\[
\partial_i\partial_j(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
\left(
\partial_i\partial_j a_n
+i x(\partial_i a_n\partial_j\tau_n+\partial_j a_n\partial_i\tau_n+a_n\partial_i\partial_j\tau_n)
-x^2a_n\partial_i\tau_n\partial_j\tau_n
\right)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

This is second-order in parameters, but still **source-linear** in EF atoms.

By contrast, \(Y12\) has source-Hessian signature:

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
=
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

That is not a second derivative of EF coefficients. It is a second differential of the local nonlinear source map \(N_0\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{parameter curvature degree }2
\neq
\text{source-Hessian degree }2.
}
\]

This distinction protects the \(Y12\) channel.

---

# 2. Consequence for \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\)

The second fundamental form of the actual-source manifold is generated by second parameter derivatives:

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\Pi_9^\perp\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)
\right\}_{i,j}.
\]

If every admissible parameter changes only:

\[
\text{center},\quad
\text{scale},\quad
\alpha\text{-tilt},\quad
\text{phase},\quad
\text{smooth cutoff},\quad
\text{gamma/background coefficients},
\]

then every element of \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) is still source-linear.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

This is the strongest positive result of this attack.

The theorem fails only if some admissible parameter \(\theta\) changes the nonlinear source map or whitening geometry so that

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta
\]

contains a term with signature

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\quad
+
\quad
\text{same-point whitening}.
\]

Ordinary EF parameters should not do that.

---

# 3. \(\alpha\)-second derivatives are probably safe

The dangerous \(\alpha\)-checks are:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha u=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha^2u=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_m\partial_\alpha u=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\lambda\partial_\alpha u=0.
\]

If \(\alpha\) is an AFE tilt, then it changes weights and branch phases:

\[
a_n^\alpha \sim a_n n^{-\alpha},
\qquad
b_n^\alpha\sim b_n n^{+\alpha},
\]

so

\[
\partial_\alpha^2(a_n^\alpha e^{i\tau_nx})
\sim
(\log n)^2 a_n^\alpha e^{i\tau_nx}
\]

plus gamma/phase corrections. Again this is source-linear.

Hence:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\partial_\alpha^2u=0
}
\]

under the usual tilt interpretation.

So \(\alpha\)-twisting can remain available for determinant branch survival without killing downstream \(Y12\)-normality, provided \(\alpha\) does not deform \(N_0\) or same-point whitening geometry.

---

# 4. What about \(E_2\)?

Now test the completion term.

If \(E_2\) is a linear regularized completion/tail of the explicit formula, then it has the same structural form as \(P_2,D_2,B_2\):

\[
E_2(t)
=
\text{linear tail/regularization functional of EF atoms and gamma data}.
\]

Then its singular jet has source-linear signature. Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(E_2)=0.
}
\]

In that case \(E_2\) does not need to be added to the \(Y12\)-tangent quotient.

If, however, \(E_2\) contains a nonlinear local correction generated by the same normal-form Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

then \(E_2\) may contain a \(Y12\)-signature component. Then one must classify it:

### Type 0 — absent

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0.
\]

Best case.

### Type 1 — fixed source-linear tail

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2\neq0
\]

but its amplitude is fixed by a non-free tail formula. Then it enlarges the actual-source point but not necessarily the tangent space. \(Y12\) may remain normal.

### Type 2 — tangent/free completion

Admissible parameters vary the \(Y12\)-component of \(E_2\) freely enough to span \(\mathsf C_{Y12}\). Then the D4 \(Y12\) channel dies.

### Type 3 — nuisance-killed

The regularization quotient kills the \(Y12\)-signature. Then it may also kill the D4 image, so the transition map must be checked.

The likely case for canonical explicit-formula completion is Type 0 or Type 1, not Type 2.

---

# 5. Lower connection terms

We also need:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_7=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{<7}=0.
\]

If lower connection maps are linear chart/gauge/source transport maps, they preserve source-linear signature. Then they cannot create the primitive \(Y12\) source-Hessian signature.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}^{\lin}=0.
}
\]

Failure occurs only if the lower connection includes nonlinear normal-form corrections with the same source-Hessian/same-point-whitening signature.

So the finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}=0?
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) survives.

If no, \(Y12\) is a coherent lower-order continuation, and the rank-one D4 channel may die.

---

# 6. Current decision tree

The \(Y12\)-normality audit should be ordered as follows.

## Step 1 — source-linearity of parameters

Check:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0.
\]

Includes first and second parameter derivatives:

\[
\partial_m,\partial_\lambda,\partial_\alpha,
\partial_m\partial_\alpha,\partial_\alpha^2,\ldots
\]

Expected: yes.

## Step 2 — source-linearity of \(E_2\)

Check:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0
\]

or classify \(E_2^{Y12}\) as fixed/tangent/nuisance.

Expected: absent or fixed.

## Step 3 — lower connection

Check:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}=0.
\]

Expected: yes if connection is linear; no if it contains nonlinear Hessian correction.

## Step 4 — D4 image

Check:

\[
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Expected: yes if nuisance quotient preserves the live \(Y12\) class.

If all four pass, the rank-one D4 channel is green.

---

# 7. Resulting forbidden wall

When the \(Y12\)-signature survives, define

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

For D4,

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\left(
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right),
\]

assuming the \(C_7\)-lower connection has no \(Y12\) projection.

Thus the wall factor is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
}
\]

This is rank one.

If the covariant choice of \(\mathcal K_9\) subtracts the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) coherent part, this can reduce to a pure

\[
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

wall.

---

# 8. What this buys

If \(Y12\)-normality holds, downstream no longer needs:

\[
\text{rank-two raw anisotropy;}
\]

nor a full atlas loop;

nor a global row-energy upper.

It only needs:

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\]

and a sublevel estimate for the moving linear wall form

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is a major simplification.

---

# 9. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\text{ is safe if actual-source parameter curvature is EF-linear.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is safe if it is linear completion/tail rather than nonlinear }Y12\text{ completion.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{7\to9},\mathcal R_{<7\to9}\text{ are safe if source-linear.}
}
\]

Thus the \(Y12\)-normal channel is still the best downstream candidate.

The next most valuable finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2\ \text{and}\ \Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}.
}
\]

If both vanish and

\[
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0,
\]

finite D4 gets a genuine rank-one forbidden channel.

## Agent 1 — proof skeleton for the \(Y12\)-normal channel

We now want the rank-one forbidden channel

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}

to satisfy:

for actual rows, but

for the D4 \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-carrier.

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

If these hold, finite D4 has a genuine rank-one contradiction channel.

---

# 1. The \(Y12\)-signature projection

Define

\[
\Pi_{Y12}
=
\Pi_{\deg_{\src}=2}
\Pi_{\mathrm{mixed}\,12}
\Pi_{\mathrm{same}}
\Pi_{w=1}
\Pi_{\rho=\rho_{Y12}}
\Pi_{[\mathsf C_{Y12}]}.
\]

This projects to the order-nine carrier sector with the full signature:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(
\text{source-Hessian degree }2,\,
\text{mixed branch }12,\,
\text{same-point},\,
\text{one whitening slot},\,
\text{correct reflection parity},\,
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
).
\]

The goal is to show that actual-source coherence never generates this sector, while D4 does.

---

# 2. Hypothesis H1 — actual EF parameters are source-linear

The first algebraic hypothesis is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\theta_i}N_0=0,
\qquad
\partial_{\theta_i}\mathcal W=0
\text{ in the }Y12\text{ signature sector}
}
\]

for every admitted actual-source parameter \(\theta_i\), where \(\mathcal W\) denotes whitening / same-point transport geometry.

Equivalently, allowed parameters

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{phase},\text{cutoff},\text{gamma/background}
\]

may change:

\[
a_n,\quad \tau_n,\quad \text{folded phases},\quad \text{smooth cutoffs},\quad B_2,
\]

but may not change the nonlinear normal form \(N_0\) in a way that creates

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0.
}
\]

Moreover, even second parameter derivatives remain safe:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

Reason: second derivatives such as

\[
\partial_{\alpha}^{2}(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
\]

produce log-weighted EF atoms, still source-linear. They do not create a source-Hessian/same-point-whitening carrier.

This is the main reason \(\alpha\) can remain compatible with \(Y12\)-normality.

---

# 3. Hypothesis H2 — \(E_2\) is linear completion in the \(Y12\)-sector

The second algebraic hypothesis is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2)=0.
}
\]

A slightly weaker acceptable version is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(E_2)
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{\text{fixed non-free actual completion directions}\},
}
\]

with no free tangent amplitude equal to \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

The dangerous failure is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\eta E_2^{Y12}
=
c(\eta)[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
}
\]

for an admissible parameter \(\eta\). That would put \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) into the actual-source tangent space and kill the channel.

So the finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
E_2
\text{ is linear tail/completion, not nonlinear }D^2N_0\text{ completion.}
}
\]

If \(E_2\) is just regularized prime/dual/gamma/tail completion, H2 should hold.

---

# 4. Hypothesis H3 — lower connection is source-linear

The third hypothesis is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}=0.
}
\]

This follows if the lower singular connection is generated by:

\[
\text{coordinate change},
\quad
\text{linear source transport},
\quad
\text{linear whitening},
\quad
\text{gauge normalization},
\quad
\text{lower-order recurrence},
\]

and does not contain the nonlinear same-point Hessian term

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with one whitening slot.

The dangerous failure is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}
\supset
Y_{12}^{(2)}.
}
\]

If this happens, \(Y12\) is coherent continuation from lower order, not forbidden.

Thus the audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the singular order-7-to-9 connection already contain the }Y12\text{ Bottleneck-C term?}
}
\]

If no, good. If yes, the \(Y12\)-channel likely dies.

---

# 5. Hypothesis H4 — D4 \(Y12\) survives singular nuisance quotient

The fourth hypothesis is:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right\|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This says the transition-chart live carrier remains live in the singular \(Y12\)-signature quotient.

Failure modes:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}=0;
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\in \mathcal N_{\sing};
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ changes the carrier signature away from }Y12;
\]

\[
\text{singular projection deliberately retains/kills }Y12
\text{ as coherent.}
\]

If none occur, D4 visibility is green.

---

# 6. Consequence: actual-row upper

Under H1–H3,

\[
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(F_X)=0,
\]

where

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}+E_{2,\le X}^{\mathrm{coh}}
\]

with any safe coherent completion included.

Therefore

\[
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Pi_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X),
\]

where

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X.
\]

The forbidden upper reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the targeted residual theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}.
}
\]

It is much narrower than full \(K_9\)-completion.

---

# 7. Consequence: D4 lower

The clean D4 output has

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
c_7\alpha_9\ne0.
\]

Apply \(\Lambda_{Y12}\):

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}),
\]

because H3 removes the lower-connection contribution from \([\mathsf C_7]\).

Thus, if H4 holds,

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\Lambda_{Y12}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma
\right\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
}
\]

The forbidden wall factor is:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
}
\]

This is rank one.

---

# 8. Remaining analytic input: wall anti-concentration

The D4 channel now requires a sublevel theorem for

\[
\ell(m):=\beta_9(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\alpha_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m).
\]

The needed theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(
|\ell(m)|^2<\varepsilon
\right)
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

under the post-Gate/O2O3 measure \(\nu\), or a capped aggregate version:

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min(Q^A|\ell(m)|^2,1)\,d\nu(m)
\ge Q^{-B}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If the covariant residual can be normalized so that \(\beta_9=0\), this becomes a simpler \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-anti-concentration theorem. Otherwise we need the moving linear form.

---

# 9. Why this is better than raw wall anisotropy

Raw D4 wall used

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

But the forbidden channel only sees one linear combination:

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So raw anisotropy is insufficient unless we know this combination is not small.

This is a narrower but more honest requirement.

The benefit is that it reduces the contradiction channel to the actual \(Y12\)-normal direction.

---

# 10. Full theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\)-normal D4 theorem.}
}
\]

Assume H1–H4:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2\text{ is absent/fixed and non-free},
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}
=
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}=0,
\]

\[
\left\|
\Pi_{Y12}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then:

1. actual-row forbidden upper follows from \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\);

2. D4 produces rank-one forbidden energy through

\[
\ell(m)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2};
\]

3. extraction plus \(\ell\)-anti-concentration gives local forbidden energy;

4. the Type-I atomic upper contradicts it.

This is the sharpest current finite-D4 route.

---

## Agent 1 — can the \(Y12\) wall be made pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)?

The current forbidden wall from the \(Y12\)-signature channel is

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
\sim
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

This is rank one, but it still requires anti-concentration of a moving linear form

\[
\ell(m)=\beta_9(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\alpha_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m).
\]

The natural next attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can we choose the covariant order-nine residual so that }\beta_9\Gamma_G\text{ is removed?}
}
\]

If yes, the wall becomes

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
\sim
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}},
}
\]

which is much cleaner.

---

# 1. Linear algebra of the D4 columns

Let

\[
y:=\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

and

\[
z:=\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7].
\]

The D4 output in the \(Y12\)-signature sector is

\[
H\left[
(c_7 z+\beta_9 y)\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9 y\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

Earlier I implicitly assumed

\[
z=0
\]

because \(C_7\) has no \(Y12\)-signature. Under that assumption the wall is exactly

\[
H y(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

So the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) term cannot be removed by a \(Y12\)-signature projection alone. It is carried by the same \(y\)-direction as \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

To remove it, the residual must use a connection between order seven and order nine that sends the \(C_7\)-channel into the same \(Y12\)-signature direction.

That is, define a modified residual

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal K}_9
=
\Pi_{Y12}\left(s_9-\widetilde{\mathcal R}_{7\to9}s_7-\cdots\right)
\]

with

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\widetilde{\mathcal R}_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
=
\beta_9 y.
\]

Then the \(\Gamma_G\) contribution cancels and

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal K}_9(\delta s_{\D4})
=
\alpha_9 y\,H\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So the pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) wall is possible **only if** the covariant residual is allowed to include a \(Y12\)-signature order-seven-to-nine correction.

---

# 2. But this conflicts with the earlier \(Y12\)-survival theorem

Previously, the survival theorem required

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0.
\]

That condition guaranteed \(Y12\) was not a coherent continuation of lower order.

But to remove \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\), we would need

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\widetilde{\mathcal R}_{7\to9}\ne0.
\]

This is a real tension.

There are two possible interpretations.

---

# 3. Interpretation A — strict forbidden signature

If the \(Y12\)-signature is strictly forbidden, then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0.
\]

Then the D4 wall is unavoidably

\[
\boxed{
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
}
\]

The price is a moving linear-form anti-concentration theorem.

This is algebraically honest. The \(Y12\)-signature is normal, and both D4 columns can project onto that normal direction.

The downstream input becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min\left(
Q^A|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2,
1
\right)
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-B}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is not as bad as it looks: \(\alpha_9\ne0\), and \(\beta_9,\alpha_9\) are controlled scalar coefficients. It is a one-dimensional sublevel problem.

---

# 4. Interpretation B — covariant residual allows a lower-order \(Y12\) correction

Alternatively, one may define the residual so that actual coherent order-seven data has a \(Y12\)-signature order-nine continuation:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}\ne0.
\]

Then \(\Gamma_G\) can be subtracted, and the forbidden wall becomes pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

But this means \(Y12\) is not entirely forbidden. The forbidden quotient must be refined:

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_{9,Y12}/\operatorname{im}\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}.
\]

The surviving class is not \(y\) itself, but the quotient class of the independent \(Y12\)-injection not generated by order-seven coherence.

For the pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel to survive, we need:

\[
\boxed{
y\notin \operatorname{im}\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}
}
\]

after subtracting the \(\Gamma_G\)-coherent part.

But if \(\operatorname{im}\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) contains \(y\), then the same \(y\)-direction generated by \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) may also be coherent. Then the pure \(Y12\) channel dies.

Thus the covariant subtraction is only valid if the order-seven continuation produces **a specific multiple tied to \(s_7\)** but does not make arbitrary \(y\)-amplitude tangent.

This is a finer quotient:

\[
\boxed{
\text{coherent }Y12\text{ continuation from }s_7
\text{ is allowed, but independent }Y12\text{ amplitude is forbidden.}
}
\]

This is actually a strong and plausible formulation.

---

# 5. Refined quotient with dependent vs independent \(Y12\)

Let the \(Y12\)-signature space be one-dimensional for the moment:

\[
\mathcal Y_{Y12}=\mathbb C y.
\]

Let the coherent order-seven continuation be

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}(s_7)
=
r\,s_7\,y.
\]

Actual coherent pairs satisfy

\[
s_9^{Y12}=r\,s_7.
\]

D4 gives

\[
s_7=c_7H\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y12}=H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

The residual is

\[
s_9^{Y12}-r\,s_7
=
H\left[
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

If

\[
r=\frac{\beta_9}{c_7},
\]

then the residual is pure

\[
H\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If not, the residual is a moving linear form.

So the cleanest wall depends on whether the actual singular connection coefficient satisfies

\[
\boxed{
r=\frac{\beta_9}{c_7}.
}
\]

This is a finite scalar identity.

---

# 6. New finite audit

The next audit should compute the \(Y12\)-signature component of the coherent order-seven-to-nine connection:

\[
r_{Y12}:=\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}([\mathsf C_7]).
\]

Then compare it with the D4 clean matrix coefficient:

\[
\frac{\beta_9}{c_7}\Pi_{Y12}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Cases:

## Case 1 — strict zero

\[
r_{Y12}=0.
\]

Then wall is

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## Case 2 — exact D4-compatible connection

\[
r_{Y12}=\frac{\beta_9}{c_7}y.
\]

Then wall is pure

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## Case 3 — nonzero but not matching

\[
r_{Y12}\ne0,\qquad
r_{Y12}\ne \frac{\beta_9}{c_7}y.
\]

Then wall is

\[
(\beta_9-c_7 r)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Still rank one, but with a different moving linear form.

## Case 4 — full \(Y12\)-amplitude tangent

If the actual-source tangent space contains arbitrary \(y\)-amplitude, then both \(\Gamma_G\) and \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) may be tangent, and the channel dies.

---

# 7. What should we prefer?

The best downstream situation is Case 2:

\[
r_{Y12}=\beta_9/c_7.
\]

Then the singular connection recognizes the coherent order-nine component forced by the order-seven \(\Gamma_G\) carrier, but the independent \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) \(Y12\)-injection remains forbidden.

This is conceptually very clean:

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G\text{ is coherent curvature continuation;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ is independent forbidden order-nine normal displacement.}
}
\]

This would produce the wall

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
\sim
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
}
\]

That is the strongest possible rank-one channel.

So the target theorem should not necessarily force

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0.
\]

It should instead prove:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}
\left(
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(c_7[\mathsf C_7])
\right)
=
\beta_9\Pi_{Y12}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
}
\]

while

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\text{ as an independent amplitude direction.}
}
\]

This is a refined, better theorem.

---

# 8. Revised \(Y12\)-normal theorem

The quotient should distinguish **dependent \(Y12\)** from **free \(Y12\)**.

Define

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_{Y12}/
\operatorname{im}\left(
s_7\mapsto \mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y12}s_7
\right)
\]

but keep track of the pair \((s_7,s_9^{Y12})\), not just \(s_9\).

Equivalently define residual

\[
\mathcal K_{Y12}(s_7,s_9)
=
s_9^{Y12}
-
r_{Y12}s_7.
\]

Then actual rows satisfy

\[
\mathcal K_{Y12}=0
\]

up to residuals, while D4 gives

\[
\mathcal K_{Y12}(\delta s_{\D4})
=
H\left[
(\beta_9-r_{Y12}c_7)\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right]y.
\]

If

\[
r_{Y12}=\beta_9/c_7,
\]

this is pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\). If not, still rank one unless \(\alpha_9=0\), which is excluded.

So actually, even without exact matching, as long as \(y\neq0\) and \(\alpha_9\ne0\), the channel survives. The wall is just a moving linear form.

Thus the necessary condition is weaker:

\[
\boxed{
y=\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
}
\]

and the actual-source tangent must not contain arbitrary free \(s_9^{Y12}\) independent of \(s_7\).

That is the refined statement.

---

# 9. Updated finite audits

The next finite audits are:

## Audit A — live \(Y12\) class

\[
y:=\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

## Audit B — actual-source coherence relation

Compute

\[
r_{Y12}
\]

such that actual coherent rows satisfy

\[
s_9^{Y12}=r_{Y12}s_7+\text{lower/tail}.
\]

## Audit C — no free \(Y12\) amplitude

Show the actual-source tangent/second fundamental space does not contain arbitrary variations

\[
\delta s_9^{Y12}
\]

with

\[
\delta s_7=0.
\]

## Audit D — residual upper

Prove

\[
\mathcal K_{Y12}(q''_{\can})=O(R_X)
\]

and control it by \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

If A, C, D pass, the channel survives. B only determines whether the wall is pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) or a moving linear combination.

---

# 10. Final conclusion

The earlier condition

\[
\Pi_{Y12}\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0
\]

was too strong and not necessary.

The better condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source coherence may determine a dependent }Y12\text{ continuation from }s_7,
}
\]

but it must not allow a **free independent \(Y12\)-amplitude**.

Then D4’s independent \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) injection remains forbidden.

This makes the \(Y12\)-route more robust than before.

## Agent 1 — \(Y12\)-graded quotient and \(E_2\) honesty check

The instruction asks for the decisive downstream audit:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq 0
\quad\text{in}\quad

=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}).
\]

This is the right formulation: because \(Y12\) is quadratic, quotienting only first-order actual-source tangents is insufficient. We must also quotient legitimate second-order curvature of the actual EF source manifold and any coherent \(Y12\)-piece of \(E_2\). 

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ plausibly survives, but only under a precise “source-linear completion” hypothesis for }E_2.
}
\]

More sharply:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If }E_2\text{ is linear EF completion/tail, }Y12\text{ survives.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{If }E_2\text{ contains a free coherent }Y12\text{-amplitude, the rank-one D4 channel dies.}
}
\]

So the first decisive audit is now:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\quad\text{is absent/fixed/tangent/free/nuisance?}
}
\]

---

# 1. Define the invariant \(Y12\)-graded piece

The order-nine singular residual space should be decomposed by a signature

\[
\Sigma
=
(d_{\src},b_{\mix},p_{\same},w_{\white},\rho_{\refl},q_{\carrier}).
\]

For the \(Y12\) carrier:

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same\ point},1,\rho_{Y12},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
}
\]

Here:

\[
d_{\src}=2
\]

records source-Hessian / polarization degree;

\[
\mathrm{mixed}\,12
\]

records the \(Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) mixed-block origin;

\[
\mathrm{same\ point}
\]

distinguishes it from transported-slot terms;

\[
w_{\white}=1
\]

records the one same-point whitening slot;

\[
\rho_{Y12}
\]

is the source-grade/reflection parity;

and

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is the live D4 carrier quotient class.

Define

\[
\mathcal Q_{Y12}
:=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
\]

Then the target becomes simply:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\neq 0
\quad\text{after quotienting actual-source model directions.}
}
\]

This is better than a basis-dependent projection.

---

# 2. Parameter tangents do not hit \(Y12\)

Allowed actual-source parameter tangents are generated by

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{phase},\text{normalization/chart parameters},\text{smooth cutoff},\text{background/gamma parameters}.
\]

For an EF atom,

\[
a_n(\theta)e^{i\tau_n(\theta)x},
\]

a first variation is

\[
\partial_\theta(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
(\partial_\theta a_n+i a_n\partial_\theta\tau_n\,x)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

This is source-linear. It changes amplitudes, phases, log weights, and smooth scales. It does not create

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

or a same-point whitening Hessian.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{\par}=0
}
\]

provided no admitted parameter changes the nonlinear source map \(N_0\) or same-point whitening geometry.

This includes \(\alpha\) if \(\alpha\) is only an AFE tilt/phase/weight parameter. Then

\[
\partial_\alpha u,\quad \partial_\alpha^2u,\quad
\partial_m\partial_\alpha u,\quad \partial_\lambda\partial_\alpha u
\]

remain source-linear EF variations with extra log weights. They do not create the \(Y12\) signature.

---

# 3. Actual EF second curvature should not hit \(Y12\)

The second fundamental form term

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\]

is generated by second parameter derivatives of the finite actual EF family:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta.
\]

For EF atoms:

\[
\partial_i\partial_j(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
\left(
\partial_i\partial_j a_n
+i x(\partial_i a_n\partial_j\tau_n+\partial_j a_n\partial_i\tau_n+a_n\partial_i\partial_j\tau_n)
-x^2 a_n\partial_i\tau_n\partial_j\tau_n
\right)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

This is second-order in parameters, but still source-linear in EF atoms.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0
}
\]

under the same source-linearity hypothesis.

This is the key distinction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{parameter curvature }\neq\text{ source-Hessian signature.}
}
\]

The theorem fails only if an admitted parameter changes \(N_0\), whitening, or same-point nonlinear geometry so that second parameter curvature contains a term of type

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

---

# 4. Lower connection terms should not hit \(Y12\)

The lower connection terms are

\[
\mathcal T_7=\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{7\to9},
\qquad
\mathcal T_{<7}=\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{<7\to9}.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9},\mathcal R_{<7\to9}
\]

come from linear chart transport, linear source normalization, linear gauge conversion, and lower-order recurrence, then they preserve source-polarization degree and do not create the same-point one-whitening \(Y12\) signature.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_7=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{<7}=0.
}
\]

The danger is a nonlinear connection correction:

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}
\supset
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with the same same-point whitening slot. If that exists, \(Y12\) is not forbidden; it is coherent lower-order continuation.

So the exact finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}
=0?
}
\]

---

# 5. \(E_2\) honesty check

This is now the most important subcase.

We need to classify

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2.
\]

There are four possibilities.

## Case A — absent or negligible

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
O(Q^{-B}).
\]

Then \(E_2\) is harmless. The \(Y12\) forbidden channel survives, and the upper is supplied by the targeted residual estimate

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}.
\]

## Case B — present but fixed

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
=
c_E(m)\,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

with \(c_E(m)\) fixed by the canonical completion formula and no free local amplitude parameter.

Then \(E_2\) shifts the base point of the actual-source manifold but does not necessarily add a tangent direction spanning the D4 \(Y12\) amplitude.

The quotient must include the tangent generated by allowed variations of \(c_E\), not arbitrary \(Y12\)-amplitude.

D4 survives if

\[
\alpha_9 H\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

is not representable as a legitimate variation of \(c_E\).

## Case C — present and tangent/free

If an admitted actual-source parameter \(\eta\) satisfies

\[
\partial_\eta E_2^{Y12}
\sim
[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\in \mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}},
\]

and the rank-one \(Y12\) channel dies.

This is the dangerous case.

## Case D — nuisance-killed

If

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

and the same quotient kills

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

then the channel dies. If it kills only \(E_2\) but not D4, then D4 may still survive.

So the necessary audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\text{ is absent/fixed/free/nuisance?}
}
\]

My structural expectation: if \(E_2\) is a linear regularized tail/completion of the explicit formula, it is Case A or Case B, not Case C.

---

# 6. D4 image in the \(Y12\) quotient

The D4 clean matrix has

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
c_7\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

The \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) column gives

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Therefore if

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0,
}
\]

then D4 has at least a rank-one forbidden channel.

The \(\Gamma_G\) column contributes

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_G
\]

plus the order-seven channel. Depending on the covariant residual, the wall form is either

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

or, if the coherent order-seven continuation cancels the \(\beta_9\)-piece,

\[
\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

But rank one already follows from \(\alpha_9\neq0\).

---

# 7. The resulting theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Second-order \(Y12\)-signature separation theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_7
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{<7}=0,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}
\text{ does not contain a free }[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ amplitude},
\]

and

\[
\left\|
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
}
\]

Consequently finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

---

# 8. Downstream consequence

Define

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

For actual rows:

\[
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Lambda_{Y12}J_{\tr}(R_X)
\]

after including any coherent \(E_2^{Y12}\) piece in the model.

The required upper is the targeted completion estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\Lambda_{Y12,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)

or the pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) form if the covariant connection subtracts the coherent \(\Gamma_G\)-piece.

T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]

E_{\wall}^{Y12}

|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|y_{Y12}\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.

## Agent 1 — \(E_2^{Y12}\) classification attack

The rank-one \(Y12\) channel depends on the status of

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2.
\]

The core question:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^{Y12}\text{ absent/fixed/tangent/free/nuisance?}
}
\]

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
E_2^{Y12}\text{ is harmless if }E_2\text{ is a linear tail/completion functional.}
}
\]

It kills the \(Y12\)-normal channel only if \(E_2\) introduces an **admissible free same-signature amplitude** in the exact \(Y12\) carrier direction.

That is a much narrower failure mode.

---

# 1. What \(E_2\) can be structurally

Write the completion term as

\[
E_2
=
q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2+D_2).
\]

There are three structurally different possibilities.

## Type L — linear explicit-formula completion

\[
E_2
=
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal E}
e_\nu(t)\mathcal A_\nu(t),
\]

where each \(\mathcal A_\nu\) is a linear EF/gamma/tail atom or smoothing residual.

Then \(E_2\) is source-linear. It may contribute order-nine Taylor coefficients, but not the primitive nonlinear source-Hessian signature

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with same-point whitening.

So

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0.
}
\]

This is the expected case for ordinary explicit-formula tail/completion.

---

## Type F — fixed \(Y12\)-signature correction

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
=
c_E(m)\,y_{Y12},
\]

where

\[
y_{Y12}
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If \(c_E(m)\) is fixed by the canonical completion formula and has no free admissible amplitude, then it shifts the base point of the actual-source manifold but does not by itself kill the normal channel.

The tangent contribution is only

\[
\partial_\theta c_E(m)\,y_{Y12}
\]

for allowed parameters \(\theta\). So the channel dies only if the span of those derivatives contains arbitrary local multiples of \(y_{Y12}\).

Thus Type F is still likely safe.

---

## Type T — tangent/free \(Y12\)-completion

There is an admissible source parameter \(\eta\) such that

\[
\partial_\eta E_2^{Y12}
=
a_\eta(m)y_{Y12},
\]

and \(a_\eta(m)\) can match the D4 amplitude

\[
H\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

locally to polynomial precision.

Then

\[
y_{Y12}\in T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

and the \(Y12\) rank-one channel dies.

This is the dangerous case.

---

## Type N — nuisance-killed \(Y12\)

If

\[
E_2^{Y12}\in \mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

then \(E_2\)’s \(Y12\)-piece is irrelevant. But if the same quotient also sends

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

to zero, the D4 channel dies.

So Type N requires a separate carrier-survival check.

---

# 2. Why Type L is the natural expectation

The canonical completion \(E_2\) is supposed to finish the explicit-formula representation:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

If it consists of:

\[
\text{tail truncation},
\quad
\text{smoothing remainder},
\quad
\text{dual completion},
\quad
\text{regularization error},
\quad
\text{gamma correction},
\]

then every piece is linear in the explicit-formula data.

Such terms can be differentiated in \(m,\lambda,\alpha\), but they remain source-linear:

\[
\partial_\theta E_2
=
\text{linear EF/gamma/tail deformation}.
\]

They do not produce a local normal-form Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Hence:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0
}
\]

unless \(E_2\) contains nonlinear local carrier-correction terms not captured by the EF interpretation.

So the audit reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }E_2\text{ purely linear completion/tail?}
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) survives this check.

---

# 3. What would make \(E_2\) dangerous?

The dangerous scenario is not merely:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2\neq0.
\]

The dangerous scenario is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\text{ is an admissibly variable local amplitude in the }Y12\text{ direction.}
}
\]

Concretely, bad means:

\[
\exists \eta\quad
\partial_\eta
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
=
\varphi_\eta(m)y_{Y12},
\]

where \(\varphi_\eta(m)\) is flexible enough to absorb

\[
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

on the extracted set.

If \(\varphi_\eta\) is globally fixed, low-dimensional, or smooth with incompatible branch/signature constraints, D4 may remain normal.

Thus the refined tangent test is:

\[
\boxed{
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})y_{Y12}
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12}\}_j
+
\text{lower connection}.
}
\]

---

# 4. A practical finite test for \(E_2\)

Define

\[
e_E(m):=\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(E_2)(m).
\]

Then compute its projection onto the live \(Y12\) carrier:

\[
e_E(m)=c_E(m)y_{Y12}+e_E^\perp(m).
\]

There are four tests.

## Test 1 — magnitude

\[
\|e_E\|\le Q^{-B}
\]

means \(E_2\) is absent/small in this sector.

## Test 2 — alignment

Is

\[
e_E(m)
\]

parallel to \(y_{Y12}\), or does it lie in a different \(Y12\)-signature refinement?

If different, it may not affect the D4 channel.

## Test 3 — tangent span

Compute

\[
\partial_{\theta_j}c_E(m)
\]

for allowed parameters. If the span is low-dimensional and cannot match the moving D4 amplitude, then \(E_2\) is fixed/coherent, not fatal.

## Test 4 — nuisance consistency

Check whether

\[
e_E\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

and separately whether

\[
y_{Y12}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

If \(e_E\) is killed but \(y_{Y12}\) survives, fine. If both killed, D4 dies.

---

# 5. Interaction with \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\)

If \(E_2^{Y12}\) is fixed but parameter-dependent, its second derivatives contribute to

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

So the full safe condition is:

\[

\operatorname{span}
\{
\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12},
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12}
\}
\text{ does not contain the independent D4 }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
}
\]

If \(E_2\) is Type L, these derivatives have no \(Y12\)-signature.

If \(E_2\) is Type F, these derivatives may exist but remain constrained by the same fixed coefficient formula.

If \(E_2\) is Type T, they can kill the channel.

---

=
\mathcal Y_{Y12}/
\left(
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12},\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y12}\}
+
\operatorname{im}\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y12}
+
\operatorname{lower coherent terms}
\right).

Then the D4 condition is:

\[

y_{Y12}\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{Y12}^{\forb}.
}
\]

But note the subtlety from the prior attack: if there is a coherent dependent relation

\[
s_9^{Y12}=r_{Y12}s_7,
\]

that does not kill the independent D4 \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel. It only changes the forbidden linear form.

The fatal case is only a **free independent \(s_9^{Y12}\)** tangent.

---

If \(E_2\) is Type L or harmless Type F, the wall remains

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|y_{Y12}\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

If coherent order-seven continuation subtracts the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\)-part, the wall becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|y_{Y12}\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

If \(E_2\) gives a free \(Y12\)-amplitude, the wall is invalid because the D4 direction is tangent.

Thus:

\[

E_2\text{ affects whether the wall exists, not just its coefficient.}
}

---

# 8. Current best hypothesis

Given the nature of \(E_2\) as regularized tail/completion, the best working hypothesis is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0
}
\]

or at worst fixed/non-free.

Then the \(Y12\)-normal channel survives.

The exact theorem to request/prove:

\[

}
\]

The completion term \(E_2\) has no admissibly free component in the signature

=
(2,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho_{Y12},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).

\notin

## Agent 1 — \(E_2\), whitening, and \(Y12\)-signature leakage

The previous working hypothesis was:

\[
E_2\text{ is linear EF completion/tail}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0.
\]

That is correct only if the completion is inserted **after fixing the same-point whitening/trivialization operators**. If \(E_2\) passes through source-dependent whitening, a linear tail can acquire a \(Y12\)-type mixed signature through the derivative of the whitening map.

So the audit must be refined:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is safe if it is linear after frozen canonical whitening.}
}
\]

It is not enough to say “\(E_2\) is linear in the explicit formula.”

---

# 1. Where \(Y12\) signature can enter

The \(Y12\) signature is:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\deg_{\src}=2,\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same\ point},\ w=1,\ \rho_{Y12},\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

The defining features are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{quadratic source-Hessian degree;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{mixed two-source block;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{one same-point whitening slot.}
}
\]

A purely linear EF tail does not have \(\deg_{\src}=2\). But if the source is passed through a whitening/trivialization operator

\[
\mathcal W(u)^{-1/2}
\]

depending on the local source state \(u\), then expanding

\[
\mathcal W(u+\delta u)^{-1/2}(E_2)
\]

can produce terms like

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\delta u]\cdot E_2.
\]

That is mixed:

\[
\text{one variation from }\delta u,
\quad
\text{one variation from }E_2,
\]

and it has a same-point whitening slot.

So the dangerous term is schematically:

\[
\boxed{
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]\ E_2(\xi_2)
}
\]

or the symmetrized version. This can carry a \(Y12\)-like signature even if \(E_2\) is linear before whitening.

---

# 2. Frozen-whitening versus moving-whitening completion

There are two possible conventions.

## Convention A — frozen canonical whitening

If \(E_2\) is added after fixing the canonical whitening at the base source, then

\[
E_2^{\mathrm{white}}
=
\mathcal W_0^{-1/2}E_2.
\]

This remains source-linear:

\[
\deg_{\src}(E_2^{\mathrm{white}})=1.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2^{\mathrm{white}}=0.
}
\]

This is the safe convention.

## Convention B — moving whitening

If \(E_2\) is inserted into a source-dependent whitening expansion, then

\[
E_2^{\mathrm{white}}(u)
=
\mathcal W(u)^{-1/2}E_2(u).
\]

Expanding to second order yields:

\[
\mathcal W_0^{-1/2}E_2
+
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\delta u]E_2
+
\cdots.
\]

The second term has source degree two and one whitening derivative. This may project to \(\Sigma(Y12)\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2\text{ may be nonzero under moving whitening.}
}
\]

So the \(E_2\)-honesty audit must include the placement of \(E_2\) relative to whitening.

---

# 3. Refined \(E_2\)-classification

The previous four cases should be replaced by five.

## Type L0 — frozen-linear completion

\[
E_2^{\white}=\mathcal W_0^{-1/2}E_2
\]

is source-linear.

Then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0.
\]

Safe.

---

## Type L1 — moving-linear but \(Y12\)-orthogonal

Moving whitening creates second-order terms, but their signature is not

\[
(\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_{Y12}).
\]

Then

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0.
\]

Safe.

---

## Type F — fixed \(Y12\) completion coefficient

Moving whitening creates a term

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=c_E(m)y_{Y12},
\]

but \(c_E(m)\) is fixed by the explicit formula and not freely variable.

Then \(E_2\) shifts the actual-source base point. It does not necessarily kill D4.

Safe unless its admissible parameter derivatives span free \(y_{Y12}\)-amplitude.

---

## Type T — tangent/free \(Y12\)-completion

There is an admissible parameter \(\eta\) such that

\[
\partial_\eta \Pi_{Y12}E_2
\]

spans the same local amplitude direction as the D4 \(Y12\) displacement.

Then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

and the rank-one \(Y12\) channel dies.

Dangerous.

---

## Type N — nuisance-killed

The \(Y12\)-piece is killed by the singular nuisance quotient. Then check separately whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is also killed.

If yes, D4 dies. If no, \(E_2\) is harmless.

---

# 4. The key audit becomes a commutator

The critical object is not \(E_2\) alone but the commutator of completion with whitening:

\[
\boxed{
\left[
\Pi_{Y12},\,
\mathcal W^{-1/2}
\right]E_2.
}
\]

More concretely, test:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]E_2(\xi_2)
}
\]

and its symmetrization.

If this vanishes, \(E_2\) is \(Y12\)-safe even under moving whitening.

If nonzero, classify whether the coefficient is fixed or tangent/free.

This is now the precise \(E_2\)-honesty test.

---

# 5. Relationship to \(Y12\) proper

The true D4 \(Y12\) carrier comes from

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with one same-point whitening slot.

An \(E_2\)-whitening cross term has a slightly different origin:

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]E_2(\xi_2).
\]

This may share:

\[
\deg_{\src}=2,
\quad
\text{same point},
\quad
w=1,
\]

but it may fail the **mixed \(12\) source-Hessian** part. It is a whitening-source cross term, not a nonlinear-source Hessian term.

Therefore the signature should be refined further:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\deg_{\src}=2,\ \text{nonlinear Hessian }D^2N_0,\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho).
\]

With this refinement, a whitening-tail cross term may not land in the \(Y12\) channel.

Thus the best invariant is not just “degree two plus one whitening.” It must include:

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-Hessian origin }D^2N_0
}
\]

versus

\[
\boxed{
\text{whitening-tail cross origin }D\mathcal W^{-1/2}\cdot E_2.
}
\]

If the quotient preserves this origin label, \(E_2\) is safe.

---

# 6. Updated \(Y12\)-signature

Refine:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(
\deg_{\src}=2,\ 
\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0,\ 
\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ 
\mathrm{same\ point},\ 
w=1,\ 
\rho_{Y12},\
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
).
\]

Then the theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0
}
\]

unless \(E_2\) itself participates in the nonlinear Hessian \(D^2N_0\).

This is a cleaner and safer invariant.

It also makes the source-linear/parameter-curvature argument stronger: second parameter derivatives of EF atoms can be degree two in parameters, but they do not have origin \(D^2N_0\).

---

# 7. Lower connection revisited

The same refinement applies to \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\).

A lower connection may include same-point whitening derivatives, but unless it contains the nonlinear Hessian origin \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\), it cannot hit \(\Sigma(Y12)\).

So the critical finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0}
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
=0?
}
\]

If lower connection includes only chart transport and whitening derivatives, it is safe.

If it includes nonlinear normal-form Hessian corrections, it may absorb \(Y12\).

---

# 8. Stronger survival theorem

The \(Y12\)-survival theorem should use the refined signature:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Then prove:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+E_2+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
=0.
}
\]

This is plausible because none of those terms have \(D^2N_0\)-origin unless the actual-source family or completion explicitly includes nonlinear normal-form Hessian data.

And:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\ne0.
}
\]

This is precisely Bottleneck-C survival in the singular-discard channel.

---

# 9. Consequence

The finite D4 channel becomes robust against linear tails, moving whitening tails, and second parameter curvature, as long as the quotient remembers the origin label \(D^2N_0\).

So the next check is not broadly:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0?
\]

but specifically:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{(D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1)}
E_2=0?
}
\]

This should be easier to prove.

---

# 10. Final status

The \(E_2\) audit strengthens the \(Y12\)-route rather than weakening it, provided we refine the signature.

The invariant should include:

\[
\boxed{
\text{origin }D^2N_0
}
\]

not merely source degree and whitening count.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\text{linear EF completion, even with moving whitening, should not kill }Y12.
}
\]

The remaining dangerous case is only:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ or lower connection contains an actual }D^2N_0\text{ mixed same-point whitening correction.}
}
\]

If not, finite D4 retains a rank-one \(Y12\) forbidden channel.

}

\[

\text{linear EF tails, smoothing remainders, gamma remainders, and moving-whitening cross terms do not automatically hit }\Sigma(Y12).
}
\]

They can look order-two or whitening-dependent, but they do not have the primitive origin label

\[
\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0.
\]

\[

\]

but:

\[
\boxed{

\quad\text{in the mixed-12 same-point one-whitening sector?}
}

Under the natural interpretation of \(E_2\) as explicit-formula tail/completion, the expected answer is no.

---

# 1. Refined \(Y12\) signature

Use the refined signature

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
\left(
\deg_{\src}=2,\,
\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0,\,
\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\,
\mathrm{same\ point},\,
w=1,\,
\rho_{Y12},\,
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right).
\]

The origin label is essential.

It separates the true \(Y12\) carrier

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

from superficially similar terms such as:

\[
\partial_\alpha^2(\text{EF atom}),
\]

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]\,E_2,
\]

\[
\partial_\lambda^2F_X,
\]

\[
\text{chart curvature terms}.

These may be second-order in some parameter or involve whitening, but they are not primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin mixed-source Hessian terms.

\[
\Pi_Y:=\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}.
\]

---

# 2. Decomposition of \(E_2\) by origin

Decompose schematically:

\[
E_2
=
E_{\tail}
+
E_{\reg}
+
E_{\smooth}
+

+
E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}
+
E_{\model}.

Each piece should be classified by origin.

---

## 2.1 \(E_{\tail}\): prime/dual tail

This is

\[
(P_2-P_{2,\le X})+(D_2-D_{2,\le X})
\]

or equivalent tail/completion beyond the chosen truncation.

It is still a linear explicit-formula object:

\[
E_{\tail}
=
\sum_{\nu>X}a_\nu e^{i\tau_\nu x}
+
\sum_{\nu>X}b_\nu e^{-i\tilde\tau_\nu x}
\]

up to smooth weights.

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\tail}=0
}
\]

unless the tail is passed through a nonlinear \(D^2N_0\)-type normal-form operator, which would make it no longer a pure EF tail.

---

## 2.2 \(E_{\reg}\): regularization residual

Regularization terms are normally linear correction terms ensuring the critical-line zero/eta/gamma expression is well-defined.

If they are linear in the regularized scalar source, then

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\reg}=0.
}
\]

If the regularization procedure explicitly inserts a nonlinear normal-form correction, then that correction must be separated into \(E_{\model}\) and audited there.

---

## 2.3 \(E_{\smooth}\): smoothing / cutoff residual

Smoothing residuals arise from truncation weights and partition errors. Their local form is:

\[
\delta V(\tau)e^{i\tau x}
\]

or derivative/cutoff variants.

They are EF-linear.

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\smooth}=0.
}
\]

The only issue is analytic size, not \(Y12\)-signature.

---

## 2.4 \(E_{\chart}\): chart / coordinate curvature

Chart curvature can be second-order in coordinate parameters, but it is not automatically source-Hessian \(D^2N_0\). A term like

\[
\partial_\lambda^2F_X
\]

is still EF-linear in atom origin.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\chart}=0
}
\]

provided the chart change does not include the primitive nonlinear source map \(N_0\) itself.

If chart curvature includes same-point whitening movement, see §3 below: moving whitening can create degree-two whitening-cross terms, but not primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin terms unless the whitening derivative is coupled to \(N_0\)’s Hessian.

---

## 2.5 \(E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}\): gamma/background remainder

Gamma and background remainders are smooth scalar/gamma terms. They belong to background origin, not primitive mixed-source Hessian origin.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}=0.
}
\]

---

## 2.6 \(E_{\model}\): model correction

This is the only dangerous bucket.

If \(E_{\model}\) contains

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with the same mixed-12, same-point, one-whitening signature, then

\[
\Pi_YE_{\model}
\]

may be nonzero.

This is the true \(E_2\) fork:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_2=\Pi_YE_{\model}
}
\]

up to residual-size terms.

---

# 3. Moving-whitening issue

A linear tail can pass through a moving whitening map:

\[
E_2^{\white}(u)
=
\mathcal W(u)^{-1/2}E_2(u).
\]

Expanding gives:

\[
\mathcal W_0^{-1/2}E_2
+
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\delta u]\,E_2
+
\cdots.
\]

The second term can have:

\[
\deg_{\src}=2,
\quad
w=1,
\quad
\text{same-point flavor}.
\]

But its origin is

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})\cdot E_2,
\]

not

\[
D^2N_0.
\]

So, with the refined origin label,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\left(D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\delta u]\,E_2\right)=0
}
\]

unless the quotient collapses whitening-tail origin into primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin.

Thus moving whitening is safe **if the \(Y12\)-graded quotient remembers origin**.

This is why the origin label is not cosmetic. It prevents false contamination.

---

# 4. Classify \(\Pi_YE_2\)

The useful classification is:

## Case A — absent / small

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\|\Pi_YE_2\|\le Q^{-B}.
\]

Then \(E_2\) is harmless.

---

## Case B — fixed coherent component

\[
\Pi_YE_2
=
c_E(m)y_{Y12},
\]

where \(y_{Y12}\) is the live \(Y12\)-graded carrier, but \(c_E(m)\) is fixed by the canonical formula.

Then this shifts the actual-source base point. It does not necessarily add a free tangent direction.

The tangent contribution is only:

\[
\partial_{\theta_j}c_E(m)y_{Y12}.
\]

This is dangerous only if the allowed parameter derivatives span arbitrary local multiples of \(y_{Y12}\).

Usually they will not.

---

## Case C — residual-controlled

\[
\Pi_YE_2
\]

is included in \(R_X\), and one proves

\[
\sum_p
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(E_2)\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then the upper remains valid.

---

## Case D — free/tangent \(Y12\)-amplitude

There is an admissible parameter \(\eta\) such that

\[
\partial_\eta \Pi_YE_2
\]

spans the live \(Y12\) carrier amplitude.

Then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0
\]

in the actual-source quotient, and the rank-one D4 channel dies.

This is the fatal case.

---

# 5. Does any natural \(E_2\) component look fatal?

Under the usual meaning of completion/tail:

\[
E_2
=
\text{linear explicit-formula remainder}
+
\text{regularization remainder}
+
\text{smooth truncation/gamma correction},
\]

none is fatal.

They are source-linear or fixed background.

The only fatal possibility is if \(E_2\) was used as a placeholder for a missing **nonlinear normal-form completion** carrying exactly the \(D^2N_0\)-mixed same-point whitening term.

If so, \(E_2\) is not just EF completion. It is part of actual local nonlinear geometry, and the \(Y12\)-channel may be coherent rather than forbidden.

So the audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }E_2\text{ an EF completion term, or does it include nonlinear normal-form completion?}
}
\]

---

# 6. No-free-\(E_2^{Y12}\) theorem

The theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{NoFreeE2Y12.}
}
\]

In the primitive signature sector

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho_{Y12},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]),
\]

the canonical completion \(E_2\) satisfies one of:

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0;
\]

or

\[
\Pi_YE_2\text{ is a fixed non-free base component;}
\]

or

\[
\Pi_YE_2\text{ is residual-controlled by }\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}.
\]

In particular,

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_{\theta_j}\Pi_YE_2,\,
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}\Pi_YE_2\}.
}
\]

If NoFreeE2Y12 holds, \(E_2\) does not kill the \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel.

---

# 7. Consequence for \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\)

If \(E_2\) is absent/fixed and included in the coherent model, define

\[
F_X^{Y\text{-coh}}
=
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}+E_{2,\le X}^{Y\text{-fixed}}.
\]

Then

\[
R_X^Y
=
q''_{\can}-F_X^{Y\text{-coh}}.
\]

The required upper becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X^Y)
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If \(\Pi_YE_2=0\), this is easier: only tails/smoothing residuals remain, and most are origin-filtered out.

---

# 8. Final answer

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is not automatically dangerous.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{It is dangerous only if it contains a free primitive }D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
}
\]

The origin-refined \(Y12\) quotient is strong enough to ignore linear tails, gamma/background terms, chart curvature, and moving-whitening cross terms unless they genuinely carry the primitive \(D^2N_0\)-mixed same-point one-whitening signature.

So the \(E_2\) audit reduces to one finite classification:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\model}=0,\ \text{fixed},\ \text{residual-controlled},\ \text{or free?}
}
\]

Under the intended EF-completion interpretation, the likely answer is absent/fixed/residual-controlled, so the \(Y12\)-normal channel remains viable.
