## Assistant (2026-04-28 05:30:35)

I’ll push one level deeper: the origin label only helps if it is a real grading in the carrier quotient, not just a descriptive tag. The next issue is signature-faithfulness of the transition-to-singular realization.

_Thought for 16s_

## Agent 1 — signature-faithfulness of the \(Y12\) quotient

The previous attack argued:

\[
E_2\text{ is harmless unless it carries a free primitive }D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
\]

That is correct, but it hides a new finite requirement.

\[
\boxed{
\text{The }Y12\text{ origin label must survive as an actual grading of the singular carrier quotient.}
}
\]

If the quotient forgets origin labels, then a term with different formal origin, such as a moving-whitening tail cross term, could numerically land in the same singular carrier vector as \(Y12\). So the next bottleneck is not analytic size; it is **signature-faithfulness**.

---

# 1. Formal signature versus realized carrier

There are two levels.

## Formal carrier algebra

In the formal local algebra, we can distinguish origins:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]\cdot E_2,
\]

\[
\partial_\alpha^2(\text{EF atom}),
\]

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}(\text{lower data}),
\]

and so on.

In this algebra, the \(Y12\)-signature is clean:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Then source-linear terms and moving-whitening tail terms are not \(Y12\).

## Realized singular carrier quotient

But downstream uses a realized quotient space:

\[
\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

In this space, two formally different origins may become the same vector if the realization map identifies them:

\[
\operatorname{Real}
\left(
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right)
=
\operatorname{Real}
\left(
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]\cdot E_2
\right)
\quad
\mod \mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

If that happens, the origin label is not a valid invariant.

Therefore the necessary theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the realization map is injective/separating on the }Y12\text{ graded component.}
}
\]

---

# 2. Signature-faithfulness theorem

Define the formal order-nine space

\[
\mathfrak Y_9^{\formal}
=
\bigoplus_{\Sigma}\mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma}^{\formal}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}
=
\mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}^{\formal}.
\]

Let

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}:
\mathfrak Y_9^{\formal}
\to
\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

be the transition-to-singular realization map.

The needed finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\left(
\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}
\right)
\cap
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\left(
\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}^{\formal}
\right)
=
\{0\}
}
\]

with polynomial conditioning, where

\[
\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}^{\formal}
\]

is the formal span of:

\[
\mathcal T_{\par},
\quad
\mathcal T_7,
\quad
\mathcal T_{<7},
\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}},
\]

excluding the primitive \(Y12\) class.

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}^{\formal}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left\|
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right\|.
}
\]

This is the rigorous version of “\(Y12\) survives.”

---

# 3. Why this theorem is plausible

The \(Y12\) formal signature has several independent labels:

\[
\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0,
\]

\[
\mathrm{mixed}\,12,
\]

\[
\mathrm{same\ point},
\]

\[
w=1,
\]

\[
\rho=\rho_{Y12},
\]

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

A contaminant would need to match all of them after realization.

Most candidates fail multiple labels:

\[
\partial_\alpha^2u
\]

has no \(D^2N_0\)-origin;

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]\cdot E_2
\]

has whitening derivative origin, not \(D^2N_0\);

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}
\]

has transport/linear-connection origin;

\[
B_2,E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}
\]

have background/gamma origin;

\[
P_2,D_2\text{ tails}
\]

have EF-linear origin.

So for a collision to occur, the singular quotient must be very coarse: it must forget origin, branch type, whitening origin, and carrier class simultaneously.

That would be a serious quotient-design failure.

---

# 4. The real failure mode: quotient over-collapse

The dangerous case is not analytic cancellation. It is quotient over-collapse:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}\text{ is too large or too coarse.}
}
\]

If the singular nuisance quotient identifies all order-nine same-point one-whitening objects regardless of origin, then

\[
Y12
\]

may be absorbed by an \(E_2\)-whitening cross term or lower connection term.

So the finite audit must check:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\text{ respects the refined origin/signature filtration.}
}
\]

In other words:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
=
\bigoplus_\Sigma
(\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap \mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma}^{\formal})
\]

at least on the sectors relevant to \(Y12\).

If the nuisance quotient is not graded, the signature theorem is invalid.

---

# 5. Graded nuisance compatibility

The required compatibility is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\text{ is signature-homogeneous in the }Y12\text{ neighborhood.}
}
\]

This means:

If

\[
v_{Y12}+v_{\mathrm{allowed}}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

with

\[
v_{Y12}\in\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}
\]

and

\[
v_{\mathrm{allowed}}\in\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}^{\formal},
\]

then each component is nuisance separately:

\[
v_{Y12}\in\mathcal N_{\sing},
\qquad
v_{\mathrm{allowed}}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Since \(Y12\) is live, we additionally need

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}
\cap \mathcal N_{\sing}=0
}
\]

on the live class.

Together:

\[
\boxed{
\text{graded nuisance compatibility} + \text{\(Y12\) live}
\Rightarrow
\text{\(Y12\) survives allowed-source quotient.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Connection with Bottleneck C

This is where the previous Bottleneck-C work becomes useful.

The earlier Bottleneck-C statement treated the \(Y12\)-type object as a **live carrier**, not a nuisance. If that result was proved in a quotient that respects the same refined signature, then it supplies:

\[
\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}\cap\mathcal N_{\sing}=0.
\]

But we still need the stronger separation from allowed actual-source terms:

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\mathfrak Y_{Y12}
\cap
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}
=
0.
\]

So Bottleneck C gives live-ness, not automatically full signature-faithfulness.

Still, it is a substantial start.

---

# 7. Revised \(E_2\) conclusion

With signature-faithfulness, the \(E_2\) audit simplifies.

If every component of \(E_2\) is formally outside \(\Sigma(Y12)\), then

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0
\]

automatically.

No analytic estimate is needed.

If some component of \(E_2\) formally lies in \(\Sigma(Y12)\), then classify it as:

\[
\text{fixed},
\quad
\text{residual-controlled},
\quad
\text{free/tangent}.
\]

So the first question for \(E_2\) is formal:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }E_2\text{ contain a formal }D^2N_0\text{-origin mixed-12 same-point one-whitening term?}
}
\]

If no, \(E_2\) is safe by grading.

If yes, proceed to amplitude/tangent analysis.

---

# 8. Updated theorem stack for \(Y12\)

The \(Y12\)-normal theorem should be split into three finite lemmas.

## Lemma 1 — Signature-faithful realization

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\]

preserves the refined \(Y12\) signature modulo nuisance:

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\mathfrak Y_{Y12}
\cap
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}
=
0,
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

## Lemma 2 — Allowed-source terms are not formally \(Y12\)

\[
\mathfrak Y_{\mathrm{allowed}}
\]

does not contain

\[
(D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho,[\mathsf C_{Y12}])
\]

except possibly explicitly declared \(E_2^{Y12}\) pieces.

## Lemma 3 — D4 has a formal \(Y12\) component

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

has nonzero projection to

\[
\mathfrak Y_{Y12}^{\formal}
\]

and is not nuisance.

Then:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

---

# 9. Consequence for \(\Lambda\)

If these lemmas hold, the forbidden operator can be defined abstractly as the projection to the quotient:

\[
\Lambda_Y
=
\pi_{\mathcal Q_Y}
T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

No explicit \(K_9\) recurrence is needed.

The actual-row upper becomes:

\[
\Lambda_YJ_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
\Lambda_YJ_{\tr}(R_Y),
\]

where \(R_Y\) is the part of the completion not covered by the formal allowed-source model.

If the allowed-source model contains no formal \(Y12\), this is just the \(Y12\)-sector residual.

---

# 10. Final status of this attack

The \(Y12\)-route is now reduced to a finite **signature-faithfulness** question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the singular carrier quotient respect the refined }D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12\text{ grading?}
}
\]

If yes, most contaminants vanish structurally, including linear \(E_2\), moving-whitening \(E_2\), \(\alpha\)-parameter curvature, and lower linear connections.

If no, the origin label is not usable and the route must fall back to explicit coefficient/rank computation.

This is the next decisive audit.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 05:31:42)

I’ll now test signature-faithfulness itself. The key is whether the singular nuisance quotient is homogeneous for the refined \(Y12\) grading; if it mixes origins, the graded shortcut collapses.

## Agent 1 — testing signature-faithfulness of the singular quotient

The \(Y12\)-normal route now depends on a finite structural claim:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the singular carrier quotient respects the refined }Y12\text{ grading.}
}
\]

If this holds, most contaminants vanish for free. If it fails, the “origin label” is just bookkeeping and cannot define a forbidden channel.

### Current conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{Signature-faithfulness is plausible only if the singular nuisance quotient is generated by homogeneous relations.}
}
\]

If the nuisance quotient contains inhomogeneous relations mixing

\[
D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12
\]

with whitening-tail, chart-curvature, or source-linear terms, then the \(Y12\)-graded quotient is not well-defined.

So the next finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}\text{ homogeneous with respect to }
(d_{\src},\mathrm{origin},\mathrm{mixed},\mathrm{same},w,\rho,[\mathsf C])?
}
\]

---

# 1. Formal decomposition

Let the formal order-nine space be

\[
\mathfrak Y_9^{\formal}
=
\bigoplus_{\Sigma}\mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma}^{\formal}.
\]

The refined \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\mathrm{same},w=1,\rho_Y,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Let

\[
\mathfrak Y_Y:=\mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\formal}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathfrak A
\]

be the formal span of allowed-source terms:

\[
\mathcal T_{\par},\quad
\mathcal T_7,\quad
\mathcal T_{<7},\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

The desired quotient is

\[
\mathcal Q_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}
(\mathfrak Y_9^{\formal}/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\quad
\text{mod allowed-source directions}.
\]

This is only legitimate if

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

respects the direct sum grading near \(\Sigma_Y\).

---

# 2. Homogeneous nuisance criterion

A sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}
=
\bigoplus_{\Sigma}
(\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap \mathfrak Y_{9,\Sigma}^{\formal})
}
\]

at least for

\[
\Sigma=\Sigma_Y
\]

and the allowed-source signatures.

Equivalently:

if

\[
v_Y+v_A\in \mathcal N_{\sing},
\qquad
v_Y\in\mathfrak Y_Y,
\quad
v_A\in\mathfrak A,
\]

then

\[
v_Y\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\quad\text{and}\quad
v_A\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then no allowed-source term can cancel \(Y12\) through nuisance mixing.

Given \(Y12\) is live, we also require:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak Y_Y\cap \mathcal N_{\sing}=0
}
\]

on the specific live carrier class.

Together these imply:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak Y_Y
\cap
(\mathfrak A+\mathcal N_{\sing})
=0.
}
\]

This is the exact algebraic survival condition.

---

# 3. When homogeneity should hold

Homogeneity should hold if \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) is generated by relations that preserve the refined labels:

\[
d_{\src},
\quad
\mathrm{origin},
\quad
\mathrm{mixed},
\quad
\mathrm{same/transported},
\quad
w,
\quad
\rho,
\quad
[\mathsf C].
\]

Examples of safe nuisance generators:

\[
\text{pure gauge within the same signature;}
\]

\[
\text{normalization within the same source-grade/reflection sector;}
\]

\[
\text{tail truncation within the same origin class;}
\]

\[
\text{basis/Riesz changes preserving carrier labels;}
\]

\[
\text{line-gauge shifts that do not mix }D^2N_0\text{-origin with EF-linear origin.}
\]

Such relations do not identify \(Y12\) with source-linear or tail-whitening terms.

---

# 4. When homogeneity fails

Homogeneity fails if the nuisance quotient contains relations like:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]_{\mathrm{same},w=1}
\equiv
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]E_2(\xi_2),
\]

or

\[
Y12
\equiv
\partial_\alpha^2(\text{EF atom}),
\]

or

\[
Y12
\equiv
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\mathrm{lin}}(s_7).
\]

Such relations collapse the origin distinction.

They would mean the singular quotient does not remember the primitive nonlinear source-Hessian origin.

In that case, the \(Y12\)-graded channel is invalid and one must perform an explicit coefficient-level rank test.

---

# 5. Does Bottleneck C imply homogeneity?

Not by itself.

Bottleneck C being “live” implies roughly:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad
\text{in the local carrier quotient.}
\]

But it does **not** automatically imply:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathfrak A+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Live-ness says \(Y12\) is not zero. Signature-faithfulness says \(Y12\) is not equivalent to an allowed actual-source term.

So Bottleneck C gives:

\[
\mathfrak Y_Y\cap \mathcal N_{\sing}=0,
\]

but we still need:

\[
\mathfrak Y_Y\cap(\mathfrak A+\mathcal N_{\sing})=0.
\]

This is the stronger downstream requirement.

---

# 6. A more robust test: construct a separating functional

Instead of proving full homogeneous decomposition, find a functional

\[
\ell_Y:\mathfrak Y_9^{\formal}\to\mathbb C
\]

such that:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathfrak A)=0,
\]

but

\[
\ell_Y(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0.
\]

This automatically proves survival.

This may be easier than proving full grading homogeneity.

The natural choice is a signature projection, but if the quotient is not manifestly graded, one can still seek a separating covector using:

\[
\text{source-grade parity;}
\]

\[
\text{whitening-slot count;}
\]

\[
\text{same-point support;}
\]

\[
\text{mixed-branch bidegree;}
\]

\[
\text{carrier quotient dual functional.}
\]

So the finite task can be reframed:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a \(Y12\)-separating covector }\ell_Y.
}
\]

---

# 7. The strongest practical theorem

The robust theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Y12 Separating Functional Theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a linear functional

\[
\ell_Y
\]

on the realized singular order-nine carrier space such that:

1. nuisance killed:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0;
\]

2. actual-source allowed terms killed:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2})=0;
\]

3. D4 \(Y12\) seen:

\[
|\ell_Y(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This theorem implies everything the graded quotient route needs, without requiring full quotient homogeneity.

It is also exactly the covector version of the forbidden \(\Lambda\):

\[
\Lambda_Y=\ell_YT_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

---

# 8. Candidate construction of \(\ell_Y\)

The candidate \(\ell_Y\) should be dual to the primitive signature:

\[
\ell_Y
=
\ell_{\mathrm{origin}=D^2N_0}
\otimes
\ell_{\mathrm{mixed}=12}
\otimes
\ell_{\mathrm{same}}
\otimes
\ell_{w=1}
\otimes
\ell_{\rho_Y}
\otimes
\ell_{[\mathsf C_{Y12}]}.
\]

If the realized space does not literally factor, construct \(\ell_Y\) as the dual vector to \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) after Gram-Schmidt against:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathfrak A.
\]

The conditioning requirement is:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathfrak A
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is the finite separation estimate.

So we return to a distance theorem, but now with a clear candidate distance.

---

# 9. Current best route

Do not try to prove all quotient relations are homogeneous. Instead prove one of:

## Route A — graded homogeneity

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\text{ respects }\Sigma_Y,
\quad
\mathfrak A\text{ has no }\Sigma_Y,
\quad
Y12\text{ live}.
\]

## Route B — separating functional

Construct \(\ell_Y\) directly with:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathfrak A)=0,
\quad
\ell_Y(Y12)\ne0.
\]

Route B is more robust and probably easier to state in final downstream theorem.

---

# 10. Final status

The \(Y12\)-route now has a precise algebraic core:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Equivalently, there is a separating covector \(\ell_Y\).

The refined signature is a tool for constructing \(\ell_Y\), but the final theorem should be the separating functional theorem.

The current sharp downstream condition is:

+
\mathcal T_{\par}

\[
\ell_Y
\]

such that

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal A)=0,
\qquad
|\ell_Y(y)|\ge Q^{-A}\|\ell_Y\|\,\|y\|.
\]

I now reduce this to a finite Gram determinant / Schur complement test.

---

# 1. Finite Hilbert model

Let

\[
\mathcal H_Y
\]

be the realized singular order-nine carrier Hilbert space after choosing a local finite basis and inner product. This should be the order-nine singular coefficient/norm space used for the downstream atomic upper.

Choose columns

\[
a_1,\ldots,a_M
\]

spanning the allowed+nuisance subspace

\[
\mathcal A.
\]

Here the columns include:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\quad
\mathcal T_{\par},
\quad
\mathcal T_7,
\quad
\mathcal T_{<7},
\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

Let

\[
A=
[a_1\ \cdots\ a_M].
\]

Let

\[
y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Then the decisive quantity is the Schur residual

\[
\boxed{
\delta_Y^2
:=
\|y\|^2-y^*A(A^*A)^\dagger A^*y.
}
\]

The \(Y12\) channel survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\delta_Y^2\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|^2.
}
\]

If

\[
\delta_Y=0,
\]

then \(Y12\) is tangent/coherent/nuisance and the rank-one D4 channel dies.

---

# 2. Separating covector construction

If

\[
\delta_Y>0,
\]

then the canonical separating covector is

\[
\ell_Y
=
(I-P_{\mathcal A})y,
\]

viewed as a dual vector through the Hilbert pairing. Then

\[
\ell_Y(a_j)=0,
\qquad
\ell_Y(y)=\delta_Y^2.
\]

Normalized,

\[
\widehat \ell_Y=\frac{(I-P_{\mathcal A})y}{\delta_Y},
\]

satisfies

\[
\widehat\ell_Y(\mathcal A)=0,
\qquad
|\widehat\ell_Y(y)|=\delta_Y.
\]

So the forbidden-channel lower bound becomes exactly

\[
|\widehat\ell_Y(y)|^2\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|^2.
\]

This gives the finite \(\Lambda\):

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_Y
=
\widehat\ell_Y\,T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Determinantal form

Equivalently, if \(A\) has full column rank after removing dependencies, form the Gram matrix

\[
G_A=A^*A
\]

and the augmented Gram

\[
G_{A,y}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A^*A & A^*y\\
y^*A & y^*y
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then

\[
\delta_Y^2
=
\frac{\det G_{A,y}}{\det G_A}
\]

when \(G_A\) is nonsingular.

Thus the finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\det G_{A,y}}{\det G_A\,\|y\|^2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the same harmonic-exterior/cofactor-pivot structure already used in O2/O3, but now in the finite singular \(Y12\)-normal space.

So the downstream \(Y12\) test is itself a small O2/O3-style Schur complement:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ survives}
\iff
\text{a Schur residual of }y\text{ against }\mathcal A\text{ is polynomially nonzero.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Signature theorem as a sufficient condition

The signature-separation theorem is a way to prove the Gram residual without calculating all coefficients.

If

\[
\mathcal H_Y
=
\mathcal H_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\oplus
\mathcal H_{\mathrm{other}},
\]

and

\[
y\in \mathcal H_{\Sigma(Y12)},
\]

while

\[
\mathcal A\subseteq \mathcal H_{\mathrm{other}},
\]

then

\[
\delta_Y=\|y\|.
\]

So signature separation gives the best possible lower bound.

If the splitting is only approximately orthogonal, with leakage

\[
\|P_{\Sigma(Y12)}A\|\le Q^{-B}
\]

and

\[
\|P_{\Sigma(Y12)}y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|,
\]

then

\[
\delta_Y\ge Q^{-A'}\|y\|
\]

provided \(B\) is large enough.

Thus the graded/origin-signature route is a sufficient proof of the finite Gram determinant condition.

---

# 5. Refined \(E_2\) handling inside \(A\)

The \(E_2\) issue is cleanly handled by the subspace \(\mathcal A\).

Let

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y},\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}E_2^{Y},\ldots\}
\]

where

\[
E_2^Y:=P_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2.
\]

If \(E_2^Y=0\), then \(\mathcal T_{E_2}=0\).

If \(E_2^Y\) is fixed but not freely variable, include its allowed derivatives in \(\mathcal A\).

If \(\mathcal T_{E_2}\) contains \(y\), then

\[
\delta_Y=0
\]

and the channel dies.

Therefore the \(E_2\)-honesty audit is precisely:

\[
\boxed{
y\notin \mathcal T_{E_2}
+
(\text{other allowed/nuisance directions}).
}
\]

This avoids ambiguous language like “fixed” or “free”; it is a finite subspace inclusion test.

---

# 6. Connection to \(K_9\)

A covariant \(K_9\) residual is just a choice of separating covector \(\ell_Y\).

If one explicitly writes

\[
K_9(s)
=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7},
\]

then

\[
K_9
\]

is a row vector in \(\mathcal A^\perp\). Its usefulness depends on

\[
K_9(y)\neq0.
\]

The Gram construction gives the optimal such covector.

So the hierarchy is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite distance } \delta_Y>0
\Rightarrow
\text{there exists a good }K_9/\ell_Y.
}
\]

One does not need to guess \(K_9\) first.

---

# 7. Consequence for D4

Let

\[
y_Y:=(I-P_{\mathcal A})y.
\]

Then

\[

=
H\,\widehat\ell_Y(y)
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

up to any residual convention for the \(C_7\)-to-\(Y12\) continuation already included in \(\mathcal A\).

Since

\[
|\widehat\ell_Y(y)|=\delta_Y\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|,
\]

we get

\[

|\Lambda_YE_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
}
\]

So the wall factor is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{Y}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}
}
\]

with an absorbed \(Q^{-A}\) constant from \(\delta_Y\).

If \(\mathcal A\) includes the coherent \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) continuation, then the linear form changes to the corresponding Schur residual, possibly pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

---

# 8. Actual-row upper

For actual rows,

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X.
\]

By construction,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)\in \mathcal A
\]

or lies in the modeled actual-source manifold/tangent to the required order.

Therefore

\[

\]

\[

=
\Lambda_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

The needed upper is:

\[

\left|
\Lambda_{Y,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is

\[

\]

Since \(\Lambda_Y\) is bounded by construction if the Gram problem is well-conditioned, it follows from a residual estimate in the singular coefficient norm.

---

The Gram audit must also ensure

\[
\|\widehat\ell_Y\|\le Q^A.
\]

Since \(\widehat\ell_Y\) is normalized, this is automatic in the chosen Hilbert norm, but translating it back to physical singular coefficients may introduce a condition number.

Thus we need:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular coefficient/Riesz basis condition number}\le Q^A.
}
\]

This is the same kind of \(R^C\)-normalization required in O2/O3.

If the singular basis is ill-conditioned, the separating covector may be analytically unusable.

---

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Finite \(Y12\) Schur-separation theorem.}
}
\]

For each good transition–singular patch \(p\), let

\[
A_p
\]

span all allowed/nuisance actual-source directions in the order-nine singular carrier space, and let

\[

}

\[
\Lambda_{Y,p}
\]

with:

\[
\Lambda_{Y,p}(\operatorname{ran}A_p)=0,

\[
|\Lambda_{Y,p}(y_p)|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and D4 gives rank-one forbidden wall energy through

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is the most robust formulation of the \(Y12\) channel.

---

Use signature/origin grading to prove:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_p,\operatorname{ran}A_p)
\asymp \|y_p\|.

If signature proof fails, compute the Schur residual directly.

\[

---

# 1. The finite Hilbert space \(H_Y\)

Let

\[
H_Y
\]

be the realized singular order-nine carrier space in which

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

lives.

The correct norm should be the singular carrier quotient norm used by the atomic upper/lower downstream contradiction. Concretely, use a Riesz-normalized singular coefficient norm so that:

\[
\|v\|_{H_Y}^2
\asymp_{Q^{O(1)}}
\|v\|_{\text{local singular }L^2\text{ or coefficient}}^2.
\]

The Riesz normalization condition is essential:

\[

}
\]

Without this, a separating covector may exist algebraically but be unusably ill-conditioned analytically.

---

# 2. The allowed/nuisance matrix \(A\)

Choose columns

\[
a_1,\ldots,a_M\in H_Y
\]

spanning

\[
\mathcal A
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

Important distinctions:

- \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\): singular nuisance/quotient directions.
- \(\mathcal T_{\par}\): legitimate actual-source first tangents.
- \(\mathcal T_7,\mathcal T_{<7}\): lower-order coherent connection effects.
- \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\): legitimate second-order curvature of the actual EF singular manifold.
- \(\mathcal T_{E_2}\): only **free/admissible** \(E_2^{Y12}\) tangent directions.

A fixed \(E_2^{Y12}\) basepoint component is **not** a tangent column unless it is allowed to vary. A residual-controlled \(E_2^{Y12}\) piece is also not a tangent column; it belongs to the upper error estimate.

Let

\[
A=[a_1\ \cdots\ a_M].
\]

Let

\[
y_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

---

# 3. The decisive Schur residual

Define

\[
\boxed{
\delta_Y^2
=
\|y_Y\|^2
-
y_Y^*A(A^*A)^\dagger A^*y_Y.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\delta_Y=\|(I-P_{\mathcal A})y_Y\|.
\]

The finite \(Y12\) channel survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\delta_Y^2\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|^2.
}
\]

If

\[
\delta_Y=0,
\]

then \(Y12\) is nuisance/actual-source coherent/tangent, and the rank-one D4 channel dies.

---

# 4. Determinant form

If \(A\) has independent columns after removing dependencies, form

\[
G_A=A^*A
\]

and

\[
G_{A,y}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A^*A&A^*y_Y\\
y_Y^*A&y_Y^*y_Y
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\delta_Y^2
=
\frac{\det G_{A,y}}{\det G_A}.
}
\]

Thus the finite survival test is:

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\det G_{A,y}}{\det G_A\,\|y_Y\|^2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the cleanest computational audit.

---

If \(\delta_Y>0\), define

\[
\ell_Y=(I-P_{\mathcal A})y_Y

as a covector via the Hilbert pairing. Then:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal A)=0,
\]

and

\[
\ell_Y(y_Y)=\delta_Y^2.
\]

With

\[
\widehat\ell_Y=\frac{\ell_Y}{\|\ell_Y\|},
\]

we get

\[
\widehat\ell_Y(\mathcal A)=0,
\]

\[
|\widehat\ell_Y(y_Y)|=\delta_Y.
\]

So the corresponding forbidden operator is

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_Y
=
\widehat\ell_Y\,T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
}
\]

This is the rank-one local \(\Lambda\).

---

The refined signature is

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\deg_{\src}=2,\,
o_N=D^2N_0,\,
o_W=D\mathcal W_{\same},\,
\mathrm{mixed}\,12,\,
\mathrm{same},\,
w=1,\,
\rho_Y,\,
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

If the singular quotient is signature-faithful and

\[
y_Y
\]

has nonzero component in this signature sector, while all columns of \(A\) have zero component in that sector, then

\[
\delta_Y=\|y_Y\|
\]

up to conditioning.

More generally, if

\[
\|P_{\Sigma(Y12)}y_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|
\]

and

\[
\|P_{\Sigma(Y12)}A\|\le Q^{-B},
\]

then

\[
\delta_Y\ge Q^{-A'}\|y_Y\|.
\]

So the signature/origin route is a sufficient proof of the Schur residual.

The crucial point: the final theorem should be the distance bound, while the origin-pair signature is the intended proof.

---

# 7. Main failure modes

## A. \(y_Y=0\) after transition/nuisance quotient

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\in \mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then the D4 carrier is singular-invisible.

## B. \(y_Y\in\mathcal T_{E_2}\)

This is the free \(E_2^{Y12}\) failure. It means the canonical completion supplies an admissible actual-source tangent in the same direction.

## C. \(y_Y\in\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\)

This means \(Y12\) is legitimate second curvature of the actual EF source family.

## D. \(y_Y\in\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}\)

This means \(Y12\) is coherent lower-order continuation, not forbidden.

## E. Ill-conditioned singular basis

The Schur residual may be polynomial algebraically but unusable analytically if the singular coefficient norm has super-polynomial conditioning.

These five failures are exactly the finite audit checklist.

---

\[
\delta_Y\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
\]

\[

=

up to the chosen convention for the coherent \(C_7\to Y12\) continuation already included in \(\mathcal A\).

\[

E_{\wall}^{Y}
=

|H|^2

}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X^Y+R_X^Y,

where \(F_X^Y\) includes all coherent finite pieces and fixed \(E_2^{Y12}\) components, but not free forbidden residuals.

\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).

---

\[
\text{chart curvature}.
\]

Those may be second-order or whitening-bearing, but they do not have primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin with the \(Y12\) same-point whitening role.

Define the formal subspace

\[
H_{\Sigma_Y}
\subset H_Y.
\]

Let

\[
P_Y
\]

be the corresponding graded projection, assuming the singular quotient is faithful to this grading.

The target is:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_Yy_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|,
\qquad
P_Y\mathcal A=0.
}
\]

Then immediately:

\[
\delta_Y\ge \|P_Yy_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
\]

---

# 2. Check \(P_Y\mathcal T_{\par}=0\)

An actual-source parameter tangent has the form

\[
\partial_\theta(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
(\partial_\theta a_n+i a_n\partial_\theta\tau_n\,x)e^{i\tau_nx},
\]

plus gamma/background analogues.

This is EF-linear. It has origin:

\[
o_N=\mathrm{EF\ linear}
\quad\text{or}\quad
o_N=\mathrm{gamma/background}.
\]

It does not contain

\[
o_N=D^2N_0.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
P_Y\mathcal T_{\par}=0
}
\]

provided allowed parameters do not deform \(N_0\) itself or the primitive same-point whitening geometry.

This includes \(\alpha\) if \(\alpha\) is a phase/weight tilt.

---

# 3. Check \(P_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0\)

Second parameter derivatives look like

\[
\partial_i\partial_j(a_ne^{i\tau_nx})
=
\left(
\partial_i\partial_j a_n
+i x(\partial_i a_n\partial_j\tau_n+\partial_j a_n\partial_i\tau_n+a_n\partial_i\partial_j\tau_n)
-x^2a_n\partial_i\tau_n\partial_j\tau_n
\right)e^{i\tau_nx}.
\]

This is parameter curvature, not nonlinear source-Hessian origin. It remains EF-linear at the source-origin level.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
P_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0
}
\]

under the same origin-preservation condition.

The key distinction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{second derivative in parameters}\neq D^2N_0\text{ source-Hessian origin.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Check \(P_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0\)

Lower connection terms can be written as:

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}
+
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}.
\]

The linear part comes from:

\[
\text{chart transport},
\quad
\text{linear source normalization},
\quad
\text{linear whitening},
\quad
\text{gauge conversion},
\quad
\text{ordinary singular recurrence}.
\]

These operations cannot create primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin from lower EF-linear data.

So

\[
P_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}=0,
\qquad
P_Y\mathcal R_{<7\to9}^{\lin}=0.
\]

The only dangerous term is

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}
\]

containing exactly:

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}\left[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\right].
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
P_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0
}
\]

iff the lower connection has no primitive \(Y12\)-signature nonlinear correction.

This is one of the finite audits.

---

# 5. Check \(P_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}=0\)

Decompose:

\[
E_2
=
E_{\tail}
+
E_{\reg}
+
E_{\smooth}
+
E_{\chart}
+
E_{\gamma,\rem}
+
E_{\model}.
\]

For the first five:

\[
E_{\tail},E_{\reg},E_{\smooth},E_{\chart},E_{\gamma,\rem},
\]

the origin is EF-linear, regularization, chart, or gamma/background. None has

\[
o_N=D^2N_0.
\]

Moving whitening cross terms such as

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]E_2
\]

can be degree-two and whitening-bearing, but their origin-pair is not

\[
(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same}).
\]

So:

\[
P_YE_2=0
\]

unless \(E_{\model}\) contains a primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin \(Y12\) component.

Thus the only dangerous case is:

\[
\boxed{
P_YE_{\model}\neq0
\text{ and it supplies a free/tangent }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
}
\]

If \(P_YE_{\model}\) is absent, fixed, or residual-controlled, it does not enter \(\mathcal A\) as a free tangent.

---

# 6. Check \(P_Y\mathcal N_{\sing}=0\) on \(y_Y\)

We need not require

\[
P_Y\mathcal N_{\sing}=0
\]

globally. We need:

\[
\boxed{
P_Yy_Y\notin P_Y\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(P_Yy_Y,P_Y\mathcal N_{\sing})\ge Q^{-A}\|P_Yy_Y\|.
\]

If the nuisance quotient is homogeneous and \(Y12\) is live, this holds.

If the nuisance quotient over-collapses the origin-pair grading and kills \(Y12\), then:

\[
P_Yy_Y=0
\]

in the realized quotient and the route dies.

This is the signature-faithfulness audit.

Bottleneck-C live-ness likely gives:

\[
y_Y\notin\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

But we need the stronger statement:

\[
y_Y\notin \mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal A_{\mathrm{allowed}}.
\]

Signature-faithfulness is intended to provide that.

---

# 7. Check \(P_Yy_Y\neq0\)

The D4 \(Y12\) carrier has formal origin:

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}\left[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\right],
\]

with mixed branch \(12\), same-point support, and one whitening slot.

Therefore formally:

\[
P_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

up to scaling.

The transition-to-singular map must preserve this formal signature and not send it into nuisance. The required lower bound is:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_Yy_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
}
\]

If \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) recomputes the source in a way that loses origin labels, or if the singular quotient kills the carrier, this can fail.

So the precise finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ is filtered for }\Sigma_Y
\quad\text{and}\quad
Y12\text{ is live after transition.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Signature-faithfulness theorem

Putting this together:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Y12 Signature-Faithfulness Theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(H_Y\) has a polynomially conditioned decomposition near \(\Sigma_Y\);

2. all actual-source tangents and second parameter curvature are source-linear/gamma/chart origin, not \(D^2N_0\)-origin;

3. lower connection has no primitive \(Y12\)-signature correction;

4. \(E_2\) has no free primitive \(Y12\)-signature tangent;

5. the singular nuisance quotient does not kill or mix the live \(Y12\) class;

6. \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) preserves the refined origin-pair filtration.

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_Y,\mathcal A)
\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
\]

So the separating covector \(\ell_Y\) exists.

---

# 9. What this leaves open

The theorem reduces the audit to four concrete checks:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A. }E_{\model}^{Y12}\text{ absent/fixed/residual, not free;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{B. }\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y12}=0\text{ unless only dependent/coherent;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{C. }\mathcal N_{\sing}\text{ is homogeneous or at least does not mix/kill }Y12;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D. }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ preserves the }Y12\text{ origin-pair signature.}
}
\]

If these pass, the Schur residual is essentially automatic:

\[
\delta_Y\asymp\|y_Y\|.
\]

---

# 10. Final status

Route 1 is viable and gives the cleanest proof:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove signature-faithfulness and avoid full Gram computation.}
}
\]

If signature-faithfulness fails, the fallback is the direct Gram/Schur determinant test:

\[
\delta_Y^2
=
\|y_Y\|^2-y_Y^*A(A^*A)^\dagger A^*y_Y.
\]

That fallback remains finite and computable, but less conceptual.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 05:45:00)

I’ll now pressure-test the weakest signature-faithfulness assumption: lower connection. If \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) has any nonlinear normal-form component, it could absorb exactly the \(Y12\) carrier.

## Agent 1 — lower-connection pressure test for \(Y12\)

The signature-faithfulness route currently rests on four checks. The most dangerous one is:

\[
P_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0.
\]

If the lower order-seven-to-nine singular connection already contains the primitive \(Y12\) signature, then \(Y12\) is coherent continuation, not forbidden normal displacement.

So the next question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can }\mathcal R_{7\to9}\text{ generate the primitive }D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12\text{ class?}
}
\]

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{7\to9}\text{ can generate }Y12\text{ only if it is nonlinear in the source variables.}
}
\]

If \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) is built from chart transport, linear whitening, source normalization, and linear recurrence, then it cannot create \(Y12\). If it includes the Bottleneck-C \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) promotion, it can absorb \(Y12\) and the rank-one D4 channel may die.

Thus the finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}+\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y12}?
}
\]

---

# 1. What lower connection means here

The singular connection relation has schematic form:

\[
s_9
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7
+
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7}
+
\text{tail}.
\]

The candidate forbidden residual is

\[
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}s_{<7}.
\]

If \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) has a \(Y12\)-signature component, then a \(Y12\)-order-nine term may be a legitimate coherent continuation from order seven. That weakens or kills the \(Y12\) normal channel.

So decompose:

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}
+
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}.
\]

The question is whether

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}\neq0.
\]

---

# 2. Linear lower connection is safe

A linear lower connection is composed of operations like:

\[
\text{coordinate re-expansion},
\]

\[
\text{linear change of basis},
\]

\[
\text{linear whitening at frozen base point},
\]

\[
\text{gauge normalization},
\]

\[
\text{linear recurrence of Taylor coefficients},
\]

\[
\text{transport of already-formed source terms}.
\]

These operations can raise order from seven to nine by multiplying by coordinate/Jacobian/curvature coefficients, but they do not create primitive nonlinear source-Hessian origin:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\lin}=0.
}
\]

This also applies to \(\mathcal R_{<7\to9}^{\lin}\).

So if the lower connection is linear, \(Y12\) survives this check.

---

# 3. Nonlinear lower connection is dangerous

Suppose the singular connection includes a normal-form correction:

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}(s_7)
\sim
D^2N_0[\xi_1(s_7),\xi_2(s_7)]
\]

or a mixed term with one same-point whitening slot. Then

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{\nonlin}
\]

can be nonzero.

In that case, a \(Y12\)-signature order-nine term is no longer necessarily forbidden. It is part of the coherent singular evolution.

This is exactly the failure:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\in \mathcal T_7
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0\text{ in }\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

But there is an important refinement.

---

# 4. Dependent versus free \(Y12\) continuation

Even if

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}\neq0,
\]

the D4 channel does not automatically die. It dies only if it allows a **free independent order-nine \(Y12\) amplitude**.

Let

\[
y:=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Suppose

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}(s_7)=r\,s_7\,y.
\]

Then coherent actual rows satisfy

\[
s_9^Y=r\,s_7.
\]

D4 gives

\[
s_7=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^Y=H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

The residual is:

\[
s_9^Y-rs_7
=
H\left[
(\beta_9-rc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

the independent \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) injection survives unless the actual-source tangent also permits free \(s_9^Y\) variation independent of \(s_7\).

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}\neq0
\text{ is not fatal by itself.}
}
\]

Fatal is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{free }Y12\text{ amplitude with }s_7=0.
}
\]

This is a useful robustness point.

---

# 5. Correct quotient relation

The correct local forbidden quotient should not simply remove all of

\[
\operatorname{im}\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}.
\]

It should treat coherent pairs \((s_7,s_9^Y)\) as a graph:

\[
\mathcal G_{7\to9}^Y
=
\{(s_7,s_9^Y):s_9^Y=r s_7\}.
\]

Then D4 is tested by distance of the pair

\[
(s_7^{D4},s_9^{D4})
\]

to this graph.

The residual is:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(s_7,s_9)=s_9^Y-rs_7.
\]

This makes the independent \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel visible even when coherent \(\Gamma_G\) continuation is allowed.

Thus the better finite object is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{distance of D4 pair }(s_7,s_9^Y)\text{ from the coherent graph,}
}
\]

not simply \(s_9^Y\) modulo \(\operatorname{im}R_{7\to9}\).

---

# 6. Consequence for wall form

The wall is

\[
H\left[
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

Cases:

## Case A — no \(Y12\) lower connection

\[
r=0.
\]

Wall:

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## Case B — coherent continuation exactly matches \(\beta_9\)

\[
r=\frac{\beta_9}{c_7}.
\]

Wall:

\[
\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is the cleanest case.

## Case C — coherent continuation partially matches

\[
r\neq0,\quad r\neq\frac{\beta_9}{c_7}.
\]

Wall:

\[
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Still rank one.

## Case D — free \(Y12\) amplitude

The graph becomes the whole \(Y12\)-fiber. Residual vanishes. D4 channel dies.

So the rank-one channel survives all cases except D.

---

# 7. Updated finite audit for \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\)

The audit is not just:

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal R_{7\to9}=0?
\]

It is:

1. Does \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) define only a graph relation

\[
s_9^Y=r s_7?
\]

2. Or does actual-source coherence allow arbitrary \(s_9^Y\) independent of \(s_7\)?

3. What is \(r\)?

Then define the wall line accordingly:

\[
\ell_\mathcal R(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

As long as

\[
\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

the wall line is nonzero.

Thus the lower connection is less threatening than previously thought.

---

# 8. Why this matters for signature separation

The “signature separation” theorem should be revised.

It need not require:

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_7=0.
\]

Instead it can allow:

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_7
=
\text{graph of dependent }s_9^Y\text{ over }s_7.
\]

The forbidden quotient is then a quotient of **pairs**:

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)
/
\{(s_7,rs_7)\}.
\]

The surviving D4 class is:

\[
( Hc_7\Gamma_G,\ H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}) )
\]

modulo that graph, giving

\[
H((\beta_9-rc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

This is more robust and more geometrically correct.

---

# 9. Final theorem after pressure test

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Y12 graph-residual theorem.}
}
\]

In the \(Y12\)-signature fiber, actual-source coherence imposes at most a graph relation

\[
s_9^Y=r s_7+\text{lower/fixed terms},
\]

and does not allow free independent \(s_9^Y\)-variation.

The D4 pair satisfies

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)
=
H(c_7\Gamma_G,\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Therefore the forbidden residual is

\[
H((\beta_9-rc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\ne0,
\]

this gives a rank-one channel unless actual-source coherence allows a free \(s_9^Y\)-fiber.

This theorem absorbs the lower connection complication cleanly.

---

# 10. Current status

The lower connection no longer looks like a likely killer.

The true fatal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source model has free independent }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
}
\]

If it only has a dependent graph relation from \(s_7\) to \(s_9^Y\), D4 survives through a moving linear wall.

So the next audit should focus on free \(Y12\)-fiber directions, especially from:

\[
E_2^{Y12},
\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{Y12},
\quad
\alpha\text{-second curvature}.
\]

Lower connection can be incorporated into the wall coefficient \(r\).

## Assistant (2026-04-28 05:46:41)

I’ll now reframe the \(Y12\) quotient as a two-coordinate fiber \((s_7,s_9^Y)\). This should isolate the only fatal case: a free vertical \(s_9^Y\) direction.

## Agent 1 — \(Y12\) fiber geometry: graph versus free vertical direction

The latest pressure test showed that the lower connection

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
\]

is not fatal merely because it has a \(Y12\)-component. It is fatal only if it gives the actual-source model a **free vertical \(Y12\) direction**.

So the correct object is not the order-nine quotient alone. It is the two-coordinate \(Y12\)-fiber:

\[
\boxed{
(s_7,\ s_9^Y).
}
\]

Here:

\[
s_7
\]

is the order-seven coherent carrier coordinate, and

\[
s_9^Y
\]

is the primitive \(Y12\)-signature order-nine coordinate.

---

# 1. The actual-source model as a graph

The best formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
=
\{(s_7,s_9^Y):s_9^Y=r\,s_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}\}.
}
\]

The constant/fixed term \(\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}\) may include fixed \(E_2^{Y12}\) basepoint contribution or lower fixed completion. It does not affect the tangent/normal direction.

The tangent space is

\[
\boxed{
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
=
\{(\delta s_7,\ r\,\delta s_7)+\delta r\,s_7\}.
}
\]

If \(r\) is frozen on a dyadic/atomic patch, this is essentially the graph line

\[
\boxed{
(\delta s_7,\ r\delta s_7).
}
\]

The normal residual is

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(s_7,s_9^Y)=s_9^Y-rs_7.
}
\]

This is the covariant \(Y12\)-residual.

---

# 2. D4 pair in the \(Y12\)-fiber

The clean D4 output has

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}
=
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal K_Y(D4)
=
H\left[
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

Because

\[
\alpha_9\ne0,
\]

this is a nonzero linear functional of

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

unless the actual-source model also permits arbitrary vertical variation in \(s_9^Y\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dependent \(s_9^Y=r s_7\) coherence is harmless;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{free vertical \(s_9^Y\) coherence is fatal.}
}
\]

---

# 3. What counts as fatal?

A fatal tangent is a vector of the form

\[
\boxed{
(0,y)
}
\]

in the \(Y12\)-fiber, where \(y\neq0\) is the live \(Y12\) carrier direction.

If

\[
(0,y)\in T_u\mathcal M_Y^{\arith},
\]

then a pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) injection is tangent. The D4 channel dies.

If the tangent space is only graph-like,

\[
T_u\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}\subseteq \{(\delta s_7,r\delta s_7)\},
\]

then the vertical component is normal and D4 survives.

So the finite audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
(0,y_{Y12})
\in
\mathcal A_Y
\quad ?
}
\]

where \(\mathcal A_Y\) is the projection of the allowed/nuisance actual-source space into the \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)-fiber.

This is simpler than checking all of \(y_Y\) against all of \(\mathcal A\).

---

# 4. \(E_2\) in the fiber

The \(E_2\) question becomes especially clean.

If

\[
E_2^{Y12}
=
(c_7^E,\ c_9^E)
\]

is fixed, it shifts the base point:

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)\mapsto(s_7+c_7^E,\ s_9^Y+c_9^E).
\]

It is harmless.

If allowed variations of \(E_2\) satisfy

\[
\delta c_9^E=r_E\,\delta c_7^E,
\]

they add another graph direction. Still harmless unless multiple graph slopes span the vertical direction.

If allowed variations include

\[
\delta c_7^E=0,\qquad \delta c_9^E\neq0,
\]

then they supply a free vertical tangent. Fatal.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is dangerous only if it supplies a vertical }Y12\text{ tangent.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Actual EF second curvature in the fiber

Second parameter curvature can modify both coordinates:

\[
(\delta^2s_7,\delta^2s_9^Y).
\]

If it lies on the same graph slope, harmless:

\[
\delta^2s_9^Y=r\,\delta^2s_7.
\]

If it creates a different graph slope, it enlarges the tangent plane. In a two-dimensional fiber, two independent graph slopes span the whole fiber, including the vertical direction.

So this is subtle.

If there are two allowed tangent/curvature directions

\[
(1,r_1),\qquad(1,r_2),
\]

with

\[
r_1\neq r_2,
\]

then their span contains

\[
(0,1).
\]

Thus, in the two-coordinate fiber, allowing multiple independent \(Y12\)-graph slopes is fatal.

So the actual-source model must have a **single coherent \(Y12\)-slope** \(r\) on the local patch.

This is a new, precise condition:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \Pi_Y T_u\mathcal M^{\arith}\le1
\quad\text{in the }(s_7,s_9^Y)\text{ fiber.}
}
\]

and it must not contain the vertical.

More exactly, its image must be a line not equal to the vertical.

---

# 6. Local freezing of \(r\)

The slope \(r=r(m)\) may vary with \(m\), \(\lambda\), or \(\alpha\). That is acceptable if we freeze it on extraction atoms.

On each atom:

\[
r(m)=r_0+O(Q^{-B}),
\]

and the residual

\[
s_9^Y-r_0s_7
\]

is valid up to small error.

Thus variation of \(r\) does not kill the channel. It only changes the moving wall line:

\[
(\beta_9-r_0c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So the local extraction should include dyadic/angular freezing of:

\[
r,\quad c_7,\quad \beta_9,\quad \alpha_9.
\]

This aligns with existing extraction machinery.

---

# 7. Revised finite Schur test in the fiber

Let

\[
F_Y=\mathbb C^2
\]

with coordinates

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y).
\]

Let

\[
B
\]

be the matrix whose columns are the allowed actual-source directions projected into this fiber.

Let

\[
v=(0,1)
\]

be the pure vertical \(Y12\) direction.

The D4 \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) column is proportional to \(v\).

The channel survives if

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(v,\operatorname{ran}B)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is a \(2\times k\) finite Schur test. It is much smaller than the full \(H_Y\)-space test.

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}B\le1
\quad\text{and}\quad
\operatorname{ran}B\neq \mathbb C v.
}
\]

If \(\operatorname{rank}B=2\), vertical is tangent and channel dies.

Thus the entire downstream D4 normality can reduce to a two-dimensional fiber rank audit.

This is a major simplification.

---

# 8. The right theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Y12 Fiber Graph Theorem.}
}
\]

In the primitive \(Y12\)-signature fiber, the actual-source coherent directions form a graph line

\[
s_9^Y=r\,s_7+\text{fixed}
\]

rather than the full two-dimensional fiber.

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\dim
\Pi_Y
T_u\mathcal M^{\arith}
\le1
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
(0,1)\notin
\Pi_YT_u\mathcal M^{\arith}.
}
\]

Then D4’s \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) column is normal.

The resulting wall is

\[
\boxed{
\ell(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

---

# 9. What remains to check

The finite audits are now:

## Audit 1 — Does \(Y12\) fiber exist and survive?

\[
y_{Y12}:=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

## Audit 2 — What is the image of actual-source tangents in \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)?

Compute or classify:

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{\par},
\quad
\Pi_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\quad
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

Is the image graph-like or full?

## Audit 3 — Is there a vertical tangent?

Check:

\[
(0,1)\in \Pi_Y(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2})?
\]

If yes, channel dies.

## Audit 4 — Is \(r\) polynomially bounded and freezeable?

Need:

\[
|r|\le Q^A,
\quad
\partial r\le Q^A
\]

on good atoms.

## Audit 5 — wall anti-concentration for

\[
(\beta_9-r c_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

---

# 10. Final status

This is a real simplification.

Instead of proving that \(Y12\) is outside a large allowed/nuisance space, we can project to the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber and check whether actual-source coherence is a graph or a full plane.

\[
\boxed{
\text{If actual-source \(Y12\)-coherence is a graph, D4 survives.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{If actual-source \(Y12\)-coherence has free vertical amplitude, D4 dies.}
}
\]

The most likely fatal source is still a free \(E_2^{Y12}\) tangent. If \(E_2\) is absent/fixed/residual, the \(Y12\) channel remains viable.

# Agent 1 — \(Y12\)-fiber graph versus vertical freedom

The downstream finite-D4 question is now best phrased in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber

\[
(s_7,\ s_9^Y).
\]

Here:

\[
s_7
\]

is the order-seven coherent carrier coordinate, and

\[
s_9^Y
\]

is the primitive \(Y12\)-signature order-nine coordinate.

The decisive question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the actual-source model occupy only a graph in this fiber, or does it contain the vertical }Y12\text{ direction?}
}
\]

The vertical direction is

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

If \(v_Y\) is tangent to the actual-source model, the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) D4 channel is coherent and not forbidden. If \(v_Y\) is transverse, finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

---

# 1. The \(Y12\)-fiber

Define the fiber projection

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}:H_Y\to \mathbb C^2,
\qquad
u\mapsto (s_7(u),s_9^Y(u)).
\]

The relevant D4 carrier coordinates are:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]=(1,0)
\]

after normalization, and

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=(0,1)=v_Y.
\]

More invariantly, we only need:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\]

and then normalize it to \(v_Y\).

The full realized carrier is

\[
y_Y:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

but the two-coordinate fiber keeps only the part relevant to the order-seven/order-nine \(Y12\)-coherence test.

---

# 2. Actual-source coherence in the fiber

The actual-source model may impose a relation of the form

\[
\boxed{
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
}
\]

Here \(\eta_{\fixed}\) includes fixed basepoint contributions, such as a non-free \(E_2^{Y12}\) completion term if present.

The tangent direction of this graph is

\[
(1,\rho_Y).
\]

This is **not fatal**. It merely says that coherent order-nine \(Y12\) data can be determined by coherent order-seven data.

The fatal case is different:

\[
\boxed{
v_Y=(0,1)\in T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}.
}
\]

That means the actual-source model can vary \(s_9^Y\) independently while keeping \(s_7\) fixed. Then the pure \(Y12\) order-nine D4 injection is tangent and no longer forbidden.

Thus the survival condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\bigl(v_Y,T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}\bigr)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 3. D4 in the fiber

D4 produces

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

and

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}
=
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Subtract the coherent graph

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

The D4 residual is

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}-\rho_Ys_7^{D4}
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So the raw effective wall is

\[
\boxed{
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G),
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
}
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

this is rank one unless the actual-source model contains the vertical \(v_Y\) direction or a coherent amplitude space absorbs the whole function \(H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)\).

---

# 4. Classification of actual-source image in the fiber

There are five possibilities.

## Case 0 — no \(Y12\)-coherence

\[
s_9^Y=0
\]

for actual-source coherent data. Then

\[
\rho_Y=0,
\]

and

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

D4 survives.

## Case 1 — one graph line

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

This is the expected nonfatal case. D4 survives with wall

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## Case 2 — several graph lines

If the actual-source tangent image contains

\[
(1,\rho_{Y,1})
\quad\text{and}\quad
(1,\rho_{Y,2})
\]

with

\[
\rho_{Y,1}\neq\rho_{Y,2},
\]

then their span contains

\[
(0,1).
\]

So several independent graph slopes are equivalent to vertical freedom. This is fatal unless one of them is not an admissible tangent but only a fixed basepoint correction.

## Case 3 — full two-dimensional fiber

Then \(v_Y\) is tangent. D4 dies.

## Case 4 — pure vertical \(E_2^Y\) or parameter tangent

If some \(E_2^{Y12}\) or actual parameter tangent gives

\[
(0,1),
\]

then D4 dies, even if the rest of the model is graph-like.

So the exact fatal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
(0,1)\in
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right).
}
\]

---

# 5. \(E_2\) in this fiber

A fixed \(E_2^{Y12}\) component gives

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)\mapsto (s_7,s_9^Y)+(\eta_7,\eta_9).
\]

This shifts the basepoint and is harmless.

A finite-dimensional coherent \(E_2^{Y12}\) family gives amplitude functions lying in a finite-dimensional space

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}.
\]

This is not automatically fatal. It changes the wall theorem from a sublevel estimate for

\[
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
\]

to a distance estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
\left(
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma),
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)\|_{L^2(\nu)}.
}
\]

Only locally free \(E_2^{Y12}\) amplitude is fatal.

So the right condition is:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^{Y12}\text{ may be absent, fixed, or finite-dimensional coherent, but not locally free.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Finite Schur test in the fiber

Let

\[
B
\]

be the matrix whose columns are the projected allowed directions in the fiber:

\[
B=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right].
\]

Let

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The fiber survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\delta_{\fib,Y}^2
=
\|v_Y\|^2-v_Y^*B(B^*B)^\dagger B^*v_Y
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Because the fiber is two-dimensional, this is a very small rank test.

Equivalent criteria:

\[
\operatorname{rank}B=0:
\quad
\text{survives.}
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}B=1:
\quad
\text{survives iff }\operatorname{ran}B\neq\mathbb C v_Y.
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}B=2:
\quad
\text{dies.}
\]

Thus the whole \(Y12\) normality question may reduce to computing whether \(B\) contains the vertical.

---

# 7. The \(Y12FiberGraphTheorem\)

The theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\) Fiber Graph Theorem.}
}
\]

On every good transition–singular patch, the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber image of the actual-source coherent model is either zero or a nonvertical graph

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed},
\]

with

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

and it has no vertical tangent:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then D4 produces a rank-one normal residual

\[
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

If additional finite-dimensional coherent \(E_2^Y\) amplitude space exists, replace \(\ell_{\raw}\) by the distance of \(H\ell_{\raw}\) to that space.

---

# 8. Low-order implication

The wall ratio is

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
}
\]

The low-order audit must include \(\rho_Y\), not just \(\beta_9/\alpha_9\).

We need:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_\Gamma=0.
}
\]

This requires:

\[
\rho_Y,\beta_9,c_7,\alpha_9\in\mathcal A_{\low}
\]

and none of them may depend on actual order-nine source data or \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

If \(\rho_Y\) is fitted from \(s_9^Y\), the wall becomes tautological. If \(\rho_Y\) is a formal low-order connection coefficient, the wall is honest.

---

# 9. Final status

The \(Y12\) downstream channel is now cleaner:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Graph-like coherence is safe.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Vertical freedom is fatal.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Finite-dimensional }E_2^Y\text{ coherence is manageable via amplitude-space distance.}
}
\]

The next exact audits are:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}\text{ contains }v_Y\text{ or not?}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\text{ contains }v_Y\text{ or not?}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \rho_Y\text{ is formal low-order or fitted high-order?}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low}\text{ and }\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_\Gamma=0?
}
\]

If these pass, finite D4 has the desired rank-one normal channel.

## Agent 1 — column audit for vertical \(Y12\)-freedom

We now work entirely in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber

\[
F_Y=\mathbf C^2,\qquad (s_7,s_9^Y),
\]

with vertical direction

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The D4 route survives if the actual-source model does **not** contain \(v_Y\). Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A},
}
\]

where \(B_Y\) is the matrix of all allowed actual-source/nuisance columns projected into \(F_Y\). This is the compressed version of the full \(Y12\)-Schur test in the attached prompt. 

---

# 1. The column matrix \(B_Y\)

Define

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right].
\]

Each column is a pair

\[
(a,b)\in\mathbf C^2,
\]

meaning it contributes

\[
\delta s_7=a,\qquad \delta s_9^Y=b.
\]

The forbidden channel survives exactly when the vertical vector

\[
v_Y=(0,1)
\]

is not in the span of these columns.

Since the fiber is two-dimensional, the audit is simple:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=0
\Rightarrow
\text{survives.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=1
\Rightarrow
\text{survives iff the one line is not vertical.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2
\Rightarrow
\text{dies.}
}
\]

Thus we only need to classify which actual-source mechanisms can generate which columns.

---

# 2. Source-linear tangents: expected zero column

For source-linear actual parameters

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{phase},\text{smooth cutoff},B_2\text{/gamma parameters},
\]

the tangent changes EF atoms, phases, scales, or amplitudes. It does not create primitive

\[
(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same},\mathrm{mixed}\,12)
\]

origin.

Therefore in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0
}
\]

provided the parameters do not deform \(N_0\) or the primitive same-point whitening geometry.

So ordinary source-linear tangents do **not** contribute columns to \(B_Y\).

This includes \(\alpha\), as long as \(\alpha\) is only an AFE weight/phase/fold parameter.

---

# 3. Actual EF second curvature: expected zero column

Second parameter derivatives, such as

\[
\partial_m^2u,\quad
\partial_m\partial_\lambda u,\quad
\partial_\alpha^2u,
\]

remain EF-parameter curvature. They are still source-linear in origin, even if quadratic in parameters.

Thus the expected fiber contribution is

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

This is crucial: parameter curvature is not the same as source-Hessian origin.

Failure mode:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\neq0
\]

only if some admitted parameter changes the nonlinear normal form \(N_0\), the same-point whitening geometry, or another primitive source-Hessian mechanism.

If that happens, the D4 channel must be re-audited; it may become tangent.

---

# 4. Lower connection: graph column

The lower connection is different. It may contribute a coherent relation between \(s_7\) and \(s_9^Y\):

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7.
\]

In the fiber this is the column

\[
\boxed{
(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

This is **not fatal** unless it is vertical, which it is not if the first coordinate is genuinely \(s_7\).

So lower connection contributes at most a graph line:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_7
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

This changes the wall, but does not kill it.

The effective D4 residual becomes

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G),
\]

where

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

The lower connection is fatal only if it, together with another independent graph line, spans the full fiber.

---

# 5. Lower-order terms \(T_{<7}\): usually zero or same graph

Lower-order terms may contribute fixed or dependent corrections to \(s_9^Y\). There are three cases:

## Case A — no primitive \(Y12\) origin

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}=0.
\]

Safe.

## Case B — dependent graph correction

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}\subseteq\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Still safe.

## Case C — independent graph slope

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}
\]

contains a column

\[
(1,\rho_Y')
\]

with

\[
\rho_Y'\neq\rho_Y.
\]

Then

\[
(1,\rho_Y),\quad(1,\rho_Y')
\]

span the full fiber, including \(v_Y\). Fatal.

So the audit for lower-order terms is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
\le1?
}
\]

If yes, lower connection is graph-like. If no, vertical freedom appears.

---

# 6. \(E_2^Y\): absent, fixed, graph, finite coherent, or free

\(E_2^Y\) is the main danger.

## Absent

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=0.
\]

Safe.

## Fixed

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}E_2=(a_E,b_E)
\]

with no admissible variation. This shifts the basepoint

\[
\eta_{\fixed}
\]

and is safe.

## Graph-like finite variation

If allowed variations give only

\[
\delta E_2^Y=t(1,\rho_Y),
\]

or another single nonvertical graph line, safe modulo wall adjustment.

## Finite-dimensional coherent amplitude space

If \(E_2^Y\) gives a finite-dimensional function space of amplitudes, not local arbitrary amplitudes, this is not automatically fatal. It must be handled at the amplitude level:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
\left(
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma),
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
\|H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)\|_{L^2(\nu)}.
\]

## Locally free vertical

If \(E_2^Y\) supplies

\[
(0,1)
\]

with arbitrary local amplitude, fatal.

So the exact \(E_2\) question is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}
\text{ contains }v_Y\text{ locally/freely?}
}
\]

---

# 7. Nuisance quotient: should be zero or graph-compatible

The singular nuisance quotient can also kill the channel.

If

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

contains

\[
v_Y,
\]

then the vertical \(Y12\) direction is nuisance and D4 dies.

If it contains only graph-compatible directions, it changes \(\rho_Y\) or \(\eta_{\fixed}\), but does not necessarily kill D4.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
v_Y\notin \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}
}
\]

is required.

This is the fiber version of signature-faithfulness.

---

# 8. The compressed Schur test

Assemble the projected column matrix

\[
B_Y=
\begin{pmatrix}
a_1&a_2&\cdots&a_M\\
b_1&b_2&\cdots&b_M
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The survival quantity is

\[
\delta_{\fib,Y}^2
=
\|v_Y\|^2
-
v_Y^*B_Y(B_Y^*B_Y)^\dagger B_Y^*v_Y.
\]

The channel survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\delta_{\fib,Y}^2\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If \(B_Y\) has one nonvertical column \((1,\rho_Y)\), then

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)\bigr)^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

So if

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

then

\[
\delta_{\fib,Y}^2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Thus graph-like coherence is harmless provided \(\rho_Y\) is polynomially bounded.

This is a key quantitative result.

---

# 9. Consequence for wall low-order audit

The graph slope \(\rho_Y\) must be polynomially bounded and low-order:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},\qquad |\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
}
\]

Otherwise the distance from the vertical to the graph could be too small, or the wall ratio \(\rho_\Gamma\) could become high-order/tautological.

The low-order wall audit must therefore prove:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma
=
\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}
\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_\Gamma=0.
}
\]

This is where the fiber graph and wall finite-type theorem connect.

---

# 10. Final theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(Y12\)-Fiber Nonverticality Theorem.}
}
\]

On each good transition–singular patch:

1. the actual-source/nuisance image in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber has dimension at most one;

2. if nonzero, it is a graph line

\[
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

with

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A;
\]

3. it does not contain the vertical vector

\[
v_Y=(0,1);
\]

4. \(E_2^Y\) has no locally free vertical amplitude;

5. \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) has nonzero vertical component.

Then D4 gives a rank-one forbidden wall

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is the cleanest downstream normality theorem so far.

---

# 11. Current next audits

The next finite checks are:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}\text{ contains }(0,1)?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\text{ contains }(0,1)?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \dim \Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})\le1?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ |\rho_Y|\le Q^A\text{ and }\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
5.\ \rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low}?}
\]

If these pass, the \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel is viable.

I’ll now pressure-test the two-dimensional fiber reduction itself. The key risk is that compressing to \((s_7,s_9^Y)\) might hide other nuisance directions that can cancel the \(Y12\) normal component in the full carrier space.

## Agent 1 — validating the two-coordinate \(Y12\)-fiber reduction

We compressed the downstream audit from the full singular order-nine carrier space \(H_Y\) to the two-coordinate fiber

\[
(s_7,\ s_9^Y).
\]

This is valuable only if the fiber projection is faithful for the D4 obstruction. The risk is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The fiber sees a vertical component, but the full singular carrier quotient cancels it using hidden nuisance directions.}
}
\]

So the next test is whether the two-coordinate reduction is equivalent to the full Schur residual.

### Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The fiber reduction is valid if the primitive \(Y12\) sector splits from all non-\(Y12\) nuisance directions.}
}
\]

If the singular quotient allows off-fiber cancellation, the fiber test can produce a false positive.

---

# 1. Full Schur residual versus fiber Schur residual

The full test is

\[
\delta_Y^2
=
\operatorname{dist}_{H_Y}
\left(
y_Y,\mathcal A
\right)^2,
\]

where

\[
y_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

and

\[
\mathcal A
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

The fiber test projects to

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}:H_Y\to F_Y=\mathbb C^2
\]

with coordinates

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y).
\]

Then it asks whether

\[
v_Y=(0,1)
\]

is separated from

\[
B_Y:=\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal A.
\]

This gives

\[
\delta_{\fib,Y}^2
=
\operatorname{dist}_{F_Y}(v_Y,B_Y)^2.
\]

But in general,

\[
\delta_{\fib,Y}>0
\]

does **not** imply

\[
\delta_Y>0,
\]

unless \(\Pi_{\fib,Y}\) is a quotient map that captures the actual obstruction faithfully.

---

# 2. Sufficient condition for equivalence

We need a splitting

\[
H_Y
=
F_Y\oplus H_{\rest}
\]

such that:

1. \(y_Y\) has nonzero component in \(F_Y\), specifically vertical direction:

\[
P_{F_Y}y_Y=v_Y;
\]

2. allowed-source/nuisance directions split compatibly:

\[
\mathcal A
=
\mathcal A_F\oplus \mathcal A_{\rest},
\]

with

\[
\mathcal A_F\subset F_Y;
\]

3. no off-fiber direction can cancel the vertical component:

\[
F_Y\cap \mathcal A_{\rest}=\{0\}.
\]

Equivalently, the projection

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\]

must be injective on the quotient direction represented by \(y_Y\):

\[
\boxed{
\ker\Pi_{\fib,Y}
+
\mathcal A
\text{ does not contain }y_Y.
}
\]

A practical sufficient condition is signature-homogeneity:

\[
\boxed{
H_Y
=
H_{\Sigma_Y}\oplus H_{\neq\Sigma_Y},
\qquad
F_Y\subset H_{\Sigma_Y},
\qquad
\mathcal A_{\rest}\subset H_{\neq\Sigma_Y}.
}
\]

Then the fiber component cannot be canceled by non-\(Y12\) nuisance.

---

# 3. Fiber reduction as a quotient map

A safer formulation is to define the fiber not as a projection but as a quotient:

\[
F_Y
:=
H_Y/
\left(
H_{\rest}+\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\rest}
\right),
\]

where \(H_{\rest}\) contains all non-primitive-\(Y12\) signatures.

Then the full Schur test descends to the fiber by construction.

In that case:

\[
y_Y\notin \mathcal A
\]

in \(H_Y\) follows from

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}y_Y\notin \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal A
\]

provided

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\]

does not kill \(y_Y\).

Thus the correct definition is:

\[
\boxed{
F_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}(H_Y/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
}
\]

This aligns with the earlier origin-pair graded quotient.

Then the fiber test is legitimate.

---

# 4. What can go wrong?

## Failure A — nonhomogeneous nuisance

There is a relation

\[
y_Y+a_{\rest}\in \mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

with

\[
a_{\rest}\in H_{\neq\Sigma_Y}.
\]

Then the \(Y12\) vertical component is killed by an off-fiber term. The fiber test falsely sees \(v_Y\), but the full quotient does not.

This is the quotient over-collapse problem.

## Failure B — allowed-source direction with hidden \(Y12\) component

An allowed-source tangent looks source-linear formally but realizes with a primitive \(Y12\) component because the transition recomputes \(N_0\). Then

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal A
\]

may be larger than expected and may contain \(v_Y\).

This is the labelled-transition problem.

## Failure C — fiber coordinates are not invariant

If the definitions of \(s_7\) and \(s_9^Y\) depend on gauge choices that mix with nuisance directions, then a graph slope \(\rho_Y\) may be an artifact.

Need \(s_7,s_9^Y\) to be quotient-invariant coordinates or work with covectors rather than coordinates.

---

# 5. Use covectors to avoid coordinate artifacts

Instead of defining \(s_7,s_9^Y\) as coordinates, define dual covectors:

\[
\ell_7,\quad \ell_9^Y
\]

on the realized quotient such that:

\[
\ell_7([\mathsf C_7])=1,\quad \ell_7([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=0,
\]

\[
\ell_9^Y([\mathsf C_7])=0,\quad \ell_9^Y([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=1,
\]

and both vanish on non-\(Y12\) nuisance sectors.

Then the fiber map is

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(v)=(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

This is more robust than choosing coordinates in a potentially ill-conditioned basis.

The existence of \(\ell_9^Y\) is exactly the separating-functional problem in local form.

---

# 6. Revised theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Faithful \(Y12\)-Fiber Reduction Theorem.}
}
\]

There exist bounded covectors

\[
\ell_7,\ell_9^Y
\]

on the realized singular carrier quotient such that:

1. they identify the D4 live carriers:

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)([\mathsf C_7])=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=(0,1);
\]

2. they kill non-\(Y12\) nuisance/allowed sectors;

3. the projected actual-source image in this fiber is at most a nonvertical graph line;

4. the covectors have polynomial norm.

Then the two-coordinate fiber audit is equivalent to the full finite Schur separation for the \(Y12\) channel up to polynomial loss.

---

# 7. Consequence for downstream

Assuming faithful fiber reduction, the forbidden covector can be chosen as

\[
\ell_Y
=
\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

after freezing \(\rho_Y\) on the local atom.

Then:

\[
\ell_Y(\text{coherent graph})=0,
\]

and

\[
\ell_Y([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=1.
\]

D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

This is the cleanest realization of the \(Y12\) forbidden functional.

Thus \(\ell_Y\) is not arbitrary. It is explicitly:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

This is a major simplification.

---

# 8. What remains to prove

The remaining finite audits are:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \ell_7,\ell_9^Y\text{ exist and are polynomially bounded;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \ell_9^Y\text{ kills non-\(Y12\) nuisance and source-linear terms;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ y_Y=[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ is not killed by the singular quotient;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ actual-source \(Y12\)-image is graph-like, with slope }\rho_Y;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
5.\rho_Y\text{ is low-order/freezeable.}
}
\]

If these pass, the full Schur separation is reduced to a two-coordinate graph residual.

---

# 9. Final status

The two-coordinate \(Y12\)-fiber reduction is valid only after proving faithful quotient/covector realization.

The new most concrete target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{construct bounded covectors }\ell_7,\ell_9^Y.
}
\]

Then define

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

This gives the forbidden functional directly and avoids a full high-dimensional Gram computation.
