## Agent 1 — constructing \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\)

We reduced the \(Y12\)-normal channel to the existence of two bounded covectors

\[
\ell_7,\qquad \ell_9^Y
\]

on the realized singular carrier quotient, with

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)([\mathsf C_7])=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)([\mathsf C_{Y12}])=(0,1).
\]

Then the actual-source graph relation

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}
\]

is killed by

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So the core finite construction is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{build bounded dual covectors to the live carrier pair }([\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
}
\]

---

# 1. Finite carrier-dual construction

Let \(H_{\sing}^{(9)}\) be the realized singular order-nine carrier Hilbert space after quotienting obvious nuisance directions. Let

\[
e_7:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

\[
e_Y:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal B
\]

be the subspace to annihilate:

\[
\mathcal B
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal S_{\mathrm{nonlive}}
+
\mathcal S_{\mathrm{source\ linear}}
+
\mathcal S_{\mathrm{wrong\ signature}},
\]

where \(\mathcal S_{\mathrm{nonlive}}\) contains all retained singular directions not belonging to the two live carriers, and \(\mathcal S_{\mathrm{source\ linear}}\) contains actual-source source-linear directions.

We want covectors satisfying

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal B)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(\mathcal B)=0.
\]

Therefore the finite object is the quotient

\[
\mathcal V_Y
=
H_{\sing}^{(9)}/\mathcal B.
\]

In this quotient, the target is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_Y]\}=2
}
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

If this holds, define \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) as the dual basis to \([e_7],[e_Y]\).

---

# 2. Gram criterion for bounded duals

Let

\[
P_{\mathcal B^\perp}
\]

be orthogonal projection onto \(\mathcal B^\perp\). Define

\[
\tilde e_7=P_{\mathcal B^\perp}e_7,
\qquad
\tilde e_Y=P_{\mathcal B^\perp}e_Y.
\]

Form the \(2\times2\) Gram matrix

\[
G_{\live}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \tilde e_7,\tilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \tilde e_7,\tilde e_Y\rangle\\
\langle \tilde e_Y,\tilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \tilde e_Y,\tilde e_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Bounded dual covectors exist iff

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If only the \(Y12\) rank-one channel is needed, we can weaken this to

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B+\mathbf C e_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
}
\]

This says \(e_Y\) is not absorbed by nuisance plus the order-seven carrier.

That is the essential finite criterion.

---

# 3. Signature proof of the Gram lower bound

The refined signature route gives a clean sufficient condition.

If

\[
e_7
\]

and

\[
e_Y
\]

belong to distinct refined signature sectors, then

\[
\langle \tilde e_7,\tilde e_Y\rangle=0
\]

up to polynomially small leakage, and

\[
\|\tilde e_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The signatures are:

\[
e_7:\quad
\Sigma_7
=
(\text{order }7,\ \text{carrier }[\mathsf C_7],\ldots),
\]

\[
e_Y:\quad
\Sigma_Y
=
(2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=D\mathcal W_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

If the singular quotient respects these signatures, then

\[
G_{\live}
\]

is block diagonal at leading order and bounded below.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{signature-faithfulness implies bounded live duals.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Relation to the previous fiber graph

Once the dual covectors exist, the \(Y12\)-fiber coordinates are not arbitrary. They are defined by

\[
s_7(v)=\ell_7(v),
\]

\[
s_9^Y(v)=\ell_9^Y(v).
\]

Then a coherent actual-source graph is exactly:

\[
\ell_9^Y(v)=\rho_Y\ell_7(v)+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

The vertical direction is

\[
v_Y=(0,1)
\]

in these dual coordinates.

This avoids gauge ambiguity: \(s_7,s_9^Y\) are now quotient-invariant coordinates defined by covectors.

---

# 5. Actual-source graph slope \(\rho_Y\)

With \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) defined, the graph slope is not vague. For coherent actual-source variations \(v_{\arith}\),

\[
\rho_Y
=
\frac{\ell_9^Y(v_{\arith})}{\ell_7(v_{\arith})}
\]

when \(\ell_7(v_{\arith})\neq0\), and consistency requires this ratio to be the same for all allowed graph directions on the local atom.

If there are two allowed directions \(v_1,v_2\) with

\[
\frac{\ell_9^Y(v_1)}{\ell_7(v_1)}
\neq
\frac{\ell_9^Y(v_2)}{\ell_7(v_2)},
\]

then the actual-source image spans the full \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)-fiber and the D4 channel dies.

So the graph theorem is equivalent to:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(v_1)&\ell_7(v_2)&\cdots\\
\ell_9^Y(v_1)&\ell_9^Y(v_2)&\cdots
\end{pmatrix}
\le1,
}
\]

and the image is not vertical.

This is another small finite rank audit.

---

# 6. The no-free-\(E_2^Y\) criterion in covector form

Let \(w_j\) be the allowed \(E_2^Y\)-variation directions. Compute

\[
(\ell_7(w_j),\ell_9^Y(w_j)).
\]

Safe cases:

\[
(\ell_7(w_j),\ell_9^Y(w_j))=(0,0)
\]

or

\[
(\ell_7(w_j),\ell_9^Y(w_j))\in\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

for all \(j\).

Fatal case:

\[
\exists j:\quad
(\ell_7(w_j),\ell_9^Y(w_j))=(0,\ast),\quad \ast\neq0,
\]

or more generally the \(E_2^Y\)-variation directions together with the graph line span \(\mathbf C^2\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\begin{pmatrix}
1&\ell_7(w_1)&\ell_7(w_2)&\cdots\\
\rho_Y&\ell_9^Y(w_1)&\ell_9^Y(w_2)&\cdots
\end{pmatrix}
=1
}
\]

is safe; rank \(2\) is fatal.

This makes the \(E_2\) audit concrete.

---

# 7. Effective forbidden covector

Assume the actual-source image is the graph line

\[
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
}
\]

kills the coherent graph.

Its action on D4 is:

\[
\ell_Y
\left(
Hc_7\Gamma_G e_7
+
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})e_Y
\right)
\]

\[
=
H\left[
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

This gives:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma
=
\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
}
\]

The forbidden wall is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)=\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
}
\]

This is now fully determined by the live duals and the graph slope.

---

# 8. Required boundedness

The covector

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

must be polynomially bounded:

\[
\|\ell_Y\|\le Q^A.
\]

This requires:

\[
\|\ell_7\|+\|\ell_9^Y\|\le Q^A
\]

and

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

So the graph slope cannot be super-polynomial. If

\[
|\rho_Y|\gg Q^A,
\]

the vertical direction is almost tangent and the wall becomes too ill-conditioned.

Thus the graph theorem must include:

\[
\boxed{
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
}
\]

---

# 9. Final finite theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Live \(Y12\) Dual Pair Theorem.}
}
\]

There exist covectors

\[
\ell_7,\ell_9^Y
\]

on the realized singular carrier quotient such that:

1. they kill nuisance and nonlive/source-linear directions;

2. they are dual to the live pair:

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_7)=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_Y)=(0,1);
\]

3. their norms are polynomially bounded;

4. the actual-source \(Y12\)-image is a nonvertical graph

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed},
\qquad
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A;
\]

5. \(E_2^Y\)-allowed variations do not add a second independent fiber direction.

Then

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

is the desired local forbidden covector.

This is a very compact finite audit.

---

# 10. Remaining exact checks

The next finite checks are now:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \operatorname{rank}\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2})\le1?}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \text{if rank }1,\text{ is the line nonvertical and slope } \rho_Y\text{ polynomial?}}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \rho_\Gamma=(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)/\alpha_9\text{ low-order?}}
\]

If these pass, the \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel is finite-green.

## Agent 1 — Live \(Y12\) dual pair and fiber Schur

### Naming convention used

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the lower \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope:

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma
\]

is the effective wall ratio after graph subtraction:

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

\[
R_{\fold}(x)
\]

is the AFE/O2O3 fold ratio:

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

I will not use \(r\) for any of these.

The graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The instruction asks for the live \(Y12\) dual pair and fiber Schur audit. This is the current finite replacement for the earlier vague \(\Lambda\)-search. 

---

# 1. Finite quotient space

Let

\[
\mathcal H_Y
\]

be the realized singular order-nine carrier quotient after labelled transition and singular realization. Its norm should be a Riesz-normalized singular carrier norm, with required conditioning

\[
\boxed{
\kappa(\mathcal H_Y)\le Q^A.
}
\]

Inside \(\mathcal H_Y\), define the realized live carriers

\[
e_7:=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

\[
e_Y:=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The target is to construct bounded covectors

\[
\ell_7,\qquad \ell_9^Y
\]

with

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_7)=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_Y)=(0,1).
\]

These covectors define the actual coordinates

\[
s_7(v)=\ell_7(v),
\qquad
s_9^Y(v)=\ell_9^Y(v).
\]

---

# 2. The subspace killed by the live duals

Let

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
\]

be the subspace that the live duals must annihilate before defining the \(Y12\)-fiber. It includes:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

singular nuisance directions;

\[
\mathcal S_{\wrong}
\]

wrong-signature directions, including source-linear EF pieces, gamma/background pieces, chart curvature, moving-whitening tail terms, and all non-\(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}\)-origin terms;

\[
\mathcal S_{\nonlive}
\]

nonlive retained singular carrier directions that are not \(e_7\) or \(e_Y\).

Then the quotient relevant for the live dual pair is

\[
\mathcal V_Y
=
\mathcal H_Y/\mathcal B_{\live}.
\]

The required finite condition is:

\[
\boxed{
[e_7],[e_Y]\text{ are linearly independent in }\mathcal V_Y
\text{ with polynomial conditioning.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Gram criterion for the live dual pair

Let

\[
P_{\mathcal B^\perp}
\]

be the orthogonal projection onto \(\mathcal B_{\live}^{\perp}\). Define

\[
\widetilde e_7=P_{\mathcal B^\perp}e_7,
\qquad
\widetilde e_Y=P_{\mathcal B^\perp}e_Y.
\]

Form

\[
G_{\live}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_Y\rangle\\
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The live dual pair exists with polynomial bounds iff

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the clean finite audit for \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\).

A weaker condition sufficient for rank-one \(Y12\) is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
}
\]

If this weaker condition fails, then \(e_Y\) is absorbed by nuisance/wrong-signature/order-seven directions, and the \(Y12\) rank-one channel dies.

---

# 4. Signature proof of the Gram bound

The refined \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=DW_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same\ point},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ 9,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

If the labelled transition and singular quotient are signature-faithful, then \(e_Y\) has a nonzero component in this sector and \(\mathcal B_{\live}\) has no component there. Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)\asymp \|e_Y\|
\]

up to polynomial conditioning.

So the signature/origin argument is a sufficient proof of the Gram condition.

The exact finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}e_Y\neq0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}(\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)=0.
}
\]

---

# 5. Fiber matrix for actual-source directions

Once \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist, define the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber map

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(v)
=
(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

Let

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right].
\]

This is a \(2\times M\) matrix. The vertical vector is

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The D4 channel survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Because the fiber is two-dimensional, the classification is exact.

---

# 6. Rank classification of \(B_Y\)

## Case 0: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=0\)

There is no actual-source \(Y12\)-fiber motion.

Then

\[
\rho_Y=0
\]

and

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

D4 survives.

---

## Case 1: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=1\), nonvertical

Then

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

for some graph slope \(\rho_Y\). This is the expected coherent case.

The graph-killing covector is

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

This is a rank-one forbidden channel, provided

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

Indeed,

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y))^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

So polynomially bounded \(\rho_Y\) gives polynomial nonverticality.

---

## Case 2: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=1\), vertical

Then

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbf C(0,1).
\]

The \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) \(Y12\) direction is actual-source coherent. D4 dies.

---

## Case 3: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2\)

Then the actual-source/nuisance image fills the whole \(Y12\)-fiber. D4 dies.

This can happen if two different graph slopes occur:

\[
(1,\rho_{Y,1}),\qquad (1,\rho_{Y,2}),
\qquad \rho_{Y,1}\neq \rho_{Y,2},
\]

or if a graph direction plus vertical \(E_2^Y\) direction are both present.

---

# 7. \(E_2^Y\) in the fiber

If \(E_2^Y\) is fixed, it contributes only to \(\eta_{\fixed}\). It does not enter \(B_Y\) as a tangent column.

If \(E_2^Y\) varies only along the graph line

\[
(1,\rho_Y),
\]

it changes the coherent graph but is still safe.

If \(E_2^Y\) contributes

\[
(0,1)
\]

with local amplitude freedom, it is fatal.

If \(E_2^Y\) gives a finite-dimensional coherent amplitude space but not local fiber freedom, the local fiber remains safe; the issue moves to the amplitude Schur theorem for

\[
F_Y=H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
\]

modulo

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

Thus local fiber normality and global amplitude absorption are distinct.

---

# 8. Wall ratio and low-order requirement

In the nonvertical graph case, define

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
}
\]

The wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)=\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

For this wall to be non-tautological and to feed bounded-charge O2/O3, we need:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y,\rho_\Gamma\in \mathcal A_{\low},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_\Gamma=0.
}
\]

If \(\rho_Y\) is fitted from actual \(s_9^Y/s_7\), the wall becomes tautological. If \(\rho_Y\) is a formal lower connection coefficient, the wall remains honest.

---

# 9. Final theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{LiveY12DualPair + FiberSchur.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(e_7,e_Y\) survive the singular quotient with

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A};
\]

2. \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) are the bounded dual covectors;

3. the projected actual-source matrix \(B_Y\) has rank at most one and is nonvertical:

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A};
\]

4. in the rank-one case, its slope \(\rho_Y\) is polynomially bounded and low-order.

Then

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

is a local forbidden covector and D4 produces the rank-one wall

\[
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

This is the desired local \(Y12\)-normal channel.

---

# 10. Current finite blockers

The exact finite blockers are now:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})<Q^{-A};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ B_Y\text{ contains }(0,1);
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \operatorname{rank}B_Y=2;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \rho_Y\text{ is high-order/fitted from }s_9^Y;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
5.\ E_2^Y\text{ is locally free vertical.}
}
\]

Any of these kills or seriously weakens the current \(Y12\) route.

is the desired condition.

---

# 1. The reduced fiber matrix

We are auditing

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right]
\subset \mathbf C^2,
\]

where the fiber coordinates are

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y),
\qquad
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The rank-one \(Y12\) channel survives iff

\[
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The main simplification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{if the only lower live input to the primitive \(Y12\) sector is one-dimensional \(s_7\), then }B_Y\text{ is graph-like.}
}
\]

So the key finite issue is not “does the lower connection touch \(Y12\)?” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{does the lower/actual-source model introduce more than one independent direction in }(s_7,s_9^Y)?
}
\]

---

# 2. Lower connection uniqueness lemma

Let

\[
L_{7\to Y}
\]

be the primitive \(Y12\)-sector part of the lower connection from order seven to order nine:

\[
L_{7\to Y}:\mathcal C_7^{\live}\to \mathcal Y_{12}^{(9)}.
\]

If the order-seven live quotient is one-dimensional,

\[
\mathcal C_7^{\live}=\mathbf C[\mathsf C_7],
\]

then

\[
\operatorname{im}L_{7\to Y}
\]

is at most one-dimensional.

Therefore the lower connection contributes at most one graph line

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
}
\]

in the fiber.

More explicitly, define

\[
\rho_Y
=
\frac{\ell_9^Y(L_{7\to Y}[\mathsf C_7])}{\ell_7([\mathsf C_7])},
\]

after the chosen normalization

\[
\ell_7([\mathsf C_7])=1.
\]

Then lower coherent order-seven motion satisfies

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7.
\]

This is safe if

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A
\]

and \(\rho_Y\) is formal/low-order, not fitted from actual order-nine data.

---

# 3. Lower-order terms \(T_{<7}\)

A lower-order term can affect the \(Y12\)-fiber only in two ways.

## Safe case

It first feeds the unique live order-seven coordinate, then continues through the same graph:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

This is still safe.

## Fatal case

It creates an independent primitive \(Y12\) order-nine component without the same \(s_7\) antecedent:

\[
(0,1)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7},
\]

or it creates a second graph line

\[
(1,\rho_Y')
\qquad
\rho_Y'\neq \rho_Y.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2
\]

and vertical freedom appears.

So the lower-order audit is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})\le 1
}
\]

and, if nonzero,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
=
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

---

# 4. Actual-source tangents and second curvature

Under the source-linear origin filter,

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
\]

This remains true even for second derivatives such as

\[
\partial_\alpha^2u,\quad \partial_m\partial_\alpha u,\quad \partial_\lambda^2u,
\]

provided the parameters only change EF weights, phases, scales, chart placement, or gamma/background terms.

They are parameter-curvature terms, not primitive

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

terms.

Thus these pieces should not contribute columns to \(B_Y\).

The only failure is if an admitted parameter changes \(N_0\), same-point whitening geometry, or the primitive normal-form construction itself.

---

# 5. \(E_2^Y\) columns

The \(E_2\)-contribution must be handled carefully.

A fixed \(E_2^Y\) component shifts

\[
\eta_{\fixed}
\]

and does **not** enter \(B_Y\) as a tangent column.

A residual-controlled \(E_2^Y\) component belongs to the upper error, not to \(B_Y\).

A finite-dimensional coherent \(E_2^Y\) amplitude space is not automatically a local tangent. It belongs to the amplitude-Schur stage:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
\left(
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma),
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\right).
\]

The fatal local case is only:

\[
\boxed{
(0,1)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}
}
\]

with extraction-scale local freedom.

A graph-like \(E_2^Y\) tangent

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

is safe.

A second independent graph slope is fatal because it spans the fiber with the first graph line.

---

# 6. Nuisance quotient

The singular nuisance quotient is safe if

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0
\]

or, at worst, if it is contained in the same graph line

\[
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

It is fatal if it contains the vertical:

\[
(0,1)\in\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

That would mean the singular quotient kills the primitive \(Y12\) direction.

Thus live \(Y12\) requires:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
(0,1)\notin\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Compressed theorem

The local \(Y12\)-normality theorem can now be stated very compactly:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(B_Y\)-Rank-One Graph Theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

with

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=(0,1)\neq0.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\bigr)^2
\ge
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}
\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So the \(Y12\) fiber has a polynomial normal direction.

---

# 8. Resulting forbidden covector

The graph-killing covector is

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

Then

\[
\ell_Y(\operatorname{ran}B_Y)=0,
\]

and D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G),
\]

where

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

---

The finite audit is now reduced to five concrete checks:

1.\ \lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}
}

so \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist with polynomial norm.

---

# 1. Why \(\rho_Y\) matters

The graph-killing covector is

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So the effective wall depends on

\[
\rho_Y.

If \(\rho_Y\) is a formal coefficient determined by the lower singular connection, then the wall is honest. If \(\rho_Y\) is chosen after seeing the actual pair \((s_7,s_9^Y)\), then

\[

\]

may vanish by definition, and the wall can become tautological.

Thus the critical audit is:

\[

\]

---

# 2. Good definition of \(\rho_Y\)

A safe definition is:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y
=
\ell_9^Y\!\left(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7\right),
}
\]

with normalization

\[
\ell_7(e_7)=1,
\qquad
\ell_9^Y(e_Y)=1.
\]

Here

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}
\]

is the **formal primitive \(Y12\)-fiber component** of the lower coherent connection.

This is safe because \(\rho_Y\) is computed from the connection operator, not from actual data.

The bad definition would be

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y^{\bad}
=
\frac{s_9^Y(q''_{\can})}{s_7(q''_{\can})}.
}
\]

That would make the residual vanish on actual rows by construction, and it would likely make

\[
\rho_\Gamma
\]

depend on actual order-nine data.

So the theorem must explicitly require:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\neq s_9^Y/s_7\text{ fitted from }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Low-order requirement

The safe coefficient must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}.
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal A_{\low}\) is the low Gram/whitening algebra generated by quantities such as

\[
g,\quad h,\quad \sqrt g,\quad \sqrt h,\quad \text{fixed carrier constants},
\]

with

\[
g=\frac{q_0}{\pi},
\qquad
h=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac13q_0^3+\frac16q''_{\can}\right),
\]

up to the current normalization conventions.

The forbidden dependencies are

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y\neq0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y\neq0,
}
\]

or dependence on actual order-nine source data

\[
s_9^Y(q''_{\can}).
\]

So the required audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{s_9^Y}\rho_Y=0.
}
\]

If these hold, \(\rho_Y\) is low-order and formal.

---

# 4. Consequence for \(\rho_\Gamma\)

Once \(\rho_Y\) is safe, define

\[
\rho_\Gamma
=
\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

The wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

Thus \(\rho_\Gamma\) is low-order if

\[
\alpha_9,\beta_9,c_7,\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}
\]

and

\[
\alpha_9
\]

is bounded away from zero:

\[
|\alpha_9|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_\Gamma=0,
\qquad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_\Gamma=0.
}
\]

This is essential for the wall finite-type theorem. If \(\rho_\Gamma\) depends on \(\Gamma_{G,2}\), then the wall

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

may be tautological.

---

# 5. Rank-one wall after formal \(\rho_Y\)

Assume:

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

with formal low-order \(\rho_Y\). Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y)^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

If

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

then

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So the vertical \(Y12\) D4 channel survives at polynomial strength.

D4 yields

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

This is now an honest rank-one wall.

---

# 6. What if \(\rho_Y\) has two formal values?

Suppose lower connection or \(E_2^Y\) supplies two graph slopes:

\[
(1,\rho_{Y,1}),\qquad (1,\rho_{Y,2}),
\qquad
\rho_{Y,1}\neq\rho_{Y,2}.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{span}\{(1,\rho_{Y,1}),(1,\rho_{Y,2})\}=\mathbf C^2.
\]

The vertical direction is absorbed:

\[
(0,1)\in\operatorname{ran}B_Y.
\]

This kills the \(Y12\) channel.

Therefore the coherent \(Y12\)-fiber model must have **one** slope, not several.

The exact condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
\le1.
}
\]

If nonzero, its image must be

\[
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

This is the one-slope theorem.

---

# 7. \(E_2^Y\) and \(\rho_Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) contributes only a fixed basepoint

\[
\eta_{\fixed},
\]

it does not affect \(\rho_Y\).

If it contributes a graph-like tangent

\[
(1,\rho_E),
\]

then it is safe only if

\[
\rho_E=\rho_Y.
\]

If

\[
\rho_E\neq \rho_Y,
\]

then \(E_2^Y\) supplies a second graph slope and kills the local \(Y12\) normal direction.

If \(E_2^Y\) contributes vertical

\[
(0,1),
\]

it also kills the channel.

Thus the \(E_2\) condition can be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
}
\]

or it must be absent/fixed/residual-controlled rather than tangent.

---

# 8. Revised local theorem

The strongest current local theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Formal One-Slope \(Y12\)-Fiber Theorem.}
}
\]

On each good transition–singular patch:

1. the live dual pair \((\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)\) exists with polynomial conditioning;

2. the actual-source/nuisance fiber image satisfies

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})
=0;
\]

3. the remaining coherent image satisfies

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y);
\]

4. the slope is formal and low-order:

\[
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{s_9^Y}\rho_Y=0;
\]

5. \(|\rho_Y|\le Q^A\).

Then D4 gives the rank-one wall

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G),
\]

with

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

---

# 9. Current next finite audits

The remaining local audits are now minimal:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \text{compute/formalize }\rho_Y
=
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \text{prove }\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \text{prove no second graph slope from }\mathcal T_{<7}\text{ or }E_2^Y.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \text{prove no vertical column from }E_2^Y\text{ or }\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
5.\ \text{prove }\rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low}.
}
\]

If these pass, the local \(Y12\)-normal layer is ready for the wall finite-type/amplitude-Schur layer.

## Agent 1 — operational one-slope audit for \(\rho_Y\)

I will keep the current naming:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{the lower }Y12\text{ fiber graph slope},
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The next step is to make the one-slope theorem testable. The key object is the graph residual

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(v):=\ell_9^Y(v)-\rho_Y\ell_7(v).
}
\]

The actual-source model is safe in the \(Y12\)-fiber precisely when

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)=0
\]

for every allowed local generator \(v\), while

\[
\mathcal K_Y(e_Y)=1.
\]

---

# 1. Define \(\rho_Y\) from the formal lower connection

Let

\[
e_7:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
e_Y:=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Assume the live dual pair has been normalized so that

\[
\ell_7(e_7)=1,\qquad \ell_9^Y(e_7)=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(e_Y)=0,\qquad \ell_9^Y(e_Y)=1.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}
\]

be the formal lower connection from the order-seven live carrier into the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber. Then define

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y:=\ell_9^Y\!\left(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7\right).
}
\]

This is the safe definition because it depends only on the formal connection, not on actual order-nine source data.

The unsafe definition is

\[
\rho_Y^{\bad}
=
\frac{s_9^Y(q''_{\can})}{s_7(q''_{\can})}.
\]

That fitted definition would make the wall tautological and must not be used.

---

# 2. The universal graph test

For any allowed generator \(v\), project to the fiber:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}v=(a(v),b(v))
=
(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

Then \(v\) lies on the coherent graph iff

\[
\boxed{
b(v)-\rho_Ya(v)=0.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(v)=0.
}
\]

Thus the one-slope theorem reduces to checking the finite list:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)=0
\quad
\text{for all }
v\in
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

The fatal cases are exactly:

\[
a(v)=0,\quad b(v)\ne0
\]

vertical freedom, or

\[
a(v)\ne0,\quad b(v)/a(v)\ne \rho_Y
\]

a second graph slope.

---

# 3. Source-linear and parameter-curvature generators

For source-linear tangents

\[
v\in\mathcal T_{\par},
\]

we expect

\[
a(v)=0,\qquad b(v)=0.
\]

So

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)=0
\]

trivially.

The same applies to actual EF second curvature

\[
v\in\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\]

provided actual parameters only change EF weights, phases, scales, chart placement, or gamma/background data. These are parameter-curvature terms, not primitive

\[
D^2N_0
\]

source-Hessian terms.

So the audit for these columns is simply:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

If either has nonzero vertical image, \(Y12\) may be actual-source curvature and the D4 channel dies.

---

# 4. Lower connection and lower-order terms

For the formal order-seven live carrier,

\[
v=\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7,
\]

we have by definition

\[
a(v)=1,\qquad b(v)=\rho_Y.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)=0.
\]

Lower-order terms are safe if their primitive \(Y12\)-fiber image factors through the same order-seven coordinate. In other words, for every

\[
v\in \mathcal T_{<7},
\]

we need

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(v)=\rho_Y\ell_7(v).
}
\]

This is a precise one-line test.

If there exists

\[
v\in\mathcal T_{<7}
\]

with

\[
\ell_9^Y(v)-\rho_Y\ell_7(v)\ne0,
\]

then lower-order coherence introduces a second graph slope or a vertical component, and the rank-one \(Y12\) channel dies locally.

So the lower-order theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{<7})=0.
}
\]

---

# 5. \(E_2^Y\) one-slope audit

For \(E_2^Y\), the same residual applies.

Allowed \(E_2^Y\)-tangent directions are safe iff

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2})=0.
}
\]

Interpretation:

- Fixed \(E_2^Y\): not a tangent, so it is absorbed into \(\eta_{\fixed}\).
- Graph-like \(E_2^Y\): \(\mathcal K_Y=0\), safe.
- Vertical \(E_2^Y\): \(a=0,b\ne0\), so \(\mathcal K_Y\ne0\), fatal.
- Second graph slope: \(\mathcal K_Y\ne0\), fatal locally.
- Finite-dimensional coherent amplitude but no local tangent: not a fiber issue; it moves to amplitude-Schur.

Thus the local no-free condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\mathrm{local}})=0.
}
\]

The amplitude-level condition is separate:

\[
\boxed{
H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)\notin \mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
}
\]

in the smooth pre-extraction \(L^2\) sense.

---

# 6. Nuisance compatibility

The nuisance quotient is safe if

\[
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0
\]

and

\[
e_Y\notin\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathbf C e_7.
\]

The first condition says nuisance directions do not create forbidden vertical residuals.

The second says the live \(Y12\) carrier survives after quotienting.

A compact sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for the live pair after projecting off nuisance and wrong-signature directions.

---

# 7. The wall after \(\mathcal K_Y\)

Once the graph residual is defined, D4 gives:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

And

\[
\boxed{
\rho_\Gamma
=
\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
}
\]

The rank-one wall is therefore

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

---

# 8. Low-order requirement for \(\rho_Y\)

The graph residual is useful only if

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is formal and low-order. Required:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{s_9^Y}\rho_Y=0.
}
\]

Then

\[
\rho_\Gamma
\]

is low-order if

\[
\alpha_9,\beta_9,c_7\in\mathcal A_{\low}
\]

and

\[
|\alpha_9|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This low-order condition is necessary for the wall finite-type theorem and for bounded-charge O2/O3.

---

# 9. Final operational theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Operational \(Y12\)-One-Slope Theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal K_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7,
\qquad
\rho_Y=\ell_9^Y(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7).
\]

Assume:

1. live duals \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist with polynomial norm;

2. \(\mathcal K_Y\) annihilates all allowed local generators:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\mathrm{local}}
)=0;
\]

3. \(e_Y\) survives:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(e_Y)=1;
\]

4. \(\rho_Y\) is formal low-order.

Then the local \(Y12\)-fiber is graph-like and nonvertical, and D4 gives the rank-one wall

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

This is the cleanest local downstream statement I can currently make.

---

# 10. Remaining blockers

The theorem can fail only if:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\text{ do not exist with polynomial conditioning;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\mathrm{local}})\ne0;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})\ne0;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{<7})\ne0;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\text{ is fitted/high-order rather than formal low-order.}
}
\]

These are now precise finite audits.

## Agent 1 — Live dual pair and one-slope \(Y12\)-fiber Schur

### Nomenclature I will use

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the lower \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope:

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma
\]

is the effective wall ratio:

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

\[
R_{\fold}(x)
\]

is the AFE/O2O3 fold-substitution ratio:

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The graph-subtracted raw wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

I will not use \(r\) for any of these.

The prompt’s key correction is that downstream D4 is now a nested Schur tower: first a local \(Y12\)-fiber Schur test, then an amplitude Schur test, then possibly a wall-trap Schur test. 

---

# 1. Realized singular quotient \(H_Y\)

Let

\[
H_Y
\]

be the realized singular order-nine carrier quotient after labelled transition and singular realization.

The finite norm should be a Riesz-normalized carrier norm satisfying

\[
\boxed{
\kappa(H_Y)\le Q^A.
}
\]

Define the live carrier vectors

\[
e_7
:=
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

\[
e_Y
:=
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The downstream \(Y12\)-fiber is valid only if the pair

\[
(e_7,e_Y)
\]

survives singular nuisance quotienting with polynomial conditioning.

---

# 2. Live dual covectors

We need bounded covectors

\[
\ell_7,\qquad \ell_9^Y
\]

such that

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_7)=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_Y)=(0,1).
\]

These define the quotient-invariant fiber coordinates

\[
s_7(v)=\ell_7(v),
\qquad
s_9^Y(v)=\ell_9^Y(v).
\]

This is cleaner than treating \((s_7,s_9^Y)\) as informal coordinates.

The graph-killing covector is then

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

---

# 3. The live-pair Gram test

Let

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
\]

be the subspace to kill before extracting the live pair. It includes

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

source-linear wrong-signature terms, chart/whitening terms not in the primitive \(Y12\) signature, and nonlive retained singular directions.

Project

\[
e_7,e_Y
\]

onto

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}^{\perp}.
\]

Let the projected vectors still be denoted

\[
\widetilde e_7,\qquad \widetilde e_Y.
\]

Define

\[
G_{\live}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_Y\rangle\\
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The strong local condition is

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

For the rank-one \(Y12\) channel, the weaker condition is enough:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
}
\]

If this weaker condition fails, the \(Y12\) carrier is absorbed by nuisance/order-seven directions and the rank-one channel is dead.

---

# 4. Local \(Y12\)-fiber matrix \(B_Y\)

Once \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) are fixed, define

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(v)
=
(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

The allowed actual-source/nuisance fiber matrix is

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right]
\subset \mathbb C^2.
\]

The vertical \(Y12\) direction is

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The local \(Y12\) normal channel survives iff

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Expected vanishings

The refined primitive \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=DW_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ 9,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Under labelled/origin-faithful transition, source-linear and EF-linear terms do not hit this fiber. Therefore the expected vanishings are

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0
}
\]

on the live primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

These fail only if:

- an admitted parameter changes \(N_0\) or same-point primitive whitening geometry;
- actual EF second curvature contains primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin terms;
- the singular quotient over-collapses the \(Y12\) origin-pair sector.

---

# 6. One-slope condition

The lower coherent terms are allowed to touch \(Y12\), but only graph-like:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

This means the actual-source lower connection may impose

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

That is not fatal. It only changes the wall coefficient.

The same condition must include any local \(E_2^Y\) tangent:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

A fixed \(E_2^Y\) basepoint is not a tangent and goes into \(\eta_{\fixed}\). A finite-dimensional coherent \(E_2^Y\) amplitude space is not automatically a local tangent; it belongs to the amplitude Schur layer.

---

# 7. Fiber Schur conclusion

If

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y\subseteq\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

then

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\bigr)^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

So if

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\bigr)\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

This proves the local fiber Schur layer.

---

# 8. D4 wall from the graph-killing covector

With

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7,
\]

we have

\[
\ell_Y(\operatorname{ran}B_Y)=0.
\]

D4 gives

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}
=
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Therefore

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
}
\]

This is the rank-one local \(Y12\) wall.

---

# 9. Exact failure modes

The local \(Y12\)-fiber layer fails in exactly these cases.

## Failure 1 — no live dual pair

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})<Q^{-A}.
\]

Then \(e_7,e_Y\) are not independently detectable with polynomially bounded covectors.

## Failure 2 — \(e_Y\) killed or absorbed

\[
e_Y\in\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7.
\]

Then the \(Y12\) carrier is not singular-live modulo nuisance/order-seven.

## Failure 3 — vertical actual-source direction

\[
(0,1)\in \operatorname{ran}B_Y.
\]

This means the actual-source model has a free \(Y12\) vertical amplitude.

## Failure 4 — two independent graph slopes

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2.
\]

This can happen if both

\[
(1,\rho_{Y,1})
\quad\text{and}\quad
(1,\rho_{Y,2})
\]

appear with

\[
\rho_{Y,1}\neq\rho_{Y,2}.
\]

Then the vertical direction is spanned.

## Failure 5 — fitted/high-order \(\rho_Y\)

If

\[
\rho_Y
=
\frac{s_9^Y(q''_{\can})}{s_7(q''_{\can})}
\]

or depends on actual order-nine source data, \(\Gamma_{G,2}\), or \(q^{(4)}\), then the wall can become tautological.

Good condition:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y=0,
\qquad
\partial_{s_9^Y}\rho_Y=0.
}
\]

---

# 10. Local theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{LiveY12DualPair + OneSlopeFiberSchur.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(e_7,e_Y\) admit bounded dual covectors:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A};
\]

2. actual-source/nuisance fiber image is one-slope graph-like:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y);
\]

3. \(\rho_Y\) is formal, polynomially bounded, and low-order.

Then

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

is a local forbidden covector, and D4 gives the rank-one wall

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

This completes the local fiber layer, conditional on the finite Gram and one-slope audits.

## Agent 1 — reducing the live-dual Gram to primitive carrier tests

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The current architecture treats D4 as a nested Schur tower: first local \(Y12\)-fiber Schur, then amplitude Schur, then possibly trap Schur. The local fiber layer requires a bounded live dual pair \((\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)\). 

---

# 1. Live pair after quotienting nuisance

Let

\[
e_7=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

\[
e_Y=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
\]

be the subspace killed before extracting the live pair:

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal S_{\wrong}
+
\mathcal S_{\nonlive}.
\]

Here \(\mathcal S_{\wrong}\) contains source-linear, gamma/background, chart, non-\(D^2N_0\)-origin, wrong-parity, and wrong-whitening-slot directions; \(\mathcal S_{\nonlive}\) contains singular carrier directions not assigned to the live pair.

Project:

\[
\widetilde e_7=P_{\mathcal B_{\live}^{\perp}}e_7,
\qquad
\widetilde e_Y=P_{\mathcal B_{\live}^{\perp}}e_Y.
\]

The live Gram is

\[
G_{\live}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_Y\rangle\\
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_7\rangle
&
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\widetilde e_Y\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

We need

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

---

# 2. Sufficient primitive projections

A clean way to prove this is to construct two bounded primitive projections

\[
\pi_7,\qquad \pi_Y
\]

on the realized singular quotient such that:

\[
\pi_7(e_7)=1,\qquad \pi_7(e_Y)=0,\qquad \pi_7(\mathcal B_{\live})=0,
\]

\[
\pi_Y(e_7)=0,\qquad \pi_Y(e_Y)=1,\qquad \pi_Y(\mathcal B_{\live})=0.
\]

Then set

\[
\ell_7=\pi_7,\qquad \ell_9^Y=\pi_Y.
\]

The required Gram lower bound follows immediately from boundedness of the projections:

\[
\|\ell_7\|+\|\ell_9^Y\|\le Q^A.
\]

So the live dual-pair theorem reduces to two primitive tests:

\[
\boxed{
\text{order-seven carrier survives and is distinguishable;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{primitive \(Y12\)-signature carrier survives and is distinguishable.}
}
\]

This is stronger and more transparent than checking a raw \(2\times2\) Gram.

---

# 3. Order-seven live test

The order-seven carrier \(e_7\) must survive the singular quotient:

\[
e_7\notin \mathcal B_{\live}.
\]

More quantitatively:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,\mathcal B_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}\|e_7\|.
\]

This is likely inherited from the existing clean D4 carrier setup: \([\mathsf C_7]\) is the order-seven live carrier receiving the \(c_7\Gamma_G\) term, with \(c_7\neq0\).

Failure here would mean the D4 clean matrix is not actually live after transition to the singular carrier quotient.

This failure is not subtle: if \(e_7\) dies, the \(Y12\)-fiber coordinates cannot be defined as intended.

---

# 4. Primitive \(Y12\)-signature live test

The \(Y12\) carrier must survive in the refined primitive signature sector

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=DW_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ 9,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

The needed condition is

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
\]

Signature-faithfulness gives this if:

\[
e_Y
\]

has nonzero primitive \(Y12\)-signature component, while

\[
e_7,\ \mathcal B_{\live}
\]

have no component in that refined signature.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}e_Y\neq0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}(\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)=0.
}
\]

This is the core finite test.

---

# 5. Why \(e_7\) should not collide with \(e_Y\)

The order-seven carrier and \(Y12\) carrier differ in multiple independent labels:

\[
\text{order }7\quad\text{versus}\quad\text{order }9,
\]

\[
\text{non-\(Y12\) carrier}\quad\text{versus}\quad D^2N_0\text{-origin mixed-12 carrier},
\]

\[
\text{different whitening/source-grade labels}.
\]

So, under a labelled transition and signature-faithful singular realization,

\[
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_Y\rangle
\]

should vanish or be polynomially small in the graded quotient.

Then

\[
G_{\live}
\]

is diagonally controlled:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})
\asymp
\min\{\|\widetilde e_7\|^2,\|\widetilde e_Y\|^2\}.
\]

Thus live-dual construction is reduced to live-ness of each individual carrier.

---

# 6. What can break the live dual pair?

There are four failure modes.

## Failure A — singular quotient kills \(e_Y\)

\[
e_Y\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then Bottleneck-C/D4 \(Y12\) is not singular-live.

## Failure B — singular quotient identifies \(e_Y\) with \(e_7\)

\[
e_Y\in\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7.
\]

Then \(\ell_9^Y\) cannot be defined independently. The two-coordinate fiber collapses to one coordinate.

## Failure C — wrong-signature space contains primitive \(Y12\)

This means the origin-pair grading is not faithful:

\[
D^2N_0\text{-origin }Y12
\]

is identified with source-linear, whitening-tail, chart, or gamma terms.

Then signature proof fails and one must use direct Schur residual.

## Failure D — ill-conditioned singular basis

Even if algebraically independent, the Gram may have

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ll Q^{-A}.
\]

Then \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist but have super-polynomial norm, making the atomic upper unusable.

---

# 7. Operational theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Primitive Live-Pair Theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. labelled transition transports formed carriers and preserves refined origin-pair labels;

2. singular realization is signature-faithful near

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\quad [\mathsf C_{Y12}];
\]

3. \(e_7\) is live:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,\mathcal B_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}\|e_7\|;
\]

4. \(e_Y\) is live relative to \(e_7\):

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|;
\]

5. the singular carrier norm is polynomially conditioned.

Then bounded covectors \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist, and the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)
\]

is well-defined.

---

# 8. Next: one-slope graph audit using the live duals

Once \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\) exist, the actual-source image is tested by

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)=\ell_9^Y(v)-\rho_Y\ell_7(v).
\]

The one-slope theorem asks:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})=0,
\]

where

\[
\rho_Y
=
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^{Y}e_7)
\]

is formal and low-order.

This produces

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

and the D4 wall

\[
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

---

# 9. Current status

The live-dual-pair problem is finite and decomposes into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{order-seven live-ness;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(Y12\)-signature live-ness;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{

}
\]

\[

}
\]

# 1. The hidden issue: one quotient, not two

It is not enough to know separately that:

\[
e_7\neq0
\]

in some order-seven carrier quotient, and

\[
e_Y\neq0
\]

in some primitive \(Y12\)-signature quotient.

We need both to survive in the **same realized singular carrier quotient**, with a common nuisance subspace

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
\]

and a common norm.

The required statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim
\operatorname{span}
\{[e_7],[e_Y]\}
=2
\quad
\text{in }
H_{\sing}^{\live}:=H_{\sing}/\mathcal B_{\live}.
}
\]

If \(e_7\) and \(e_Y\) are live in different quotients but collide in the combined singular quotient, then the fiber coordinates \((s_7,s_9^Y)\) are not valid.

So the actual finite condition is the **joint live-pair condition**:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
}
\]

---

# 2. What \(\mathcal B_{\live}\) may include

The common nuisance subspace should include:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

true singular nuisance;

\[
\mathcal S_{\wrong}
\]

wrong-signature terms, including EF-linear, gamma/background, chart-origin, moving-whitening-tail, wrong parity, wrong same-point/transport type;

\[
\mathcal S_{\nonlive}
\]

nonlive retained carrier classes;

and possibly source-linear modeled actual-source directions that are irrelevant to the live pair.

But it must **not** include:

\[
e_7,\qquad e_Y,
\]

nor any linear combination that identifies them.

Thus the quotient design condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal B_{\live}\cap\operatorname{span}\{e_7,e_Y\}=0.
}
\]

Quantitatively:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
(\operatorname{span}\{e_7,e_Y\},\mathcal B_{\live})
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Signature separation of \(e_7\) and \(e_Y\)

A sufficient proof is a two-signature decomposition:

\[
H_{\sing}^{\live}
=
H_7\oplus H_Y\oplus H_{\rest},
\]

where

\[
e_7\in H_7,
\qquad
e_Y\in H_Y,
\]

and

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}\subset H_{\rest}.
\]

For \(e_Y\), the refined signature is

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=DW_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}\,12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ 9,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

For \(e_7\), we need an analogous signature, say

\[
\Sigma(7)
=
(\text{order }7,\ \text{carrier }[\mathsf C_7],\ \text{its source-grade/reflection label},\ \text{its whitening status}).
\]

The important point is:

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(7)\neq\Sigma(Y12)
}
\]

in at least these labels:

\[
\text{order }7\neq9,

\[
[\mathsf C_7]\neq[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

\[
o_N\neq D^2N_0\text{ mixed-12 }Y12
\]

unless the order-seven carrier also has a hidden \(Y12\)-like origin, which would be a serious collision.

\[
\langle \widetilde e_7,\widetilde e_Y\rangle=0
\]

or at least polynomially small.

Then

\[
G_{\live}
\]

is well-conditioned if each diagonal term is.

---

# 4. Order-seven live-ness

Order-seven live-ness asks:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,\mathcal B_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}\|e_7\|.
}
\]

This is not identical to old \(C_7\)-live-ness in the transition chart. It must hold after:

\[
\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab},
\qquad
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing},
\qquad
/\mathcal B_{\live}.
\]

Failure modes:

1. \(e_7\) becomes singular nuisance.
2. \(e_7\) is absorbed by source-linear actual-source directions.
3. \(e_7\) is killed by a gauge/normalization relation.
4. labelled transition does not preserve its carrier identity.

If order-seven live-ness fails, the two-coordinate graph model collapses. However, the pure \(Y12\) rank-one channel might still survive without \(s_7\) if lower graph coherence is absent. In that fallback, set \(\rho_Y=0\) and only use \(e_Y\). But then any \(\Gamma_G\)-subtraction via \(s_7\) is unavailable.

---

# 5. \(Y12\)-live-ness

\(Y12\)-live-ness asks:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|.
}
\]

This is the key rank-one condition.

The refined origin-pair label

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

is designed exactly to prove this: source-linear, chart, gamma, whitening-tail, and lower linear terms do not share this label.

The dangerous collisions are:

1. \(e_Y\in\mathcal N_{\sing}\): nuisance kills \(Y12\).
2. \(e_Y\in\mathbf C e_7+\mathcal B_{\live}\): \(Y12\) collapses into order-seven.
3. \(e_Y\) equals an allowed free \(E_2^Y\) tangent.
4. labelled/raw realization mismatch makes \(e_Y\) not represent the actual singular direction.

If none occur, \(\ell_9^Y\) exists.

---

# 6. Constructing the duals

Assume

\[
G_{\live}
\]

has polynomial lower bound.

Let

\[
\widetilde E
=
[\widetilde e_7\ \widetilde e_Y].
\]

Then the dual covectors are given explicitly by

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7\\
\ell_9^Y
\end{pmatrix}
=
G_{\live}^{-1}
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \widetilde e_7,\cdot\rangle\\
\langle \widetilde e_Y,\cdot\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Polynomial conditioning of

\[
G_{\live}^{-1}
\]

gives

\[
\|\ell_7\|+\|\ell_9^Y\|\le Q^A.
\]

This is the explicit finite construction.

---

# 7. Then the fiber graph audit is valid

Once the duals exist, define

\[
\mathcal K_Y(v)
=
\ell_9^Y(v)-\rho_Y\ell_7(v).
\]

The one-slope theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y
\left(
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\right)=0,
}
\]

with \(\rho_Y\) formal and low-order.

Then D4 gives:

\[
\mathcal K_Y(D4)
=
H
\left(
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Thus the live-dual theorem is the gateway to all later layers.

---

# 8. New fallback if only \(e_Y\) is live

If \(e_Y\) survives but \(e_7\) is ill-conditioned or killed, there is a weaker rank-one route:

define a covector

\[
\ell_Y
\]

such that

\[
\ell_Y(e_Y)=1,
\qquad
\ell_Y(\mathcal B_{\live})=0.
\]

Then D4 gives

\[
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

without graph subtraction.

This corresponds to

\[
\rho_Y=0.
\]

It is less refined but still potentially viable. It loses the ability to subtract coherent order-seven continuation, but it avoids dependence on \(e_7\).

So order-seven live-pair failure is not necessarily fatal if \(Y12\)-live-ness alone holds and lower graph coherence is absent or can be ignored.

---

# 9. Final live-dual theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Joint Live-Pair Theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
e_7=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
e_Y=\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].

\[
\mathcal B_{\live}
\]

be the common nuisance/wrong-signature/nonlive subspace.

If

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_7,\mathcal B_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}\|e_7\|,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|,
\]

then bounded covectors

\[
\ell_7,\ell_9^Y
\]

exist and define the \(Y12\)-fiber coordinates. If only the second condition holds without \(e_7\), a weaker \(Y12\)-only rank-one route remains possible with \(\rho_Y=0\).

---

# 10. Current status

The live-dual layer is now a precise finite Gram problem.

The next concrete audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(e_Y,\mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|e_Y\|?
}

Signature-faithfulness is the preferred proof. Direct Gram computation is the fallback.

}
\]

is enough.

If this fails, \(Y12\) is absorbed by nuisance or by the order-seven carrier, and the \(Y12\) rank-one channel is dead.

---

# 3. Fiber matrix \(B_Y\)

After defining \(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y\), set

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(v)=(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

Define the allowed-source fiber image

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right]
\subset \mathbb C^2.
\]

The vertical \(Y12\) direction is

\[
v_Y=(0,1).
\]

The fiber Schur condition is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(v_Y,\operatorname{ran}B_Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the local \(Y12\)-normality test.

---

# 4. Expected vanishings

With labelled transition preserving the primitive origin pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}),
\]

we expect:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0
}
\]

on the live primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

The rationale is that source-linear tangents, actual EF parameter curvature, gamma/background terms, chart curvature, and ordinary whitening-tail terms do not have primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin mixed-12 same-point one-whitening signature.

These vanishings fail only if the actual parameter family or singular realization recomputes \(N_0\) or collapses origin labels.

---

# 5. One-slope condition

Lower connection and lower-order coherent terms may touch the \(Y12\) fiber, but they are safe if they form a single nonvertical graph line:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

This gives actual-source coherence in the form

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

It is not fatal. It only changes the wall.

If instead the allowed image contains

\[
(0,1)
\]

or two independent graph lines

\[
(1,\rho_{Y,1}),\quad (1,\rho_{Y,2}),\qquad \rho_{Y,1}\neq \rho_{Y,2},
\]

then \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2\) and vertical \(Y12\)-freedom appears. That kills the rank-one \(Y12\) channel.

---

# 6. Fiber Schur lower bound

If

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

then

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\bigr)^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

Thus, if

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\bigl((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\bigr)\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

So a one-slope, polynomially bounded actual-source fiber image gives the desired local normal direction.

---

# 7. D4 output

D4 has

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}
=
H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Applying

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
}
\]

This is the local rank-one \(Y12\) wall.

---

# 8. Local theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{LiveY12DualPair + OneSlopeFiberSchur.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(e_7,e_Y\) admit bounded dual covectors:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A};
\]

2. labelled transition and singular realization preserve the primitive \(Y12\) origin-pair filtration;

3. actual-source/nuisance fiber image is one-slope:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y);
\]

4. \(\rho_Y\) is formal, not fitted from actual order-nine data, and

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

Then

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7
\]

is a local forbidden covector and D4 produces

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

---

# 9. Failure modes

The local \(Y12\)-fiber layer fails if:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})<Q^{-A};
\]

\[
e_Y\in \mathcal B_{\live}+\mathbf C e_7;
\]

\[
(0,1)\in \operatorname{ran}B_Y;
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2;
\]

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is fitted from actual \(s_9^Y/s_7\), depends on \(\Gamma_{G,2}\), \(q^{(4)}\), or actual order-nine source data;

or \(E_2^Y\) is locally free vertical.

These are now exact finite blockers.

---

# 10. Immediate next audits

The next mathematical checks are:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}?
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})\text{ is one-dimensional or zero?}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)?
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
4.\ \rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}\text{ and is not fitted?}
}
\]

If these pass, the local \(Y12\) layer is green and the proof moves to amplitude Schur and wall/trap Schur.

I’ll now attack the one-dimensionality of \(\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})\). The key is whether every lower contribution factors through the single live order-seven coordinate \(s_7\), rather than entering \(s_9^Y\) independently.

_Thought for 9s_

## Agent 1 — one-dimensionality of the lower \(Y12\)-fiber image

I will keep the established naming:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{the }Y12\text{-fiber graph slope},
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The current local \(Y12\) condition is:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

I now attack the lower part:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

This is the next finite blocker in the local \(Y12\)-fiber Schur layer. 

---

# 1. The single-live-coordinate principle

The lower connection can only produce multiple slopes if there is more than one independent lower live coordinate feeding the primitive \(Y12\) order-nine fiber.

Let

\[
\mathcal L_{<9}^{Y}
\]

be the lower carrier data that can feed the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber. The desired structural statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \mathcal L_{<9}^{Y}=1,
\qquad
\mathcal L_{<9}^{Y}=\mathbf C[\mathsf C_7].
}
\]

If true, any lower coherent contribution to \(s_9^Y\) factors through \(s_7\). Then the lower image in the fiber is necessarily a graph line:

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)=(t,\rho_Yt).
\]

So the local lower theorem reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{there is only one lower live antecedent for primitive }Y12,\text{ namely }[\mathsf C_7].
}
\]

---

# 2. Formal lower connection map

Define the lower-to-\(Y12\) connection

\[
\mathcal L_Y:
\mathcal L_{<9}^{Y}\to \mathbb C y_Y,
\]

where

\[
y_Y
\]

is the primitive \(Y12\) order-nine carrier direction.

The fiber coordinate map is

\[
v\mapsto (\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

For a lower datum \(u\in\mathcal L_{<9}^{Y}\), its fiber image is

\[
\left(
s_7(u),
s_9^Y(u)
\right)
=
\left(
\ell_7(u),
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal L_Yu)
\right).
\]

If

\[
u=a[\mathsf C_7],
\]

then

\[
s_7(u)=a,
\]

and

\[
s_9^Y(u)=a\rho_Y.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_7
=
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

This is the safe graph case.

---

# 3. Lower-order terms \(<7\)

The danger is that some lower term \(u_{<7}\) has

\[
s_7(u_{<7})=0
\]

but

\[
s_9^Y(u_{<7})\neq0.
\]

That would give a vertical column

\[
(0,1)
\]

and kill the \(Y12\) channel.

A second danger is that \(u_{<7}\) has

\[
s_7(u_{<7})\neq0,
\]

but

\[
\frac{s_9^Y(u_{<7})}{s_7(u_{<7})}\neq\rho_Y.
\]

That gives a second graph slope and again spans the full fiber.

Therefore the exact lower-order condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal L_Yu_{<7})
=
\rho_Y\ell_7(u_{<7})
\quad
\text{for all }u_{<7}\in\mathcal T_{<7}.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\mathcal K_Y:=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
}
\]

---

# 4. Why \(<7\) terms should vanish or factor through \(s_7\)

Most lower-order terms should have no primitive \(Y12\)-signature at all, because \(Y12\) has the refined origin pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}),
\]

mixed branch \(12\), same-point status, one whitening slot, and order nine.

Lower terms \(<7\) are expected to be either:

1. source-linear/gamma/chart terms, hence invisible in \(Y12\);

2. lower normal-form terms without the \(D^2N_0\)-mixed-12 same-point signature;

3. gauge/normalization artifacts killed by \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\);

4. lower coherent data that first contributes to the live order-seven class, then continues via \(\rho_Y\).

So the natural structural theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7}
\subseteq
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_7.
}
\]

If this holds, one-slope follows.

---

# 5. Potential failure: hidden lower Bottleneck-C antecedent

The one real danger is a hidden lower Bottleneck-C antecedent: a lower-order carrier that is not \([\mathsf C_7]\), but whose nonlinear promotion produces the primitive \(Y12\) sector at order nine.

This would be a term

\[
u_{<7}
\]

such that

\[
\ell_7(u_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal L_Yu_{<7})\neq0.
\]

This is exactly a vertical column.

So the finite audit should ask:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is there any lower source-Hessian/same-point-whitening seed feeding }Y12\text{ independently of }C_7?
}
\]

If yes, the \(Y12\)-fiber is vertically free and the current rank-one channel dies.

If no, lower-order terms are safe.

---

# 6. Connection to Bottleneck C

Earlier Bottleneck-C analysis singled out the promoted mixed-block carrier

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

as the first live obstruction carrier. That supports the idea that there is **no earlier independent lower seed** producing the same primitive \(Y12\)-signature.

If \(Y12\) is truly first live at order nine, then all \(<7\) antecedents should be absent or factor through the known order-seven carrier.

So we can phrase the needed finite result as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{No earlier independent \(Y12\)-antecedent.}
}
\]

This is a more precise reuse of the Bottleneck-C ledger.

---

# 7. The one-slope theorem

The lower one-slope theorem can now be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Lower \(Y12\)-One-Slope Theorem.}
}
\]

In the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber, the image of all lower coherent terms satisfies

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
=
0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

where

\[
\rho_Y
=
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal R_{7\to9}^Ye_7)
\]

is a formal connection coefficient.

Equivalently,

\[
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\mathcal K_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

---

# 8. Low-order requirement for \(\rho_Y\)

The theorem must include:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\Gamma_{G,2}}\rho_Y=0,
\quad
\partial_{q^{(4)}}\rho_Y=0,
\quad
\partial_{s_9^Y}\rho_Y=0.
}
\]

This prevents \(\rho_Y\) from being fitted from actual order-nine data.

If \(\rho_Y\) depends on

\[
s_9^Y(q''_{\can}),
\]

then

\[
\mathcal K_Y(q''_{\can})=0
\]

by construction, and the wall becomes tautological.

Thus \(\rho_Y\) must come from the formal connection operator, not from the actual row.

---

# 9. What this buys

If Lower \(Y12\)-One-Slope holds, and \(E_2^Y\) is not locally free, then

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y\subseteq\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y)^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

for polynomially bounded \(\rho_Y\).

D4 gives the rank-one local wall

\[
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

---

# 10. Remaining local blockers

The lower part now has only three blockers:

\[
\boxed{
1.\ \text{an independent lower vertical antecedent }u_{<7};}
\]

\[
\boxed{
2.\ \text{a second lower graph slope } \rho_Y'\neq \rho_Y;}
\]

\[
\boxed{
3.\ \rho_Y\text{ is fitted/high-order rather than formal low-order.}
}
\]

If these are excluded, the lower connection is safe.

The next local issue after this is \(E_2^Y\): whether it is absent/fixed/finite coherent, or locally free vertical.

I’ll now attack the \(E_2^Y\) local-fiber contribution, separate from amplitude-level \(E_2^Y\). The key is whether \(E_2\) adds a tangent column in \((s_7,s_9^Y)\), not merely a fixed or finite coherent amplitude.

## Agent 1 — local \(E_2^Y\) fiber audit

I will keep the established notation:

\[
\rho_Y=\text{the }Y12\text{-fiber graph slope},
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The current local theorem is:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

We already reduced the lower connection part to a one-slope condition. Now the remaining local danger is

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}.
}
\]

---

# 1. Separate local-fiber \(E_2^Y\) from amplitude-level \(E_2^Y\)

There are two different \(E_2^Y\) issues.

## Local fiber issue

Does \(E_2\) contribute a local tangent column in

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y)?
\]

That is,

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\subset \mathbf C^2.
\]

If this contains

\[
(0,1)
\]

or a second graph slope, the local \(Y12\) channel dies.

## Amplitude issue

Even if no local fiber tangent appears, \(E_2\) might define a finite-dimensional coherent amplitude space

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

that can absorb the wall function

\[
F_Y=H\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma).
\]

That is handled later by amplitude Schur.

So this attack concerns only:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}.
}
\]

---

# 2. Classification of \(E_2^Y\) in the local fiber

Let

\[
E_2^Y:=\Pi_YE_2
\]

be the primitive \(Y12\)-signature component, if any.

There are five cases.

---

## Case A — absent

\[
E_2^Y=0.
\]

Then

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}=0.
\]

Safe.

---

## Case B — fixed basepoint

\[
E_2^Y(p)=\eta_{\fixed}(p)y_Y
\]

with no admissible local variation.

This shifts

\[
s_9^Y
\]

by a fixed amount:

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

It does **not** contribute a tangent column to \(B_Y\).

Safe locally.

Amplitude-level question: none, unless \(\eta_{\fixed}\) varies in a coherent family across blocks.

---

## Case C — graph-like tangent

Allowed \(E_2\)-variation gives

\[
\delta(s_7,s_9^Y)=t(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\subseteq \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Safe locally. It only modifies the coherent graph.

---

## Case D — finite-dimensional coherent amplitude

\(E_2^Y\) produces functions

\[
\eta_a(p)y_Y
\]

over patches \(p\), with coefficients constrained to a finite-dimensional coherent space:

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\eta_a\}.
\]

This is **not** automatically a local fiber tangent. Locally, at a fixed patch, it looks vertical, but its coefficient is not locally free.

So it should not be inserted into \(B_Y\) as a local tangent unless the proof treats each patch independently after extraction.

Instead it belongs in amplitude Schur:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}).
\]

Safe locally, but may still block amplitude extraction if \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\) absorbs \(F_Y\).

---

## Case E — locally free vertical amplitude

There is an admissible variation at the extraction scale:

\[
\delta(s_7,s_9^Y)=(0,\eta(p))
\]

with \(\eta(p)\) arbitrary or sufficiently atom-local.

Then

\[
(0,1)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}.
\]

Fatal for the local rank-one \(Y12\) channel.

---

# 3. Operational local test

The local \(E_2\) theorem should say:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}=0.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})=0,
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal K_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

This is the exact finite local check.

If \(E_2^Y\) is finite-dimensional coherent but not locally free, then it should be excluded from

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\]

and placed into

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}.
\]

This distinction prevents over-quotienting.

---

# 4. How to identify local freedom

A coherent \(E_2^Y\)-space

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

is locally free if, on a typical extraction block \(\Omega\), its restriction has dimension comparable to the number of atoms in the block or has high leverage atom-local basis elements.

A sufficient safe condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \mathcal C_Y^{E_2}|_{\Omega}\le Q^C
}
\]

with leverage bound

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{p\in\Omega}
\frac{K_{\mathcal C}(p,p)}
{\dim(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}|_{\Omega})/\nu(\Omega)}
\le Q^C.
}
\]

Then \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\) is finite coherent, not locally free. It belongs to amplitude Schur.

A fatal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
K_{\mathcal C}(p,p)\text{ has atom-local spikes matching extraction atoms,}
}
\]

or

\[
\dim \mathcal C_Y^{E_2}|_{\Omega}
\asymp
\#\{\text{atoms in }\Omega\}.
\]

Then \(E_2^Y\) can absorb the wall locally.

---

# 5. Origin-filtering expectation

If \(E_2\) is linear EF completion/tail, then its primitive \(Y12\)-projection should be zero:

\[
E_2^Y=0.
\]

If moving whitening creates cross terms, their origin is

\[
D\mathcal W \cdot E_2,
\]

not

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}[D^2N_0].
\]

So, with the origin-pair label, they should not enter \(E_2^Y\).

Thus the expected case is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_2=0
}
\]

or residual-controlled, not locally tangent.

The only dangerous bucket remains an \(E_{\model}\) term that explicitly contains primitive

\[
D^2N_0
\]

mixed-12 same-point one-whitening data.

---

# 6. The \(E_2\)-local theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{NoLocalFree \(E_2^Y\) Fiber Theorem.}
}
\]

In the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber, the \(E_2\)-contribution is one of:

1. absent;
2. fixed basepoint \(\eta_{\fixed}\);
3. graph-like tangent in \(\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)\);
4. finite-dimensional coherent amplitude \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\) with polynomial leverage;
5. residual-controlled.

It is not an extraction-local vertical tangent.

Equivalently,

\[
(0,1)\notin
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local}
\not\supset
\mathbf C(1,\rho_E)
\quad
\text{for any }\rho_E\neq\rho_Y.
\]

Then \(E_2\) does not kill the local \(Y12\)-fiber channel.

---

# 7. What remains after this local theorem

If \(E_2^Y\) is absent/fixed/residual, nothing remains locally.

If \(E_2^Y\) is finite coherent, the next theorem is amplitude Schur:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{E_2})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_Y\|_{L^2(\nu)}^2.
}
\]

If \(E_2^Y\) is locally free, the rank-one \(Y12\) route dies and one must use rank-two D4, a different carrier, or higher order.

---

# 8. Final local status

The \(E_2\) local audit reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\local})=0
}
\]

or no local tangent exists.

The likely outcome, if \(E_2\) is true EF completion, is that \(E_2\) is absent/residual in the primitive \(Y12\) origin sector. The dangerous scenario is a model-completion term with free primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin \(Y12\) amplitude.
